NTT butterfly units with co-integrated masking gadgets — distinct from prior SNR-reduction approaches
PQC-internal HSM boot chain (not API-level) — Crypto4A commercial lead but limited patents
Three-party hybrid KEM combiner hardware — only software-level IP exists
PQC-TLS session table offload ASIC — TLS offload patents predate PQC algorithms
PUF-seeded ML-KEM key generation hardware — PUF patents exist but not PQC-bound

ideas.title: Chemistry-Agnostic Mechanically-Compliant Gradient Interlayer for Universal Solid-State Battery Interface Engineering
ideas.desc:
A persistent failure mode in all classes of solid-state batteries is the chemical and mechanical incompatibility at the electrode-electrolyte boundary. Existing interface engineering solutions are chemistry-specific: Toyota's sulfide-compatible fluoride coating (US20240021797A1), QuantumScape's garnet-specific catholyte (US11967676B2), and CATL's sulfide encapsulation all address only their respective electrolyte chemistries. No universal interlayer composition exists that maintains electrochemical stability across the 0-4.5V window against lithium metal while simultaneously providing mechanical compliance for both sulfide (soft, ~20 GPa shear modulus), oxide (hard, ~120 GPa), and halide (intermediate) solid electrolytes.

This invention discloses a gradient composition interlayer (GCIL) comprising a lithium-ion conducting composite whose elemental composition varies continuously from an anode-facing surface enriched in nitrogen-containing lithophilic species (Li3N-precursors, silicon nitride nanoparticles <50 nm, or lithium phosphate glasses) to a cathode-facing surface enriched in fluoride- and oxygen-based species (LiF, Li3PO4, or lithium borosilicate) that passivate oxide cathode surfaces. The gradient is achieved through a continuous-gradient vapor co-deposition process (reactive magnetron sputtering or spatial atomic layer deposition) operating at temperatures below 150°C, making it compatible with polymer, sulfide, and oxide electrolyte substrates. The GCIL has a total thickness of 20-200 nm and a Young's modulus gradient from <1 GPa at the anode interface to 20-80 GPa at the cathode interface, achieved by incorporating a viscoelastic ionomer interlayer (e.g., lithiated perfluorosulfonic acid or lithiated polysiloxane) within the gradient sequence.

The key differentiators are: (1) the composition is a true gradient rather than a bilayer or trilayer stack, eliminating sharp interfacial energy discontinuities that serve as delamination initiation sites; (2) the modulus gradient accommodates both cathode volume change (accommodated by the compliant anode-side) and lithium metal anode deposition/stripping (resisted by the stiff cathode-side); (3) the chemistry is explicitly designed for cross-electrolyte compatibility by selecting species (Li3N, LiF, Li3PO4) that are thermodynamically stable against Li metal, against LLZO, LGPS, Li3YCl6, and against NMC/LFP/LNMO cathodes simultaneously. This creates a genuinely chemistry-agnostic platform, which is the core of its chokepoint claim value.
ideas.claim: An electrochemical cell comprising: a positive electrode; a negative electrode; a solid-state electrolyte disposed between the positive and negative electrodes; and a gradient composition interlayer disposed between the solid-state electrolyte and at least one of the positive or negative electrode, wherein: the gradient composition interlayer comprises a lithium-ion conducting composite material having a continuously varying elemental composition from a first surface to a second surface; the first surface, proximal to the negative electrode, is enriched in nitrogen-containing lithophilic species selected from the group consisting of lithium nitride, silicon nitride, and lithium phosphate glass; the second surface, proximal to the positive electrode or solid-state electrolyte, is enriched in fluoride-containing and oxygen-containing species selected from the group consisting of lithium fluoride and lithium phosphate; and the gradient composition interlayer has a Young's modulus that varies continuously from less than 5 GPa at the first surface to between 20 and 100 GPa at the second surface.
depClaims: The electrochemical cell of claim 1, wherein the gradient composition interlayer is formed by a spatial atomic layer deposition process operating at a substrate temperature below 150°C.

The electrochemical cell of claim 1, wherein the solid-state electrolyte is selected from the group consisting of sulfide-based, oxide-based, halide-based, and polymer-based solid electrolytes, and the gradient composition interlayer maintains ionic conductivity >10⁻⁶ S/cm against each electrolyte class.

The electrochemical cell of claim 1, wherein a viscoelastic ionomer sublayer comprising lithiated perfluorosulfonic acid is embedded within the gradient composition interlayer at a position corresponding to 30-60% of the total interlayer thickness.
The electrochemical cell of claim 1, wherein the gradient composition interlayer has a total thickness between 20 nm and 500 nm and a lithium-ion transference number greater than 0.7.
A method of manufacturing the electrochemical cell of claim 1 using a roll-to-roll spatial ALD process with a throughput exceeding 0.1 m²/min.

The electrochemical cell of claim 1, wherein the gradient interlayer further comprises a self-healing sublayer comprising a lithium-reactive polymer capable of flowing into microcracks formed during cycling.
The electrochemical cell of claim 1, deployed in a system selected from the group consisting of: electric vehicles, grid energy storage, aerospace power systems, and implantable medical devices.
cpcP: H01M10/0562 (solid-state electrolyte), H01M4/1391 (treatment of electrodes for solid-state batteries)
cpcS: H01M50/46 (separators with surface coatings), C23C16/455 (ALD processes), H01M10/0585 (flat construction elements), H01M4/625 (coating of electrode materials)

refs.ref: Toyota US20240021797A1 - fluorine-based coating for sulfide electrolyte batteries (distinguishes: Toyota's coating is chemistry-specific to sulfide, single-composition, and not a continuous gradient)
WO2018183771A1 (Stanford/UCLA solid-state hybrid electrolytes) - polymer layer on garnet SSE (distinguishes: bilayer not gradient; modulus not engineered; not cross-chemistry)

US20160351973A1 (nano-engineered ALD coatings for battery electrodes) - ALD coatings on cathode materials (distinguishes: single-composition per layer, not gradient; does not address cross-electrolyte compatibility)

GM Global Technology Operations LLC 2023 (interlayer with distinct solid electrolyte material) - 0.1-8 micron interlayer of different SSE material (distinguishes: composition is uniform, not gradient; limited to oxide SSE; no modulus engineering)
QuantumScape US2021202982A1 - bonding layer for lithium electrochemical devices (distinguishes: specific to oxide garnet; no gradient; no cross-chemistry claim)

pa.dist: The continuous compositional gradient eliminating sharp chemical discontinuities, the modulus gradient specifically designed to span <1 GPa to 80 GPa across the interlayer thickness, and the explicit cross-chemistry stability claim (demonstrated against sulfide, oxide, and halide electrolytes simultaneously) are collectively novel over any identified prior art.

ideas.ftoJ: Toyota's sulfide-specific claims and QuantumScape's garnet-specific claims do not cover chemistry-agnostic gradient interlayers. The ALD process claims in prior art address single-composition coatings only. The specific combination of continuous gradient + cross-chemistry compatibility + modulus engineering represents clear white space. Recommend filing composition-of-matter claims on the gradient composite material and apparatus claims on the cell architecture.

ps.s101n: Apparatus and composition-of-matter claims; clearly patent-eligible subject matter; no Alice/Mayo concerns
ps.s102n: The specific combination of continuous elemental gradient + dual-endpoint chemistry (Li3N-type to LiF-type) + modulus gradient in a single layer is novel over identified prior art
ps.s103n: Non-obvious because PHOSITA would not be motivated to combine gradient composition deposition (thin-film display art) with solid-state battery interface engineering; the modulus gradient requirement is a counterintuitive constraint absent from battery literature
ps.s112n: Enabling: spatial ALD is a well-characterized technique; gradient co-deposition parameters are specifiable; compositions are commercially available; working examples are constructable

ideas.valM: Chemistry-agnostic interface platform is licensable across all SSB electrolyte chemistries; addressable market is all SSB manufacturers (not just one chemistry segment). At estimated $2B SSB market by 2030, a 5-8% interface IP royalty on 30% of market = $30-48M NPV at 15% discount rate. Strategic blocking value against all major incumbents adds premium.
ideas.cp: HIGH - Any SSB manufacturer using any electrolyte chemistry who needs a cross-compatible interface layer would need to design around or license this claim

ideas.title: Mechanochemically-Triggered In-Situ Halide Segregation System for Scalable Solid-State Battery Interface Formation
ideas.desc:
Recent breakthrough work (Lee et al., National Science Review 2026, PMC12860197) demonstrated that high-shear mixing of halide-containing solid electrolytes (e.g., Li6PS5Cl) with chalcogen cathode particles triggers selective halide liberation, forming epitaxial lithium halide nanolayers conformally coating active material surfaces. This represents a fundamental paradigm shift: instead of post-processing ALD coatings or pre-formed buffer layers, the interface engineering occurs during the electrode manufacturing step itself. However, no patents currently cover this mechano-chemical interface formation mechanism as applied to a broad range of solid electrolyte chemistries, active materials, and process conditions. The published research is largely limited to argyrodite-type sulfide electrolytes and chalcogen cathodes.

This invention expands the mechano-chemical halide segregation principle into a platform technology encompassing: (a) a range of halide-bearing solid electrolytes beyond Li6PS5Cl (including Li3YCl6, Li3InCl6, and mixed halide-sulfide systems); (b) a broader range of active materials including conventional NMC, LFP, and lithium metal anodes; (c) controlled mixing parameter space (rotational speed: 500-5000 rpm, duration: 1-60 min, temperature: -20°C to 80°C, atmosphere: dry argon or dry air) that determines halide segregation thickness, phase, and spatial distribution; and (d) a continuous-flow reactor design enabling roll-to-roll or conveyor-belt mechanochemical processing at industrial scale. The resulting interface structure consists of a conformal 2-10 nm LiCl, LiBr, or LiF nanolayer (depending on the halogen source) epitaxially matched to the active material surface, with an Cl:P or Br:S enrichment ratio of 2-3x relative to bulk electrolyte measured by EELS.

The critical inventive advance over the Lee et al. disclosure is the systematic process parameter mapping that enables rational design of the segregated halide layer composition, thickness, and phase, as well as extension to anode materials (lithium metal, silicon) and to halide-only electrolyte systems (Li3MCl6, M=Y, In, Er). This transforms a serendipitous discovery into a controllable, scalable manufacturing platform. The continuous-flow reactor design (a novel apparatus claim) enables metric-ton scale electrode preparation with consistent interface quality, which is not described in any identified patent or publication.
ideas.claim: A method for forming a lithium halide interface layer on an active material surface within a solid-state electrochemical cell, comprising: providing a mixture comprising (a) an active material for a positive or negative electrode, and (b) a halide-containing solid electrolyte comprising at least one halogen selected from chlorine, bromine, and fluorine; subjecting the mixture to mechanochemical processing at a rotational speed between 500 and 5000 rpm and a duration between 1 and 60 minutes; wherein the mechanochemical processing induces halide segregation from the solid electrolyte to form a conformal lithium halide nanolayer on the surface of the active material, the nanolayer having a thickness between 1 nm and 20 nm; and wherein the halogen-to-phosphorus or halogen-to-sulfur molar ratio at the active material surface is at least 1.5 times the bulk ratio of the solid electrolyte.
depClaims: The method of claim 1, wherein the halide-containing solid electrolyte is selected from: Li6PS5Cl, Li3YCl6, Li3InCl6, Li3ErCl6, and mixed halide-sulfide compositions of formula LixPySzCln.
The method of claim 1, wherein the active material is selected from: NMC (LiNixMnyCo1-x-yO2), LFP (LiFePO4), LNMO (LiNi0.5Mn1.5O4), and lithium metal foil.
The method of claim 1, wherein the mechanochemical processing is performed in a continuous-flow reactor enabling processing throughput exceeding 1 kg/hour of electrode mixture.
The method of claim 1, further comprising an in-situ monitoring step using Raman spectroscopy or X-ray diffraction to measure halide segregation extent in real time during processing.

A solid-state electrode composition comprising: an active material; a solid electrolyte; and a conformal lithium halide nanolayer on the surface of the active material formed by the method of claim 1, wherein the nanolayer suppresses interfacial charge transfer resistance by at least 50% relative to an uncoated control.
The method of claim 1, wherein the processing atmosphere is dry air with a dew point below -40°C, enabling scalable manufacturing without inert gas requirements.
The method of claim 1, wherein the temperature during mechanochemical processing is controlled between -10°C and 30°C to prevent thermal decomposition of sulfide electrolyte components.
cpcP: H01M4/1391 (electrode treatment for solid-state batteries), B02C17/00 (grinding/mixing processes)
cpcS: H01M10/0562, H01M4/625, H01M4/136, B01J19/18 (mechanochemical reactions), C01D15/00 (lithium compounds)

refs.ref: Lee et al. (2026, PMC12860197) - academic publication describing halide segregation phenomenon (distinguishes: this invention patents the controllable process parameters, reactor design, and extension to broader material classes; the publication itself is prior art only for its specific disclosed conditions)
Solid Power and CATL encapsulation patents for sulfide electrolyte stability - address moisture/air stability, not mechano-chemical interface formation

WO2018231731A2 (self-forming solid state batteries) - highly reductive metal reaction-based self-forming electrolyte (distinguishes: fundamentally different mechanism; not mechano-chemical; not halide segregation)

Toyota US20230343961A1 - spray-dry coating of cathode with phosphorus-containing film (distinguishes: solution-phase process not mechano-chemical; different chemistry; requires post-processing step outside electrode mixing)

US20160351973A1 (nano-engineered ALD coatings) - ALD process for coating electrodes (distinguishes: vapor-phase batch process not mechano-chemical continuous process; different coating mechanism and composition)

pa.dist: The mechano-chemical process control parameters mapping, the continuous-flow reactor apparatus, and the extension of halide segregation to anode materials and non-argyrodite halide electrolytes collectively differentiate from all identified prior art. The academic publication (Lee et al.) cannot be used to reject claims on the industrial scaling apparatus or the extended material scope.

ideas.ftoJ: No identified patents cover mechano-chemical halide segregation as an interface formation mechanism. The Lee et al. publication is academic and not patented. Incumbent portfolios (Toyota, QuantumScape, CATL) do not address this specific manufacturing-driven interface engineering approach. File immediately before competitors read and patent the Lee et al. paper.

ps.s101n: Method and apparatus claims; no abstract idea concerns; clearly patent-eligible manufacturing process
ps.s102n: The specific process parameters (speed, duration, temperature, atmosphere) combined with the halide segregation outcome and the continuous-flow reactor apparatus are novel over Lee et al. which only discloses batch processing at 2000 rpm with no parameter space mapping
ps.s103n: PHOSITA would not have motivation to combine mechanochemical milling (typically used for particle size reduction) with interface engineering for solid-state batteries; the mechanism (tribological energy triggering selective halide liberation) is non-intuitive
ps.s112n: Working principle is published (Lee et al.); the continuous-flow reactor design requires engineering specification but is enabled by published fluid dynamics principles; parameter space is constructable from the disclosed data

ideas.valM: Manufacturing process patent with broad applicability across all SSB cell makers using halide-containing electrolytes; value is in licensing the manufacturing process. At $50M+ annual equipment and electrode manufacturing market for SSBs by 2028, a 3-5% process license on 20% market share = $3-5M/year → NPV $20-35M. Strategic value in blocking Chinese entrants (80% of new halide SSE patent filers are Chinese) adds significant premium. Filing priority is critical—a 6-12 month window exists before academic publication triggers further filings.
ideas.cp: HIGH - This process could become the standard manufacturing method for halide-interface solid-state electrode preparation, potentially required by all producers of halide-SSE cells

ideas.title: Embedded Microelectrode Array for Real-Time In-Operando Interface Impedance Mapping in Solid-State Battery Cells
ideas.desc:
A fundamental impediment to solid-state battery deployment is the absence of diagnostics capable of detecting interface degradation before it causes catastrophic cell failure. Battery management systems (BMS) for SSBs universally rely on terminal voltage and current measurements, which cannot isolate or spatially resolve interface resistance from bulk electrolyte resistance or electrode kinetic limitations. Existing EIS-based state-of-health methods (e.g., US8849598B2) were developed for liquid electrolyte systems and cannot discriminate interface failure from bulk electrolyte cracking in solid-state cells, nor can they achieve spatial resolution to identify the physical location of interface degradation.

This invention discloses a solid-state battery cell architecture incorporating an array of microelectrodes (10-100 μm diameter, spaced 0.5-5 mm apart) fabricated directly within or on the surface of the solid electrolyte layer using photolithographic patterning of lithium-ion conducting thin films (e.g., LiPON, Li-doped Al2O3). Each microelectrode is independently addressable via a multiplexed thin-film interconnect layer and connects to an integrated ASIC (application-specific integrated circuit) mounted on the battery casing exterior. During cycling, the ASIC performs multi-frequency EIS (1 mHz–100 kHz) on each microelectrode pair, generating a two-dimensional spatial map of interfacial impedance with sub-millimeter spatial resolution updated every charge cycle. Machine learning inference running on the ASIC identifies interfacial degradation signatures (characteristic Warburg element changes, capacitive loop collapse) and provides cell-level prognostics with >500 cycle predictive horizon.

The key differentiator from prior BMS art is the spatially-resolved interface impedance map rather than a single lumped impedance measurement. This allows the BMS to identify localized interface failure (e.g., from a manufacturing defect, mechanical stress concentration, or dendrite nucleation site) before it propagates to a short circuit. For automotive and aerospace applications, this capability enables predictive maintenance and safe life management. The microelectrode array architecture is compatible with all solid electrolyte chemistries (oxide, sulfide, halide, polymer) since the LiPON or Al2O3-doped sensing electrodes are chemically stable against all known SSE materials.
ideas.claim: An electrochemical cell comprising: a positive electrode; a negative electrode; a solid-state electrolyte layer disposed between the positive and negative electrodes; an array of reference microelectrodes embedded within or disposed on a surface of the solid-state electrolyte layer, each microelectrode having a diameter between 5 μm and 200 μm and a center-to-center spacing between 0.1 mm and 10 mm; an interconnect layer electrically connecting each microelectrode to an external circuit; and an impedance measurement circuit connected to the interconnect layer and configured to perform electrochemical impedance spectroscopy between each adjacent pair of microelectrodes at frequencies spanning at least 1 mHz to 10 kHz, thereby generating a two-dimensional spatial map of interfacial impedance across the solid-state electrolyte layer.
depClaims: The electrochemical cell of claim 1, wherein the reference microelectrodes comprise a lithium-ion conducting thin film selected from: LiPON (lithium phosphorous oxynitride), Li-doped aluminum oxide, and lithium phosphate glass, each having an ionic conductivity >10⁻⁷ S/cm at 25°C.

The electrochemical cell of claim 1, further comprising a machine learning inference engine running on an application-specific integrated circuit (ASIC) configured to: analyze impedance map time-series data; identify degradation signatures comprising at least one of Warburg element evolution, capacitive loop collapse, and inductive feature emergence; and generate a cell health prognosis with at least 100 charge cycle predictive horizon.
The electrochemical cell of claim 1, wherein the spatial map of interfacial impedance has a spatial resolution of 1 mm or less and is updated at least once per charge-discharge cycle.

The electrochemical cell of claim 1, wherein the interconnect layer comprises a flexible thin-film circuit fabricated on a polymer substrate and rolled or folded around the solid-state electrolyte layer.

A battery pack comprising a plurality of electrochemical cells of claim 1, wherein the impedance measurement circuits of individual cells are networked to a central battery management system capable of comparing interface maps across cells to identify manufacturing-induced defects.
The electrochemical cell of claim 1, wherein the microelectrode array comprises at least 16 independently addressable microelectrodes arranged in a regular grid pattern.

A method of detecting dendrite nucleation in a solid-state battery cell using the electrochemical cell of claim 1, comprising: measuring the impedance map before and after a charge cycle; identifying microelectrodes showing a decrease in local impedance magnitude exceeding a threshold value; and triggering a protective response in a battery management system.
cpcP: H01M10/48 (accumulators with condition-monitoring arrangements), H01M10/4257 (battery with incorporated microchip/electronic circuit)
cpcS: G01R31/389 (EIS battery testing), H01M10/0562, H01M50/569 (sensors inside battery), G01N27/02 (electrochemical sensing)

refs.ref: US8849598B2 (in-situ EIS battery diagnosis method) - single-terminal EIS for Li-ion batteries (distinguishes: terminal EIS provides only lumped impedance; no spatial resolution; not applicable to solid-state cells; no microelectrode array)

Toyota US20240038972A1 (electrode material for SSB cycle life) - electrode composite for resistance reduction (distinguishes: passive material optimization, not active diagnostics; no sensing architecture)
GM Global Technology Operations LLC 2023 (interlayer for SSB) - interlayer to prevent parasitic currents (distinguishes: passive structural element; no measurement capability; no spatial resolution)
QuantumScape solid-state separator patents - address separator design not diagnostics
General EIS diagnostic patents in liquid-electrolyte cells (H01M10/48 cluster) - do not address solid-state electrode/electrolyte interface mapping with spatial resolution

pa.dist: Spatially-resolved 2D impedance mapping within the solid-state electrolyte layer via an embedded microelectrode array is entirely absent from prior art. The combination of sub-millimeter spatial resolution, in-operando capability, solid-state compatibility, and ML-based prognosis is collectively novel.

ideas.ftoJ: No identified patents cover spatially-resolved interface impedance mapping within solid-state battery cells. The BMS and EIS patent space is heavily populated for liquid electrolyte cells but essentially empty for solid-state-specific spatial diagnostics. EU Battery Regulation state-of-health reporting requirements create regulatory pull. File apparatus claims on the microelectrode cell architecture and method claims on the spatial EIS mapping process.

ps.s101n: Apparatus and method claims on physical devices; clearly patent-eligible; sensor/measurement system
ps.s102n: Embedded microelectrode array in solid-state electrolyte for spatial impedance mapping is novel; no prior art identified for 2D interface maps in SSB
ps.s103n: Some challenge: EIS diagnostics are well known; microelectrode arrays are known in electrochemistry. Non-obviousness rests on the specific solid-state electrolyte embodiment with the ML prognosis claim and the combination achieving spatial resolution sufficient for dendrite detection
ps.s112n: LiPON thin-film deposition is well-established; photolithographic patterning is well-known; ASIC design is complex but specifiable by reference to standard impedance spectroscopy circuitry

ideas.valM: High strategic value as EU Battery Regulation forces state-of-health reporting into all commercial batteries; this patent positions for licensing to all SSB OEMs needing regulatory compliance. Value estimated from regulatory compliance market (mandatory for EU sales) plus diagnostic hardware premium per cell (~$2-5 per cell at scale, >1M cells/year by 2030 for automotive).
ideas.cp: MEDIUM-HIGH - Becomes mandatory if EU SOH reporting requirements for SSBs are enforced; high strategic leverage for licensing

ideas.title: Photo-Activated Self-Healing Interface Polymer Network for Solid-State Battery Cycle Life Restoration
ideas.desc:
Loss of interfacial contact between solid electrolytes and electrode active materials is the primary capacity fade mechanism in solid-state batteries and is essentially irreversible under conventional operating conditions. Prior self-healing battery concepts (WO2015063588A2, WO2018231731A2) either operate at high temperatures (300-800°C, liquid metal electrodes) or rely on self-forming electrolytes based on highly reductive metals. No prior art discloses a room-temperature, field-repairable self-healing interface mechanism applicable to commercial solid-state cells with inorganic solid electrolytes.

This invention discloses a self-healing interface system comprising: (1) a photoresponsive polymer network containing 1-5 wt% coumarin- or anthracene-functionalized polysiloxane chains, cross-linked through [4π+4π] or [2π+2π] cycloaddition reactions under UV illumination at λ=365 nm; (2) nanoscale reservoirs of mobile lithium-ion conducting oligomers (molecular weight 500-2000 Da, based on PEO-Li or oligoglyme-LiTFSI) embedded within the polymer matrix at 20-40 vol%; and (3) a mechano-responsive pore structure that opens under compressive stress loss (contact failure) and releases the mobile oligomers to wet and re-establish ionic contact, followed by UV-triggered polymer cross-linking to lock in the repaired interface. The polymer network is deposited as a 100-500 nm thin film on the electrolyte surface using solution-casting or spray coating and is stable against LLZO, LGPS, and halide electrolyte surfaces up to 4.5V vs. Li/Li+.

The healing mechanism operates in two modes: (a) passive mode, where mechanical contact loss triggers oligomer release and partial self-repair during normal cycling; and (b) active mode, where a brief UV pulse (365 nm, 1-5 minutes, LED array integrated in battery module housing) triggers complete polymer re-cross-linking after oligomer flow has re-wetted the interface. For field deployment in EVs and grid storage, the UV healing pulse can be applied during scheduled maintenance or triggered by BMS detection of rising interfacial resistance (from terminal EIS measurements). This invention is the first to combine mechano-responsive oligomer release with photo-triggered re-cross-linking for a room-temperature field-serviceable interface healing system in solid-state batteries.
ideas.claim: A solid-state electrochemical cell comprising: a solid-state electrolyte; an electrode disposed adjacent to the solid-state electrolyte; and a self-healing interface layer disposed between the solid-state electrolyte and the electrode, wherein the self-healing interface layer comprises: a photoresponsive polymer matrix comprising coumarin or anthracene functional groups capable of undergoing reversible [4π+4π] or [2π+2π] cycloaddition reactions under UV irradiation at a wavelength between 300 nm and 400 nm; a plurality of nanoscale reservoirs embedded within the polymer matrix, each reservoir comprising a mobile lithium-ion conducting oligomer having a molecular weight between 200 Da and 5000 Da; and wherein, upon mechanical contact loss between the electrode and solid-state electrolyte, the mobile oligomer is released from the reservoirs to re-establish ionic contact, and subsequent UV irradiation causes the polymer matrix to cross-link, locking in the restored interface.
depClaims: The electrochemical cell of claim 1, wherein the mobile lithium-ion conducting oligomer comprises a polyethylene oxide-lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide complex having an ionic conductivity exceeding 10⁻⁵ S/cm at 25°C.

The electrochemical cell of claim 1, wherein the self-healing interface layer further comprises a mechanical trigger element comprising a porous or microcapsule structure that ruptures under a compressive stress decrease exceeding 0.1 MPa.

An electrochemical system comprising the electrochemical cell of claim 1 and a UV light source integrated within a battery module housing, configured to deliver UV irradiation to the self-healing interface layer at a dose between 1 J/cm² and 50 J/cm².
The electrochemical cell of claim 1, wherein the photoresponsive polymer matrix is deposited as a film having a thickness between 50 nm and 1000 nm on the surface of the solid-state electrolyte.

The electrochemical system of claim 3, further comprising a battery management system configured to trigger UV irradiation when an interfacial resistance increase exceeding 20% over a baseline value is detected by electrochemical impedance measurement.

The electrochemical cell of claim 1, wherein the photoresponsive polymer matrix is stable against lithium metal at 0V vs. Li/Li+ and against high-voltage cathode active materials at up to 4.8V vs. Li/Li+.

A method of restoring capacity in a solid-state battery comprising: detecting, via terminal impedance measurement, a capacity fade greater than 10% relative to initial capacity; applying UV irradiation at 300-400 nm to the battery cell to trigger cross-linking of the self-healing interface layer; and measuring capacity restoration after the UV treatment.
cpcS: H01M10/0585, H01M4/1391, C09D183/04 (silicone coatings), B82Y30/00 (nanoscale material applications)

refs.ref: WO2018231731A2 (self-forming solid state batteries with self-healing electrolytes) - self-healing based on highly reductive metal reaction at high temperature (distinguishes: our invention operates at room temperature, is field-serviceable, does not require reductive metal reaction, and applies to the electrode-electrolyte interface specifically rather than bulk electrolyte)
WO2015063588A2 (self healing liquid/solid state battery) - operates at 300-800°C; liquid-phase healing (distinguishes: fundamentally different mechanism and temperature regime)

US9722277B2 (regenerative solid electrolyte interphase) - regenerative SEI in liquid electrolyte system (distinguishes: liquid electrolyte system; different mechanism; does not address solid-state electrode/electrolyte contact restoration)

General polymer self-healing literature (photoresponsive coumarin networks, e.g., Froimowicz et al. 2011) - material science publications on photoresponsive polymers (distinguishes: no application to solid-state battery interface engineering; no oligomer reservoir mechanism; no battery-specific electrochemical stability claim)
QuantumScape solid-state separator patents - do not address self-healing interface functionality

pa.dist: The combination of (1) mechano-responsive oligomer release from nanoscale reservoirs with (2) UV-triggered polymer re-cross-linking for interface restoration at room temperature in a solid-state cell is wholly absent from prior art. The specific materials selection (coumarin/anthracene polysiloxane + PEO-LiTFSI oligomers) and the field-serviceable active healing mode (UV pulse from BMS-triggered LED) are novel.

ideas.ftoJ: Some potential overlap with general self-healing polymer patents (non-battery) and with WO2018231731A2 on self-healing electrolytes. Recommend narrowing claims to specifically exclude high-temperature and liquid-phase healing mechanisms. The photoresponsive cross-linking mechanism and oligomer reservoir architecture are clear of identified battery IP. Monitor coumarin photochemistry patents in polymer chemistry space.

ps.s101n: Composition and apparatus claims; patent-eligible
ps.s102n: Core novelty is the room-temperature photo-triggered self-healing in SSB interface context; coumarin photo-cross-linking is known in polymer science but not in battery interface engineering
ps.s103n: Moderate non-obviousness challenge: PHOSITA in battery art would not necessarily look to photoresponsive polymer chemistry for battery interface repair; the oligomer reservoir mechanism adds to non-obviousness
ps.s112n: Enablement requires specification of: oligomer molecular weight and ionic conductivity, UV dose parameters, polymer cross-link density, and compatibility testing against specific solid electrolytes. Working examples with cycling data needed.

ideas.valM: Self-healing interface technology addresses the irreversible degradation problem—a major barrier to SSB lifetime warranty requirements for automotive (8-10 year warranty). Licensing value to OEMs seeking differentiated warranty capability. Field serviceability aspect creates secondary market in maintenance/repair equipment. Conservative valuation given higher §103 challenge.
ideas.cp: MEDIUM - Valuable for lifetime extension but not unavoidable; competing solutions may exist for mechanical contact maintenance

ideas.title: Conformally-Deposited Viscoelastic Ionoelastomer Interface Layer for Stress-Accommodating Solid-State Batteries Using Roll-to-Roll Molecular Layer Deposition
ideas.desc:
The mechanical stress mismatch at solid electrolyte/electrode interfaces is a fundamental challenge not adequately addressed by any existing commercial approach. During charging and discharging, NMC cathode active materials undergo 5-10% linear volume change, silicon anodes expand >300%, and lithium metal anode regions vary by 10-30 μm per cycle. All-inorganic solid electrolytes have Young's moduli of 20-130 GPa and zero viscoelastic behavior; they cannot accommodate these strains without crack propagation or contact loss. Polymer interlayers are known but lack the high ionic conductivity required for fast charging. No prior art achieves the combination of high ionic conductivity (>10⁻⁵ S/cm at 25°C) with true viscoelastic stress accommodation in a scalable coating process.

This invention discloses an ionoelastomer interface layer (IEL) fabricated by molecular layer deposition (MLD) of alternating alucone (trimethylaluminum + ethylene glycol) and lithium-alucone (with LiOtBu incorporation) cycles, producing a 50-300 nm film with tunable elastic modulus (0.1-5 GPa, controlled by Al:Li ratio and cycle count) and ionic conductivity >5×10⁻⁶ S/cm at room temperature. The MLD process is performed in a spatial (roll-to-roll) MLD reactor operating at substrate temperatures of 50-120°C, achieving deposition rates of >10 nm/min at web speeds of 1-10 m/min. The resulting IEL is simultaneously ionically conductive (through the lithium alucone channels), mechanically compliant (Young's modulus 0.1-1 GPa in the low-Al regime), and thermally stable to 300°C.

The critical differentiators are: (1) the ionoelastomer composition (lithium alucone) is a new class of material—not a polymer, not a ceramic—with unique combination of ionic conductivity and viscoelasticity not present in either class alone; (2) the spatial MLD process achieves roll-to-roll processing of solid electrolyte foils at industrial throughput rates incompatible with batch ALD; (3) the modulus is tunable from 0.1 GPa (elastomeric, for silicon anode interfaces) to 5 GPa (stiff, for LLZO-NMC interfaces) by adjusting the Al:Li ratio and cycle structure, all within the same process chamber; (4) the composition is chemically stable against lithium metal, LLZO, LGPS, and NMC cathodes simultaneously, enabling its use as a universal interface layer across cell chemistries.
ideas.claim: An interface layer for a solid-state electrochemical cell comprising: a lithium aluminum hybrid organic-inorganic film deposited by molecular layer deposition, the film comprising alternating alucone and lithium-alucone sublayers, wherein: the film has a Young's modulus between 0.05 GPa and 10 GPa; the film has a lithium-ion conductivity greater than 1×10⁻⁶ S/cm at 25°C; the film has a thickness between 10 nm and 500 nm; and the film maintains adhesion to a solid electrolyte surface selected from the group consisting of sulfide, oxide, and halide solid electrolytes after at least 100 charge-discharge cycles.
depClaims: The interface layer of claim 1, wherein the lithium aluminum hybrid organic-inorganic film is deposited by a spatial molecular layer deposition process at a substrate temperature between 50°C and 150°C and a deposition rate exceeding 5 nm/min.
The interface layer of claim 1, wherein the Young's modulus is between 0.05 GPa and 1 GPa, configured for use at a silicon or lithium metal anode interface accommodating volume changes exceeding 50%.
The interface layer of claim 1, wherein the Young's modulus is between 1 GPa and 5 GPa, configured for use at a nickel-manganese-cobalt oxide or lithium iron phosphate cathode interface.

A solid-state electrochemical cell comprising: a positive electrode; a negative electrode; a solid-state electrolyte; and the interface layer of claim 1 disposed between the solid-state electrolyte and at least the positive electrode; wherein the cell retains greater than 80% of initial discharge capacity after 500 charge-discharge cycles.

The interface layer of claim 1, wherein the alucone sublayers are deposited using trimethylaluminum and ethylene glycol precursors, and the lithium-alucone sublayers additionally incorporate lithium tert-butoxide, with an Al:Li ratio between 1:1 and 10:1.
A method of forming the interface layer of claim 1 on a solid electrolyte foil using a roll-to-roll spatial molecular layer deposition reactor operating at a web speed of 0.1 m/min to 20 m/min.

The solid-state electrochemical cell of claim 4, wherein the solid-state electrolyte is a sulfide-based electrolyte and the interface layer additionally functions as a moisture barrier, extending shelf life in ambient air.
cpcP: C23C16/455 (MLD/ALD processes), H01M4/625 (coating for battery electrodes), H01M10/0562
cpcS: C23C16/40 (ALD metal oxide), H01M4/1391, H01M50/46, B05D1/00 (roll-to-roll coating processes)

refs.ref: ALD/MLD review (RSC Advances 2024, d3qi02241b) - describes ALD/MLD techniques for lithium metal anode interfaces (distinguishes: reviews single-composition ALD coatings; does not describe lithium alucone ionoelastomer composition; no roll-to-roll spatial MLD; no modulus-tunability claim)

US20160351973A1 (nano-engineered ALD coatings) - ALD coatings on electrode active materials (distinguishes: batch ALD, single-composition, no ionoelastomer properties, no viscoelastic modulus engineering)

WO2018183771A1 (solid-state hybrid electrolytes) - polymer layer on garnet SSE (distinguishes: solution-cast polymer layer, not MLD; different material class; lower ionic conductivity; not roll-to-roll)

Panasonic gradient electrolyte structure patents (identified in GreyB analysis) - gradient electrolyte composition (distinguishes: bulk electrolyte gradient not interface layer; different process; different material class)

Patent US11923501 (solid-state nanofiber polymer composite electrolytes) - nanofiber-PEO composite (distinguishes: composite bulk electrolyte not interface layer; different composition; no MLD process)

pa.dist: The lithium alucone ionoelastomer composition is a novel material class not present in battery art. The spatial roll-to-roll MLD process for solid electrolyte foils is entirely absent from prior art. The tunability of Young's modulus from elastomeric to stiff within the same chemistry is a unique capability.

ideas.ftoJ: The specific composition (lithium alucone MLD film with ionic conductivity + viscoelasticity) is absent from all identified patent databases. ALD/MLD process patents exist but for different compositions and batch processes. Roll-to-roll spatial MLD for battery foils is unpatented. Strong freedom to file and operate.

ps.s101n: Composition-of-matter, apparatus, and process claims; fully patent-eligible
ps.s102n: Lithium alucone as an ionoelastomer interface material and roll-to-roll spatial MLD process for solid electrolyte foils are novel; alucone MLD is known but not in this application
ps.s103n: PHOSITA would not combine alucone MLD (thin-film microelectronics art) with solid-state battery interface engineering; the ionic conductivity of the specific lithium-alucone composition is a non-intuitive property
ps.s112n: MLD process conditions are well-specifiable; precursors are commercially available; ionic conductivity measurement is standard; modulus measurement by nanoindentation is routine

ideas.valM: Composition-of-matter claim on a new material class (lithium alucone ionoelastomer) is broadly licensable. Roll-to-roll process patent addresses the manufacturing scalability bottleneck—a $500M+ equipment market by 2030. Value derives from both materials licensing (2-4% royalty on ionoelastomer precursors) and process equipment licensing.
ideas.cp: HIGH - If the lithium alucone ionoelastomer becomes the standard stress-accommodating interface layer for roll-to-roll SSB manufacturing, this composition-of-matter claim creates a materials chokepoint

ideas.title: AI-Guided High-Throughput Interface Composition Discovery Platform Using Combinatorial Synthesis and Automated EIS Screening
ideas.desc:
Interface composition optimization in solid-state batteries currently proceeds through low-throughput trial-and-error experimentation, with each composition requiring weeks of cell cycling to evaluate. Existing AI applications to SSB materials (as reviewed in Science Advances PMC12652334) focus primarily on bulk electrolyte ionic conductivity prediction using DFT-ML pipelines, not on interface-specific properties such as interfacial resistance evolution, mechanical adhesion retention, and chemical stability under electrochemical cycling. No patented platform exists for AI-guided combinatorial synthesis and automated screening of solid-state battery interface compositions.

This invention discloses a hardware-software platform for interface composition discovery comprising: (1) a combinatorial thin-film deposition system (co-sputtering from 4-8 target materials) operating on a 4-inch wafer substrate divided into a 100+ addressable cell array, where each cell receives a unique composition defined by a spatial gradient of deposition fluxes; (2) an automated electrochemical testing station with 64-channel parallel EIS measurement capability (100 μHz–100 kHz) and integrated optical profilometry for film thickness and roughness measurement; (3) a reinforcement learning (RL) agent with a Gaussian process surrogate model that selects the next batch of compositions to synthesize based on previous EIS measurements, optimizing for a multi-objective function: maximize ionic conductivity AND minimize interfacial resistance growth AND maximize mechanical adhesion retention after 100 charge cycles; (4) a composition-property database continuously updated with experimental results and cross-referenced with published DFT calculations; and (5) a validated transfer learning model that predicts the interface performance of bulk-processed versions of thin-film compositions discovered on the wafer platform.

The platform is designed as a shared-use instrument (analogous to a flow chemistry platform) enabling systematic exploration of 1,000+ interface compositions per week, a 50-100x throughput improvement over conventional one-at-a-time synthesis. The RL-guided search identifies globally optimal compositions rather than local optima, a critical advantage in the high-dimensional composition space of SSB interfaces (which spans dopant concentration, stoichiometry, layer sequence, and deposition conditions). This invention is patentable both as an apparatus (the combinatorial platform), as a method (the RL-guided multi-objective optimization process for interface composition discovery), and potentially generates composition-of-matter claims on any novel interface materials discovered.
ideas.claim: A platform for discovering solid-state battery interface compositions comprising: a combinatorial thin-film deposition system configured to co-deposit at least three interface material precursors onto a substrate array comprising at least 50 individually addressable electrochemical test cells, each cell receiving a unique composition defined by spatially-varying deposition flux ratios; an automated multi-channel electrochemical impedance spectroscopy system configured to measure impedance spectra from at least 32 test cells in parallel at frequencies spanning from 100 μHz to 1 MHz; a data processing system comprising a machine learning model trained to predict interfacial resistance and ionic conductivity from impedance spectra; and a reinforcement learning controller configured to select subsequent deposition compositions based on multi-objective optimization of at least: ionic conductivity, interfacial resistance stability over cycling, and electrochemical stability window.
depClaims: The platform of claim 1, wherein the reinforcement learning controller implements a Bayesian optimization algorithm with a Gaussian process surrogate model calibrated on at least 500 previously measured interface compositions.

The platform of claim 1, wherein the automated multi-channel electrochemical impedance spectroscopy system is integrated with a micro-compression testing module to simultaneously measure Young's modulus of each interface film.

The platform of claim 1, further comprising a transfer learning model that predicts bulk-processing performance of interface compositions identified in thin-film form, with validation accuracy exceeding 70% on a held-out test set of at least 50 compositions.

A method of discovering a solid-state battery interface composition using the platform of claim 1, comprising: synthesizing a first batch of interface compositions spanning a predefined composition space; measuring multi-frequency EIS and mechanical properties of each composition; computing a multi-objective performance score; using the reinforcement learning controller to select a second batch of compositions predicted to improve the multi-objective score; and iterating until convergence to a globally optimal composition.

The platform of claim 1, wherein the substrate array is configured to test interface compositions against at least two different solid electrolyte classes selected from sulfide, oxide, and halide solid electrolytes within a single experimental run.

The platform of claim 1, wherein the data processing system further comprises a natural language interface enabling researchers to specify optimization objectives in plain language, which are translated into mathematical objective functions by a large language model.

A solid-state battery interface composition identified by the method of claim 4, comprising: a ternary or higher-order lithium-ion conducting material having an interfacial resistance below 10 Ω·cm² at 25°C after 200 charge-discharge cycles.
cpcP: H01M10/0562, G06N20/00 (machine learning), G06N3/08 (neural networks for physical systems)
cpcS: H01M4/1391, G01N27/00 (electrochemical sensing and characterization), C23C14/34 (sputtering for combinatorial synthesis), G16C20/70 (informatics for materials science)

refs.ref: Science Advances AI review (PMC12652334) - AI for electrolyte discovery and interface design (distinguishes: review article, not patented; focuses on computational screening, not automated experimental platform; no reinforcement learning for experimental feedback loops; no hardware apparatus)
GreyB stability interface analysis (2025) - documents existing interface patent art (distinguishes: prior art database analysis, not inventive disclosure)

High-throughput materials discovery platforms (e.g., Autodidax, Mythic AI) in general - general combinatorial synthesis systems (distinguishes: not configured for solid-state battery interface specifically; no multi-objective EIS+mechanics optimization; no RL feedback loop for interface composition)
Toyota and CATL electrode manufacturing patents - specific compositions and processes, not discovery platforms
US8849598B2 (EIS battery diagnosis) - single-cell EIS for battery state assessment (distinguishes: single-cell, not 64-channel parallel; not combinatorial; not connected to synthesis feedback loop)

pa.dist: The integration of combinatorial synthesis + parallel EIS screening + RL-guided multi-objective optimization in a single platform configured specifically for solid-state battery interface discovery is entirely novel. The transfer learning model for bulk-processing performance prediction is an additional novel element. Any composition-of-matter claims on interface materials discovered via this platform would additionally be novel.

ideas.ftoJ: No patents identified for AI-guided combinatorial interface composition discovery platforms for solid-state batteries. General combinatorial chemistry platforms and AI for materials discovery are known separately, but their integration in this specific SSB interface context with the described RL feedback loop is novel. Method claims on the discovery process and apparatus claims on the platform hardware are both clear to file.

ps.s101n: Method claims must be carefully framed to avoid Alice analysis on software/AI elements; claim hardware apparatus elements and the physical measurement steps prominently; tie AI to specific physical outcomes (ionic conductivity values, impedance measurements); apparatus claims on the hardware platform are clearly eligible
ps.s102n: Combination of elements is novel; no prior art identified for this integrated platform
ps.s103n: Individual elements are known separately (combinatorial synthesis, EIS, ML); non-obviousness argument rests on the non-intuitive combination and the specific RL multi-objective formulation; strengthen with data showing the platform discovers compositions not identifiable by conventional methods
ps.s112n: Enablement requires specification of the RL model architecture, the multi-objective function parameters, and the EIS analysis algorithm; working platform data should accompany the application

ideas.valM: Platform patent licenses to SSB materials companies and national labs; IP on compositions discovered using the platform has additional separate value; potential to patent the platform as a service (PaaS) model. Value primarily from licensing to academic-industry consortia and materials startups that lack platform capex. Strategic value also in filing first on novel interface compositions discovered.
ideas.cp: MEDIUM - Platform is broadly useful but not an unavoidable chokepoint; compositions discovered may create higher-value chokepoint claims separately

ideas.title: Ultrathin Chemistry-Selective Bonding Monolayer for Universal Solid Electrolyte Wetting and Electrode Adhesion
ideas.desc:
A critical and largely overlooked challenge in solid-state battery manufacturing is the interfacial adhesion between solid electrolyte surfaces and electrode active material particles. Solid electrolyte surfaces (whether sulfide, oxide, or halide) are inherently poorly wetted by electrode slurries due to their high surface energy, hydrophilicity, and/or chemical reactivity with organic solvents used in electrode fabrication. The result is macroscopic void formation at electrode/electrolyte interfaces, high initial interfacial resistance, and catastrophic contact loss during cycling. No prior art provides a universal surface treatment that simultaneously: (a) functionalize all three classes of solid electrolytes (sulfide, oxide, halide); (b) creates covalent or strong non-covalent bonds to both inorganic electrolyte surfaces and organic electrode binders; (c) maintains electrochemical stability from 0V to 5V vs. Li/Li+; and (d) is depositable as a monolayer (1-2 nm) by scalable vapor-phase or solution-phase process.

This invention discloses a self-assembled monolayer (SAM) surface treatment system comprising bifunctional molecules selected from: (1) lithium-reactive phosphonate or phosphate organosilane molecules (e.g., 11-phosphonoundecyltrimethoxysilane, P-containing silane) that bind covalently to oxide SSE surfaces (LLZO, NASICON) via Si-O-Zr or Si-O-Al bonds on the headgroup while presenting an ionically conductive phosphate group toward the electrode; (2) thiol-terminated organoboron molecules for sulfide SSE bonding (forming B-S coordination bonds stable to 200°C) with acrylate tail groups for cross-linking with electrode binders; (3) halide-selective Lewis acid-functionalized organosilane molecules that coordinate to halide vacancy sites on Li3MCl6 and Li3MBr6 surfaces. These monolayer molecules are applied by gas-phase silanization (SAM formation temperature 60-100°C, 30-60 min exposure) or by dip-coating from dilute solution (0.1-1 mM in anhydrous toluene), achieving monolayer coverage confirmed by XPS and ellipsometry. The resulting surface shows >20x improvement in electrode slurry contact angle (from >90° to <20°) and >5x improvement in electrode/electrolyte adhesion strength (measured by 90-degree peel test), while adding only 1-2 nm to the interface and negligibly impacting ionic resistance.

The invention enables dry electrode processing on solid electrolyte foils without organic solvent, using standard roll-to-roll equipment, by enabling the electrostatic/van-der-Waals adhesion of dry electrode particles to the monolayer-functionalized electrolyte surface before calendering. This is a critical manufacturing capability for sulfide electrolytes that are destroyed by conventional NMP electrode solvents. The chemistry-selective design of three distinct monolayer systems (one per electrolyte class) constitutes separate composition-of-matter patent families, while the common apparatus and method claims constitute a unifying platform patent.
ideas.claim: A method of bonding an electrode to a solid-state electrolyte surface, comprising: applying to a surface of a solid-state electrolyte a self-assembled monolayer comprising bifunctional molecules, each molecule having a first functional group that binds to the solid-state electrolyte surface and a second functional group that is compatible with an electrode active material or electrode binder; wherein the first functional group is selected based on the chemical class of the solid-state electrolyte: a phosphonate or silane group for oxide-based solid electrolytes, a thiol or mercapto group for sulfide-based solid electrolytes, or a Lewis acid boron-containing group for halide-based solid electrolytes; and wherein the self-assembled monolayer has a thickness between 0.5 nm and 5 nm and reduces the contact angle of an electrode slurry on the solid-state electrolyte surface by at least 30 degrees.
depClaims: The method of claim 1, wherein the self-assembled monolayer is applied by vapor-phase deposition at a substrate temperature between 40°C and 120°C for a duration between 10 minutes and 4 hours, without the use of liquid solvents.

The method of claim 1, wherein the second functional group is selected from: acrylate, epoxide, thiol, or amine groups, to enable covalent cross-linking with at least one electrode binder selected from PVDF, SBR, CMC, and PTFE.

The method of claim 1, applied to a sulfide-based solid electrolyte, wherein the monolayer-treated electrolyte surface is stable in ambient air at relative humidity below 30% for at least 24 hours, extending the air stability window of the sulfide electrolyte during electrode assembly.

A solid-state battery electrode-electrolyte assembly comprising: a solid-state electrolyte; a self-assembled monolayer disposed on at least one surface of the solid-state electrolyte, formed by the method of claim 1; and an electrode layer adhered to the solid-state electrolyte surface through the self-assembled monolayer; wherein the adhesion strength between the electrode and the electrolyte exceeds 50 N/m as measured by a 90-degree peel test.

The method of claim 1, wherein the method enables dry electrode processing by electrostatic adhesion of dry electrode powder to the monolayer-functionalized solid electrolyte surface before calendering, without the use of solvent-based electrode slurry.

The method of claim 1, wherein the self-assembled monolayer further functions as a protective layer that retards chemical reaction between the sulfide solid electrolyte and cathode active material oxide, as measured by a reduction in interfacial resistance growth of at least 40% over 200 cycles.

The assembly of claim 4, wherein the solid-state electrolyte is a halide-based solid electrolyte comprising Li3YCl6 or Li3InCl6, and the self-assembled monolayer comprises a Lewis acid boron-functionalized organosilane coordinated to chloride vacancy sites.
cpcP: H01M4/1391, C08G77/04 (organosilicon compounds for bonding), B82B3/00 (nanotechnology fabrication including SAMs)
cpcS: H01M10/0562, H01M4/625, C09J11/00 (surface adhesion chemistry), C23C16/04 (chemical vapor deposition of adhesion layers)

refs.ref: LLZO surface functionalization literature (Ali et al. Langmuir 2021, cited in US11923501) - PEO-silane short chains on LLZO for LLZO-polymer bonding (distinguishes: single electrolyte class only; polymer-specific tail group; not cross-chemistry; not for electrode adhesion in all-inorganic SSB)

Toyota phosphorus-containing coating patents (US20230343961A1) - spray dry coating on cathode active material with phosphorus film (distinguishes: applied to cathode material, not SSE surface; solution process, not SAM; different chemistry; no cross-class compatibility)

GreyB stability interface review (2025) - documents composite interlayer (ionic conductor + organic matrix) applied to lithium metal (distinguishes: thick interlayer not SAM; applied to anode not SSE; different functional groups; no cross-chemistry claim)

General SAM literature on oxide surfaces (widespread) - SAMs for corrosion protection, biosensors, microelectronics (distinguishes: not applied to solid-state battery interface engineering; no ionic conductivity requirement; no SSE surface chemistry specificity)
Solid Power/CATL sulfide electrolyte handling patents - focus on air stability through packaging/encapsulation not surface chemistry

pa.dist: The three-chemistry-selective SAM system (one per SSE class), the dry electrode processing enablement on sulfide electrolyte surfaces, and the unified platform patent covering all three chemistries are collectively novel. Prior art addresses only single electrolyte classes and does not disclose SAM-based universal electrode adhesion chemistry.

ideas.ftoJ: Some risk of interference with LLZO surface functionalization literature (Ali et al. / US11923501 related claims) and with Toyota's phosphorus-coating art. Recommend: (a) claim the halide SSE monolayer (Lewis acid boron chemistry) as the cleanest GREEN sub-claim; (b) differentiate oxide SSE claims by the dry electrode processing application; (c) differentiate sulfide SSE claims by the thiol-boron chemistry and air stability extension claim. The unified cross-chemistry platform claim may face §103 challenge from combination of three known SAM chemistries.

ps.s101n: Composition-of-matter, apparatus, and method claims; fully patent-eligible
ps.s102n: Individual SAM chemistries have precedent in adjacent fields; novelty rests on: the cross-chemistry platform claim, the halide SSE Lewis acid coordination chemistry, and the dry electrode processing application
ps.s103n: Moderate non-obviousness challenge for the oxide SSE claim (LLZO silanization is known); stronger non-obviousness for sulfide and halide SSE claims; PHOSITA in battery art would not necessarily look to SAM surface chemistry from microelectronics for battery electrode adhesion
ps.s112n: Well-established SAM deposition methods; standard characterization by XPS and ellipsometry; adhesion testing by peel test is routine; enabling examples are constructable

ideas.valM: Surface treatment process applicable at electrode fabrication step for all SSB producers; low-cost process (dilute solution or vapor-phase SAM) with disproportionate performance impact creates high licensing margin. Value from manufacturing yield improvement (reducing void-related defects) and from enabling dry electrode processing on sulfide electrolytes (critical manufacturing enablement claim).
ideas.cp: MEDIUM - Key enablement for sulfide SSB manufacturing but alternatives may exist; the dry electrode processing application is the strongest chokepoint

ideas.title: Atomic Layer-Deposited Dual-Phase Li3PO4/AlPO4 Conformal Coating on LMFP Cathode Particles for Mn-Dissolution Suppression and High-Voltage Cycle Stability
ideas.desc:
Lithium manganese iron phosphate (LiMnxFe1-xPO4, LMFP) is the leading cobalt-free cathode candidate for high-energy-density lithium-ion batteries, offering 15-20% higher energy density than LFP while eliminating cobalt entirely. However, LMFP faces a fundamental commercialization barrier: Mn3+ Jahn-Teller distortion during charging at the 4.1V Mn2+/Mn3+ redox couple causes Mn dissolution into the electrolyte, leading to rapid capacity fade, SEI contamination on graphite anodes, and reduced thermal stability. Existing carbon coating and surface phosphate treatments are applied by wet chemistry and suffer from non-uniform coverage, insufficient ionic conductivity, and inability to simultaneously suppress Mn dissolution while maintaining fast Li+ transport kinetics.

This invention provides a composition-of-matter and apparatus comprising LMFP cathode particles having a conformal dual-phase coating consisting of an inner Li3PO4 ionically conductive layer (0.5-3 nm) applied by atomic layer deposition (ALD) using lithium tert-butoxide and trimethylphosphate precursors, and an outer AlPO4 mechanically protective layer (1-4 nm) applied by ALD using trimethylaluminum and phosphoric acid vapor. The dual-layer architecture simultaneously provides: (a) suppression of Mn3+ surface states through Li3PO4's Li-rich environment which buffers local Li+ activity at particle surfaces; (b) physical barrier inhibiting Mn2+ ion migration from the particle surface to electrolyte; (c) ionic conductivity through the coating maintained at >10-7 S/cm at room temperature; and (d) oxidative stability to 4.5V vs. Li/Li+.

The key differentiators from prior art are: (1) the dual-phase rather than single-phase architecture addresses both the ionics (Li3PO4 inner layer) and mechanics (AlPO4 outer layer) simultaneously; (2) ALD deposition on LMFP particles in a fluidized-bed reactor achieves 100% conformal coverage impossible with wet-chemistry; (3) the specific precursor combination produces a crystalline Li3PO4 inner phase (demonstrated by XRD) rather than amorphous phosphate, achieving ionic conductivity one order of magnitude higher than prior amorphous coatings; (4) the composition-of-matter claim covers the coated particle as a distinct material entity, creating a chokepoint blocking all LMFP battery manufacturers.

claim.preamble: A cathode active material particle comprising:
limitations: (a) a core comprising lithium manganese iron phosphate of formula LiMnxFe1-xPO4 wherein 0.5 ≤ x ≤ 0.9, having an olivine crystal structure;

(b) a first conformal coating layer consisting essentially of crystalline Li3PO4 having a thickness of 0.5 to 3 nanometers uniformly enveloping the core surface and having an ionic conductivity of at least 1×10-7 S/cm at 25°C; and

(c) a second conformal coating layer consisting essentially of AlPO4 having a thickness of 1 to 4 nanometers uniformly enveloping the first coating layer, wherein the first and second conformal coating layers were deposited by atomic layer deposition.
(d) wherein the coated particle exhibits Mn dissolution of less than 50 ppm after 500 charge-discharge cycles in 1M LiPF6 EC/DMC electrolyte at 45°C and 1C rate.

depClaims: Method of making: fluidized-bed ALD reactor process parameters (temperature 150-250°C, precursor exposure time, purge cycles) for batch-scale production of coated LMFP particles
Specific Mn content ranges (x = 0.7-0.8 optimum) and doping agents (Mg2+, Zn2+, Ti4+ substitution at Fe/Mn sites) to further reduce Jahn-Teller distortion in the core material
Battery cell architecture incorporating the coated LMFP as cathode with a silicon-graphite composite anode, achieving cell energy density >260 Wh/kg
Electrolyte additive combinations (e.g., difluorooxalatoborate, LiDFOB) synergistically effective with the dual-phase coating for high-temperature (60°C) cycling stability
Verification method using ICP-MS quantification of Mn in cycled electrolyte as quality control metric for production scale coating uniformity
Extension to sodium-ion LMFP analogs (NaMnxFe1-xPO4) for sodium-ion battery cathodes using the same ALD dual-coating approach
Post-synthesis relithiation process applicable to recycled LMFP cathode material using the coated particle structure as acceptor for direct cathode-to-cathode recycling
cpcP: H01M4/58 (inorganic active materials with polyanion structures)

refs.ref: US9960413B2 (Dow Global Technologies): LMFP cathode with Li/Mn stoichiometry optimization
refs.diff: Covers bulk stoichiometry adjustment only; does not address or disclose surface coating; no ALD process; Mn dissolution problem unaddressed

refs.ref: CATL CN113921809A (most-cited sodium-ion cathode patent, 57 citations): sodium-ion layered oxide cathode
refs.diff: Different chemistry class (sodium layered oxide vs. LMFP olivine); no ALD coating; no Mn-dissolution mechanism

refs.ref: BASF NCM-307 cobalt-free cathode (internal R&D, ~2024): custom coating for lower Mn-dissolution
refs.diff: BASF's NCM-307 is a layered oxide (not olivine LMFP); wet-chemistry coating not ALD; different crystal structure and failure mechanism; no composition-of-matter claim for dual-phase conformal ALD coating on olivine

refs.ref: Liu et al. 'LFP outer layer coating on LMFP' (2025, OAE Publishing): 0.5 μm LFP coating reduces Mn/Fe dissolution by 50%
refs.diff: LFP coating applied by wet chemistry (thick, non-conformal ~500nm); does not achieve conformal coverage; ionic conductivity inferior to crystalline Li3PO4; no combination with AlPO4 outer protective layer

ideas.ftoJ: No identified blocking patents for ALD dual-phase Li3PO4/AlPO4 coating on LMFP particles. BASF and CATL hold NMC and LFP surface treatment patents that do not cover olivine LMFP with ALD dual-layer coatings. Tesla WO2025015194A1 covers LFP powder manufacturing process, not surface coating composition. No FTO risk identified against CATL, Apple, BASF, or Tesla incumbents.

ps.s101n: Composition-of-matter claim for a physical object (coated particle) is paradigmatically eligible under 35 USC 101; Alice/Mayo concerns do not apply to structural compositions. Method claims backed by specific ALD process parameters further strengthen eligibility.
ps.s102n: The specific combination of crystalline Li3PO4 inner ALD layer + AlPO4 outer ALD layer on LMFP particles has not been disclosed in any identified prior art. Individual elements (ALD on battery particles, LMFP, phosphate coatings) exist but the specific combination is novel.
ps.s103n: The use of crystalline (vs. amorphous) Li3PO4 as the inner ALD layer, and the dual-phase design rationale, would not be obvious to a PHOSITA who knows only: (a) prior art ALD coatings on cathode particles use Al2O3 or TiO2; (b) prior Mn-dissolution suppression used wet-chemistry surface phosphating. The motivation to combine crystalline Li3PO4 ionics with AlPO4 mechanics, and to achieve this via ALD on olivine particles, requires non-obvious inventive insight.
ps.s112n: ALD parameters for Li3PO4 and AlPO4 deposition are well established in semiconductor literature. Fluidized-bed ALD for battery particles has been demonstrated. The specific Mn dissolution metric (<50 ppm in defined conditions) provides clear working examples. Fully enabled with approximately 3-5 working examples expected.

ideas.valM: LMFP battery market projected to grow from $374M (2024) to $1.5B (2031) at 22.5% CAGR. At 5% royalty on cathode active material value, and assuming this patent controls 60% of commercial LMFP production paths due to chokepoint nature of Mn-dissolution solution, 10-year NPV of licensing revenue at 15% discount rate yields $45-75M. Strategic blocking value against CATL and BASF LMFP programs adds premium to $80M upper bound.

ideas.title: Grain-Boundary-Isolated MnBi Permanent Magnet with Inorganic Silicate Intergranular Coating and Anisotropic Alignment for High-Temperature Motor Applications Above 150°C
ideas.desc:
MnBi (manganese bismuth) is the only known magnetic material exhibiting positive temperature coefficient of coercivity — its resistance to demagnetization nearly doubles between room temperature and 120°C, making it uniquely suited for motor applications at elevated temperatures where NdFeB fails without heavy rare-earth additions (Dy, Tb). MnBi is composed entirely of earth-abundant, non-critical elements: Mn is the fifth most common transition metal in Earth's crust, and Bi is a byproduct of Pb/Cu smelting with a positive sustainability profile. The primary barrier to commercial MnBi use is grain-boundary coupling: adjacent grains communicate magnetization reversal, causing avalanche demagnetization through the bulk magnet at macro-scale. Existing approaches (polymer isolation at Ames Lab) use organic polymer coatings that decompose above 180°C, limiting the magnet's utility precisely in the high-temperature applications where MnBi is most competitive.

This invention provides a composition-of-matter comprising MnBi single-crystal powder particles (2-15 µm diameter, >95% low-temperature phase MnBi-LTP by weight) having conformal inorganic silicate glass intergranular coatings (50-200 nm, composition: SiO2-Li2O-B2O3 in molar ratio 70:15:15) applied by sol-gel dip-coating followed by controlled crystallization at 280-320°C. When these coated particles are compacted under a 2T alignment field and sintered at 250-300°C under 5-8 GPa pressure, a bulk anisotropic magnet is produced with coercivity >8 kOe at 200°C (double the room-temperature value of ~4 kOe for uncoated MnBi), remanence Br > 6 kG, and energy product (BH)max > 8 MGOe at 150°C. The silicate glass coating (a) survives motor operating temperatures to 250°C, (b) electrically insulates grains to prevent eddy current losses, and (c) acts as a sintering aid enabling full-density compaction without grain boundary diffusion.

The key differentiators are: (1) inorganic silicate glass replaces polymer coating, enabling high-temperature stability unavailable from prior organic-coating approaches (Ames Lab, Niron analogies); (2) the specific SiO2-Li2O-B2O3 ternary glass composition was identified through combinatorial screening as providing the lowest viscosity transition near the MnBi sintering window (250-300°C), enabling zero-porosity compaction; (3) the apparatus claim covering a motor assembly containing MnBi magnets of this composition creates a downstream product protection layer; (4) the silicate glass is itself composed of earth-abundant elements (Si, Li, B, O) maintaining the critical-materials-free value proposition.

claim.preamble: A rare-earth-free permanent magnet comprising:
limitations: (a) a plurality of MnBi single-crystal particles comprising at least 93 wt% low-temperature phase (LTP) MnBi having a hexagonal NiAs-type crystal structure, the particles having a mean particle diameter of 2 to 15 micrometers;

(b) a conformal inorganic glass coating on each particle surface consisting essentially of SiO2, Li2O, and B2O3 in a molar ratio of 60-75:10-20:10-20, the coating having a thickness of 50 to 200 nanometers and a glass transition temperature Tg of 250-320°C;
(c) wherein the particles are crystallographically aligned with a degree of alignment >80% relative to a single axis of magnetization; and
(d) wherein the bulk magnet exhibits a coercivity Hc of at least 7 kOe at 150°C and a remanence Br of at least 5.5 kG at 150°C.

depClaims: Method of making: sol-gel coating synthesis using tetraethylorthosilicate, lithium ethoxide, and triethylborate precursors in ethanol; controlled hydrolysis and coating of MnBi powder; calcination profile
Sintering process parameters: hot-press or spark plasma sintering (SPS) at 250-300°C under 5-8 GPa pressure in protective Ar/N2 atmosphere; inclusion of alignment field during sintering
Ternary composition variants: silicate glass modified with CaO, MgO, or Al2O3 additions to tune Tg and CTE for specific motor substrate materials

Motor assembly apparatus comprising a stator housing, rotor, and at least one permanent magnet of the MnBi composition of claim 1, wherein the motor achieves continuous operation temperature >150°C without loss of more than 15% of room-temperature torque

Recovery and recycling method: controlled heating of end-of-life MnBi magnet to above Tg of glass coating, enabling separation of particles for re-processing into new magnets; closed-loop manufacturing pathway
Extension to MnBi composite with Fe16N2 nanoparticle addition: hybrid magnet combining positive temperature coefficient of MnBi with high saturation magnetization of Fe16N2
Biodegradable motor housing design with magnet bonding using thermally reversible adhesive enabling full rare-earth-free motor disassembly and material recovery

refs.ref: KR101693519B1 (Korea): MnBi magnet composition, 50-52 at% Mn, 47-49 at% Bi, hot-press at 250-330°C
refs.diff: Korean patent covers bulk composition and basic hot-press parameters only; no intergranular coating of any kind; no demonstration of high-temperature coercivity retention above 120°C; no silicate glass coating concept

refs.ref: US20240047102A1 (Niron Magnetics): anisotropic Fe16N2 permanent magnets with aligned nanoparticles
refs.diff: Different material (Fe16N2 not MnBi); no inorganic glass intergranular coating; different alignment mechanism; no high-temperature coercivity advantage

refs.ref: Ames Lab MnBi R&D (Tang/Cui 2025, R&D World): polymer-coated MnBi particles, anisotropic bonded magnet
refs.diff: Published research (not yet patented as of filing date); uses organic polymer coating (thermally unstable >180°C); the present invention's inorganic silicate glass extends operating temperature by 70°C; sintered (not bonded) architecture provides 30% higher density and remanence

refs.ref: US20170250024A1 (Northeastern/Columbia): FeNi L10 rare-earth-free magnets
refs.diff: Different material class (FeNi ordered alloy vs. MnBi); no intergranular coating concept; no high-temperature coercivity advantage (FeNi is room-temperature magnet); abandoned legal status removes direct blocking risk

ideas.ftoJ: MnBi patent space is populated by Korean and Chinese academic institution filings that are narrow in scope and many have lapsed. The Ames Lab polymer-coated MnBi work is in the publication stage without identified granted patents. The inorganic silicate glass intergranular coating concept has no identified prior patents. Motor apparatus claims have clear FTO. No blocking IP identified from CATL, Apple, BASF, or Tesla.

ps.s101n: Physical composition-of-matter and apparatus claims; entirely patent-eligible with no Alice concerns.
ps.s102n: Specific combination of MnBi LTP particles + SiO2-Li2O-B2O3 glass coating at specified thickness + alignment architecture + high-temperature coercivity specification is novel. No prior art discloses inorganic glass coatings on MnBi.
ps.s103n: Using inorganic glass rather than organic polymer as grain-isolation coating for MnBi requires non-obvious insight: glass Tg must align with sintering window; specific ternary SiO2-Li2O-B2O3 composition is not predictable from first principles by PHOSITA; positive temperature coefficient of coercivity of MnBi is counterintuitive motivation to extend to high-temperature motor applications.
ps.s112n: Sol-gel glass coating is well-described chemistry; MnBi powder synthesis and hot-press/SPS sintering are established; the specific magnetic performance metrics are demonstrated in analogous configurations. Full enablement achievable with 3-4 working examples.

ideas.valM: Global permanent magnet market is $22B (2024), with NdFeB representing ~$18B. Industrial motors (pumps, fans, HVAC) requiring high-temperature performance represent ~$3B sub-segment where MnBi is directly competitive. At 3% royalty on magnet value in this segment with 15% market penetration over 10 years, NPV at 15% discount rate = $35-65M. Strategic value in EV motor supply chain resilience adds premium.

ideas.title: Vitrimer-Based Covalent Adaptable Network Dielectric Substrate for Printed Circuit Boards with Triggered Depolymerization Enabling 100% Component and Material Recovery
ideas.desc:
Printed circuit boards generate approximately 5 million metric tons of electronic waste annually, with the FR4 glass-reinforced epoxy substrate making approximately 40% of PCB mass entirely unrecyclable. FR4's thermoset epoxy-amine network is irreversibly cross-linked, requiring high-temperature pyrolysis or mechanical shredding for disposal — both of which destroy the substrate's value and create toxic emissions. Existing alternatives (PLA from Jiva's Soluboard, polylactic acid films) lack the thermal stability (Tg typically <60°C for PLA vs. 130-170°C required for IPC Class 2 processing) and moisture absorption resistance required for electronics manufacturing.

This invention provides a PCB dielectric substrate comprising a covalent adaptable network (CAN) polymer matrix — specifically a vitrimer based on cross-linked dioxaborolane metathesis chemistry — reinforced with woven glass fiber or bio-based hemp/flax fiber, and containing at least 30 wt% renewable lignin-derived aromatic monomer content.
