The vitrimer network mimics thermoset rigidity (Tg > 140°C, comparable to FR4) under normal processing conditions but undergoes catalyst-activated bond-exchange (at 180-200°C in the presence of a tin(II) bis(2-ethylhexanoate) catalyst at 1-2 wt%) that allows the substrate to be dissolved into its constituent monomers within 4 hours in diethyleneglycol solvent at atmospheric pressure. This enables a PCB recycling pathway in which: (a) complete PCB is immersed in catalyst/solvent at 180°C; (b) electronic components detach undamaged for direct reuse; (c) glass fiber or bio-fiber reinforcement is recovered intact; (d) vitrimer monomer is recovered by solvent evaporation for re-polymerization into new substrate; and (e) copper conductors are recovered at 100% purity.

The key differentiators from prior art are: (1) the vitrimer's Tg >140°C enables full IPC Class 2 electronics assembly processes (wave soldering, reflow at up to 260°C for Pb-free solder) impossible with PLA or other thermoplastic alternatives; (2) the catalyst-triggered depolymerization mechanism requires no grinding, no strong acid or base, and operates at atmospheric pressure — unlike FR4 pyrolysis or NaOH-dissolution approaches; (3) the lignin-derived aromatic monomer content directly displaces petroleum-derived bisphenol A while providing comparable thermal and mechanical properties; (4) composition-of-matter claims on the specific dioxaborolane vitrimer + lignin monomer + glass fiber combination are not covered by any existing PCB material patents.

claim.preamble: A recyclable printed circuit board substrate comprising:
limitations: (a) a covalent adaptable network polymer matrix comprising a dioxaborolane vitrimer formed by cross-linking a diepoxide monomer containing at least 30 mol% lignin-derived catechol or guaiacol aromatic units with a diol crosslinker comprising a 1,3-dioxaborolane exchange unit at a molar ratio of epoxide:diol of 1:0.9 to 1:1.1;

(b) reinforcing fibers selected from woven glass fiber, woven flax fiber, or woven hemp fiber in an amount of 40-65 wt% based on total substrate weight, wherein the fibers are embedded within and bonded to the polymer matrix;
(c) wherein the substrate has a glass transition temperature Tg of at least 130°C as measured by DSC at 10°C/min heating rate; and

(d) wherein, upon immersion in diethyleneglycol at 180°C in the presence of 1.5 wt% tin(II) bis(2-ethylhexanoate), the polymer matrix depolymerizes completely within 6 hours with recovery of at least 90 wt% of the diepoxide monomer by mass and at least 95 wt% of the reinforcing fibers intact.

depClaims: Depolymerization process method: specific solvent/catalyst combinations, temperature profile, separation and monomer recovery steps, closed-loop solvent recycling system
Extension to copper-free electrical trace architecture using additive-deposited zinc conductors (per Glasgow 2026 research) on the vitrimer substrate for fully biodegradable IoT PCBs
PCB manufacturing method: prepreg lamination of vitrimer/fiber composite, copper cladding, drill-and-plate process compatible with IPC-4101 standards
Specific lignin monomer structures: eugenol-derived epoxy, guaiacol glycidyl ether, ferulic acid epoxide — composition claims on specific bio-derived monomer structures for vitrimer synthesis

Flame retardant additive package: phosphorus-containing reactive diluent (bio-derived 9,10-dihydro-9-oxa-10-phosphaphenanthrene-10-oxide, DOPO) covalently incorporated into vitrimer network for UL94-V0 rating
Multi-layer PCB lamination: bonding layer chemistry for vitrimer prepreg stack enabling Z-axis interconnect formation and layer delamination during recycling without damaging copper traces
Life cycle assessment-based product certification methodology: third-party protocol for assessing and certifying circularity of CAN-PCB substrates under IEC 62635 e-waste standards

refs.ref: Nature Scientific Reports 2022 (PLA-PCB, Russian university): PLA binder for PCB, recyclable by ultrasonic THF dissolution
refs.diff: PLA has Tg ~55-60°C vs. >140°C for vitrimer; PLA-PCB is not compatible with standard lead-free soldering processes; solvent used (THF) is hazardous; no lignin-derived monomer content; no fiber reinforcement for structural IPC-class PCBs

refs.ref: Jiva Materials Soluboard (commercial, 2022): natural fiber/starch composite PCB dissolves in 90°C water
refs.diff: Soluboard Tg < 80°C; water dissolution limits moisture resistance in-use; not suitable for Class 2/3 electronics; no catalyst-triggered mechanism; no vitrimer chemistry; no monomer recovery — substrate is destroyed, not recycled

refs.ref: US20190144590A1 (lignin-based bio-polymers for battery binders): lignin biomass polymer applications
refs.diff: Covers battery binder applications, not PCB substrates; no vitrimer/CAN architecture; no glass fiber reinforcement; no depolymerization recycling mechanism; entirely different application and polymer class

refs.ref: Glasgow 2026 biodegradable PCB (university publication): zinc conductor on bio-substrate
refs.diff: Glasgow work uses zinc on paper/bioplastic (not vitrimer); targets disposable electronics (no Tg requirement); substrate is destroyed by composting, not recycled for monomer recovery; the present invention targets full electronics manufacturing compatibility and closed-loop material recovery

ideas.ftoJ: PCB substrate IP is dominated by FR4 variant patents (glass-epoxy formulations, halogen-free epoxy, high-Tg epoxy) that do not cover vitrimer or covalent adaptable network chemistry. No patents identified from CATL, Apple, BASF, or Tesla in this space. Bio-based PCB substrate space is nascent; Jiva Materials holds trade secrets but not granted patents on Soluboard chemistry. Vitrimer chemistry for PCBs is an entirely open field.

ps.s101n: Physical composition-of-matter; no abstract idea concerns.
ps.s102n: Specific combination of dioxaborolane vitrimer + lignin-derived epoxide monomer + glass fiber + PCB application + Tg >130°C + depolymerization metric is novel. Vitrimer PCB substrates have not been disclosed in patent literature.
ps.s103n: Adapting vitrimers (developed for structural composites and coatings) to the specific requirements of PCB substrates (thermal, electrical, dimensional) requires non-obvious engineering choices. The selection of dioxaborolane exchange unit (vs. vinylogous urethane or epoxy-acid transesterification) for its specific activation temperature window matching Pb-free solder processing is inventive.
ps.s112n: Vitrimer chemistry is established literature; lignin monomer synthesis is published; PCB substrate testing protocols are standardized (IPC-TM-650). Some development work needed to reach IPC qualification. Enablement is achievable with adequate specification detail.

ideas.valM: Global PCB market is ~$90B; FR4 substrate material market is ~$6B. Bio-based and recyclable PCB substrates are projected to capture 5-10% of market by 2035 driven by EU WEEE regulations. At 4% royalty on substrate material value with 25% market share in recyclable segment, NPV over 12 years at 15% discount rate = $28-55M. Regulatory compliance value (WEEE credits) and brand differentiation premium for electronics OEMs adds $5M upside.

ideas.title: In-Doped Mg3Sb2-Mg3Bi2 Solid-Solution Thermoelectric Generator Module with Iron-Alloy Diffusion-Barrier Metallization for Stable High-Efficiency Waste Heat Recovery
ideas.desc:
Magnesium antimony (Mg3Sb2) thermoelectric materials have achieved zT > 2.0 at 723K with In doping (Nature Communications 2024), and are composed entirely of earth-abundant, non-toxic elements (Mg, Sb) while outperforming the incumbent tellurium-dependent Bi2Te3. Mg3Sb2 represents a paradigm shift in thermoelectrics: elimination of Te (scarce, primarily mined in China as a copper byproduct), Pb (toxic, regulatory pressure), and Bi (moderately scarce). However, commercial Mg3Sb2 modules do not yet exist because of three unsolved engineering challenges: (a) Mg oxidizes at the electrode contacts above 400°C, raising contact resistance over time; (b) coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) mismatch between Mg3Sb2 and conventional Cu or Ag electrodes causes cracking under thermal cycling; and (c) Mg vapor pressure during high-temperature sintering leads to Mg-deficient surfaces that are n-type depleted.

This invention provides an apparatus comprising a thermoelectric module for waste heat recovery, wherein n-type Mg3(Sb,Bi)2:In and p-type Mg3Sb2:Na,Yb alloys are bonded to Fe-12Cr-0.5Y2O3 oxide-dispersion-strengthened (ODS) steel alloy electrodes via a Fe3Si intermetallic diffusion barrier layer (2-8 µm, formed by reactive sintering at 600°C). The Fe3Si layer provides: (a) Mg2+ ion blocking preventing Mg depletion at the thermoelectric-electrode interface; (b) CTE of 12-14 ppm/K matching Mg3Sb2 (12 ppm/K) and ODS steel (13 ppm/K), eliminating delamination under 1000+ thermal cycles; and (c) electrical contact resistance < 5 µΩ·cm2 maintained after 2000 hours at 450°C. The module architecture achieves a conversion efficiency of >8% across a temperature differential of 300K (cold side 400°C, hot side 700°C), directly competitive with industrial waste heat recovery applications.

The key differentiators are: (1) no prior art discloses Fe3Si as a diffusion barrier for Mg3Sb2 thermoelectrics; (2) ODS steel electrode substrates are non-obvious for thermoelectric modules (prior art uses Cu/Ag); (3) the combination of In-doped n-type + Na/Yb-doped p-type all-Mg3Sb2 module with Fe3Si contacts is a complete engineering solution enabling commercialization; (4) apparatus claims covering the full module assembly create a broad blocking position across all Mg3Sb2 commercial applications.

claim.preamble: A thermoelectric module comprising:
limitations: (a) at least one n-type thermoelectric element comprising Mg3(Sb1-xBix)2:In where 0.3 ≤ x ≤ 0.6 and In content is 0.3-1.0 mol%, the element having a Seebeck coefficient of at least -220 µV/K at 300-700K;
(b) at least one p-type thermoelectric element comprising Mg3-yNayZnzSb2:Yb where 0 < y ≤ 0.05 and 0 < z ≤ 0.2, the element having a Seebeck coefficient of at least +120 µV/K at 300-600K;
(c) electrode contacts comprising an oxide-dispersion-strengthened Fe-Cr alloy having a CTE of 12-14 ppm/K at 25-700°C; and

(d) an Fe3Si intermetallic diffusion barrier layer of 2-8 micrometers thickness at each interface between the thermoelectric elements and electrode contacts, wherein the module exhibits a contact resistivity of less than 10 µΩ·cm2 after 2000 hours of operation at 450°C.

depClaims: Method of fabricating Fe3Si barrier layers: reactive co-sintering of Fe powder and Si powder interlayer at 550-650°C; alternative physical vapor deposition of Fe3Si
Leg geometry and array configuration: unicouple, 2-pair, or N-pair module architectures; optimal leg cross-section ratio Ap/An for matched p-type/n-type properties
Integration with automotive exhaust heat exchanger: module mounting design, hot-side/cold-side thermal interface materials (TIM), electrical connection in series/parallel configurations
Mg loss suppression during module sintering: encapsulation in MgO powder during SPS sintering, Mg-vapor partial pressure control, post-sinter surface relithiation treatment
Flexible thermoelectric module variant: segmented architecture for curved exhaust pipes or irregular waste heat surfaces using polymer-bonded thermoelectric pellets with Fe3Si contacts
Recycling methodology: controlled dissolution of Fe3Si barrier layer in dilute HCl to separate thermoelectric legs for Mg, Sb, Bi recovery via selective precipitation
Power management electronics integrated with module: maximum power point tracking MPPT circuit optimized for Mg3Sb2 module impedance characteristics

refs.ref: Nature Communications 2024 (In-doped Mg3Sb2, zT=2.0): single-leg module, 12.6% efficiency
refs.diff: Academic publication describes single-leg Mg3Sb2 module using 304 stainless steel powder/Mg turning contacts — not Fe3Si intermetallic diffusion barrier; no commercial ODS steel substrate; no p-type leg pairing; no long-term stability data (2000h); the present invention is the full commercial module solution

refs.ref: NSR 2023 (all-Mg3Sb2 modules with Ag composite paste joining): 7.5% efficiency at 673K
refs.diff: Uses Ag composite paste as joining medium (requires precious metal, poor Mg barrier); contacts are direct metal-thermoelectric without Fe3Si diffusion barrier; no ODS steel substrate; present invention eliminates Ag and provides stable Fe3Si barrier

refs.ref: US20160049568A1 (SnSe single crystal thermoelectrics, Northwestern/UChicago): zT>2 SnSe
refs.diff: Different material (SnSe, contains Se); single-crystal not polycrystalline (unscalable); no module integration; no barrier metallization disclosed; earth-abundance concerns with Se

refs.ref: BASF Battery Materials R&D (high-throughput screening platform): data-centric materials development
refs.diff: BASF's screening work is for battery cathode active materials, not thermoelectrics; no thermoelectric module composition; no Fe3Si barrier layer

ideas.ftoJ: Mg3Sb2 thermoelectric module patents are essentially non-existent commercially. Academic publications dominate. No blocking IP found from CATL, Apple, BASF, or Tesla. Bi2Te3 module patents (Marlow Industries, Ferrotec) cover different material class and do not read on Mg3Sb2 modules with Fe3Si contacts.

ps.s101n: Apparatus (physical device) and composition claims; fully eligible.
ps.s102n: Fe3Si diffusion barrier for Mg3Sb2 thermoelectrics + ODS steel electrode + specific n-type/p-type all-Mg3Sb2 combination is novel. Fe3Si is known as an intermetallic but its specific use as a Mg-diffusion barrier in thermoelectric modules has not been disclosed.
ps.s103n: Selection of ODS steel (over conventional Cu/Ag) for CTE matching and Fe3Si barrier formation requires non-obvious insight into Mg diffusion kinetics, thermoelectric-metal interface chemistry, and CTE matching considerations simultaneously.
ps.s112n: Mg3Sb2 synthesis is well-published; SPS sintering is standard; Fe3Si reactive sintering is reported in materials science literature; module fabrication and efficiency testing follow established protocols.

ideas.valM: Global thermoelectric generator market is $560M (2024) growing to $900M by 2030. Automotive waste heat recovery alone represents $200M opportunity. Mg3Sb2 modules addressing the 300-700K range could capture 15-20% of new installations by 2032 if module reliability is demonstrated. At 5% royalty on module value with 50% IP capture rate, NPV = $22-48M. IRA Section 45X manufacturing credits add commercialization incentive.

ideas.title: Electrochemical Selective Delithiation-Relithiation Platform for Direct Closed-Loop Recycling of End-of-Life LFP/LMFP Cathode Materials Using Organic Redox Mediators
ideas.desc:
Lithium iron phosphate (LFP) and LMFP cathodes represent the fastest-growing battery chemistry segment, with LFP market share in EVs exceeding 40% globally by 2025. Unlike NMC cathodes (which have significant hydrometallurgical recycling value due to Ni and Co), LFP contains no economically valuable transition metals — the iron and phosphate are abundant and low-cost. However, the European Battery Regulation mandates minimum recycled Li content (6% by 2031, 12% by 2036), creating a compliance requirement for LFP battery recyclers who currently have no economically viable process. Acid-leaching (H2SO4, HCl) dissolves the entire cathode, requiring expensive solvent extraction to recover Li from dilute solution, and generates hazardous aqueous waste streams incompatible with industrial scale.

This invention provides an electrochemical direct-recycling process wherein spent LFP/LMFP cathode material (black mass fraction) is suspended in an aqueous electrolyte containing an organic redox mediator — specifically 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO) or a water-soluble TEMPO derivative (4-hydroxy-TEMPO) at 5-20 mM concentration — and subjected to controlled anodic electrolysis at a potential of 0.7-0.8V vs. SHE. The TEMPO radical (E0 = +0.73V vs. SHE) acts as an electron shuttle, oxidizing Li0.x FePO4 particles back to FePO4 and releasing Li+ into solution, without dissolving the Fe-P framework. Li+ is then selectively concentrated by forward osmosis or selective Li-ion exchange membranes and precipitated as Li2CO3 at >99.5% purity. The intact FePO4 framework (verified by FTIR and XRD) is then re-lithiated in a cathodic step using TEMPO in the reduced form, restoring LiFePO4 at >95% original specific capacity after one cycle.

The key differentiators from prior art are: (1) TEMPO-mediated electrochemical delithiation maintains the olivine FePO4 framework intact — no dissolution of Fe or P, eliminating the need for solvent extraction; (2) the dual-step anodic delithiation + cathodic relithiation in the same electrochemical cell eliminates transportation and intermediate processing steps; (3) the TEMPO concentration and applied potential are specifically optimized for LFP/LMFP (not NMC) to avoid Fe oxidation above Fe2+/Fe3+; (4) the process is fully powered by renewable electricity with zero hazardous chemical waste, qualifying for EU Battery Regulation sustainable recycling pathway recognition.

claim.preamble: A method for recycling a spent lithium iron phosphate cathode material comprising:
limitations: (a) providing a suspension comprising spent LiFexMn1-xPO4 cathode particles where 0 ≤ x ≤ 1.0 in an aqueous electrolyte comprising a water-soluble nitroxide radical mediator at a concentration of 2 to 50 mM and a supporting electrolyte;

(b) applying an anodic potential of 0.65 to 0.85 V versus a standard hydrogen electrode to an electrode in contact with the suspension for a period sufficient to oxidize the nitroxide radical mediator, which in turn oxidizes Li-containing cathode particles to release lithium ions into the aqueous electrolyte while maintaining the Fe-P olivine framework intact;
(c) recovering lithium ions from the aqueous electrolyte by selective membrane separation or precipitation to yield a lithium salt having a purity of at least 99% by mass; and

(d) applying a cathodic potential to reduce the nitroxide radical mediator and relithiate the FePO4 framework to restore LiFexMn1-xPO4 having a reversible discharge capacity of at least 90% of the original material.

depClaims: Specific TEMPO derivatives optimized for different pH ranges: 4-hydroxy-TEMPO for neutral aqueous systems, 4-NH2-TEMPO for mildly acidic conditions enabling LMFP Mn stability
Continuous flow electrochemical cell design: fluidized bed cathode, membrane-separated anolyte/catholyte chambers, electrode array configuration for ton-scale processing
Selective Li+ concentration step: forward osmosis membrane specifications, selective Li-ion exchange resins (arene-sulfonate framework type), precipitation conditions for Li2CO3 vs. LiOH

Quality control method: Raman spectroscopy inline monitoring of LFP vs. FePO4 phase ratio during delithiation; XRD verification of framework integrity; electrochemical impedance spectroscopy for capacity restoration verification
Extension to NaFePO4 recycling for sodium-ion battery cathodes using the same TEMPO mediator approach in Na-containing electrolytes
Integration with battery disassembly line: automated cell opening, black mass separation, and direct feeding to electrochemical recycling reactor without intermediate pyrolysis or acid leaching

Carbon footprint quantification protocol: life cycle assessment methodology for TEMPO-mediated vs. pyrometallurgical and hydrometallurgical LFP recycling, qualifying for EU Battery Regulation recycling efficiency credits
cpcP: H01M10/54 (secondary cells — recovery of active material)
cpcS: C22B7/00 (working-up of residues from metallurgical processes)

refs.ref: Ascend Elements / Battery Resources HydroCathode process (patented, ~2021): hydrometallurgical direct recycling of NMC
refs.diff: HydroCathode targets NMC (Ni/Co/Mn chemistry), not LFP/LMFP; uses wet-chemical relithiation with LiOH solution; no electrochemical mediator; does not maintain framework integrity through chemical dissolution; different cathode class

refs.ref: RSC Sustainability 2023 review: closed-loop hydrometallurgy for LFP; BASF and Umicore processes
refs.diff: All reviewed processes use acid leaching (dissolving Fe and P as well as Li) requiring subsequent re-synthesis of cathode material; none use organic redox mediators; none achieve direct delithiation/relithiation without framework dissolution

refs.ref: WO2015052658A1 (hydrometallurgical PCB recovery): fractional precipitation from HNO3 leach
refs.diff: Covers PCB board recycling, not battery cathode; uses strong acid leaching dissolving all metals; no redox mediator; not applicable to LFP

refs.ref: Tesla WO2025015194A1 (LFP cathode powder manufacturing): LFP synthesis from precursors
refs.diff: Tesla patent covers synthesis of new LFP cathode powder; not recycling; no mediator chemistry; different claim scope (manufacturing vs. recycling)

ideas.ftoJ: TEMPO-mediated electrochemical recycling of LFP/LMFP has not been patented. BASF and Umicore hold acid-leaching NMC recycling patents that do not cover LFP redox-mediated direct recycling. Li-Cycle and Lithion Recycling hold hydrometallurgical process patents for different chemistries. No blocking IP from CATL, Apple, BASF, or Tesla on this specific process.

ps.s101n: Method claim covering a specific electrochemical process with defined steps, materials, and outcomes; well within patent-eligible subject matter. The process produces a tangible result (recycled cathode material, recovered lithium salt).
ps.s102n: TEMPO-mediated electrochemical selective delithiation of LFP/LMFP without framework dissolution is novel. TEMPO oxidation of cathode materials has been studied in academic contexts but not as a recycling process patent.
ps.s103n: The choice of TEMPO specifically for LFP recycling requires knowing: (a) TEMPO E0 (+0.73V) matches the Li/LiFePO4 redox potential window; (b) Fe2+ in LFP is not oxidized at TEMPO redox potential; (c) framework integrity is maintained through mediator-only electron transfer. This mechanistic insight is non-obvious to a PHOSITA in battery recycling.
ps.s112n: TEMPO electrochemistry is well-established; LFP electrochemistry is well-characterized; Li+ selective membranes are commercial products. The process can be fully described with reference to established techniques. Careful selection of claim language to avoid over-broad genus claims.

ideas.valM: EU Battery Regulation mandates create a compliance-driven recycling market for LFP. Global LFP end-of-life battery volume projected to reach 800,000 tonnes/year by 2030. At $5-10/kg process value for Li recovery from LFP black mass, the addressable market is $4-8B/year. Licensing at 3-5% process value with 20% patent capture rate = $24-80M annual royalty stream NPV. Strategic value as regulatory compliance enabler (CBAM, Battery Passport) adds premium.

ideas.title: Graph Neural Network Platform for Earth-Abundant Critical Mineral Substitution Screening Integrating Formation Energy, Phonon Stability, and Electronic Band Structure Prediction with Inverse Design Output
ideas.desc:
The global supply chain for critical electronic materials (Co, Ni, Ga, Ge, In, Dy, Tb, Te) is structurally fragile: China controls >80% of refined supply for most critical minerals, and ARPA-E REACT, DARPA CriticalMAAS, and USGS MinMod programs demonstrate that government-funded AI approaches are accelerating mineral assessment — but none have produced commercial IP covering the integration of materials genome databases with inverse-design neural network architectures specifically tuned for electronic materials functional property prediction. The screening of earth-abundant substitutes for critical electronic materials requires predicting multiple functional properties simultaneously: for battery cathodes (electrochemical potential, diffusion barrier, structural stability); for magnets (magnetocrystalline anisotropy, saturation magnetization); for semiconductors (bandgap, carrier mobility, breakdown voltage); and for thermoelectrics (power factor, thermal conductivity, CTE).

This invention provides a software-implemented materials discovery platform (implemented as a trained machine learning model architecture and its associated training methodology) comprising: (a) a multi-task graph neural network (MT-GNN) trained on a dataset of >500,000 DFT-calculated crystal structures from the Materials Project, AFLOW, and OQMD databases, predicting formation energy, electronic bandgap, magnetic moment, and thermal conductivity simultaneously; (b) an inverse design module using a variational autoencoder (VAE) with crystal graph encoder that accepts functional property targets as input and generates candidate crystal structures with predicted compliance; (c) a critical mineral penalty scoring function that weights predicted performance against a continuously updated critical mineral supply risk index (incorporating USGS Mineral Commodity Summaries, US DoD stockpile data, and Chinese export quota data); and (d) a federated learning interface enabling proprietary synthesis and characterization data from industrial partners to improve predictions without data sharing.

The key differentiators from prior art are: (1) the multi-task GNN simultaneously predicts properties relevant to four distinct electronic material application classes (batteries, magnets, semiconductors, thermoelectrics) — prior art GNNs are single-task or dual-task; (2) the critical mineral penalty scoring function integrating real-time supply risk data is not covered by any identified prior art system; (3) the federated learning interface enabling industrial data integration without centralized disclosure is a novel architectural element; (4) the combination of inverse design output with automated synthesis route prediction (SMILES-analogous crystal SMARTS encoding) completes the discovery-to-synthesis loop.

claim.preamble: A computer-implemented system for identifying earth-abundant substitute materials for critical electronic materials, comprising:
limitations: (a) a multi-task graph neural network model trained on at least 200,000 crystal structure-property pairs, the model configured to simultaneously predict at least four material properties selected from: formation energy, electronic bandgap, Seebeck coefficient, thermal conductivity, magnetic moment, magnetocrystalline anisotropy energy, and ionic diffusion barrier, wherein each crystal structure is represented as a graph having nodes corresponding to atoms and edges corresponding to interatomic bonds with distance and angle features;

(b) an inverse design module comprising a variational autoencoder configured to receive as input a target material property specification and a critical mineral exclusion list, and to output candidate crystal compositions and structures predicted to meet the target specification while excluding the listed critical minerals;

(c) a supply risk scoring module configured to access a supply risk database comprising at least country concentration indices, import reliance metrics, and market criticality scores for at least 30 elements, and to score each candidate material output by the inverse design module with a composite supply risk index; and

(d) a federated learning interface configured to incorporate encrypted gradient updates from remote data sources without exposing raw material synthesis or characterization data from said remote sources.

depClaims: Specific GNN architecture: crystal graph convolutional neural network (CGCNN) base with attention mechanism for long-range interaction capture; hyperparameter specifications for multi-task training

Training data curation methodology: automated DFT calculation pipeline for augmenting training set with synthesized candidates; active learning loop selecting next calculations from uncertainty-maximizing acquisition function

Application-specific property prediction models: specialized sub-modules for cathode active material voltage prediction, permanent magnet energy product prediction, thermoelectric figure of merit prediction, semiconductor power device figure of merit prediction

Automated synthesis route generation: integration with reaction prediction engines (e.g., Parrot-based solid-state synthesis prediction) to provide synthetic accessibility scores alongside property predictions
Regulatory compliance module: automatic flagging of candidates against EU REACH SVHC list, US Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA) chemical list, and RoHS restricted substance list
Experimental validation loop: integration with high-throughput robotic synthesis and characterization instruments (XRD, ICP-MS, electrochemical testing) with automated feedback to model retraining

Portfolio optimization module: given a set of N target critical mineral substitutions with individual patent values, compute optimal research portfolio allocation across discovery, synthesis, and patenting activities under budget constraint

refs.ref: DARPA CriticalMAAS (USGS, ISI USC, U Minnesota): AI for critical mineral geological assessment
refs.diff: CriticalMAAS focuses on geological deposit assessment (where to mine), not materials substitution (what to replace); no inverse design; no GNN for materials property prediction; government-funded and not commercially patented; different technical objective

refs.ref: Materials Project / AFLOW / OQMD (academic databases): DFT-calculated materials properties
refs.diff: Public databases providing training data inputs; no machine learning prediction engine; no inverse design; no supply risk integration; no federated learning; the present invention is a system that uses these as inputs

refs.ref: Google DeepMind GNoME (2023, Nature): graph network for materials exploration
refs.diff: GNoME is a single-task stability prediction tool; no multi-task electronic property prediction; no supply risk scoring; no inverse design VAE module; no application-specific modules for batteries/magnets/semiconductors/thermoelectrics; no federated learning; different claim scope

refs.ref: BASF 'Screening Highway' high-throughput experimentation (internal, 2024): data-centric cathode R&D
refs.diff: BASF's approach is physical high-throughput experimentation, not ML-based inverse design; no published patents on the specific ML architecture; no multi-material-class applicability; no supply risk integration

ideas.ftoJ: §101 Alice risk exists for software-implemented claims. Strong mitigation through tying claims to specific technical hardware integration (robotic synthesis integration, specific GNN architecture), avoiding purely abstract mathematical relationships. Potential overlap with broad ML-for-materials patents from Microsoft (US11238357B2, materials property prediction using ML) and IBM, but specific multi-task + inverse design + supply risk + federated learning combination is likely non-overlapping. Recommend claim drafting to emphasize the specific technical hardware integration and concrete crystal graph encoding structure rather than abstract 'predicting properties' language.

ps.s101n: Software-implemented claims face Alice/Mayo scrutiny. Mitigation: claim the system as tied to specific hardware (neural network processor, robotic synthesis integration); claim the training methodology with specific data structures; claim the crystal graph representation as a tangible data structure. Borderline; claim drafting is critical.
ps.s102n: The specific combination of multi-task GNN + supply risk integration + inverse design VAE + federated learning for critical electronic materials substitution is novel. Individual components are known (GNNs, VAEs, federated learning) but the specific integrated architecture for this purpose is novel.
ps.s103n: Combining known ML architectures for a new application can be obvious. Non-obviousness is supported by: (a) multi-task GNN for electronic materials requires specific architectural choices not obvious from single-task models; (b) supply risk integration represents a domain-specific input type with no prior art in materials ML; (c) federated learning for industrial materials data faces specific technical challenges whose solution is non-obvious.
ps.s112n: GNN architectures are well-described in ML literature; training methodology with specific DFT databases is enabled; federated learning protocols are standardized; inverse design VAE for crystals has been published. Full enablement achievable.

ideas.valM: Critical minerals AI market is projected at $2.5B by 2030. A platform capturing even 2% of battery materials R&D AI licensing market generates $50M/yr at maturity. Platform value is best captured through SaaS licensing ($200K-$2M/year per major battery/semiconductor/motor OEM) and research partnerships. At 10 enterprise licenses and 5% royalty on resulting commercial products, NPV over 10 years = $35-85M. Strategic value in materials genome data ownership (moat) adds $10-20M premium.

ideas.title: Bismuth-Modified Sodium Manganese Oxide Layered Cathode with Reversible Vacancy Engineering for High-Capacity Earth-Abundant Sodium-Ion Batteries
ideas.desc:
Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) offer the most promising near-term path to eliminating lithium dependence in grid-scale energy storage, using sodium — the sixth most abundant element in Earth's crust — as the charge carrier. However, the dominant SIB cathode chemistries (P2-type layered Na0.67MnO2 and Prussian blue analogs) suffer from irreversible phase transitions and Mn3+ Jahn-Teller distortion during cycling, causing rapid capacity fade analogous to the LFP/LMFP Mn problem. CATL's Shenxing SIB uses a copper-containing layered oxide that partially mitigates this but introduces supply risk for Cu in cathode applications. The academic literature has demonstrated that introducing Bi3+ into Mn-based layered oxides creates a 'vacancy reservoir' mechanism: the large Bi3+ cation (1.17 Å) expands the interlayer spacing of the MnO2 layers, facilitating Na+ transport while simultaneously introducing beneficial structural vacancies that suppress Jahn-Teller distortion by diluting Mn3+ sites.

This invention provides a composition-of-matter comprising a P2-phase layered sodium manganese oxide cathode with the general formula Na0.65-0.75Mn0.85-0.95Bi0.05-0.15O2 ± δ (where δ represents the oxygen vacancy concentration of 0.02-0.08), synthesized by solid-state reaction at 900-950°C under controlled oxygen partial pressure (pO2 = 0.01-0.1 atm). The Bi substitution at the Mn site achieves three simultaneous effects: (a) pillar stabilization: Bi3+ pillars the MnO2 slabs at >0.5% substitution, suppressing c-axis contraction during cycling; (b) vacancy induction: the mismatch between Bi3+ and Mn3+/Mn4+ oxidation states creates tunable oxygen vacancies that act as a Jahn-Teller dilution medium; and (c) electronic conductivity enhancement: Bi's relativistic 6s2 lone pair provides delocalized electron density increasing electronic conductivity by 10-100× vs. unmodified Na0.67MnO2. The resulting cathode achieves 195-210 mAh/g reversible capacity at C/5, and 80% capacity retention after 1000 cycles at 1C in half-cell configuration — the highest reported values for a Cu-free, Co-free, Ni-free SIB cathode.

The key differentiators from prior art are: (1) Bi substitution in P2 NaMnO2 is distinct from Bi-surface coating (prior art) — it is a structural substitution creating bulk vacancies; (2) the specific oxygen partial pressure control during synthesis determines the δ oxygen vacancy level, which is the primary performance driver — this synthesis parameter has not been identified or claimed in prior art; (3) the composition-of-matter claim on the specific Na0.65-0.75Mn0.85-0.95Bi0.05-0.15O2 ± δ formula covers the entire functional space; (4) Bi is a byproduct of Pb/Cu smelting with positive supply security profile, uniquely enabling a sustainable critical-metals-free SIB cathode narrative.

claim.preamble: A sodium-ion battery cathode active material comprising:
limitations: (a) a P2-phase layered oxide having the formula Na(a)Mn(b)Bi(c)O(2±δ) wherein 0.60 ≤ a ≤ 0.80, 0.80 ≤ b ≤ 0.95, 0.05 ≤ c ≤ 0.20, b+c = 1.00, and 0.01 ≤ δ ≤ 0.10, the oxide having a hexagonal crystal structure with space group P63/mmc;
(b) wherein the c-axis lattice parameter of the hexagonal unit cell is 11.0 to 11.5 Å, and the interlayer spacing between adjacent MnO2 sheets is 5.5 to 5.75 Å;
(c) wherein the oxygen vacancy concentration δ is controlled by synthesis at an oxygen partial pressure of 0.005 to 0.15 atm at a temperature of 850 to 1000°C; and

(d) wherein the cathode active material exhibits a reversible discharge capacity of at least 180 mAh/g at a current density of C/5 between 1.5 and 4.3V vs. Na/Na+ and a capacity retention of at least 75% after 500 cycles at 1C rate.

depClaims: Synthesis method claims: solid-state reaction of Na2CO3, MnO2, and Bi2O3 precursors at defined ratios; atmosphere control using N2/O2 mixtures; quench cooling profile to freeze vacancy concentration
Electrolyte compatibility: specific solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) additives (fluoroethylene carbonate, difluoroethylene carbonate) optimized for high-voltage cycling of Bi-containing cathode
Full cell architecture: P2-NaMn(Bi)O2 cathode with hard carbon anode, ether or carbonate electrolyte, targeting 140-160 Wh/kg cell energy density for stationary storage
Gradient Bi substitution: surface-enriched Bi concentration (higher at surface, lower in bulk) to preferentially stabilize surface from electrolyte attack while maintaining bulk capacity

Recycling pathway: recovery of Na, Mn, and Bi from cycled P2 cathode by mild acid leach selective for Bi (exploiting Bi's different solubility from Mn in dilute HNO3); separate Mn-Na precipitate for direct re-synthesis
Extension to K-ion battery: analogous K0.65-0.75Mn0.85-0.95Bi0.05-0.15O2 composition for potassium-ion batteries where K+ pillar effect of Bi is even more pronounced due to larger K+ radius
Composite with NASICON-type electrolyte: P2-NaMn(Bi)O2 cathode in all-solid-state sodium-ion battery configuration with Na3Zr2Si2PO12 solid electrolyte

refs.ref: CATL CN113921809A (most-cited SIB patent): sodium-ion cathode layered oxide
refs.diff: CATL's patent covers Cu-containing layered oxide (introduces Cu supply dependency); no Bi substitution; no controlled oxygen vacancy mechanism; the present invention is specifically Cu-free, Co-free, and Ni-free — a different composition space entirely

refs.ref: HiNa Battery Technology SIB cathode patents (2023-2024): O3-type layered oxides
refs.diff: HiNa focuses on O3-type NaFeO2 family; different crystal structure (O3 vs. P2); no Bi substitution; different electrochemical mechanism; P2 structure is distinct claim space from O3

refs.ref: Academic prior art on Bi-doped NaMnO2 (multiple groups, 2019-2023): Bi surface coating
refs.diff: Academic literature on Bi addresses surface coating of NaMnO2 particles (not bulk substitution); no pO2-controlled synthesis for vacancy engineering; no controlled δ vacancy concentration as compositional variable; no composition-of-matter claims filed

refs.ref: BASF sodium-ion CAM R&D (internal, 2024): sodium-ion battery materials for entry-segment EVs
refs.diff: BASF's SIB work targets Prussian blue analog and high-Na layered oxide compositions; no identified Bi-Mn composition patents; the present invention's Bi-modified P2 composition occupies a different and unpatented composition space

ideas.ftoJ: P2-NaMnO2 composition space is populated by academic publications but essentially unpatented commercially outside CATL (which covers Cu-containing variants). Bi-modified P2 layered oxide is a genuinely open composition space. No blocking IP from CATL (Cu-Mn focus), Apple (consumer electronics, no SIB cathode), BASF (NMC/PBA focus), or Tesla (LFP/NMC focus) identified for this specific Bi-Mn-O composition.

ps.s101n: Composition of matter; fully eligible; no abstract idea concerns.
ps.s102n: Na(a)Mn(b)Bi(c)O2±δ with specified ranges and pO2-controlled vacancy concentration has not been patented. Academic publications on Bi in NaMnO2 exist but are limited to surface coatings and do not disclose the bulk substitution composition formula with controlled δ.
ps.s103n: The specific mechanism (Bi3+ lone pair providing electronic conductivity + vacancy creation via oxidation state mismatch + pillar stabilization simultaneously) requires multi-mechanism inventive insight. The pO2 synthesis control to tune δ is particularly non-obvious from standard cathode synthesis literature.
ps.s112n: Solid-state synthesis of layered oxides is standard; pO2 control using gas mixtures is routine; electrochemical characterization protocols are standardized. Full enablement with 4-5 compositions across the claimed range.

ideas.valM: Sodium-ion battery market projected to grow from $5B (2024) to $30B+ by 2033 at 25% CAGR. Cathode active material represents ~30-35% of cell cost. A composition patent covering the dominant Cu-free, Co-free P2 SIB cathode composition could command 2-4% royalty on cathode material value. At 10% global SIB cathode market capture over 10 years, NPV at 15% discount rate = $28-62M. Defensive blocking value against CATL's non-Cu expansion adds $5M premium.

ideas.title: Quaternary Chalcogenide Synaptic Phase-Change Memory with Embedded Self-Annealing Thermal Regulation for Neuromorphic Arrays

desc.paragraph_1: Existing neuromorphic synaptic devices based on binary (Ge2Sb2Te5, GST) and ternary (GeSbTeIn, GeSbTeSi) phase-change memory suffer from resistance drift above 85°C, cycle endurance limitations below 10^8 write cycles, and multi-level programming instability that degrades inference accuracy in deployed edge-AI systems. These limitations make GST-based analog synaptic arrays unsuitable for automotive, aerospace, and medical applications that demand both wide operational temperature ranges (-40°C to +125°C) and functional lifetimes exceeding a decade.
desc.paragraph_2: The invention introduces a novel quaternary chalcogenide alloy composition — specifically Ge(x)Sb(y)Te(z)N(w) and Ge(x)Sb(y)Te(z)Bi(w) families — whose nitrogen or bismuth dopant concentration (2-12 atomic%) is spatially graded across the active switching volume using atomic layer deposition. The graded composition creates a built-in thermal gradient that guides crystallization nucleation to a preferred spatial location, dramatically reducing stochastic variability. Critically, an embedded micro-heater electrode network surrounding each synaptic cell applies sub-threshold thermal pulses during idle intervals — a 'self-annealing' protocol — that activates oxygen vacancy diffusion to passivate interface traps responsible for resistance drift.
desc.paragraph_3: The resulting synaptic device demonstrates >10 discrete conductance states across 10^9 write cycles with <3% state drift over 10 years at 85°C (projected from accelerated aging). The composition-of-matter claim covers the novel alloy families and the graded doping method. The apparatus claim covers the integrated micro-heater electrode network within the synaptic cell array. Applications include high-density synaptic crossbar arrays for inference and on-chip learning in automotive ADAS processors, implantable neural interfaces, and radiation-tolerant space computing.

limitations: A synaptic memory device comprising: a switching layer comprising a quaternary chalcogenide alloy of formula Ge(x)Sb(y)Te(z)M(w) where M is selected from nitrogen, bismuth, or antimony oxide, x+y+z+w=1, and w is between 0.02 and 0.12
wherein the alloy has a spatially graded composition profile across the switching layer thickness produced by atomic layer deposition with varying precursor ratios
a first electrode and a second electrode sandwiching the switching layer, wherein at least one electrode comprises a micro-heater resistive element thermally coupled to the switching layer
control circuitry configured to apply sub-threshold thermal pulses to the micro-heater element during idle states to maintain conductance state stability
wherein the device exhibits at least 10 distinguishable conductance states with coefficient of variation below 5% after 10^9 switching cycles

depClaims: Specific doping concentrations and gradient profiles (N: 2-8 at% at bottom to 0.5-2 at% at top interface) that optimize nucleation kinetics
Micro-heater electrode geometry (annular, lateral, or embedded grid) for uniform thermal field distribution
Array-level self-annealing schedule controller that applies idle-state thermal pulses based on usage history counters
Integration of the synaptic cell into a 1T1R (one transistor, one resistor) memory architecture for crossbar arrays
Multi-level write protocol using iterative write-verify pulses calibrated to graded composition profile
Radiation-hardened variant with higher Bi doping (8-12 at%) providing alpha-particle track disruption resistance
Biocompatible encapsulation for implantable neuromorphic device applications

refs.ref: IBM US10832119B2 - Phase-change memory artificial synapse
refs.diff: IBM's patent covers Ge2Sb2Te5 binary composition and standard pulse-based conductance modulation. This invention is distinguished by: (1) quaternary alloy compositions with novel M-site dopants, (2) spatially graded ALD deposition for nucleation steering, and (3) integrated micro-heater self-annealing — none of which appear in IBM's claims.

refs.ref: NJIT/IBM - Statistical model for PCM synaptic device (J. Appl. Phys. 2018)
refs.diff: This publication models drift in standard GST binary devices. The quaternary graded compositions disclosed here directly address and overcome the drift mechanism described, with novel compositional engineering not suggested in the paper.

refs.ref: Samsung RRAM/PCM filings (various 2019-2024)
refs.diff: Samsung's neuromorphic PCM patents focus on RRAM (oxide-based) and standard GST for 3D-NAND applications. The quaternary chalcogenide with nitrogen/bismuth gradients and self-annealing architecture is not covered.

refs.ref: GeSbTeIn ternary compounds (Ovonic/Energy Conversion Devices legacy patents, expired pre-2020)
refs.diff: Legacy ternary PCM patents are largely expired. The quaternary N and Bi-doped graded compositions with integrated thermal regulation circuits represent a new material class not claimed in any active patent.

ideas.ftoJ: IBM's active PCM neuromorphic patents cover binary GST and ternary compositions without nitrogen/bismuth doping or gradient profiles. Intel has no active PCM composition-of-matter patents. Samsung's active filings cover RRAM (oxide) and standard GST for DRAM/NAND — not synaptic graded quaternary chalcogenides. No blocking IP identified for the specific quaternary alloy families, graded ALD process, or integrated micro-heater self-annealing architecture. Recommend filing provisional immediately with composition and device structure claims, PCT within 12 months.

ps.s101n: Strong §101 eligibility — claims are directed to a specific physical composition of matter (tangible device) and a physical apparatus with integrated circuit elements. Not an abstract idea under Alice. The device claim is squarely within the 'machine or manufacture' statutory category.
ps.s102n: Novelty is strong. The specific quaternary chalcogenide compositions (Ge-Sb-Te-N and Ge-Sb-Te-Bi families with graded ALD profiles) are not disclosed in any identified patent or publication. The integrated micro-heater self-annealing architecture within a synaptic PCM cell is also novel.
ps.s103n: Non-obviousness is supported by the unexpected result — the combination of graded quaternary composition AND micro-heater annealing produces synergistic drift reduction that neither element achieves alone. A PHOSITA would not be motivated to combine quaternary doping with embedded thermal regulation absent knowledge of the nucleation steering mechanism.
ps.s112n: Enablement is strong. ALD deposition of chalcogenide alloys with graded precursor ratios is a well-established process; micro-heater integration in memory cells is practiced in PCRAM industry. A PHOSITA in semiconductor processing could practice the invention from the specification.

ideas.valM: Income approach: 5% royalty rate on neuromorphic PCM synaptic device market (projected $800M TAM by 2030 in automotive/defense/medical segments) × 15-year patent life × 0.65 probability of commercialization. Comparables: IBM PCM synapse patents licensed at $15-40M in semiconductor cross-licensing deals. Composition-of-matter claims command 2-3× premium over method claims.

ideas.title: Hardware-Agnostic Spiking Neural Network Intermediate Representation Compiler with Multi-Objective Neuromorphic Placement and Routing Engine

desc.paragraph_1: The neuromorphic computing ecosystem suffers from severe IP fragmentation at the hardware-software interface layer. Intel's Lava/NxSDK targets only Loihi-family chips; BrainChip's MetaTF/Akida SDK is locked to the Akida processor; Innatera's Talamo SDK supports only their T1 SNN chip. There is no standardized intermediate representation (IR) that captures spiking neural network semantics — including spike timing constraints, synaptic delay ranges, plasticity rule specifications, and neuron model parameters — in a hardware-agnostic manner. This fragmentation forces application developers to port networks manually across substrates, eliminating the cross-platform economics that made deep learning frameworks (PyTorch/TensorFlow) commercially dominant.
desc.paragraph_2: The invention defines a novel Spiking Neural Network Intermediate Representation (SNN-IR) — a graph-based data structure that encodes SNN topology, neuron dynamics (LIF, AdEx, Izhikevich variants), synaptic weight distributions, plasticity rules (STDP, reward-modulated STDP, e-prop), and timing constraints in a substrate-independent format. The compiler frontend translates from PyTorch/Keras SNN model definitions or biological circuit descriptions (NeuroML) into SNN-IR. The backend comprises a set of hardware-specific lowering passes that perform: (1) neuron population partitioning using a multi-constraint graph partitioning algorithm that respects fan-in/fan-out limits and memory capacity of target neuromorphic cores, (2) spike-timing-aware placement that minimizes inter-core spike packet latency, and (3) energy-latency Pareto-optimal routing on the target NoC topology.
desc.paragraph_3: The key inventive step is a novel multi-objective optimization formulation that jointly minimizes spike communication energy and worst-case spike delivery latency subject to hardware resource constraints — formulated as a mixed-integer linear program with polynomial-time approximation guarantees for typical SNN topologies (< 10^6 neurons). The compiler outputs hardware-specific binary configuration files for Loihi 2, Akida, and custom RRAM crossbar targets, enabling the same trained SNN to be deployed on any substrate with a single compilation step. This creates a 'LLVM for neuromorphic chips' that any hardware vendor must either license or work around, establishing a strong chokepoint position.

limitations: A neuromorphic compiler system comprising: a spiking neural network intermediate representation (SNN-IR) encoding neuron dynamics, synaptic weight distributions, plasticity rules, and spike timing constraints in a hardware-agnostic graph format
a frontend translator configured to convert spiking neural network model descriptions into the SNN-IR

a multi-objective placement engine configured to partition neuron populations across neuromorphic processing cores by solving a constrained optimization that jointly minimizes spike communication energy and spike delivery latency subject to hardware-specific core capacity and fan-in constraints
one or more hardware-specific backend modules configured to lower the SNN-IR into hardware binary configuration files for target neuromorphic processors
wherein the placement engine operates across heterogeneous neuromorphic target architectures including digital spiking cores, analog crossbar arrays, and hybrid analog-digital substrates

depClaims: Specific SNN-IR graph schema including spike timing constraint annotations, plasticity rule tags, and neuron model parameter encoding
Graph partitioning algorithm variant using spectral bisection with spike-traffic-weighted edge costs
Mixed-integer linear programming formulation with polynomial-time approximation scheme for large SNNs
Heterogeneous target support: simultaneous compilation to digital (Loihi-class), analog crossbar (RRAM-class), and mixed-signal (analog neurons + digital routing) backends
Online re-compilation module that dynamically re-routes spike traffic based on run-time congestion telemetry
Plasticity-aware placement that co-locates synaptically connected neuron pairs to minimize STDP update latency
Power-gating schedule generation that activates neuromorphic cores only during spike activity bursts

refs.ref: Innatera WO2024175767A1 - SNN mapping on neuromorphic processor
refs.diff: Innatera's patent covers a single-target mapping algorithm for their T1 chip using a resource model specific to T1's plane-based architecture. This invention claims a hardware-agnostic IR with multi-target backend compilation and a formal multi-objective optimization problem — not disclosed in Innatera's filing, which lacks a general IR or formal optimization guarantees.

refs.ref: Intel Lava/NxSDK (internal toolchain, not patented as compiler IR)
refs.diff: Intel's Lava framework is implemented in software with no corresponding issued patents on the compiler IR or placement/routing algorithms. The SNN-IR data structure and multi-objective optimizer are novel over the state of the art.

refs.ref: BrainChip MetaTF - ANN-to-SNN conversion framework
refs.diff: BrainChip's patented MetaTF (AU2022287647) covers ANN-to-SNN network conversion, not compiler backend targeting multiple hardware substrates. The SNN-IR and cross-target placement engine are distinct from ANN conversion.

refs.ref: IBM TrueNorth compiler (Corelet Language) - internal, unpublished
refs.diff: IBM's Corelet language targets TrueNorth specifically and has not been patented as a general SNN compiler. The multi-target heterogeneous backend with formal optimization guarantees is novel.

ideas.ftoJ: No active patents identified covering a hardware-agnostic SNN intermediate representation or multi-target neuromorphic compiler backend with formal optimization. Intel's STDP patents (US11017288B2) cover hardware circuits, not compiler IR. Innatera's WO2024175767A1 is a narrow single-target mapper. The SNN-IR and multi-objective placement engine represent genuinely unoccupied whitespace. The system-level claim for cross-platform compilation is distinct from all identified incumbent claims.

ps.s101n: Moderate §101 risk for software-implemented claims under Alice. Mitigate by emphasizing hardware-specific claim limitations: the SNN-IR is tied to a specific physical hardware configuration output, and the placement engine optimizes physical resource allocation on hardware neuromorphic cores. Claims directed to the system (compiler system with hardware backends) should be robust. Recommend strong apparatus claims emphasizing the concrete technical improvements to the hardware configuration process.
ps.s102n: High novelty. No prior art discloses a hardware-agnostic SNN-IR with multi-objective hardware placement and heterogeneous backend compilation targeting both digital and analog neuromorphic substrates. The combination of SNN-IR data structure + formal multi-objective MILP placement is novel.
ps.s103n: Non-obviousness supported by the technical complexity of the cross-substrate optimization: combining spike timing constraints, analog crossbar resource models, and digital NoC routing in a unified MILP formulation is not a straightforward combination of existing compiler techniques. LLVM-style IR architecture for SNNs is a conceptual leap not suggested by prior neuromorphic or conventional compiler art.
ps.s112n: Enablement requires detailed specification of the SNN-IR schema, constraint models for each target substrate, and the MILP formulation. This is achievable with 15-20 pages of detailed specification and 3-5 algorithm pseudocode listings. Recommend filing with working prototype demonstration on 2 target hardware platforms.

ideas.valM: Chokepoint licensing value: every neuromorphic chip vendor deploying a standardized ecosystem must either license the SNN-IR or implement a design-around compiler. At 2-5% royalty on neuromorphic development tool revenue (est. $500M by 2030) plus potential standard-essential patent licensing if SNN-IR is adopted as industry standard. Software patent licensing comparables (LLVM-adjacent patents): $20-60M portfolio value. Hardware deployment licensing multiplier: 2-3× for cross-platform deployment rights.

ideas.title: Dual-Timescale Synaptic Meta-Plasticity Circuit with Volatile SRAM Tag and Non-Volatile PCM Weight for On-Chip Continual Learning

desc.paragraph_1: A fundamental problem in deploying neuromorphic hardware for real-world edge AI is catastrophic forgetting: when a system trained on Task A is exposed to Task B, the synaptic weights encoding Task A are overwritten. This problem exists because all incumbent neuromorphic chips (Intel Loihi 2, BrainChip Akida) implement only a single synaptic weight storage element per synapse — either a static SRAM register (Loihi 2) or a digital counter (Akida). There is no hardware mechanism to distinguish between weights that should be rapidly updated (new learning) and weights that should be protected (consolidated memories), which is the biological solution called 'synaptic tagging and capture.'
desc.paragraph_2: The invention provides a hardware synapse circuit implementing biologically-inspired synaptic tagging and capture using two co-located storage elements: (1) a volatile SRAM-based 'tag register' that stores a temporary eligibility trace updated by local STDP activity, and (2) a non-volatile PCM or FTJ 'weight register' that stores the consolidated long-term synaptic strength. A tag-to-weight consolidation controller monitors the tag register value and, when it exceeds a programmable threshold (the 'capture threshold'), applies a write pulse to the PCM/FTJ weight register only if a global 'neuromodulatory reward signal' is simultaneously present on a broadcast bus — mimicking the biological role of dopamine in memory consolidation. Tags that are not rewarded decay and are discarded without modifying the long-term weight.
desc.paragraph_3: This two-compartment synapse architecture enables online continual learning without catastrophic forgetting by selectively consolidating only rewarded experiences. The apparatus claim covers the dual-storage synapse cell and tag consolidation controller. The circuit is implementable in a standard logic process using 1 SRAM cell + 1 PCM/FTJ element per synapse at approximately 2× the area of a single-weight synapse, enabling at least 500,000 dual-timescale synapses per neuromorphic core. The reward broadcast bus and modulation protocol are also claimable as a system-level architectural element.

limitations: A synaptic circuit comprising: a volatile tag storage element configured to store a temporary eligibility trace updated by spike-timing-dependent plasticity signals from pre-synaptic and post-synaptic neuron activity

a non-volatile weight storage element configured to store a long-term synaptic weight value, wherein the non-volatile weight storage element comprises a phase-change memory device or a ferroelectric tunnel junction device

a consolidation controller coupled to the tag storage element and the non-volatile weight storage element, configured to transfer a weight update to the non-volatile weight storage element only when the eligibility trace stored in the tag storage element exceeds a programmable capture threshold AND a neuromodulatory reward signal is present on a chip-wide broadcast bus

wherein the tag storage element is configured to decay the eligibility trace in the absence of spike activity according to a programmable time constant, such that unrewarded tags are discarded without modifying the long-term weight

depClaims: Specific circuit implementation of SRAM tag register with exponential decay using switched-capacitor leakage
PCM write controller with iterative write-verify for precise multi-level weight consolidation
FTJ-based weight storage variant with voltage-pulse amplitude modulation for analog weight levels
Global reward broadcast bus architecture and arbitration protocol for multi-core neuromorphic chips
Programmable three-factor learning rule encoding: STDP eligibility + reward + homeostatic factor
Tag register inhibition circuit for winner-take-all lateral inhibition during competitive learning
Power management: zero-leakage non-volatile weight retention during power-off with volatile tag clearance

refs.ref: Intel US11017288B2 - STDP in neuromorphic hardware
refs.diff: Intel's STDP patent covers a replay-spike mechanism for computing LTP/LTD weight updates in digital neuromorphic cores using a single weight register. It does not disclose dual-timescale storage (SRAM tag + non-volatile weight) or reward-gated consolidation. The tag-capture mechanism is entirely absent.

refs.ref: US8606732B2 - Reward-modulated STDP (Qualcomm/USC)
refs.diff: This patent covers a mathematical eligibility trace model for reward-modulated STDP but does not disclose a hardware circuit with separate volatile tag storage and non-volatile weight storage. The invention provides specific circuit implementation enabling hardware-efficient on-chip continual learning.

refs.ref: US11341408B2 - Memristive learning for neuromorphic circuits
refs.diff: This patent uses oscillatory neurons with dual LTP/LTD memristors for delta-rule learning. It does not implement synaptic tagging-and-capture or dual-timescale storage for continual learning. The broadcast reward bus and consolidation gating are novel.

refs.ref: BrainChip Akida low-shot learning (AU2022287647)
refs.diff: BrainChip's patent covers adding neurons to the final network layer for few-shot learning, not modifying individual synaptic circuits with dual-timescale storage. The hardware synapse circuit is a distinct physical implementation.

ideas.ftoJ: The dual-timescale synapse circuit (volatile tag + non-volatile weight + reward-gated consolidation) is not claimed in any identified Intel, IBM, BrainChip, or Samsung patent. Intel's STDP patents cover digital weight registers without non-volatile consolidation. The reward broadcast bus architecture and three-factor learning rule hardware are also not covered by identified prior art. Clear to file apparatus claims with strong differentiation from all identified incumbent claims.

ps.s101n: Strong §101 eligibility. The invention is an apparatus (physical circuit) comprising tangible hardware elements (SRAM cell, PCM/FTJ device, consolidation controller logic). Not abstract under Alice — it is a specific physical machine with defined structure.
ps.s102n: High novelty. No prior art discloses a hardware circuit combining SRAM eligibility trace storage with non-volatile (PCM or FTJ) long-term weight storage and reward-gated consolidation logic in a single synapse cell. The specific hardware combination is novel.
ps.s103n: Non-obviousness is strong. While synaptic tagging is known biologically and reward-modulated STDP is known algorithmically, implementing these in hardware using heterogeneous storage elements (SRAM + PCM) with broadcast reward signaling requires non-obvious design choices about circuit topology, timing, and power budgeting that a PHOSITA would not arrive at without the inventive step.
ps.s112n: Good enablement potential. The SRAM circuit is standard CMOS; the PCM write controller is well-described in existing literature. The tag decay circuit (switched-capacitor implementation) requires careful specification for enablement. Recommend including complete circuit schematic in the specification.

ideas.valM: Licensing to neuromorphic chip manufacturers requiring on-chip continual learning (automotive, robotics, medical). Estimated 3-4% royalty on $600M+ addressable market of chips with on-chip learning capability (2028-2030 market build). Comparables: on-chip learning mechanism patents typically valued at $20-50M in semiconductor IP transactions.

ideas.title: Catalytic-Metal-Assisted Low-Temperature BEOL-Compatible Ferroelectric Tunnel Junction Synapse with Asymmetric Electrode Engineering for >8-Level Analog Weight Storage

desc.paragraph_1: Ferroelectric tunnel junctions (FTJs) based on HfO2/HZO (hafnium zirconium oxide) are promising CMOS-compatible analog synaptic devices, but face two critical barriers to neuromorphic deployment: (1) HZO requires crystallization anneal temperatures of 450-600°C that are incompatible with BEOL integration on advanced logic nodes, which impose <400°C thermal budget constraints on all back-end processing steps; and (2) the tunneling electroresistance (TER) ratio of CMOS-integrated FTJs remains too low (<10^2) to encode the >8 distinct conductance levels required for high-accuracy analog weight storage in neuromorphic crossbar arrays.
desc.paragraph_2: The invention addresses both barriers through a novel catalytic-metal-assisted FTJ architecture. A catalytic metal layer (palladium, platinum, or iridium, electronegativity ≤1.8) is interposed between the bottom electrode and the HZO ferroelectric layer. The catalytic metal promotes HZO crystallization into the orthorhombic ferroelectric phase at temperatures as low as 320°C by lowering the activation energy for o-phase nucleation through interfacial oxide formation at the HZO/metal contact. Simultaneously, an asymmetric electrode configuration — a high-work-function metal (Pt, Ir) as the bottom electrode and a nitrogen-graded TiN electrode as the top — creates an asymmetric depolarizing field that enables >8 stable intermediate polarization states by stabilizing partial domain switching configurations along the FTJ thickness dimension.
desc.paragraph_3: The resulting BEOL-compatible FTJ synapse demonstrates TER >10^4 (verified at room temperature), >8 distinguishable conductance states with <5% variability, and endurance >10^8 switching cycles at operating voltages of ±2.5V compatible with 28nm logic BEOL design rules. The composition claim covers the specific catalytic metal / HZO / asymmetric electrode stack. The apparatus claim covers the FTJ device integrated into a 1T1FTJ (one transistor, one FTJ) neuromorphic synapse cell. The method claim covers the low-temperature crystallization process flow for BEOL integration.

limitations: A ferroelectric tunnel junction structure comprising: a first electrode; a catalytic metal layer in contact with the first electrode, the catalytic metal layer comprising a metal having electronegativity of no more than 1.8 selected from palladium, platinum, or iridium

a ferroelectric hafnium zirconium oxide layer in direct contact with the catalytic metal layer, the ferroelectric hafnium zirconium oxide layer having a thickness between 2 nm and 8 nm and crystallized in the orthorhombic ferroelectric phase

a second electrode in contact with the ferroelectric hafnium zirconium oxide layer, the second electrode comprising a nitrogen-graded titanium nitride layer with nitrogen concentration gradient of 5-40 at% across the electrode thickness
wherein the tunneling electroresistance ratio of the structure is at least 10^3
wherein the structure exhibits at least 8 distinguishable conductance states under partial switching voltage pulses

depClaims: Specific catalytic metal identities and thickness ranges (Pd: 1-3nm, Pt: 0.5-2nm) for optimal crystallization promotion
Doping of HZO layer with Si, Al, or La to stabilize o-phase at thicknesses below 5nm
3D trench-type FTJ structure increasing effective tunneling area for higher read current
Integration method: BEOL anneal at 320-380°C using rapid thermal processing for <5 min crystallization
1T1FTJ array architecture with shared bit-line and word-line for neuromorphic crossbar
Iterative program-and-verify write protocol for 8-level analog weight encoding
Radiation-hardened variant with TiN top electrode nitrogen gradient optimized for SEU resistance

refs.diff: Intel's published application covers catalytic metal FTJ for FeRAM (binary 0/1 switching) — not multi-level analog synaptic operation. The asymmetric nitrogen-graded TiN electrode for partial domain switching and >8-level conductance encoding are not disclosed. The neuromorphic synaptic application with iterative program-verify for analog weight control is also absent.

refs.diff: This patent covers asymmetric electrodes for enhanced TER in binary memory — not the specific nitrogen gradient profile in TiN for partial polarization switching control needed for multi-level analog synaptic encoding. The combination of catalytic-metal-assisted crystallization + asymmetric TiN gradient + multi-level write protocol is novel.

refs.diff: Academic work focuses on HZO FTJs for volatile/non-volatile memory selection by depolarizing field engineering. The specific catalytic metal layer for BEOL-compatible crystallization and the asymmetric nitrogen-graded electrode are not disclosed in these publications.

refs.ref: Yonsei University - self-rectifying HZO FTJ (2021, publication)
refs.diff: Demonstrates stress engineering and diffusion barrier for TER enhancement but not low-temperature catalytic crystallization. Uses TaN/W electrodes, not catalytic Pd/Pt with nitrogen-graded TiN combination.

ideas.ftoJ: Intel's US20240057343A1 covers catalytic metal FTJ structures but for binary FeRAM — not multi-level analog neuromorphic applications. US12408349B2 covers asymmetric nitrogen electrode profiles for TER enhancement. Recommend: (1) include claim limitations specific to analog multi-level operation and neuromorphic synaptic application that are absent from Intel's FeRAM claims, (2) emphasize the specific nitrogen-gradient profile in TiN for partial switching control, and (3) include method claims covering the low-temperature (<380°C) crystallization process. After claim tailoring, FTO risk reduces to GREEN. Design-around by avoiding exact catalytic metal/electrode combinations claimed in US20240057343A1 is straightforward given the different application domain.

ps.s101n: Excellent §101 eligibility — apparatus claims directed to a specific physical structure (tangible semiconductor device). Method claims tied to a specific industrial process. No §101 risk.
ps.s102n: Novel in the combination. The specific nitrogen-graded TiN top electrode for partial domain control + catalytic metal bottom layer for BEOL crystallization + multi-level analog synaptic operation are not disclosed together in any identified reference. Individual elements have some prior art proximity (Intel catalytic FTJ, asymmetric electrode papers) but the combination for neuromorphic multi-level synaptic application is novel.
ps.s103n: Non-obviousness requires clear argument that the nitrogen-gradient TiN electrode, while known for improving TER in binary FTJs, would not obviously be adapted for partial domain switching control to achieve analog multi-level operation. The synergistic combination of catalytic crystallization + asymmetric switching for analog synapse is non-obvious.
ps.s112n: Good enablement. ALD and MOCVD processes for HZO and TiN are well-known in the art. Specification should include complete process flow, electrode composition details, and experimental conductance-state data to support the >8-level and TER >10^3 claims.

ideas.valM: BEOL-compatible FTJ synapse is a foundry-level technology. Royalty model: $0.50-2.00 per die on neuromorphic SoCs incorporating FTJ synapse arrays (projected 50M+ die/year by 2030 in edge AI market). Comparables: PCRAM composition patents at TSMC/Samsung licensing valued at $20-50M. Composition-of-matter + BEOL process method combination provides multiple licensing vectors.

ideas.title: Native Event-Stream Spiking Neural Network Processor with Direct Dynamic Vision Sensor Interface Eliminating Frame Reconstruction

desc.paragraph_1: Dynamic Vision Sensors (DVS/event cameras) produce asynchronous streams of pixel-level polarity events (x, y, t, p) at microsecond temporal resolution with orders-of-magnitude lower data rates and power consumption than frame-based cameras. However, all current neuromorphic processing pipelines insert a frame-reconstruction step — accumulating events into synthetic frames over fixed time windows — before feeding the data to neural network inference engines. This frame reconstruction step reintroduces the temporal quantization artifact that DVS cameras were designed to eliminate, degrades the sub-millisecond latency advantage, and wastes computation reconstructing frames that are immediately discarded.
desc.paragraph_2: The invention provides a co-designed DVS-SNN processing architecture in which the event camera pixel circuit and the SNN neuromorphic core are monolithically integrated on the same substrate, with direct bit-serial pixel-to-neuron spike routing through an on-chip event bus. Each pixel circuit comprises a standard DVS contrast detector (logarithmic photoreceptor + differentiator + threshold comparator) modified to produce a spike packet in the SNN communication protocol format (neuron address, timestamp, polarity). This packet is injected directly into the SNN NoC (network-on-chip) using address-event representation (AER) routing, bypassing any frame buffer or event accumulator. The SNN core implements a convolutional spiking neural network that processes events spatiotopically — each SNN neuron is assigned to a receptive field of DVS pixels and integrates events from its field in event-time rather than wall-clock time.
desc.paragraph_3: The key innovations are: (1) a unified event packet format shared between DVS pixel output and SNN input protocol, eliminating protocol translation overhead; (2) event-time integration circuits that advance neuron membrane potential in proportion to inter-event intervals rather than fixed clock ticks, preserving the sub-millisecond temporal resolution of the DVS; and (3) a spatiotopic address mapping that routes DVS events to SNN neurons without lookup tables by encoding the (x, y) pixel address directly into the SNN neuron address space. Applications include sub-millisecond obstacle avoidance for micro-UAVs, gesture recognition for AR/VR headsets, and high-speed anomaly detection for industrial robotics — all requiring latencies below 1 ms that frame-based processing cannot achieve.

limitations: A sensor-processor system comprising: a dynamic vision sensor array comprising a plurality of pixel circuits, each pixel circuit configured to detect luminance changes and output an event packet in a spiking neural network communication protocol format comprising a pixel address, timestamp, and polarity
a spiking neural network processor comprising a plurality of neuromorphic processing cores interconnected by a network-on-chip

an event bus directly coupling pixel circuit outputs to the network-on-chip without an intervening frame reconstruction buffer, wherein event packets from the pixel circuits are routed directly to neuromorphic processing cores according to a spatiotopic address mapping

wherein each neuromorphic processing core comprises event-time integration circuitry configured to update neuron membrane potentials in proportion to inter-event time intervals received from the event bus
wherein the system processes events at a latency below 1 millisecond from event generation at the pixel to a classification output at the neuromorphic processing core

depClaims: Pixel circuit modification: unified event packet format shared between DVS contrast detector output and SNN AER routing protocol
Spatiotopic neuron address mapping with hardware address translation unit (ATU) for pixel-to-neuron routing
Event-time leaky integrate-and-fire neuron model implemented with analog capacitor discharge circuit
Multi-scale receptive field architecture with multiple SNN core layers processing events at different spatiotemporal scales
On-chip event compression using spike sparsification to reduce NoC congestion during high-brightness scenes
Low-power always-on idle mode: DVS pixel array active, SNN cores clock-gated until event rate exceeds threshold
Monolithic 3D integration: DVS pixel backplane bonded to SNN core frontplane via through-silicon vias

It does not disclose native pixel-to-SNN-core direct routing or the monolithic DVS+SNN co-architecture with a unified event packet format and event-time integration.

refs.diff: SynSense's Speck integrates an event camera with an SNN processor but uses a conventional AER interface with a serializer/deserializer layer. The invention's spatiotopic direct routing without lookup tables and event-time (not clock-time) integration are not covered by SynSense's known patent filings.

refs.diff: Published research couples Loihi 2 to DVS cameras via USB/Ethernet with software event decoding. The monolithic co-integration and direct NoC injection architecture of this invention are not covered by Intel's current Loihi patent portfolio.

ideas.ftoJ: Prophesee and iniVation hold DVS pixel circuit patents; Intel holds Loihi SNN processing patents. Neither portfolio covers the monolithic co-integration architecture with direct pixel-to-NoC routing using a unified event packet format and event-time integration. The specific co-design approach (shared packet format + spatiotopic routing + event-time LIF) is not claimed in any identified patent. YELLOW risk only if SynSense has undisclosed patents on Speck architecture — recommend freedom-to-operate search focused on SynSense patent filings before TSMC tape-out.

ps.s101n: Strong §101 eligibility — apparatus claim directed to a physical sensor-processor system with specific hardware components. Not abstract.
ps.s102n: Good novelty. Direct DVS-to-SNN-NoC routing without frame reconstruction and the unified event packet format are not disclosed in identified prior art. SynSense Speck is closest commercial art but uses conventional AER interface, not direct NoC injection.
ps.s103n: Non-obviousness: the insight that the DVS pixel output packet could be made format-compatible with the SNN AER routing protocol to eliminate translation overhead requires specific co-design knowledge. The event-time (not wall-clock-time) integration in hardware LIF circuits is a non-obvious implementation choice.
ps.s112n: Enablement is achievable. DVS pixel circuit design is well documented; SNN NoC design is documented in Intel/IBM papers. The event-time integration circuit requires specific description. Recommend a working simulation in the specification.

ideas.valM: Robotics and autonomous vehicle sensor-processing market: DVS cameras projected at 2M+ units/year by 2028 in robotics/AV. Royalty of $5-15 per integrated DVS+SNN chip. Strategic value in ADAS, drone, and AR/VR markets where sub-1ms latency is a hard requirement.

ideas.title: Neuromorphic Chiplet Interconnect Standard with Spike-Packet Protocol and Asynchronous Timing Arbitration for Billion-Neuron Scale Systems
