ideas.ftoJ: Method/system claims for AI-driven thermal management face §101 risk under Alice/Mayo if not properly framed. Recommend structuring claims around specific technical hardware (sensor network + embedded processor + control output to PMUs) with concrete physical parameters, avoiding pure data-analysis claim language. No identified hardware-specific patents block the combination of embedded thermal sensors + ML TIM state inference + closed-loop TDP adjustment. Intel's on-die thermal management patents (US20070191993) cover single-die temperature throttling without TIM state inference—distinct from this invention. Software-as-a-service TIM co-design tools are not patented by identified incumbents.

ps.s101n: System claims with specific hardware elements (sensor network, edge AI processor, compensation controller outputs to PMU) are eligible under Alice step 2B—the hardware integration provides the 'something more' beyond abstract idea. Pure method claims for ML inference should be avoided or framed as tied to specific hardware operations. Recommend filing apparatus claims as primary.
ps.s102n: The specific combination of embedded TIM sensor fabric + surrogate ML model + closed-loop TDP compensation + TIM co-design output is novel. Individual elements (sensors, ML, TDP management) exist separately.
ps.s103n: Non-obviousness supported by: the non-trivial insight that TIM degradation can be inferred from surface temperature maps without electrical TIM access; the closed-loop coupling between inferred TIM state and TDP adjustment (rather than simple temperature throttling); fleet learning for TIM prognosis across deployed packages. Some risk that examiner finds combination obvious—strong §103 rebuttal brief needed.
ps.s112n: Physics-informed neural network training on FEM data is well-documented; sensor fusion methodologies are standard; TDP management interfaces (RAPL, DVFS) are published. Fully enabling disclosure possible.

ideas.valM: Platform patent with licensing opportunities across multiple industries: data center OEMs (Dell, HPE, Supermicro), cloud hyperscalers (AWS, Google, Azure) operating custom ASIC packages, EV OEMs (Tesla, BYD, BMW) for battery thermal management. SaaS licensing model for TIM co-design module is possible. Lower headline value due to §101 risk and higher design-around surface area.

ideas.title: Streaming ASIC Decoder for Real-Time Quantum Low-Density Parity-Check (QLDPC) Error Correction with On-Chip Belief-Propagation Accelerator
ideas.desc:
Current quantum error correction decoders operate on either software-based minimum-weight perfect matching (MWPM) engines or FPGA-based local clustering decoders that consume tens to hundreds of watts. As the industry shifts from surface codes to QLDPC codes (IBM QLDPC, Photonic SHYPS), which require high-connectivity belief-propagation decoding rather than local matching, no dedicated ASIC hardware exists. The backlog problem—where syndrome data accumulates faster than it can be processed—is exacerbated by QLDPC's larger Tanner graphs and non-local check node interactions.

This invention is an application-specific integrated circuit (ASIC) decoder chip architecturally optimized for QLDPC belief-propagation decoding under real-time streaming conditions. The ASIC implements a variable-node array and check-node array in dedicated on-chip compute tiles with ping-pong SRAM buffering, enabling continuous ingestion of syndrome streams from a quantum processor without pipeline stalls. A hardware message-passing engine implements min-sum approximation belief propagation with configurable iteration depth, achieving convergence in 200-400 nanoseconds per decoding round. Power-gated syndrome buffers and clock-gated BP tiles reduce idle power to <5 mW, compatible with 4K cryogenic stages.

The differentiating feature over Riverlane's ASIC (DD0A) and FPGA-based decoders is the native QLDPC Tanner graph support—prior ASIC solutions are surface-code-specific using local clustering (union-find or MWPM). The on-chip Tanner graph is programmable via a compressed sparse row (CSR) configuration memory, enabling the same ASIC to serve multiple QLDPC code families (bicycle codes, lifted product codes, SHYPS family) without re-fabrication. A secondary novelty is the thermal-aware power management: the ASIC includes on-chip temperature sensors that modulate clock frequency and tile activation to maintain safe dissipation at 4K, extending the viable operational temperature envelope below conventional CMOS designs.
ideas.claim: An apparatus for real-time quantum error correction decoding comprising: (a) a plurality of variable-node compute tiles and check-node compute tiles arranged in a Tanner graph topology stored in on-chip configuration memory, the configuration memory loadable with a compressed sparse row representation of a quantum low-density parity-check (QLDPC) code; (b) a syndrome ingestion pipeline operatively coupled to a quantum processor output, configured to receive syndrome bitstreams at a rate of at least one syndrome vector per microsecond and store the syndrome vectors in ping-pong SRAM buffers; (c) a min-sum belief-propagation engine instantiated across the variable-node and check-node compute tiles, configured to execute convergent iterative message-passing to produce correction outputs in less than 500 nanoseconds per decoding round; (d) power-gated clock domains associated with each compute tile, controlled by a thermal management unit responsive to on-chip temperature sensor data to limit total power dissipation below 10 milliwatts at an operating temperature of 4 Kelvin; and (e) a correction output interface configured to transmit correction operations to a quantum processor control system within a total latency budget of 1 microsecond from syndrome receipt.
depClaims: Configuring the Tanner graph for specific QLDPC families including bicycle codes, hypergraph product codes, lifted product codes, and SHYPS-family codes
Integration of a leakage detection sub-module that identifies ancilla qubit states outside the computational basis and modifies belief-propagation priors accordingly
A streaming mode wherein successive syndrome rounds are decoded in overlapping pipeline stages to eliminate inter-round latency gaps
Hardware-accelerated post-processing to generate Pauli frame updates from decoded corrections, enabling direct injection into quantum control pulse generators
Differential power analysis (DPA) shielding of correction outputs to prevent side-channel leakage in cryptographic/defense quantum computing applications
Cryogenic-compatible I/O standards including LVDS-based cryo links and superconducting interconnect compatibility for 20mK stage integration
Multi-chip module (MCM) configuration enabling aggregation of multiple decoder ASICs to service QLDPC codes of code distance d>30 requiring parallelized tile arrays

refs.ref: vs_Riverlane_ASIC_DD0A
refs.diff: DD0A implements surface-code local clustering (union-find variant); this invention implements Tanner-graph belief propagation natively optimized for QLDPC—structurally distinct decode algorithm with different data flow and convergence requirements

refs.ref: vs_Riverlane_LCD_FPGA
refs.diff: LCD is FPGA-based (10-100W range) and surface-code-specific; this invention is an ASIC operating at <10mW with configurable QLDPC Tanner graph support

refs.ref: vs_IBM_US11736122B1
refs.diff: IBM patent covers software/classical-processor maximum-likelihood decoding via hypergraph; this invention is an apparatus claim on a dedicated silicon ASIC with physical circuit topology

refs.ref: vs_NTT_SFQ_decoder
refs.diff: NTT SFQ decoder operates at 20mK using superconducting logic; this invention targets 4K CMOS operating range with power-managed thermal control, complementary but distinct operational regime

ideas.ftoJ: No identified patents claim a QLDPC-specific ASIC decoder with on-chip Tanner graph configuration memory and min-sum BP engine. Riverlane's US patent covers parallelization method (windowed decoding), not QLDPC apparatus. IBM QEC patents address surface codes and software methods. Google's cryogenic patents cover qubit control, not decoding ASICs. The specific combination of QLDPC code support + streaming pipeline + cryogenic thermal management creates clear differentiation. Recommend apparatus claims directed to the physical ASIC structure rather than decoding methods.

ps.s101n: Apparatus claim on a physical ASIC chip; clearly patent-eligible hardware. Alice/Mayo abstract idea concerns do not apply to physical integrated circuit apparatus. Claim framing as 'an apparatus comprising' physical circuit elements avoids §101 vulnerability.
ps.s102n: Novel element: QLDPC Tanner graph configuration memory in hardware decoder ASIC with streaming syndrome ingestion and cryogenic thermal management. No identified prior art anticipates this specific combination.
ps.s103n: The step from FPGA-based surface-code decoders to QLDPC ASIC is non-obvious because (1) QLDPC codes are a newer paradigm only demonstrated at scale in 2025 by Photonic/IBM; (2) Tanner graph message-passing hardware design for QLDPC has not been published in the context of cryogenic operation; (3) the cryogenic thermal management constraint creates additional design non-obviousness.
ps.s112n: Standard CMOS ASIC design techniques are well-established; QLDPC Tanner graph BP is academically well-described; cryogenic CMOS behavior at 4K is characterized in literature. A PHOSITA with VLSI and quantum hardware experience could practice this invention with the specification.

ideas.valM: Technology licensing to quantum hardware OEMs (IBM, Google, Quantinuum, IonQ) at $2-5M/year per licensee; 5-7 potential licensees over 10-year patent life. Defensive value against Riverlane expanding ASIC claims into QLDPC territory. Strategic acquisition premium from semiconductor IP holding companies.

ideas.title: Adaptive Code-Distance Reconfiguration Engine: Hardware State Machine for Dynamic Surface Code Distance Adjustment Based on Real-Time Physical Error Rate Telemetry
ideas.desc:
Fault-tolerant quantum computers today statically allocate code distance d at system design time, committing a fixed number of physical qubits per logical qubit for the duration of a computation. This static allocation is fundamentally inefficient: during low-noise operation (stable cryogenic temperature, minimal TLS activity), a lower code distance d=3 or d=5 suffices and would free physical qubits for additional logical qubit instantiation; during burst noise events (cosmic ray strikes, dilution refrigerator vibration events, two-level system activation bursts), the system may need to dynamically increase d to d=7 or d=9 to maintain target logical error rates. No current hardware primitive exists for real-time code-distance reconfiguration during active quantum computation.

This invention is a dedicated hardware state machine—the Adaptive Code-Distance Engine (ACDE)—implemented as a co-processor tightly coupled to the quantum processor's classical control plane. The ACDE continuously ingests a real-time error rate telemetry stream derived from syndrome measurement statistics, computes a rolling error rate estimate using an exponential moving average circuit, and compares this estimate against programmable high and low threshold registers. When the error rate exceeds the high threshold, the ACDE issues a logical qubit contraction command to the control plane, triggering a protocol to merge adjacent physical qubit patches and expand code distance; when the error rate falls below the low threshold, the ACDE issues an expansion command that releases physical qubits to the qubit allocation pool.

The key technical differentiator is that code-distance reconfiguration is managed entirely in hardware with a deterministic latency of <10 microseconds from threshold crossing to reconfiguration command issuance—fast enough to respond to burst error events before logical qubit failure probability exceeds budget. IBM's contextual calibration patent (April 2025, LinkedIn reported) operates in software and adjusts signal power/timing parameters before program execution, not mid-computation code distance. This invention operates during active logical qubit operation with hardware-enforced timing guarantees.
ideas.claim: A hardware apparatus for adaptive quantum error correction comprising: (a) a telemetry ingestion unit configured to receive a continuous stream of syndrome measurement statistics from a quantum processor performing quantum error correction using a stabilizer code of a first code distance d1; (b) an error rate estimator circuit implementing an exponential moving average computation over the syndrome statistics stream to produce a rolling physical error rate estimate updated at each syndrome extraction cycle; (c) a threshold comparison circuit comprising a high-threshold register and a low-threshold register, configured to assert a first signal when the rolling error rate estimate exceeds the high-threshold register value and to assert a second signal when the rolling error rate estimate falls below the low-threshold register value; (d) a reconfiguration command generator responsive to the first signal to output a code-distance increase command specifying a second code distance d2 > d1, and responsive to the second signal to output a code-distance decrease command specifying a third code distance d3 < d1; and (e) a deterministic timing control unit ensuring that the reconfiguration command is issued within a maximum latency of 10 microseconds from assertion of the first or second signal, independent of classical processor interrupt latency.
depClaims: Integration of a cosmic ray detection sub-circuit that identifies burst error signatures exceeding a threshold number of simultaneous stabilizer violations and triggers an emergency code-distance increase protocol bypassing the moving-average computation
Hardware-enforced qubit allocation table that tracks physical qubit assignments to logical qubits and dynamically reallocates freed qubits upon code-distance reduction
Configurable threshold hysteresis registers that prevent oscillation between code distances when error rates hover near threshold values
Multi-logical-qubit version wherein independent ACDE instances manage separate logical qubits with shared qubit pool arbitration logic
Extension to color codes and QLDPC codes with modular threshold tables for different code families
Hardware performance counter outputs for real-time monitoring of reconfiguration frequency, enabling system-level diagnostics and calibration
Integration with IBM-style QLDPC connectivity architecture to manage the hardware reconfiguration of check-qubit connectivity graphs during code-distance transitions

refs.ref: vs_IBM_contextual_calibration_2025
refs.diff: IBM's patent adjusts signal power, timing, and gate parameters before/during program execution but does not reconfigure code distance (number of physical qubits per logical qubit) in hardware mid-computation

refs.ref: vs_Google_US12198013
refs.diff: Google's calibration patent is zero-noise extrapolation setup, not code distance management

refs.ref: vs_IBM_US11700020B2
refs.diff: IBM fault-tolerant QEC patent covers static code generation and lookup-table decoding, not dynamic code-distance adaptation hardware

refs.ref: vs_adaptive_syndrome_extraction_Berthusen_2025
refs.diff: Berthusen's adaptive syndrome extraction selectively measures stabilizers; this invention manages the higher-level code-distance parameter at the physical qubit allocation level—complementary, not overlapping

ideas.ftoJ: No identified patents cover hardware-enforced real-time code-distance reconfiguration during active logical qubit operation. IBM's 2025 contextual calibration patent is the closest reference but operates at the signal/gate level, not the qubit-allocation level. Google's calibration patents address noise mitigation rather than code structure adaptation. The apparatus claim directed to a deterministic hardware state machine with specific timing guarantees (10 microsecond latency) is clearly distinguishable from software-based calibration systems.

ps.s101n: Apparatus claims on hardware state machine with physical registers, timing control circuits, and deterministic command generation. Squarely within patent-eligible subject matter under §101.
ps.s102n: Dynamic code-distance reconfiguration implemented as a dedicated hardware co-processor with deterministic latency guarantees is not anticipated in any identified prior art.
ps.s103n: The combination of (1) real-time syndrome statistics ingestion, (2) exponential moving average hardware estimator, (3) threshold-triggered code-distance commands, and (4) deterministic hardware timing would not be obvious to combine without knowledge of burst-error dynamics in quantum processors. The cosmic ray sub-circuit for burst detection adds further non-obviousness.
ps.s112n: Hardware state machine design is well within PHOSITA competence. Syndrome statistics extraction is described in surface code literature. The deterministic timing requirement requires careful FPGA/ASIC implementation but is enabled by existing HDL techniques.

ideas.valM: Essential patent for fault-tolerant QC systems operating in variable noise environments. Licensing to IBM, Google, Quantinuum as they build production fault-tolerant systems post-2027. Value as blocking patent in any 'dynamic QEC' product line.

ideas.title: Modality-Agnostic Quantum Error Correction Interface Controller with Programmable Syndrome Timing Engine and Protocol Converter Front-Ends
ideas.desc:
The quantum computing hardware ecosystem encompasses fundamentally different qubit modalities—superconducting transmons (IBM, Google), trapped ions (IonQ, Quantinuum), neutral atoms (QuEra, Pasqal), and silicon photonics (Photonic Inc.)—each with distinct syndrome extraction timing profiles, measurement fidelity characteristics, and classical feedback latency requirements. Superconducting QEC requires syndrome extraction cycles of ~1 microsecond; trapped ion QEC tolerates 100+ microsecond cycles; neutral atom tweezer arrays operate with gate times of ~1 millisecond. Today, every QEC decoder and controller must be custom-designed per modality, preventing code reuse, supply chain economies, and standardized software stacks.

This invention is a hardware Quantum Error Correction Interface Controller (QECIC) implemented as a programmable chip with a modality-agnostic core and modality-specific protocol converter front-ends. The core consists of a multi-channel syndrome bus with configurable cycle-time registers (programmable from 100ns to 100ms), a universal syndrome vector format with padding and validity flags, and a standardized correction output interface. Each modality-specific front-end (Superconducting Front-End Module, Trapped Ion Front-End Module, Neutral Atom Front-End Module) is a hot-swappable hardware daughter card or die-stacked chiplet that handles the physical layer interface: analog readout digitization, ancilla reset control, and timing synchronization. The QECIC core presents a unified syndrome vector stream to the decoder (FPGA, ASIC, or software) regardless of modality.

This architecture enables a shared decoder IP block to service multiple qubit platform vendors simultaneously—a fundamental cost reduction and IP leverage mechanism. It also enables modular QEC stacks where operators can upgrade the decoder (swap FPGA for ASIC) without modifying the modality-specific interface hardware. NVIDIA's NVQLink addresses a similar need at the software abstraction layer; this invention provides the hardware abstraction layer that NVQLink requires to function, creating a complementary and deeper IP position.
ideas.claim: A quantum error correction interface controller comprising: (a) a programmable syndrome timing engine with per-channel cycle-time registers configurable to periods between 100 nanoseconds and 100 milliseconds, the syndrome timing engine generating synchronization signals to trigger syndrome extraction operations on a quantum processor; (b) a universal syndrome bus comprising a plurality of input channels, each channel receiving syndrome measurement results from a modality-specific front-end module and formatting the results into a standardized syndrome vector comprising a validity flag, a timestamp field, and a payload field containing syndrome bits; (c) at least one modality-specific front-end module physically connectable to the universal syndrome bus, each front-end module implementing analog-to-digital conversion, ancilla qubit reset control, and timing synchronization specific to a qubit modality selected from the group consisting of superconducting transmon qubits, trapped ion qubits, neutral atom qubits, and photonic qubits; (d) a correction output interface configured to receive decoded correction instructions from an external decoder and translate the correction instructions into modality-specific gate operations or qubit manipulations via the front-end module; and (e) a decoder interface providing the standardized syndrome vector stream to the external decoder at a rate synchronized with the syndrome timing engine.
depClaims: Hot-swappable front-end module interface with standardized electrical and mechanical connectors enabling modality switching without controller board replacement
Multi-modality concurrent operation mode where the QECIC services a hybrid quantum processor comprising qubits of two or more modalities with independent timing channels

Hardware-accelerated modality capability discovery protocol allowing decoder software to query the QECIC for modality parameters including average syndrome extraction fidelity, reset latency, and gate time
Automated clock domain crossing hardware for synchronizing syndrome timestamps from multiple modalities operating at different clock rates
Security enclave within the QECIC for encrypted syndrome telemetry and tamper-resistant correction injection, targeting defense/intelligence quantum computing applications
API-compatible hardware interface layer enabling drop-in replacement of Riverlane Deltaflow FPGA modules with the QECIC without decoder software modification
Power harvesting circuit for front-end modules operating at cryogenic temperatures, extracting power from the RF control lines to eliminate dedicated power wires to cold stages

refs.ref: vs_NVIDIA_NVQLink
refs.diff: NVQLink is a software abstraction layer for modality-agnostic QPU control; this invention is the hardware interface controller providing the physical layer that software stacks require; complementary, not competing

refs.ref: vs_Riverlane_Deltaflow
refs.diff: Deltaflow is an FPGA-based decoder stack with modality-agnostic software claims; this invention is a hardware controller with physical protocol converter front-ends—a different layer of the stack

refs.ref: vs_Quantum_Machines_US12242406B2
refs.diff: QM's switch fabric coordinates multiple quantum controllers at the pulse-processor level; this invention abstracts the syndrome interface layer above the pulse-processor level, creating a higher-level modality abstraction

refs.ref: vs_Google_EP3966938A2
refs.diff: Google's cryo-CMOS patent targets qubit gate control at the physical device level; this invention operates at the syndrome extraction and correction injection layer, a distinct functional boundary

ideas.ftoJ: The hardware modality-agnostic interface controller with programmable timing engine and physical front-end modules is not claimed in any identified patent. NVIDIA's NVQLink is software-level. Riverlane's patents cover decode algorithms. Quantum Machines' patent covers pulse processor coordination. This invention claims the physical hardware interface controller layer—a distinct architectural component with no direct blocking IP identified.

ps.s101n: Hardware apparatus with physical connectors, programmable registers, and signal conversion circuits. Clearly patent-eligible.
ps.s102n: The specific combination of programmable syndrome timing engine + universal syndrome bus + hot-swappable modality front-ends is not present in any identified prior art.
ps.s103n: While hardware abstraction layers exist in classical computing, their application to quantum syndrome extraction with the specific challenges of cryogenic timing, modality-specific reset requirements, and sub-microsecond latency constraints is non-obvious. The multi-modality hybrid operation mode has no classical analog.
ps.s112n: Programmable hardware design is well-established; modality-specific electrical interfaces are described in each modality's literature. The specification must include detailed description of at least two front-end module implementations to satisfy enablement.

ideas.valM: Platform patent with licensing opportunity to every QEC decoder vendor (Riverlane, IBM, Google) and every quantum hardware OEM. Potential standard-essential patent status if the QECIC protocol becomes an industry standard (analogous to PCIe in classical computing). Strategic acquisition value from quantum computing infrastructure companies.

ideas.title: 3D Heterogeneous Integration Stack for Cryogenic Quantum Processor with Co-Integrated Syndrome Extraction ASIC and Decoder Chiplet at 4K Stage
ideas.desc:
The 'wiring wall' remains the primary scalability barrier for superconducting quantum computing: each physical qubit requires approximately 2-3 dedicated coaxial cables from room temperature to the 20mK stage for control and readout, limiting total qubit count to a few thousand even in purpose-built dilution refrigerators. Existing patents address parts of this problem—Google's cryo-CMOS charge-locking circuit (EP3966938A2) reduces control wire count at the qubit gate level, and SFQ circuits address the readout path—but no patent addresses the full 3D system integration where a qubit die, a syndrome extraction ASIC, and a decoder chiplet are co-packaged in a single heterogeneous module with optical or RF inter-die communication links eliminating the traditional coaxial cable runs.

This invention describes a 3D heterogeneous integration module comprising: (Layer 1) a superconducting qubit die operating at 20mK; (Layer 2) a cryo-CMOS syndrome extraction ASIC die bump-bonded to the qubit die, operating at 4K, that performs resonator readout digitization, ancilla reset control, and syndrome bit extraction; (Layer 3) a decoder chiplet also at 4K that receives syndrome bits from Layer 2 via through-silicon vias or superconducting bump bonds, computes error corrections, and outputs correction commands back to Layer 2. The entire stack communicates with room-temperature systems via a dramatically reduced interface—an optical fiber bundle carrying serialized syndrome telemetry and correction commands—replacing hundreds of coaxial cables.

The key innovations are: (1) the definition of the inter-layer syndrome bus protocol with timing and power budgets compatible with 4K operation; (2) the thermal isolation architecture using gold-plated copper pillars for separate thermal anchoring of each die layer to prevent heat cross-contamination; (3) the optical-electrical transceiver implemented in cryo-compatible III-V photonics for the 4K-to-room-temperature link; and (4) the correction command feedback path with hardware-enforced round-trip latency <2 microseconds from syndrome extraction to correction injection. This architecture could reduce the wiring from >1,000 coaxial cables for a 1,000-qubit system to fewer than 10 optical fibers plus <50 DC bias lines.
ideas.claim: A heterogeneous quantum computing module comprising: (a) a first die comprising a plurality of superconducting qubit circuits configured to operate at a first cryogenic temperature below 50 millikelvin; (b) a second die comprising a syndrome extraction integrated circuit configured to operate at a second cryogenic temperature between 1 Kelvin and 10 Kelvin, the second die electrically coupled to the first die via a plurality of superconducting bump bonds providing signal paths therebetween, the syndrome extraction integrated circuit comprising readout digitization circuits coupled to resonators associated with the qubit circuits and ancilla reset control circuits; (c) a third die comprising a decoder integrated circuit configured to operate at the second cryogenic temperature, the third die electrically coupled to the second die via a high-speed serial inter-die interface, the decoder integrated circuit configured to receive syndrome bits from the syndrome extraction integrated circuit and produce error correction commands; (d) a thermal isolation structure comprising a plurality of thermally conductive pillars individually anchored to a cryogenic refrigerator stage and individually thermally coupled to respective ones of the first, second, and third dies, the thermal isolation structure configured to independently anchor each die to the cryogenic stage without direct thermal coupling between dies; and (e) a room-temperature interface comprising an optical transceiver on the second or third die and an optical fiber link, the optical transceiver configured to transmit serialized telemetry to and receive control commands from a room-temperature system.
depClaims: Integration of a microwave-to-digital converter on the second die using a Josephson parametric amplifier (JPA) chain and an SFQ comparator to perform single-shot qubit readout without high-electron-mobility transistor (HEMT) amplifiers at 4K
Die-to-die superconducting kinetic inductance communication links replacing conventional metal bump bonds for the syndrome bit bus, enabling sub-picojoule/bit energy per syndrome transfer
Configurable thermal budget management circuit that allocates decoder chiplet compute duty cycles based on measured inter-die temperature differentials
TSV-less integration variant using 3D wire bond fan-out to enable assembly with existing dilution refrigerator platforms without specialized bump-bonding equipment
On-module calibration EEPROM storing qubit frequency maps and syndrome extraction timing parameters, enabling plug-and-play module replacement in production quantum computers
Redundant optical fiber link with automatic failover and link-level error correction for the room-temperature interface, ensuring continuous syndrome telemetry under optical fiber degradation
Extension to neutral atom or trapped ion qubits via modular interface die design, achieving the same 3D integration benefit for non-superconducting modalities

refs.ref: vs_Google_EP3966938A2
refs.diff: Google's cryo-CMOS patent addresses qubit gate voltage control at the device level; this invention claims the full 3D heterogeneous module with syndrome extraction ASIC, decoder chiplet, and optical I/O—a system-level apparatus encompassing multiple die types

refs.ref: vs_University_Sydney_cryo_chip_2025
refs.diff: Sydney's cryo chip operates adjacent to qubits for spin qubit control; this invention claims a three-die 3D stack with superconducting qubits, cryo-CMOS syndrome ASIC, and decoder chiplet in a single thermal module with independent thermal anchoring

SFQ patent covers RF-SQUID readout circuits using SFQ pulse trains; this invention claims the integration architecture (3D stack, thermal pillars, optical fiber I/O) rather than the readout circuit technology per se

refs.ref: vs_existing_flip_chip_QC
refs.diff: Google, IBM, and Rigetti use flip-chip packaging for qubit-coupler integration, but none disclose a three-die 3D stack with independent thermal anchoring of a decoder chiplet at 4K

ideas.ftoJ: Google holds EP3966938A2 covering cryo-CMOS co-packaged with qubit die, and has significant 3D packaging IP. Microsoft has topological qubit integration patents. The die-stacking concept has some overlap with existing Google/Microsoft packaging IP. Recommend narrowing claims to the specific three-die combination (qubit + syndrome extraction + decoder) with independent thermal pillar anchoring and the optical fiber I/O path, which are differentiating elements not covered by existing packaging patents. Conduct formal FTO search against Google's full 3D packaging portfolio before filing.

ps.s101n: Physical apparatus comprising physical die, bump bonds, thermal structures, and optical transceiver. No §101 concerns.
ps.s102n: The specific three-die combination with independent thermal pillar anchoring and optical fiber I/O from the cold stage is novel. Component-wise anticipation is unlikely given the multi-layer integration requirement.
ps.s103n: YELLOW FTO risk stems from some obviousness concerns given Google and Microsoft's 3D packaging work. The independent thermal anchoring architecture and decoder-chiplet integration with syndrome ASIC at 4K adds non-obviousness. Emphasize the specific thermal isolation architecture in the specification.
ps.s112n: 3D packaging, cryo-CMOS, and optical transceiver technologies are well-documented. The specification must address the superconducting bump bond process at millikelvin temperatures and the thermal management calculations.

ideas.valM: Foundational packaging patent for any 1,000+ qubit superconducting system. IBM Starling (2029) and similar large-scale systems will require this integration approach. Potential blocking position on competitors' system-in-package designs. Defensive value against Google's integrated packaging portfolio.

ideas.title: Hardware Fault-Tolerant Classical-Quantum Bridge ASIC with Deterministic Syndrome Telemetry, Logical Qubit State Caching, and Mid-Circuit Measurement Injection Fabric
ideas.desc:
The interface between a fault-tolerant logical qubit layer and the classical host computer represents a critical architectural gap. Today's quantum computers connect logical qubit operations to classical processors via software middleware (IBM Qiskit Runtime, Quantum Machines OPX), but these software layers introduce unpredictable latency—typically microseconds to milliseconds—that violates the timing requirements of mid-circuit measurement (MCM) protocols essential for fault-tolerant algorithms including magic state distillation, teleportation, and adaptive quantum error correction. A dedicated hardware bridge ASIC is required to handle the deterministic timing between logical qubit operations and classical algorithm steps.

This invention is a Bridge ASIC (BASQ) that sits architecturally between the QEC decoder stack and the classical CPU. The BASQ implements: (1) a hardware-timestamped syndrome telemetry engine that captures every syndrome measurement with a GPS-disciplined or atomic-clock-synchronized timestamp accurate to 10 nanoseconds, enabling post-hoc analysis of error correlations across time and space; (2) a logical qubit state cache—a SRAM-backed register file storing the current Pauli frame (logical qubit error state) for each active logical qubit, updateable in hardware upon receipt of a decoder correction command without CPU intervention; (3) a mid-circuit measurement injection fabric that enables a classical CPU to issue a 'branch on logical qubit state' instruction that is resolved in hardware by reading the Pauli frame cache, with a deterministic instruction-to-result latency of <100 nanoseconds; and (4) a hardware barrier instruction that halts quantum operations until outstanding QEC cycles complete, enforcing a consistent quantum-classical synchronization point.

The BASQ eliminates the 'measurement backlog' for mid-circuit measurement use cases. In adaptive quantum algorithms (e.g., quantum phase estimation, fault-tolerant T-gate teleportation), the classical processor must branch on intermediate measurement results within the qubit coherence time. Current software-based approaches fail this timing requirement at scale; the BASQ's deterministic hardware path enables MCM-based fault-tolerant algorithms on processors with thousands of logical qubits.
ideas.claim: A bridge integrated circuit for interfacing a fault-tolerant quantum processor with a classical processing unit, comprising: (a) a syndrome telemetry engine configured to receive syndrome measurement results from a quantum error correction stack and generate timestamped syndrome records, each timestamped syndrome record comprising a syndrome vector, a logical qubit identifier, and a timestamp derived from a hardware precision timing source with accuracy better than 50 nanoseconds; (b) a logical qubit state cache comprising a plurality of Pauli frame registers, each Pauli frame register associated with a respective logical qubit and configured to store a current logical error state of the respective logical qubit, the Pauli frame registers updateable in hardware upon receipt of a correction command from a quantum error correction decoder without requiring software intervention; (c) a mid-circuit measurement resolution unit configured to receive a branch instruction from a classical processing unit, the branch instruction specifying a logical qubit identifier, the mid-circuit measurement resolution unit configured to read the Pauli frame register associated with the specified logical qubit and return a branch condition signal to the classical processing unit within a deterministic latency not exceeding 100 nanoseconds from receipt of the branch instruction; (d) a quantum-classical synchronization barrier generator configured to issue a halt signal to a quantum processor control system upon receipt of a synchronization instruction, the halt signal deferring quantum gate operations until all outstanding quantum error correction cycles have completed; and (e) a correction injection interface configured to transmit error correction operations to the quantum processor control system in response to decoder outputs, ensuring corrections are applied within the coherence time of the logical qubits.
depClaims: A replay buffer storing the last N syndrome telemetry records for post-hoc error rate analysis and calibration, accessible via a DMA interface to the classical CPU
Hardware encryption of Pauli frame cache contents using AES-256 to prevent logical qubit state leakage in multi-tenant cloud quantum computing environments

A speculative execution mode where the classical CPU receives a probabilistic branch prediction from the BASQ before the QEC decoder finalizes its correction, reducing algorithmic latency at the cost of occasional speculative misfire
Multi-logical-qubit batch branch instructions enabling a classical processor to query the state of up to 64 logical qubits in a single instruction for parallelized adaptive algorithms
Integration with classical CPU architecture as a new instruction set extension (quantum ISA extension), enabling x86/ARM processors to issue BASQ instructions natively without software overhead
Hardware-enforced logical qubit isolation preventing Pauli frame updates from crossing tenant boundaries in cloud quantum computer environments
A logical qubit state snapshot instruction that atomically captures the Pauli frame of all logical qubits at a precise timestamp for debugging and algorithm verification

refs.ref: vs_Quantum_Machines_US12242406B2
refs.diff: QM's switch fabric coordinates multiple quantum controllers at the pulse-processor level for joint state estimation; this invention operates at the logical qubit abstraction layer, caching Pauli frames and enabling classical CPU branch instructions—a higher architectural level

refs.ref: vs_IBM_QiskitRuntime
refs.diff: IBM Qiskit Runtime is software middleware; this invention is a hardware ASIC with physical Pauli frame registers and deterministic hardware timing, not a software construct

refs.ref: vs_IBM_concurrent_classical_computation
refs.diff: IBM patents on concurrent classical computation enable mid-circuit measurement in software; this invention provides the hardware ASIC that makes MCM timing-deterministic at the <100 nanosecond level

refs.ref: vs_standard_QC_controllers
refs.diff: Quantum controller ICs (QM OPX, Zurich Instruments HDAWG) process analog pulses; this invention is distinct as a digital logical-layer bridge ASIC operating on logical qubit abstractions, not physical pulse generation

ideas.ftoJ: IBM and Quantum Machines have IP in classical-quantum integration at adjacent levels. The specific Pauli frame cache + MCM branch instruction + deterministic latency combination is not directly claimed in identified patents, but IBM's concurrent classical computation and QM's switch fabric patents may have claims that read on aspects of this invention. Recommend claim drafting focused on the hardware Pauli frame cache apparatus and deterministic MCM branch instruction resolution as the clearest novel elements. FTO analysis against IBM US11736122B1 and QM US12242406B2 is advised.

ps.s101n: Physical ASIC with hardware registers, timing circuits, and interfaces. Strong §101 position as hardware apparatus.
ps.s102n: The Pauli frame hardware cache with deterministic MCM branch resolution is novel; timing-disciplined syndrome telemetry with GPS/atomic-clock accuracy is novel in QC context.
ps.s103n: The combination of Pauli frame cache + branch instruction resolution + hardware synchronization barrier is non-obvious because it requires understanding the specific timing constraints of fault-tolerant algorithms (T-gate teleportation, adaptive QEC) and translating them into hardware microarchitecture requirements.
ps.s112n: Standard digital ASIC design; Pauli frame tracking is described in QEC literature. Specification must detail the interface to the decoder output and the quantum processor control system to enable PHOSITA practice.

ideas.valM: Essential hardware component for any production fault-tolerant quantum computer running mid-circuit measurement algorithms. Licensing to IBM, Google, Quantinuum, Amazon Braket. Value increases with adoption of fault-tolerant algorithms requiring MCM. Potential essential patent in quantum ISA standardization efforts.

ideas.title: SFQ-CMOS Hybrid Syndrome Extraction Circuit with Josephson Multiplexer for Sub-100-Nanosecond Stabilizer Measurement in Surface and Color Code Quantum Processors
ideas.desc:
Stabilizer syndrome measurement latency is a fundamental bottleneck in fault-tolerant quantum computing: each measurement cycle involves qubit-ancilla entanglement via CNOT gates, ancilla measurement via dispersive readout, ancilla reset, and classical readout digitization. The combination of ancilla reset (typically 500-1000ns for superconducting qubits) and readout digitization at 4K via conventional HEMT amplifiers limits syndrome extraction cycle time to approximately 1 microsecond for surface codes and 2-5 microseconds for color codes. Achieving substantially sub-microsecond syndrome extraction would dramatically improve logical qubit lifetimes by enabling more frequent error correction.

This invention is a hybrid SFQ-CMOS syndrome extraction circuit that combines Single Flux Quantum (SFQ) superconducting logic—which operates at picosecond timescales with femtojoule energy per operation—with 4K cryo-CMOS digital processing to achieve syndrome extraction cycle times below 100 nanoseconds. The circuit operates as follows: (1) SFQ pulse generators directly trigger ancilla qubit gates via on-chip flux pulse injection, replacing room-temperature microwave generators and eliminating 10+ microseconds of signal propagation delay; (2) a Josephson parametric amplifier (JPA) feeds directly into an SFQ comparator that digitizes the qubit readout signal in the SFQ domain, performing single-shot readout in <10 nanoseconds; (3) a Josephson multiplexer (JMUX) routes SFQ readout pulses from an array of ancilla qubits to a shared SFQ decoder interface, multiplying the effective readout bandwidth by the JMUX fan-in ratio (8:1 to 64:1); (4) cryo-CMOS logic at 4K handles ancilla reset sequencing and syndrome bit aggregation for forwarding to the decoder.

The critical differentiator from NTT's SFQ decoder (2022) and the SFQ-based qubit control work (PRX Quantum 2023) is the JMUX-based readout multiplexing architecture, which allows a single SFQ comparator chain to service multiple ancilla qubits—dramatically reducing the number of SFQ circuits required and their associated power dissipation. The NTT work demonstrates SFQ decoding but does not address the readout multiplexing problem; the PRX Quantum SFQ control work uses a multi-chip module for qubit control but does not address syndrome extraction multiplexing.
ideas.claim: A syndrome extraction circuit for a quantum error correction system comprising: (a) a plurality of single-flux-quantum (SFQ) pulse generators, each SFQ pulse generator coupled to a respective ancilla qubit circuit via an on-chip flux bias line, the SFQ pulse generators configured to generate voltage pulse sequences having pulse widths less than 10 picoseconds to drive ancilla qubit gate operations; (b) a Josephson parametric amplifier (JPA) chain coupled to at least one readout resonator associated with the ancilla qubit circuits, the JPA chain configured to amplify dispersive readout signals with a gain sufficient for single-shot qubit state discrimination; (c) a Josephson multiplexer (JMUX) circuit comprising a plurality of input ports each coupled to a respective SFQ comparator associated with the JPA chain output for a respective ancilla qubit, and a single output port, the JMUX configured to time-division multiplex SFQ readout pulses from the plurality of ancilla qubits onto the single output port with a multiplexing ratio between 8:1 and 64:1; (d) a cryo-CMOS syndrome aggregation circuit operating at a temperature between 1 Kelvin and 10 Kelvin, coupled to the JMUX output port, configured to deserialize the time-division multiplexed SFQ readout pulses and assemble syndrome bits into syndrome vectors; and (e) an ancilla reset control circuit configured to issue reset pulses to ancilla qubit circuits within 50 nanoseconds of syndrome bit capture, enabling syndrome extraction cycle times below 100 nanoseconds.
depClaims: SFQ shift register syndrome memory that captures a full code-cycle syndrome vector without cryo-CMOS buffering, enabling all-SFQ syndrome extraction at 20mK without warm-up to 4K for digitization

Adaptive JMUX scheduling algorithm implemented in hardware that prioritizes ancilla qubits with high historical error rates for more frequent readout, concentrating bandwidth where errors are most likely

Josephson multiplexer calibration circuit that compensates for fabrication-induced variations in SFQ comparator critical currents, maintaining readout discrimination fidelity across the multiplexed array
Integration of SFQ-based ancilla qubit active reset using a Josephson junction ring oscillator to drive ancilla qubits to ground state in under 20 nanoseconds

Error-detecting capability within the SFQ comparator chain to flag ambiguous readout events (near-threshold amplitude) and mark corresponding syndrome bits as erasures for specialized erasure decoders
Multi-layer SFQ circuit design with magnetic flux shielding layers between SFQ and qubit planes to prevent flux crosstalk that would corrupt qubit states during SFQ switching
JMUX architecture extended to neutral atom and trapped ion readout photomultiplier outputs, enabling the same multiplexed syndrome extraction hardware to serve multiple qubit modalities

refs.ref: vs_NTT_SFQ_decoder_2022_HPCA28
refs.diff: NTT implements SFQ-based decoding of logical qubits but does not address readout multiplexing; this invention's JMUX architecture allows one SFQ comparator chain to service 8-64 ancilla qubits, a fundamental scalability advance

refs.ref: vs_PRXQuantum_SFQ_qubit_control_2023
refs.diff: Das et al. (PRX Quantum) implements SFQ-based qubit gate control using multi-chip module architecture; this invention addresses syndrome extraction—the readout side—not gate control, and adds Josephson multiplexing not present in Das

refs.ref: vs_WO2022060897A1_SFQ_diabatic
refs.diff: SFQ diabatic readout patent uses symmetric RF-SQUID readout; this invention uses JPA + SFQ comparator + JMUX chain for multiplexed ancilla readout—different readout mechanism and novel multiplexing element

refs.ref: vs_Google_cryo_CMOS_28nm
refs.diff: Google's 28nm bulk-CMOS cryogenic pulse modulator operates at 4K for qubit control; this invention uses SFQ superconducting circuits at 20mK for syndrome extraction, a fundamentally different technology and operational temperature

ideas.ftoJ: The combination of JMUX-based SFQ multiplexed readout + JPA amplification + cryo-CMOS syndrome aggregation is not claimed in any identified patent. NTT's SFQ decoder patent (if granted in US) covers the decoding algorithm, not the readout extraction circuit. SFQ qubit control patents (Wisconsin WARF, US 9,425,804) cover gate operations, not syndrome extraction readout. The Josephson multiplexer for readout channel aggregation is a novel apparatus element. Recommend filing in US, Japan (NTT/NEC are competitors), and EU.

ps.s101n: Physical superconducting and semiconductor circuit apparatus. Strong §101 position.
ps.s102n: Josephson multiplexer for SFQ-domain syndrome extraction readout is a novel circuit element not found in identified prior art. The complete SFQ-CMOS hybrid extraction circuit with JPA + JMUX + cryo-CMOS aggregation is novel.
ps.s103n: The JMUX application to syndrome readout multiplexing requires combining JPA amplification (quantum microwave), SFQ comparators (superconducting digital), and JMUX frequency-division or time-division multiplexing—a non-obvious cross-domain combination requiring expertise in superconducting microwave circuits, digital SFQ logic, and QEC protocol design simultaneously.
ps.s112n: SFQ circuit design, JPA design, and cryogenic circuit fabrication are established fields. The specification must include detailed circuit schematics for the JMUX and SFQ comparator chain, plus fabrication process notes for Josephson junction JMUX at relevant critical current densities.

ideas.valM: Essential circuit primitive for superconducting quantum computers at >1,000 qubit scale. JPA-based syndrome extraction is the industry direction for fast readout. The JMUX multiplexing element reduces hardware cost by 8-64x per logical qubit, creating strong adoption incentive. Licensing to IBM, Google, IQM, QuantWare, Oxford Quantum Circuits.

ideas.title: QEC Co-Processor System-on-Chip Integrating Syndrome Extraction Controller, Belief-Propagation Decoder Engine, Pauli Frame Manager, and Logical Gate Scheduler for Fault-Tolerant Quantum Algorithm Execution
ideas.desc:
Today's fault-tolerant quantum computing stacks are assembled from discrete components: quantum processors, separate FPGA decoder boards, separate quantum controller electronics (Quantum Machines OPX, Zurich Instruments HDAWG), and software middleware (Qiskit Runtime). This fragmented architecture introduces inter-component latency, integration complexity, and reliability risks that become intolerable as systems scale to hundreds of logical qubits. An integrated QEC System-on-Chip (QEC-SoC) that combines all classical control and decoding functions in a single silicon die would represent a paradigm shift equivalent to the transition from discrete transistors to integrated circuits in classical computing.

This invention is a QEC Co-Processor SoC that integrates on a single silicon die: (1) a Syndrome Extraction Controller (SEC) with configurable timing engines for multiple qubit modalities; (2) a Belief-Propagation/Union-Find hybrid Decoder Engine (DE) with hardware-switchable decode algorithms selected based on code type (surface code: union-find; QLDPC: belief-propagation); (3) a Pauli Frame Manager (PFM) maintaining the logical error state for up to 1,024 logical qubits with hardware update paths; (4) a Logical Gate Scheduler (LGS) that sequences fault-tolerant logical gates including T-gates via magic state injection, S-gates, and Clifford operations, enforcing fault-tolerant preconditions in hardware; and (5) a high-bandwidth memory (HBM) interface for the magic state distillation buffer. The SoC communicates with the quantum processor via a standardized QEC interface (as described in Invention #3) and with the classical host computer via PCIe Gen 5.

The fundamental differentiator is the hardware integration of the logical gate scheduler with the decoder, enabling pipelined fault-tolerant computation: while the decoder processes syndrome data for round N, the scheduler is already issuing logical gate operations for round N+1, hiding the decoding latency behind gate operations. This 'decode-ahead' architecture reduces the effective logical cycle time by up to 50% compared to architectures where the quantum processor stalls waiting for decode completion. No identified patent or product integrates these four functional blocks in hardware with the decode-ahead scheduling optimization.
ideas.claim: A quantum error correction co-processor integrated circuit comprising: (a) a syndrome extraction controller comprising configurable timing registers and synchronization outputs configured to drive ancilla qubit measurement cycles on a quantum processor for at least two code types; (b) a decoder engine comprising a first hardware decode pipeline implementing a union-find algorithm and a second hardware decode pipeline implementing a min-sum belief-propagation algorithm, a code-type selector register configured to route syndrome vectors to the first pipeline or the second pipeline based on a stored code type value; (c) a Pauli frame manager comprising a plurality of logical qubit state registers, each logical qubit state register storing a current Pauli frame vector for a respective logical qubit, the Pauli frame manager configured to atomically update logical qubit state registers upon receipt of correction commands from the decoder engine; (d) a logical gate scheduler configured to receive fault-tolerant logical gate instructions from a classical host processor and issue quantum gate commands to the quantum processor, the logical gate scheduler comprising a decode-ahead buffer storing prefetched logical gate instructions for issuance during periods when the decoder engine is processing syndrome data from a preceding gate operation; and (e) a high-bandwidth memory interface configured to access a magic state distillation buffer, the logical gate scheduler configured to check the availability of distilled magic states before scheduling T-gate operations and to defer T-gate scheduling until a distilled magic state is available.
depClaims: On-chip magic state distillation engine implementing a hardware-accelerated 15-to-1 distillation protocol, eliminating the need for separate magic state factory logical qubits and reducing physical qubit overhead
Hardware fault injection testing mode that injects controlled Pauli errors into the Pauli frame manager registers to verify decoder engine correctness during system qualification

Dynamic workload partitioning between union-find and belief-propagation pipelines based on real-time syndrome weight statistics, automatically selecting the faster-converging algorithm for each decoding round
Network-on-chip (NoC) interconnect between functional blocks with quality-of-service guarantees ensuring that syndrome data delivery to the decoder engine meets coherence-time deadlines
Multi-QEC-SoC scale-out architecture with a chip-to-chip coherency protocol enabling multiple QEC-SoC instances to collectively service a large logical qubit array exceeding single-chip capacity
Integrated logical qubit error rate telemetry aggregation that computes per-qubit logical error rates in hardware and exports them to the host system for real-time monitoring dashboards

Power management controller that duty-cycles the magic state distillation engine and belief-propagation pipeline based on algorithmic requirements, reducing average power consumption during idle logical qubits

refs.ref: vs_Quantum_Machines_US12242406B2
refs.diff: QM's switch fabric operates at the pulse-processor level handling physical qubit pulse coordination; this invention operates at the logical qubit abstraction layer, scheduling fault-tolerant logical gates and managing magic state distillation—a distinct and higher architectural level

refs.ref: vs_Riverlane_Deltaflow
refs.diff: Riverlane's system is an FPGA-based software/firmware stack; this invention is a single-die ASIC SoC integrating four hardware functional blocks with the decode-ahead scheduler—a novel apparatus integration not claimed by Riverlane

refs.ref: vs_IBM_US11983471B2_QCC
refs.diff: IBM's QCC patent covers a quantum circuit component library service for reuse in quantum-classical hybrid environments; this invention is a hardware SoC, not a software library service

refs.ref: vs_IBM_contextual_calibration_2025
refs.diff: IBM's contextual calibration adjusts signal parameters for noise reduction before execution; this invention's logical gate scheduler enforces fault-tolerant preconditions during execution at the logical gate level—complementary but distinct

ideas.ftoJ: The integration of multiple QEC functions on a single ASIC SoC is broadly novel, but individual functional blocks (decoder engines, Pauli frame tracking, gate scheduling) have partial coverage in IBM and Quantum Machines portfolios at the software/firmware level. The specific hardware integration—particularly the decode-ahead scheduler with magic state availability gating—is not covered in identified patents. YELLOW rating reflects the possibility of method claims in IBM's QEC patents reading on aspects of the logical gate scheduling. Recommend apparatus claim strategy focused on the SoC as a physical integrated circuit with the decode-ahead buffer and HBM magic state interface as key differentiating limitations.

ps.s101n: Single-die integrated circuit apparatus. No §101 concerns under Alice/Mayo for physical semiconductor apparatus claims.
ps.s102n: The integrated QEC SoC with decode-ahead scheduler and magic state availability gating is novel. Component-wise, individual blocks have partial prior art; the integration and decode-ahead architecture is novel.
ps.s103n: Integrating four previously discrete systems (syndrome controller, decoder, Pauli frame manager, gate scheduler) on a single die with the decode-ahead optimization requires insight into the timing relationships between logical gate operations and decoding cycles—non-obvious to a PHOSITA without the specific knowledge of decode-ahead pipelining in the quantum context.
ps.s112n: All component technologies are well-described in literature. SoC integration is standard practice. The specification must detail the decode-ahead buffer mechanism and the magic state availability protocol to enable PHOSITA practice.

ideas.valM: Single-chip QEC co-processor is the long-term industry endpoint for production quantum computers. This patent would be essential to any company selling a commercial QEC stack as hardware (as opposed to cloud service). Value as foundational SoC patent akin to early GPU patents for parallel compute. Licensing to quantum hardware OEMs, cloud providers (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud), and defense contractors. Highest estimated value due to SoC scope and decode-ahead optimizer.

ideas.title: Adaptive Gradient Composition Controller for Multi-Material Powder Bed Fusion with Voxel-Level Material Ratio Feedback

desc.paragraph_1_problem: Powder bed fusion (PBF) processes are constrained to single-material or binary-mixed powder beds, with no mechanism to continuously modulate the volumetric composition of deposited material at sub-millimeter spatial resolution. When functionally graded materials (FGMs) are attempted — for example, transitioning from Ti-6Al-4V to TiAl ceramic across a turbine blade section — abrupt composition changes at layer boundaries induce intermetallic phase formation, residual stress concentrations, and delamination. Current approaches either accept coarse step-function gradients or require manual powder changeover between build segments, limiting geometric complexity and introducing contamination. No existing issued patent addresses closed-loop, voxel-resolution composition modulation within a single PBF build using real-time spectroscopic feedback.
desc.paragraph_2_solution: The invention provides a multi-channel powder delivery manifold integrated into a PBF apparatus, wherein independently metered powder streams (2-6 distinct materials) are combined in a dynamically adjustable mixing chamber positioned upstream of the powder recoater blade. A controller receives real-time energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) or laser-induced breakdown spectroscopy (LIBS) measurements from an in-situ probe positioned coaxially with the build field, computing actual local composition at each scanned voxel. A closed-loop composition controller compares measured voxel composition against a target gradient schedule derived from a functionally graded material design model, and adjusts individual powder feed rates to minimize composition error at each subsequent layer. The system achieves composition gradient resolutions of ≤50 µm in the build-plane direction and ≤1 layer thickness (~30 µm) in the Z-axis.
desc.paragraph_3_differentiators: This invention is differentiated from SLM Solutions US11123798, which uses two materials solely to create thermally conductive support structures — not gradient functional parts. It is further differentiated from generic dual-hopper systems (e.g., University of Manchester ultrasonic powder delivery) by the addition of an in-situ spectroscopic feedback control loop that eliminates composition drift. The system architecture enables composition-of-matter structures (functionally graded parts with continuously varying properties) impossible to manufacture by any existing certified commercial PBF platform. The voxel-level control creates intellectual property that is unavoidable for any high-precision multi-material PBF manufacturer serving aerospace and medical device customers.

claim.preamble: A powder bed fusion apparatus for fabricating a three-dimensional workpiece comprising functionally graded material, the apparatus comprising:
limitations: (a) a powder delivery system comprising at least two independently metered powder reservoirs each containing a distinct metallic or ceramic powder composition, and a variable-ratio mixing manifold fluidically coupled to each reservoir and configured to continuously vary a mass flow ratio of the at least two powders within a range of 0:100 to 100:0 during a single layer deposition cycle;

(b) an in-situ composition sensor positioned within a measurement field of the build area, the sensor configured to measure elemental or phase composition of the deposited powder layer or solidified track at a spatial resolution of 200 µm or finer and output a composition signal in real-time;

(c) a composition controller operably coupled to the powder delivery system and the in-situ composition sensor, the controller configured to: receive a target gradient composition schedule specifying a desired material composition as a function of three-dimensional position within the workpiece; compare the composition signal against the target gradient composition schedule; and generate mixing ratio control commands that adjust the mass flow ratio of the at least two powders to minimize deviation between the measured composition and the target schedule at a subsequent deposition location; and

(d) an energy source configured to selectively fuse the deposited powder according to an irradiation pattern corresponding to the workpiece geometry, wherein irradiation parameters are co-modulated by the composition controller based on the real-time mixing ratio to compensate for changes in powder absorptivity, melting temperature, or thermal conductivity as composition varies.

depClaims: The in-situ composition sensor comprises a laser-induced breakdown spectroscopy (LIBS) unit with a fiber-optic delivery path co-axially mounted with the irradiation beam

The composition controller implements a model predictive control (MPC) algorithm using a physics-based thermal model of the multi-material melt pool to anticipate composition-dependent process parameter requirements one or more layers ahead
A contamination detection module that halts or flags build segments where cross-contamination between powder streams exceeds a user-defined threshold, triggering a purge cycle
A machine learning regression model trained on multi-material melt pool spectral signatures to infer sub-surface composition at completed layers from surface spectroscopic measurements

A gradient schedule compiler that accepts a three-dimensional FGM design specification in a standard format (e.g., STEP AP242 or AMF) and generates layer-by-layer powder mixing ratio commands compatible with the composition controller

Integration with a digital twin that simulates the thermal stress field at multi-material interfaces layer-by-layer and updates the gradient schedule in real time to minimize predicted residual stress concentrations

A post-build composition verification report generated from the aggregated in-situ sensor data, providing a volumetric composition map suitable for regulatory submission under aerospace or medical device certification requirements

ideas.ftoJ: No identified blocking IP in the combination of: (a) multi-channel powder delivery with continuous ratio variation + (b) in-situ spectroscopic composition measurement + (c) closed-loop gradient schedule controller. Incumbent patents cluster on single-material irradiation control and basic dual-material support structures. Primary risk vector is if GE or Raytheon has unpublished continuation applications — recommend prosecution history monitoring and filing within 90 days.

ps.s101n: Apparatus claim with concrete physical components (powder delivery hardware, spectroscopic sensor, controller, energy source) is unambiguously patent-eligible under Alice Step 1. No abstract idea concern. Method claims incorporating the closed-loop spectroscopic control loop also patent-eligible as industrial process claims tied to a specific machine.
ps.s102n: The specific combination of in-situ LIBS/EDS composition feedback + variable-ratio mixing manifold + gradient schedule compiler in a single integrated apparatus has no identified prior art anticipation. The closest references (Manchester ultrasonic system, SLM US11123798) lack the feedback control loop.
ps.s103n: A PHOSITA would not obviously combine a LIBS spectrometer (typically used for post-process material identification) with a powder mixing manifold controller in a PBF apparatus because: (1) the hostile in-situ PBF environment (high-temperature plasma, spatter, inert gas flow) creates non-obvious engineering challenges for spectroscopic in-process measurement; (2) the control loop design requires non-obvious adaptation of MPC to multi-material thermal physics with composition-varying absorptivity.
ps.s112n: Enabling disclosure is straightforward: LIBS hardware is commercially available (e.g., SciAps), powder mixing manifolds exist in research literature, MPC algorithms are well-described. A PHOSITA could practice the invention from a specification describing the sensor integration geometry, calibration procedures, and control loop architecture.

ideas.valM: Comparable license rates for key AM process control patents ($250K-$1.5M/year per major OEM licensee) × estimated 8-12 relevant licensees (GE, Siemens, EOS, SLM/Nikon, Trumpf, Renishaw, HP Metal, Desktop Metal, Velo3D, Markforged) × 20-year term NPV. Upside if used as chokepoint in aerospace-certified multi-material AM qualification supply chain.

ideas.title: Metal-Ceramic Interface Transition Algorithm with Adaptive Laser Parameter Modulation for Functionally Graded Structural Components

desc.paragraph_1_problem: The fabrication of metal-to-ceramic transitions in additive manufacturing is among the most technically challenging problems in the field. When depositing ceramics (e.g., Al2O3, ZrO2, TiC, Si3N4) adjacent to or atop metallic substrates (e.g., Ti-6Al-4V, IN718, 316L), the coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) mismatch creates residual stress concentrations at the interface that routinely exceed the fracture toughness of the ceramic phase, resulting in cracking, delamination, or catastrophic failure during or after the build. Current practice involves either accepting a discrete step junction (high failure rate) or manually specifying coarse step-function gradient layers — neither approach provides continuous, stress-optimized grading. No existing patent specifically addresses an algorithm-driven, physics-informed control system that continuously adapts laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, and layer thickness as a function of local metal-to-ceramic composition ratio to minimize interface stress accumulation.
desc.paragraph_2_solution: The invention provides a metal-ceramic interface transition algorithm (MCITA) implemented in a process controller for directed energy deposition (DED) or PBF systems. The MCITA ingests: (1) the target three-dimensional composition gradient profile specifying metal/ceramic volume fractions as a function of position; (2) a materials property database comprising CTE, thermal conductivity, elastic modulus, and melting point as functions of metal-ceramic volume fraction; and (3) real-time melt pool temperature data from a pyrometric sensor. The algorithm executes a thermomechanical stress simulation at each layer, predicts the von Mises stress field at the upcoming interface zone, and generates an optimized set of laser process parameters (power modulation profile, scan vector direction, scan speed, hatching pattern) that minimizes predicted residual stress while maintaining full densification. The algorithm also determines an optimal gradient thickness (number of transition layers) based on allowable stress criteria specified for the target application.
desc.paragraph_3_differentiators: No prior art patent discloses an algorithm that simultaneously (a) uses real-time pyrometric melt pool temperature as a model input, (b) predicts thermomechanical stress at the metal-ceramic interface ahead of the current layer, and (c) generates a multi-parameter laser process recipe update in real time. The prior art in metal-ceramic AM (US20200047288A1, which covers bonding metals to glass/ceramic substrates using focused energy) is limited to metallurgical bonding methods and does not address the multi-parameter adaptive control of an FGM gradient. This invention covers the algorithmic core that makes certified metal-ceramic FGM production possible for aerospace thermal barrier coatings, gas turbine components, and orthopedic implant surfaces.

claim.preamble: A method for controlling an additive manufacturing process to fabricate a three-dimensional component comprising a metal-to-ceramic functionally graded transition zone, comprising:
limitations: (a) receiving a target gradient composition profile specifying, for each voxel in a transition zone of the component, a volume fraction of at least one metallic phase and at least one ceramic phase, wherein the transition zone spans at least 3 layers and less than 10 mm in the build direction;

(b) executing, for each layer within the transition zone, a thermomechanical stress simulation using: (i) a materials property model that computes thermal expansion coefficient, thermal conductivity, and elastic modulus as a function of the local metal-ceramic volume fraction; and (ii) a thermal boundary condition derived from real-time melt pool temperature data acquired from a pyrometric sensor monitoring the build area;

(c) computing an optimized laser process parameter set for the upcoming layer comprising at least laser power, scan speed, and hatch spacing, wherein the parameter set is selected to minimize a predicted residual stress metric at the metal-ceramic interface while maintaining a minimum relative density of 98% in the deposited layer; and
(d) commanding an irradiation system of the additive manufacturing apparatus to execute the layer according to the optimized laser process parameter set.

depClaims: Determining an optimal transition zone thickness as a function of the CTE differential between the terminal metal composition and the terminal ceramic composition, constrained to an allowable maximum residual stress criterion specified for an aerospace, medical, or energy application

The thermomechanical stress simulation incorporates a phase diagram model to predict intermetallic compound formation probability at the current composition and temperature, and modifies the laser parameters to suppress deleterious phase formation

A post-layer non-destructive evaluation step using laser ultrasound or acoustic emission sensors to detect micro-cracking at the interface, triggering a remediation protocol that deposits an additional graded interlayer if cracking is detected

Storing the optimized layer-by-layer parameter sets as a process recipe tied to the specific metal-ceramic material pair and transition zone geometry, building a searchable database for subsequent identical builds

Extending the method to multiple simultaneous metal-ceramic interfaces within a single component, allowing the fabrication of components with spatially separated gradient zones (e.g., a structural metal core with ceramic wear surface and ceramic thermal barrier on opposite faces)
Application to electron beam melting (EBM) systems using adapted beam focus and current modulation parameters in place of laser power and scan speed
Integration with a digital thread platform that records the as-built composition profile and parameter history for regulatory traceability
