ideas.ftoJ: The specific combination of real-time pyrometric feedback + thermomechanical simulation + multi-parameter laser optimization for metal-ceramic FGMs is clear. Raytheon's US20240393765A1 covers pre-build defect code generation for multi-laser single-material builds — distinct from real-time metal-ceramic gradient control. Recommend filing method claims broadly and system claims narrowly.

ps.s101n: Method claims involving a physical machine (laser AM system), real-time sensor inputs, and a physical output (manufactured component with reduced residual stress) are patent-eligible. Risk: if claimed too abstractly as 'a method of simulating stress and adjusting parameters' without tying to specific AM hardware, examiner may raise Alice concern. Recommend claim framing that explicitly ties algorithm execution to physical sensor inputs and machine control commands.
ps.s102n: The specific combination of pyrometric real-time input + thermomechanical simulation + multi-parameter laser recipe output in an integrated AM control loop for metal-ceramic FGMs has no identified anticipating reference.
ps.s103n: A PHOSITA would not obviously combine a thermomechanical stress simulation (typically done off-line in FEA tools like ANSYS) with a real-time pyrometric sensor control loop in a metal-ceramic AM context, because: (1) off-line simulation assumes fixed material properties; the inventive step requires a composition-dependent materials property model updated in real-time; (2) metal-ceramic systems present unique non-obvious challenges (ceramic phase brittle fracture threshold, anisotropic CTE) not present in metal-metal systems.
ps.s112n: Enablement requires disclosing: composition-dependent materials property models (available in published ceramics literature), pyrometer integration specifications, and the MPC or optimization algorithm. Moderate complexity but achievable in a detailed specification with worked examples for Ti6Al4V/TiC and IN718/ZrO2 systems.

ideas.valM: Critical for aerospace thermal barrier coating (TBC) market ($3.8B global, growing 7% CAGR) and orthopedic implant AM ($1.2B). Licensing to TBC manufacturers and orthopedic AM service bureaus. Blocking position for anyone producing certified metal-ceramic FGM aerospace components via AM.

ideas.title: In-Situ Multi-Material Defect Detection System Using Hyperspectral Melt Pool Imaging and Material-Specific Anomaly Classifiers

desc.paragraph_1_problem: Existing in-situ monitoring systems for metal AM (optical emission sensing, thermal cameras, photodiodes) are calibrated for single-material builds and use fixed threshold-based anomaly detection models. When applied to multi-material builds, these systems generate extremely high false-positive rates because the spectral emission signature, melt pool size, cooling rate, and surface morphology all change dramatically as composition varies — behaviors that a single-material trained classifier interprets as defects. Conversely, material-boundary-specific defects such as intermetallic crack nucleation, insufficient fusion at heterogeneous interfaces, or elemental segregation at composition gradients are invisible to single-material monitoring systems because they do not cause the expected single-material anomaly signatures. The result is that multi-material AM parts cannot currently be monitored in real time during production, blocking regulatory certification for flight-critical or implantable applications.
desc.paragraph_2_solution: The invention provides a hyperspectral imaging system integrated into a multi-material AM apparatus, comprising a spectrally resolved detector array (400-2500 nm range) that captures per-pixel spectral emission data from the active melt pool and surrounding heat-affected zone at frame rates synchronized with the scanner (≥5 kHz). The system maintains a library of material-specific spectral baseline models — one for each material in the build and one for each anticipated transition composition — generated from a controlled calibration procedure. A defect classification engine running on a dedicated processing unit applies a composition-aware anomaly classifier at each scan location: it first identifies the current expected composition at that location from the build schedule, selects the corresponding spectral baseline model, and then evaluates whether the measured melt pool spectral signature deviates from that baseline in ways indicative of specific defect classes (porosity, intermetallic formation, insufficient fusion, delamination precursors, or elemental segregation). An automated response system can issue real-time alerts, adjust process parameters, or flag regions for targeted post-build inspection.
desc.paragraph_3_differentiators: SLM Solutions US11685122 covers sensor data analysis for single-material PBF quality monitoring. The key inventive distinction here is the composition-aware baseline switching — the system dynamically selects which anomaly detection model to apply based on the expected local material composition at each scan point, which is novel and non-obvious. This composition-aware multi-model architecture is the unavoidable element for any high-value multi-material AM quality system. The invention additionally covers the calibration methodology for building the material-specific spectral baseline library, which is a separate and valuable IP asset.

claim.preamble: A quality monitoring system for a multi-material additive manufacturing apparatus, comprising:
limitations: (a) a hyperspectral detector array positioned to capture spectral emission data from a melt pool region during fabrication of a three-dimensional workpiece from at least two distinct material compositions, the detector array configured to acquire spectral data across a wavelength range of at least 400 nm to 1600 nm at a frame acquisition rate synchronized with the scan velocity of the additive manufacturing apparatus;

(b) a composition map module operably coupled to a build schedule of the workpiece, the composition map module configured to output, for each spatial location in the build area at each time step, a predicted local material composition based on the build schedule;

(c) a spectral baseline library comprising, for each of the at least two material compositions and for at least one intermediate composition between them, a reference spectral model representing a defect-free melt pool emission signature at nominal process parameters; and

(d) a composition-aware defect classifier operably coupled to the hyperspectral detector array, the composition map module, and the spectral baseline library, the classifier configured to: retrieve the reference spectral model corresponding to the predicted local material composition at a current scan location; compute a deviation metric between the acquired spectral data at the current scan location and the retrieved reference spectral model; and generate a defect classification signal categorizing any detected deviation as one or more defect types from the group consisting of: porosity, intermetallic phase formation, insufficient inter-layer fusion, delamination precursor, and elemental segregation.

depClaims: The spectral baseline library is generated by a calibration procedure comprising fabricating coupons of each material composition and transition composition under nominal process parameters and recording the corresponding melt pool spectral signatures

The defect classifier uses a machine learning model (convolutional neural network or transformer architecture) trained on labeled hyperspectral melt pool image datasets for each material composition, enabling detection of defect signatures that are not captured by scalar threshold comparisons

An adaptive recalibration module that updates reference spectral models in real time as the composition at a given location deviates from the scheduled composition, compensating for powder mixing variability or contamination

A confidence map output that assigns a defect probability score to each voxel in the completed workpiece, formatted for integration with X-ray CT inspection records to create a combined non-destructive evaluation report for regulatory submission

A real-time closed-loop response mode in which the defect classifier output triggers automatic adjustment of laser power or scan speed within the current layer to remediate detected insufficient fusion events before the next layer is deposited

Integration with a blockchain-based digital quality record system that stores the per-voxel defect classification data with cryptographic integrity for airworthiness certification or FDA 510(k) submission

A multi-laser embodiment wherein each laser in a multi-laser system is assigned a distinct spectral monitoring channel, enabling per-laser defect attribution in a concurrent multi-material, multi-laser build

ideas.ftoJ: Clear. Composition-aware hyperspectral multi-model defect classification for multi-material AM is not found in any identified incumbent portfolio. GE and Raytheon hold melt pool monitoring patents for single-material builds; none extend to the composition-aware classifier architecture.

ps.s101n: System claim with hardware components (detector array, processing unit, physical AM apparatus) is clearly patent-eligible. Machine learning classifier is tied to a physical measurement system and a physical manufacturing process output — no Alice concern under current USPTO guidance for ML-integrated physical process monitoring.
ps.s102n: Composition-aware baseline switching combined with hyperspectral detection at AM melt pool frame rates is novel. No anticipating reference identified.
ps.s103n: Non-obvious because: (1) hyperspectral melt pool imaging at ≥5 kHz is technically challenging and requires non-obvious optical design choices to manage spatter and plasma emission; (2) the composition-aware model selection architecture is not suggested by any single prior art reference; (3) the calibration methodology for building multi-composition spectral baseline libraries is not described in the prior art.
ps.s112n: Enabling disclosure requires specification of detector array hardware (available commercially from e.g., Headwall Photonics, Specim), synchronization architecture, ML model training procedure, and calibration coupon methodology. Moderate complexity — achievable with detailed specification.

ideas.valM: Quality monitoring systems for AM are a $400M+ annual market. Composition-aware monitoring is mandatory for aerospace-certified multi-material builds. Potential licensing to OEM equipment manufacturers plus AM service bureaus. High value as a blocking patent on in-process certification infrastructure.

ideas.title: Embedded Sensor Array Architecture for Multi-Material Additive Manufacturing with Material-Compliant Encapsulation and Wireless Data Extraction

desc.paragraph_1_problem: Structural health monitoring of additively manufactured components requires sensors embedded within the part itself, not surface-mounted, to detect internal stress, temperature gradients, and crack nucleation at critical interfaces. In single-material AM, embedded fiber Bragg grating (FBG) sensors and printed thermocouples have been demonstrated, but in multi-material AM the problem is compounded: the sensor encapsulant and interconnect must survive CTE mismatch stresses at material interfaces, the embedding process must not disrupt the surrounding gradient composition, and data must be extractable from a fully encapsulated metallic structure without wired penetrations. Current attempts at embedded sensing in AM lack: (1) encapsulation materials specifically graded to bridge the CTE differential between the sensor element and the surrounding multi-material matrix; (2) embedding protocols that pause and resume the gradient schedule without introducing composition discontinuities; and (3) near-field data transmission architectures compatible with metallic enclosure.
desc.paragraph_2_solution: The invention provides an embedded sensor array architecture for multi-material AM comprising three interconnected innovations: First, a compositionally graded encapsulant — a printed sacrificial shell surrounding each sensor node whose composition is graded from a material compatible with the sensor substrate (e.g., silicon carbide or alumina for ceramic sensors) to the surrounding metallic matrix material, using the gradient composition controller (as described in Invention 1) to print the encapsulant as an integral feature of the component. Second, an embedding protocol that pauses gradient composition delivery at the sensor location, deposits the encapsulant and sensor element in a defined cavity, then resumes the gradient schedule with compositional continuity algorithms to avoid step-function interfaces. Third, a near-field inductive coupling architecture using embedded planar coil traces printed from a high-conductivity multi-material (e.g., copper-rich FGM transition to structural alloy) that enables wireless power delivery and data extraction from the embedded sensor array through metallic walls using frequency-modulated inductive coupling at 100 kHz-1 MHz.
desc.paragraph_3_differentiators: Lawrence Livermore National Security's Computed Axial Lithography (CAL) technology embeds sensors in photopolymer resins — not applicable to high-temperature metal AM. EurekaPatsnap analysis (2025) confirms that embedded sensor AM IP is largely focused on polymer and polymer-composite systems. The inventive distinction is the CTE-graded compositional encapsulant printed in-situ as part of the gradient composition build, combined with the near-field inductive data extraction architecture compatible with metallic enclosure. This combination is non-obvious because the graded encapsulant requires the gradient composition controller (Invention 1) as a prerequisite, creating an interdependency that forms a natural patent thicket.

claim.preamble: A multi-material additively manufactured structural component comprising an embedded sensor array, the component comprising:
limitations: (a) a structural body fabricated from at least two distinct material compositions with a functionally graded transition zone between them;

(b) at least one sensor node embedded within the structural body, the sensor node comprising a sensing element configured to measure at least one physical parameter selected from the group consisting of: strain, temperature, acoustic emission, and pressure;

(c) a compositionally graded encapsulant surrounding the sensor node, the encapsulant comprising a first composition at a boundary adjacent to the sensor node that is compositionally compatible with a substrate material of the sensing element, and a second composition at a boundary adjacent to the structural body that is compositionally matched to the local material composition of the structural body at the embedding location, wherein the encapsulant composition varies continuously between the first and second compositions over a distance of 0.1 mm to 5 mm; and

(d) an inductive coupling structure embedded within the structural body and electrically connected to the sensor node, the inductive coupling structure comprising at least one planar conductive coil printed from a composition having an electrical conductivity of at least 10^6 S/m, configured to transmit sensor data wirelessly through at least 5 mm of metallic material using frequency-modulated inductive coupling.

depClaims: The sensing element comprises a fiber Bragg grating (FBG) sensor encapsulated in a ceramic-to-metal graded sleeve, enabling temperature and strain measurement at the metal-ceramic interface of the structural body
The inductive coupling structure is connected to an energy harvesting circuit also embedded within the structural body, enabling passive sensor operation without external power connections
A plurality of sensor nodes distributed across the functionally graded transition zone at a spatial density sufficient to reconstruct the full strain field at the interface during operational loading

A method of fabricating the component comprising: printing a first portion of the structural body up to the sensor embedding layer; depositing the sensor node and its compositionally graded encapsulant using the multi-material delivery system; and resuming printing of the structural body with a composition continuity algorithm that ensures the gradient schedule is re-engaged without discontinuity
The compositionally graded encapsulant transitions from a ceramic-rich composition at the sensor boundary to the metallic structural material within 10 or fewer deposited layers

The embedded sensor array is configured to transmit real-time structural health data during operational service of the component, and the data transmission protocol is compliant with IRIG-106 or IEEE 802.15.4 wireless standards adapted for near-field metallic enclosure environments
Application of the apparatus to a gas turbine blade or vane, wherein the sensor array monitors thermal and mechanical loading at the metal-to-thermal-barrier-coating interface during engine operation

ideas.ftoJ: Composition-of-matter claim (the manufactured article with graded encapsulant) is a strong FTO position. No identified issued patents cover the combination of CTE-graded encapsulant + embedded inductive coupling in a metallic multi-material AM part. The composition-of-matter structure is particularly difficult for competitors to design around.

ps.s101n: Composition-of-matter / manufactured article claim is inherently patent-eligible. No abstract idea concern.
ps.s102n: The combination of: (1) CTE-graded encapsulant printed in-situ as part of the multi-material build, (2) near-field inductive coupling through metallic enclosure, and (3) the specific gradient encapsulant composition profile — is novel with no identified anticipation.
ps.s103n: Non-obvious: the CTE-graded encapsulant concept requires the gradient composition controller of Invention 1 as a technical prerequisite — a PHOSITA would not obviously combine these without motivation from the specific problem of sensor survival at metal-ceramic interfaces. Inductive coupling through thick metallic walls at the specified frequency range is also a non-obvious engineering solution.
ps.s112n: The specification must detail: graded encapsulant composition profiles for at least 2-3 sensor/matrix material pairs, the embedding protocol for composition continuity, and the inductive coupling coil geometry and frequency specifications. Achievable with detailed examples.

ideas.valM: Structural health monitoring for aerospace components is a $1.5B market. The embedded sensing capability enables lifecycle monitoring that reduces MRO costs and enables extended component service intervals — high willingness-to-pay from aerospace OEMs. Composition-of-matter claims create particularly strong IP position.

ideas.title: Multi-Material Interface Digital Twin with Physics-Informed Neural Network for Real-Time Interface Property Prediction and Build Correction

desc.paragraph_1_problem: Digital twin models currently deployed in additive manufacturing (e.g., Siemens NX AM simulation, ANSYS Additive Print) are designed for single-material builds and predict distortion, residual stress, and porosity based on a homogeneous material property assumption. When applied to multi-material builds, these models fail at interface locations where material properties change discontinuously — they cannot predict interface-specific failure modes such as diffusion-driven intermetallic formation, stress concentration at CTE mismatch boundaries, or composition-dependent solidification microstructure evolution. The result is that no commercially available digital twin can serve as a validated surrogate model for multi-material AM build planning, making certification by similarity of multi-material components infeasible.
desc.paragraph_2_solution: The invention provides a multi-material interface digital twin (MMIDT) system comprising a physics-informed neural network (PINN) trained on both: (1) simulated data from a high-fidelity finite element thermomechanical model of multi-material interfaces (capturing CTE mismatch stress, diffusion-zone growth, and intermetallic phase formation kinetics); and (2) real-time in-situ sensor data from multi-material AM builds (melt pool temperature, acoustic emission, and layer thickness measurements). The PINN architecture encodes the governing partial differential equations of heat transfer, mass diffusion, and mechanics as physics constraints in the loss function, ensuring physical consistency even in regimes with sparse training data. The MMIDT outputs, for each planned build layer, a predicted composition-property map, an interface defect probability field, and a recommended process parameter adjustment — enabling pre-build simulation-based qualification and real-time build correction simultaneously.
desc.paragraph_3_differentiators: Existing AM digital twin patents and products (Siemens G05B19-class patents, ANSYS simulation integration) are physics-simulation tools that operate off-line and cannot ingest real-time sensor data or generate real-time corrections. The inventive elements are: (1) the PINN architecture that enforces physical governing equations while learning from noisy real-time sensor data — enabling the twin to remain predictive at novel interface compositions; (2) the multi-material interface-specific training procedure; and (3) the bidirectional coupling — the twin both predicts future build behavior and receives sensor data to update its state estimate. This MMIDT architecture forms the computational infrastructure layer for next-generation multi-material AM certification workflows.

claim.preamble: A digital twin system for multi-material additive manufacturing, comprising:
limitations: (a) a physics-informed neural network (PINN) comprising a first set of network parameters trained to minimize a loss function that includes at least: (i) a data fidelity term measuring the deviation between PINN outputs and training data derived from multi-material additive manufacturing builds; and (ii) a physics residual term measuring the degree to which the PINN outputs satisfy a set of governing equations comprising heat conduction with composition-dependent thermal properties and mass diffusion at material interfaces;

(b) a sensor data ingestion module configured to receive real-time process measurements from at least one in-situ sensor monitoring a multi-material additive manufacturing apparatus during fabrication of a workpiece, and to update the state of the PINN with the received measurements;

(c) an interface prediction module configured to execute the PINN to generate, for each planned build layer, a predicted composition-property field specifying at least local thermal conductivity, elastic modulus, and coefficient of thermal expansion as a function of three-dimensional position within the workpiece, and a predicted defect probability field specifying the likelihood of at least two defect types selected from the group consisting of: intermetallic phase formation, porosity, insufficient fusion, and interfacial delamination; and

(d) a build correction recommender configured to receive the predicted defect probability field and generate process parameter adjustment commands for at least one of: laser power, scan speed, layer thickness, or powder feed rate, the adjustments targeted to reduce the predicted defect probabilities below a user-specified threshold.

depClaims: The PINN is pre-trained on a dataset of at least 10 distinct metal-metal and metal-ceramic material pairs using a transfer learning architecture that allows fine-tuning to a new material pair with fewer than 50 experimental build samples

The physics residual term further includes a term enforcing the Gibbs free energy minimization condition for phase stability at the predicted local composition and temperature, enabling prediction of intermetallic phase formation

An uncertainty quantification module that outputs confidence intervals for the predicted defect probabilities, flagging layers where prediction uncertainty exceeds a threshold for enhanced post-build inspection

Integration with the in-situ composition sensor described in Invention 1, such that the measured composition data serves as a real-time correction input to the PINN state estimate, maintaining digital-physical synchronization

A multi-fidelity architecture in which a low-fidelity fast model (surrogate) handles real-time layer-by-layer corrections and a high-fidelity model (full FEA) is invoked offline to periodically recalibrate the surrogate

A regulatory submission module that generates a standardized as-predicted build report in a format compliant with FAA Advisory Circular AC 33.15-1 or equivalent regulatory guidance for AM qualification
Extension to Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM) multi-material builds, wherein current and voltage waveforms replace laser power parameters as the primary control inputs

ideas.ftoJ: Mild yellow: Siemens holds G05B19 and G06F30 patents on manufacturing simulation and process twins that could have broad claim language potentially covering some PINN-based AM simulation. Recommend: (1) claim the PINN loss function formulation with physics residual terms as the inventive core — this specific architecture is not in any identified Siemens claim; (2) explicitly limit claims to multi-material interface-specific physics; (3) conduct a freedom-to-operate analysis against Siemens' recent G06F30/23 and G06N20/00 filings before publication.

ps.s101n: PINN systems for manufacturing process control have been accepted as patent-eligible by the USPTO when tied to physical machines and producing physical outputs. Risk: broad claims on a PINN for process prediction could attract Alice rejections if not firmly anchored to specific physical sensor inputs and physical machine control outputs. Claim strategy: tie PINN outputs explicitly to physical process parameter adjustment commands.
ps.s102n: The specific combination of PINN with multi-material interface physics constraints + real-time sensor state update + multi-material defect probability prediction for AM is novel.
ps.s103n: A PHOSITA in AM would know FEA simulation and would know neural networks, but would not obviously combine them as a PINN with physics residual terms specifically encoding CTE mismatch and diffusion kinetics at multi-material AM interfaces, because the specific physics formulation for AM interface phenomena is not described in any prior art ML+AM combination.
ps.s112n: Requires disclosure of: PINN loss function mathematical formulation, training dataset specifications, governing PDE terms for at least 2 material pairs, and sensor integration architecture. Technical but fully enableable.

ideas.valM: AM simulation software market is $500M+ growing at 25% CAGR. MMIDT would be essential infrastructure for aerospace multi-material AM qualification. Licensing to Siemens, Ansys, Autodesk, and AM equipment manufacturers. High strategic value as certification enablement IP.

ideas.title: Multi-Laser Wavelength-Selective Coordination System for Simultaneous Processing of Dissimilar Absorptivity Metal Combinations in Powder Bed Fusion

desc.paragraph_1_problem: Multi-material AM with metals having significantly different optical absorptivity (e.g., highly reflective copper or aluminum combined with absorptive titanium, steel, or nickel superalloys) presents an unresolved fundamental problem: a single laser wavelength cannot efficiently process both materials simultaneously. Standard PBF systems use 1064 nm Nd:YAG or fiber laser wavelengths at which copper has <5% absorptivity (requiring dangerously high power densities that cause spatter and keyholing), while at 450-532 nm (blue-green) wavelengths copper absorptivity rises to 40-60% but titanium and nickel absorptivity decreases. When depositing a Cu-Ti or Cu-IN718 gradient, no single laser wavelength can simultaneously maintain optimal energy coupling for both phases, resulting in either under-fused copper inclusions or keyholed, evaporated titanium — both catastrophic for functional gradient quality. This fundamental constraint has blocked the commercial deployment of copper-containing FGM structures for thermal management applications in aerospace and electronics.
desc.paragraph_2_solution: The invention provides a multi-laser wavelength-selective coordination (MLWSC) system for PBF comprising: a first laser subsystem operating at 400-550 nm (blue-green range) optimized for high-absorptivity metals including copper, gold, and aluminum alloys; a second laser subsystem operating at 900-1100 nm (near-IR range) optimized for transition metals, nickel superalloys, and titanium alloys; and a wavelength-coordination controller that receives the local expected composition at each scan point from the gradient composition build schedule and dynamically assigns scan vectors to the appropriate laser subsystem or specifies a co-illumination pattern using both laser subsystems simultaneously for transition compositions. For transition composition zones (e.g., 30-70% Cu/Ti), the controller computes a weighted co-illumination parameter that proportionally activates both laser subsystems to deliver matched energy density to both phases, using a composition-dependent absorptivity model to calculate the required power split.
desc.paragraph_3_differentiators: SLM Solutions US11639028 covers coordination of multiple irradiation units in overlapping irradiation regions for productivity improvement in single-material builds — not for multi-wavelength absorptivity matching. The '808 nm multi-laser PBF thesis (University of Sheffield) demonstrates wavelength effects on single-material Ti6Al4V microstructure — not multi-wavelength multi-material coordination. The inventive distinction is the composition-driven dynamic wavelength assignment and co-illumination weighting — the controller's algorithm that computes the power split between wavelength-specific lasers as a function of local material composition. This constitutes a chokepoint claim: any manufacturer of copper-containing metal FGM parts via PBF must either use this coordination approach or accept prohibitive defect rates.

claim.preamble: A powder bed fusion apparatus for fabricating a workpiece from a mixture comprising at least one high-absorptivity metal having an optical absorptivity at 1064 nm of less than 20% and at least one low-absorptivity metal having an optical absorptivity at 1064 nm of greater than 50%, comprising:
limitations: (a) a first laser subsystem configured to emit radiation at a first wavelength in the range of 400-550 nm;
(b) a second laser subsystem configured to emit radiation at a second wavelength in the range of 900-1100 nm;

(c) an optical delivery system configured to direct radiation from the first laser subsystem and the second laser subsystem to a common scan field on a powder bed, wherein the delivery system is configured to co-illuminate a target location on the powder bed with radiation from both the first and second laser subsystems simultaneously;

(d) a wavelength-coordination controller operably coupled to the first laser subsystem, the second laser subsystem, and a gradient composition build schedule specifying the expected local composition at each scan location, the controller configured to: retrieve the expected local composition at a current scan location from the build schedule; compute a composition-dependent power split parameter specifying the relative contribution of the first and second laser subsystems to the total energy delivered at the scan location, wherein the power split is calculated using an absorptivity model that predicts the optical absorptivity of the mixed composition at the first and second wavelengths; and command the first and second laser subsystems to deliver power according to the computed power split to achieve a target energy density at the scan location that is within 10% of an optimal energy density for full densification of the local composition.

depClaims: The absorptivity model is a linear mixture rule model that interpolates between the known absorptivities of the pure component materials at the first and second wavelengths as a function of volume fraction, with empirical correction factors for binary composition-absorptivity non-linearity

The wavelength-coordination controller further adjusts the scan speed and hatch spacing as functions of the local composition and the computed absorptivity to maintain a consistent volumetric energy density regardless of composition-driven absorptivity variation

A spatter suppression module that reduces the power split to the first laser subsystem when the first laser subsystem power exceeds a keyholing threshold for the current high-absorptivity metal composition, and compensates by pre-heating with the second laser subsystem to improve absorption

The system further comprises a third laser subsystem at a wavelength matched to the peak absorptivity of a ceramic phase, enabling concurrent metal-ceramic multi-material processing with wavelength-selective laser assignment

A calibration procedure that measures the actual absorptivity of each candidate material composition at both wavelengths under representative build conditions and stores the results in the absorptivity model database

Application to a directed energy deposition (DED) system wherein the wavelength-selective laser sources illuminate a common blown-powder interaction zone, with composition determined by the relative flow rates of co-deposited dissimilar powder streams

A method for designing a copper-titanium or copper-nickel functionally graded heat exchanger component using the apparatus, wherein the gradient transitions from a copper-rich thermally conductive core to a titanium-rich structurally robust outer shell

ideas.ftoJ: Clear. The specific combination of dual-wavelength laser systems with composition-driven dynamic power split for multi-material metal PBF is not found in any identified incumbent patent. SLM Solutions' multi-laser patents are all single-wavelength single-material systems. Raytheon's multi-laser analysis tool covers build planning, not hardware wavelength selection.

ps.s101n: Apparatus claim with concrete hardware components. Fully patent-eligible.
ps.s102n: Dual-wavelength PBF laser system with composition-driven power split controller is novel — no anticipating reference identified.
ps.s103n: High non-obviousness: while wavelength-dependent absorptivity of metals is known, applying this knowledge in a real-time composition-driven dual-laser coordination system within a PBF apparatus requires non-obvious engineering solutions including: co-beam delivery optics design, cross-laser interference mitigation, composition-dependent absorptivity model calibration, and scan vector assignment algorithm — a PHOSITA would not combine these without undue experimentation.
ps.s112n: Highly enableable: laser hardware specifications are standard, absorptivity data for metals is published, and the control algorithm is straightforward to describe. Strong claim to enablement.

ideas.valM: Copper-containing FGMs are critical for thermal management in aerospace power electronics, EV motor windings, and next-gen heat exchangers. The copper AM market alone is $180M and growing rapidly. This patent would be unavoidable for any PBF manufacturer entering the copper-alloy or copper-FGM market. Highest estimated value in this portfolio due to breadth of application.

ideas.title: Selective Multi-Zone Post-Processing System for Residual Stress Relief at Dissimilar Material Interfaces in Additively Manufactured Components

desc.paragraph_1_problem: Multi-material AM components accumulate severe residual stresses at dissimilar material interfaces due to differential thermal contraction during solidification, CTE mismatch during cool-down, and differential phase transformation strains. Standard post-processing methods — furnace stress relief, hot isostatic pressing (HIP), and shot peening — apply uniform thermal or mechanical treatment to the entire component, which is destructive when applied to multi-material parts: a stress relief temperature optimal for the metallic phase (e.g., 900°C for Ti-6Al-4V) may cause thermal degradation, oxidation, or grain growth in the ceramic or polymer phase; shot peening optimized for the metal surface will damage ceramic coatings; and HIP pressures designed for one alloy create unacceptable deformation in a softer adjacent material. No existing patent addresses spatially selective stress relief specifically designed for the heterogeneous residual stress fields in multi-material AM components.
desc.paragraph_2_solution: The invention provides a selective multi-zone post-processing system comprising: (1) a residual stress characterization module using synchrotron X-ray diffraction (or lab-scale neutron diffraction or digital image correlation) to generate a three-dimensional residual stress map of the as-built multi-material component; (2) a zone assignment algorithm that segments the component into material-specific treatment zones based on the residual stress map and material phase boundaries, and for each zone computes an optimal localized treatment prescription (temperature, pressure, surface treatment energy) constrained by the thermal/mechanical tolerance of adjacent materials; and (3) a multi-zone actuator system — comprising a programmable laser heating array, a localized plasma/induction heating system, and/or a robotic ultrasonic peening system with composition-aware parameter control — that delivers the prescribed treatment to each zone independently without thermally or mechanically affecting adjacent zones. The system enables zone-specific annealing, targeted compressive stress introduction, or localized HIP treatment at length scales of 0.5-5 mm.
desc.paragraph_3_differentiators: Existing AM post-processing patents (furnace HIP for metal AM components, shot peening for fatigue improvement) treat the entire component uniformly. The inventive combination of residual stress mapping + zone assignment algorithm + multi-actuator selective treatment is novel for the multi-material AM context. The zone assignment algorithm — which must compute compatible treatment windows that respect the thermal and mechanical constraints of all materials within the thermal influence zone of each treatment point — is the core inventive element and is unavoidable for any certified multi-material AM repair or post-processing workflow. This patent would also cover selective post-processing of repaired multi-material aerospace components where only the repair zone (deposited new material) requires stress relief while the parent component must not be affected.

claim.preamble: A method for post-processing a multi-material additively manufactured component to reduce residual stress at one or more dissimilar material interfaces, comprising:
limitations: (a) generating a three-dimensional residual stress map of the component using a non-destructive measurement technique, the map specifying at least a principal stress tensor at a plurality of measurement points within the component encompassing at least one dissimilar material interface;

(b) executing a zone assignment algorithm that: segments the component into a plurality of treatment zones based on the residual stress map and the spatial distribution of material phases within the component; for each treatment zone, identifies a material composition and a thermal or mechanical tolerance profile of that zone and each adjacent zone within a thermal or mechanical influence radius of a treatment actuator; and computes a zone-specific treatment prescription for each treatment zone comprising at least a target temperature range, treatment duration, and maximum allowable mechanical strain, constrained such that the computed treatment does not cause thermal or mechanical conditions in any adjacent zone that exceed the tolerance profile of the adjacent zone's material composition;

(c) applying the zone-specific treatment prescription to each treatment zone using a programmable actuator configured to deliver localized thermal or mechanical energy to a region no larger than the spatial extent of the treatment zone, without applying the treatment to adjacent zones beyond the influence radius; and
(d) verifying, by measurement or simulation, that residual stresses at the dissimilar material interface are reduced by at least 20% compared to the as-built condition.

depClaims: The non-destructive measurement technique comprises synchrotron X-ray diffraction, neutron diffraction, or contour method residual stress measurement, providing stress data with spatial resolution of 0.5 mm or finer at the dissimilar material interface

The programmable actuator comprises a scanning laser heat treatment system with a beam diameter of 0.1-10 mm, configured to deliver localized annealing treatments to the metallic phase of the multi-material interface while maintaining the ceramic or polymer phase below its degradation temperature

The zone assignment algorithm incorporates a thermal influence model that predicts the temperature field generated by the actuator at a given treatment prescription, using composition-dependent thermal conductivity values from the materials database, to ensure that the constraint on adjacent zone temperatures is enforced

Application to selective laser shock peening at metal-ceramic interfaces, wherein a high-energy pulsed laser generates compressive shock waves in the metallic phase at the interface without fracturing the ceramic phase

A robotic delivery embodiment wherein a robotic arm carrying an ultrasonic peening device navigates the component surface guided by the zone assignment map, applying peening treatment parameters specific to each surface material zone

Integration with a repair workflow for field-repaired multi-material aerospace components: the residual stress characterization is performed on the as-repaired component and the selective post-processing ensures the repair deposit is stress-relieved while the parent material heat-affected zone is treated separately

A method of qualifying a selective post-processing procedure under aerospace certification requirements by documenting the residual stress map before and after treatment, demonstrating compliance with a target stress state specified in a design-allowable database

ideas.ftoJ: Clear. Selective zone-specific stress relief with a constraint-aware zone assignment algorithm for multi-material AM components is not found in any identified incumbent portfolio. GE and Raytheon hold process-specific AM patents but none address the specific multi-material zone assignment and constraint-aware actuator coordination described here.

ps.s101n: Method claim tied to physical process steps (measurement, actuation, thermal/mechanical treatment) on a physical object. Fully patent-eligible.
ps.s102n: The zone assignment algorithm + multi-actuator selective delivery + multi-material constraint computation is novel. Slight risk: broad claims on 'localized heat treatment' could face 102 challenges from laser annealing prior art; claims must specify the multi-material constraint computation as a distinguishing element.
ps.s103n: Non-obvious: a PHOSITA would know localized laser annealing and would know HIP, but would not obviously create a multi-zone constraint-aware system for multi-material parts without motivation from this specific multi-material AM context. The constraint calculation that ensures treatment of one zone does not degrade adjacent incompatible materials is the non-obvious inventive step.
ps.s112n: Requires specification of the thermal influence model, zone assignment algorithm, constraint database format, and at least one worked example for a specific metal-ceramic material pair. Achievable.

ideas.valM: Post-processing market for AM components is $300M+ and growing. Selective stress relief is required for certification of multi-material aerospace repair components — a high-value niche. Licensing potential to aerospace MRO providers, AM post-processing service bureaus, and HIP system OEMs. Also applicable to nuclear and defense component repair programs.

ideas.title: Hardware-Enforced Differential Privacy Engine for Federated Learning Edge Devices via Trusted Execution Environment-Bounded Noise Injection

desc.paragraph_1: Problem: Current differential privacy (DP) implementations in federated learning rely on software-layer noise injection (Gaussian or Laplace mechanisms applied to gradient tensors before transmission). This software approach is vulnerable to side-channel attacks, privileged-access bypasses, and malicious firmware that can suppress or reduce noise injection — rendering the privacy guarantee unenforceable. Regulators and enterprise customers increasingly require hardware-attested privacy guarantees, particularly in healthcare (HIPAA) and financial services (GLBA), where a software-only DP claim is unauditable.
desc.paragraph_2: Solution: A specialized hardware subsystem — instantiated as a discrete privacy processing unit (PPU) or as a hardened IP block within an edge AI accelerator (e.g., integrated into a neural processing unit or on-chip AI ASIC) — executes noise sampling, calibration, and injection entirely within a Trusted Execution Environment (TEE). The PPU maintains an unforgeable privacy budget ledger (ε/δ accounting) in tamper-resistant memory, enforcing that gradient tensors passing to the communication stack have been noise-injected at or above the configured DP threshold. A hardware attestation certificate (generated via device-specific private key in a secure element) is co-transmitted with each gradient update, allowing the FL aggregation server to cryptographically verify that privacy was enforced at the originating device without trusting the device's OS or application stack.
desc.paragraph_3: Differentiators: Unlike NVIDIA Clara's software-level DP (sparse vector technique) or Apple's on-device DP (software), this invention anchors the privacy guarantee in silicon. The hardware ledger prevents budget exhaustion attacks where adversaries iteratively query a device to reconstruct training data. The attestation-coupled gradient approach creates a novel 'privacy-provenance' chain auditable under HIPAA/GDPR without exposing raw gradients. The architecture is compatible with heterogeneous edge device classes (IoT sensors, medical devices, automotive ECUs) via a standardized PPU interface specification.

claim.preamble: A privacy processing unit (PPU) for federated learning, the PPU comprising:
limitations: (a) a secure enclave implemented in hardware, configured to execute differential privacy noise injection on gradient tensors produced by a local machine learning model training process, wherein the noise injection executes exclusively within the secure enclave without exposure to a host operating system or application layer;

(b) a tamper-resistant privacy budget register storing a cumulative privacy expenditure value (ε, δ), the register being writable only by authenticated instructions executing within the secure enclave;

(c) a noise calibration engine configured to dynamically select noise magnitude based on local sensitivity analysis of gradient tensors and the remaining privacy budget stored in the privacy budget register;

(d) an attestation module configured to generate a cryptographically signed certificate attesting to the noise injection parameters, privacy budget consumption, and gradient hash, using a device-unique private key stored in a hardware security module co-located with the PPU;
(e) a gradient output interface configured to transmit noise-injected gradient tensors and the signed attestation certificate as a unified payload to a federated learning aggregation server.

depClaims: PPU instantiated as an IP block within a neural processing unit (NPU) or AI ASIC for mobile/embedded platforms
Privacy budget ledger with automatic FL round-based reset and configurable per-layer sensitivity tracking
Remote attestation protocol enabling the FL aggregation server to reject gradient updates from PPUs with tampered or expired attestation certificates
Side-channel hardening of the noise sampling RNG against timing and power analysis attacks
Multi-tenant PPU operation with per-federated-task privacy budget isolation for devices participating in multiple simultaneous FL federations
Integration with TEE frameworks (ARM TrustZone, Intel TDX, RISC-V PMP) via a standardized PPU abstraction API
Adaptive privacy budget management using a Renyi DP accountant implemented in hardware for tighter privacy bounds

refs.ref: McMahan et al., 'Deep Learning with Differential Privacy' (CCS 2016) — Google
refs.diff: Software-layer DP with no hardware attestation; vulnerable to OS-level bypass; invention anchors enforcement in silicon TEE

refs.ref: NVIDIA Clara Train SDK — sparse vector technique DP (software, NVFlare framework)
refs.diff: NVIDIA's DP is application-layer code with no hardware attestation certificate; invention requires hardware-signed privacy provenance

refs.ref: Apple US20240202594A1 — Private training of artificial intelligence (edge device FL)
refs.diff: Apple's patent covers encrypted weight updates but does NOT disclose hardware-enforced DP with tamper-resistant budget ledger or attestation certificates

refs.ref: US12335379B1 — Privacy-preserving transformer with encrypted dimensionality reduction
refs.diff: Focuses on input layer dimensionality reduction; does not address gradient-level DP hardware enforcement or FL-specific privacy budget accounting

ideas.ftoJ: No identified patents claiming hardware-enforced differential privacy with TEE-bound noise injection AND attestation certificate co-transmission for FL. Apple, Google, and NVIDIA all implement DP in software. IBM's Byzantine-robust FL patents do not address DP hardware. ARM TrustZone and Intel TDX patents cover general TEE architecture, not FL-specific DP enforcement. Recommend claim language specifying 'tamper-resistant privacy budget register' and 'attestation certificate co-transmitted with gradient payload' to maximize distance.

ps.s101n: Apparatus/hardware claims for a physical PPU chip component with specific structural elements (secure enclave, tamper-resistant register, attestation module, hardware security module) are patent-eligible under Alice Step 2B — the hardware integration constitutes a concrete technical improvement over abstract software-layer DP. Recommend leading with apparatus claims to anchor §101 eligibility.
ps.s102n: No identified prior art combining: (1) hardware-resident TEE for FL gradient DP enforcement + (2) tamper-resistant privacy budget ledger + (3) cryptographic attestation certificate co-transmitted with gradients. Each element individually has antecedents, but the specific combination as an FL-dedicated hardware subsystem is novel.
ps.s103n: PHOSITA would not obviously combine TEE/HSM technology (telecom/banking security domain) with FL-specific DP budget accounting and gradient attestation without specific motivation from FL's unique threat model (gradient inversion attacks, budget exhaustion). The specific attestation-coupled gradient output interface creates a non-obvious system architecture.
ps.s112n: Highly enabled — ARM TrustZone APIs, Intel SGX SDK, hardware HSM specifications, and Renyi DP accountant mathematics are well-documented. PHOSITA with FL and hardware security background can readily implement. Recommend including implementation examples using ARM CryptoCell-712 or Secure Enclave co-processor.

ideas.valM: Hardware IP licensing (per-device royalty) across medical device, automotive ECU, and industrial IoT FL deployments. Assuming 50M+ FL-capable edge devices with DP requirements (medical wearables, AV sensor units, smart factory PLCs) at $0.30-$0.80/device royalty. Strategic value as blocking IP against software-DP competitors seeking regulatory certification in GDPR/HIPAA markets.

ideas.title: Cryptographically Verifiable Federated Unlearning System with Zero-Knowledge Proof of Contribution Erasure

desc.paragraph_1: Problem: GDPR Article 17 (right to erasure) and CCPA require that organizations delete a data subject's information upon request, including any model 'knowledge' derived from that data. In federated learning, a client's training contribution is embedded in the global model's weights through multiple rounds of aggregation, making simple data deletion insufficient. Existing federated unlearning (FU) approaches (FedEraser, FUKD, model-contrastive unlearning) require computationally expensive retraining or provide only approximate forgetting with no cryptographic verifiability — meaning an organization cannot prove to a regulator or auditor that erasure has occurred to a legally defensible standard.
desc.paragraph_2: Solution: A federated unlearning system wherein each FL training round generates a cryptographic commitment (e.g., Pedersen commitment or vector commitment) to the client's gradient contribution, stored on an append-only audit log (distributed ledger or secure append-only database). Upon an erasure request, the system executes a targeted unlearning algorithm that applies client-specific gradient negation (calibrated using the stored commitments) to reverse the target client's contribution from the global model. A zero-knowledge proof (ZKP) — specifically a zk-SNARK — is generated attesting that: (i) the unlearning transformation was correctly applied to reverse the committed contributions, (ii) no other client's contributions were altered, and (iii) the resulting model parameters satisfy a statistical indistinguishability bound from a model trained without the target client. This ZKP is publishable to regulators as cryptographic proof of GDPR erasure compliance without revealing model weights or other client data.
desc.paragraph_3: Differentiators: All prior FU systems (FedEraser, Liu et al. 2020, MICCAI 2024 FCU framework, OpenReview BMT) focus on algorithmic efficiency of forgetting but provide zero cryptographic verifiability. This invention uniquely couples gradient commitment accounting during training with ZKP-based erasure attestation — transforming federated unlearning from a best-effort computational process into an auditable, legally defensible compliance operation. The approach is applicable to any FL aggregation protocol (FedAvg, FedProx, SCAFFOLD) and is architecture-agnostic. The ZKP generation can be offloaded to a specialized proof-generation service, making it practical for resource-constrained edge deployments.

claim.preamble: A computer-implemented method for verifiable federated unlearning in a federated learning system comprising a plurality of client devices and an aggregation server, the method comprising:
limitations: (a) during each federated learning training round, generating a cryptographic commitment to each client device's gradient contribution and storing the commitment in an append-only contribution audit log maintained by the aggregation server;
(b) receiving a data erasure request identifying a target client device whose contributions are to be removed from a trained global model;

(c) executing a contribution-reversal unlearning algorithm on the global model using the stored cryptographic commitments associated with the target client device to produce an unlearned global model;

(d) generating a zero-knowledge proof attesting that: (i) the unlearning transformation correctly reversed all committed contributions of the target client device, (ii) contributions of non-target client devices were not modified beyond a predetermined statistical bound, and (iii) the unlearned global model satisfies a forgetting criterion defined by a differential privacy-based indistinguishability metric;
(e) publishing the zero-knowledge proof to a regulatory compliance interface accessible to authorized regulatory auditors without exposing model weights or non-target client data.

depClaims: Commitment scheme selection: Pedersen commitments for additively homomorphic gradient summation tracking; KZG polynomial commitments for batch proof generation
ZKP circuit optimization for gradient unlearning using Groth16 or PLONK proof systems to reduce proof generation time
Incremental unlearning for partial data erasure (specific data samples rather than full client removal) with granular commitment sub-records
Automated GDPR compliance report generation incorporating the ZKP as a machine-readable erasure certificate
Multi-client batch unlearning where multiple simultaneous erasure requests are processed with a single batched ZKP
Integration with on-chain smart contract storage for the contribution audit log and ZKP verification, enabling decentralized regulatory compliance
Continual learning compatibility — unlearning protocol that maintains model performance on retained data through constrained optimization post-unlearning

refs.ref: FedEraser (Liu et al., 2020) and subsequent federated unlearning literature
refs.diff: All prior FU work addresses computational efficiency of forgetting; none disclose cryptographic commitment tracking during training or ZKP-based erasure attestation

refs.ref: MICCAI 2024 — 'Enable the Right to be Forgotten with Federated Client Unlearning' (FCU framework)
refs.diff: MCU + FGMP approach provides approximate forgetting via frequency-domain filtering; no cryptographic verifiability; targets medical imaging but no ZKP compliance certification

refs.ref: OpenReview 2024 — 'Achieving Exact Federated Unlearning with Improved Performance' (BMT method)
refs.diff: Bi-Models Training provides exact unlearning but no cryptographic proof of erasure; regulator cannot verify compliance from BMT output alone

refs.ref: Nature 2024 WFB — blockchain-based FL watermarking framework
refs.diff: WFB uses blockchain for watermark/ownership tracking, not contribution commitment or erasure ZKP; invention applies commitment-ZKP paradigm to data erasure compliance rather than IP ownership

ideas.ftoJ: No identified patents combining gradient commitment schemes during FL training with ZKP-based erasure attestation for regulatory compliance. IBM's FL patents (US11948096B2, US12175338B2) address Byzantine robustness, not unlearning verification. Google FL patent portfolio focuses on training efficiency. ZKP technology patents (Matter Labs, StarkWare) operate in blockchain/transaction domain without FL-specific application. The regulatory compliance interface for GDPR attestation is entirely unpatented terrain.

ps.s101n: Method claims with concrete technical steps (commitment generation, ZKP computation, regulatory interface publication) are directed to a specific technical problem in distributed computing. ZKP generation is a concrete mathematical transformation applied to model parameters — not an abstract idea. Alice Step 2A Prong 2 satisfied by the specific combination with an FL system. Frame as 'improvement to distributed machine learning systems' to strengthen §101 posture.
ps.s102n: Highly novel combination: cryptographic gradient commitments + ZKP of erasure correctness + regulatory compliance interface. No prior art found disclosing this triad. Individual ZKP systems, FL training, and unlearning algorithms are known but their specific combination for GDPR-certified erasure is novel.
ps.s103n: PHOSITA would not combine FL gradient aggregation systems with ZKP proof generation systems for erasure attestation without recognizing the GDPR regulatory requirement as the motivating bridge. The ZKP circuit design specific to gradient contribution reversal is technically non-trivial and requires cross-domain expertise (FL + ZK cryptography).
ps.s112n: ZKP systems (circom, libsnark, bellman) and FL frameworks (NVFlare, Flower, FedML) are well-documented. Commitment scheme mathematics are standard cryptography. PHOSITA can implement with full specification provided in patent. Recommend including pseudocode for the contribution-reversal algorithm and ZKP circuit specification.

ideas.valM: Licensing to FL platform providers (NVIDIA NVFlare, IBM Federated Learning, PySyft) seeking GDPR certification. Enterprise SaaS value from regulatory compliance tooling in EU/UK markets. Healthcare and financial services FL deployments subject to GDPR must implement verifiable unlearning — addressable market of $500M+ in compliance tooling by 2028. Strategic blocking patent against GDPR-compliance-certified FL products.

ideas.title: Decentralized Byzantine-Fault-Tolerant Federated Learning Aggregation with Provenance-Anchored Gradient Forensics

desc.paragraph_1: Problem: Byzantine attacks — where malicious FL participants submit strategically crafted gradient updates to poison the global model — represent the most dangerous threat to production FL deployments. Existing Byzantine-robust methods (Krum, Bulyan, FLTrust, and IBM's US12175338B2 vulnerability-weighted aggregation) rely on a trusted central aggregator as a single point of failure: if the aggregator is compromised, all Byzantine defenses collapse. Furthermore, current methods lack an auditable forensic trail — when a poisoning attack is detected post-deployment, system operators cannot reconstruct which participant's updates caused the corruption, precluding legal remedies and regulatory incident reporting.
desc.paragraph_2: Solution: A decentralized Byzantine-fault-tolerant (BFT) FL aggregation architecture wherein gradient updates from participating clients are gossiped through a peer-to-peer validation mesh before aggregation. Each participating node independently executes a lightweight anomaly detector (based on cosine similarity, loss surface curvature analysis, and statistical outlier scoring) and contributes its validation verdict to a BFT consensus protocol (specifically, a variant of HotStuff or Tendermint adapted for gradient validation). A gradient provenance ledger records a hash-linked chain of all submitted gradient updates, their validation scores, and the consensus decision for each FL round. This ledger is cryptographically tamper-evident (Merkle-tree structured) and can be queried post-deployment to forensically identify the round, participant, and specific gradient characteristics associated with a detected poisoning event.
desc.paragraph_3: Differentiators: IBM's US12175338B2 introduces vulnerability-weighted aggregation at a central aggregator but retains the single-point-of-failure architecture. The arXiv AAMAS'24 decentralized Byzantine FRL paper operates in federated reinforcement learning and lacks the gradient provenance ledger. This invention uniquely combines: (1) serverless BFT consensus for gradient validation, (2) decentralized aggregation eliminating single-point trust, and (3) a tamper-evident forensic ledger enabling post-hoc attribution — meeting both security and regulatory incident-reporting requirements (EU AI Act Article 62 mandating serious incident logging for high-risk AI).

claim.preamble: A decentralized federated learning aggregation system comprising:
limitations: (a) a plurality of federated learning participant nodes, each node configured to: (i) train a local machine learning model on locally-held data; (ii) generate a gradient update; and (iii) broadcast the gradient update to a peer-to-peer validation mesh comprising at least a quorum of other participant nodes;

(b) a gradient validation module executing on each participant node, configured to evaluate received gradient updates using a multi-criterion anomaly scoring function comprising cosine similarity deviation, loss surface curvature divergence, and statistical distribution outlier detection, and to produce a signed validation verdict;

(c) a Byzantine-fault-tolerant consensus protocol engine configured to reach agreement among a quorum of participant nodes on the set of gradient updates to include in each aggregation round, requiring a threshold fraction of signed validation verdicts classifying a gradient update as non-anomalous before inclusion;

(d) a gradient provenance ledger comprising a Merkle-tree-structured append-only data structure recording, for each aggregation round: participant node identifiers, gradient update hashes, individual validation scores, consensus decisions, and aggregated model checkpoint hashes;

(e) a forensic query interface configured to receive a model integrity challenge and return a verifiable proof-of-provenance identifying the specific gradient updates, participant nodes, and aggregation rounds contributing to a specified model checkpoint.

depClaims: BFT consensus variant using an asynchronous Byzantine agreement protocol tolerant to up to f < n/3 malicious participants without liveness assumptions
Lightweight validation module for resource-constrained edge nodes using compressed gradient fingerprinting rather than full gradient comparison
Integration of provenance ledger with EU AI Act Article 62 incident reporting API for automated regulatory disclosure
Cross-federation gradient reputation scores — persistent reputation scores for participants across multiple FL training sessions
Sybil-resistance mechanism requiring verifiable compute proofs (proof-of-work or TEE attestation) from participant nodes before validation vote acceptance
Adaptive BFT quorum size that dynamically adjusts based on detected network partition conditions or suspected Byzantine fraction
Forensic ledger export in standardized format (e.g., JSON-LD with provenance ontology) for third-party security audit tooling

refs.ref: IBM US12175338B2 — Byzantine-robust FL with vulnerability-weighted gradient aggregation
refs.diff: IBM patent retains centralized aggregator; invention eliminates single-point-of-failure through P2P BFT consensus. IBM patent has no forensic provenance ledger. Distinct architectural approach.

refs.ref: IBM US11948096B2 — Adaptively adjusting participant influence in FL updates
refs.diff: IBM patent focuses on influence weight adjustment at a central aggregator; invention distributes the entire validation and aggregation process across participant nodes

refs.ref: arXiv:2401.03489 — Decentralized Byzantine fault-tolerant federated policy gradient (AAMAS'24)
refs.diff: Academic paper covers federated reinforcement learning; does not disclose gradient provenance ledger or forensic query interface; no patent filed; invention extends to general FL and adds regulatory-grade auditability

refs.ref: SecurePDM (IECO 2023) — BFT FL for predictive maintenance using PCA anomaly detection
refs.diff: Industry paper focuses on centralized PCA-based anomaly detection; retains central aggregator; no decentralized consensus or tamper-evident provenance ledger

ideas.ftoJ: IBM holds blocking IP on centralized Byzantine-robust aggregation (US12175338B2). However, the decentralized P2P BFT consensus approach and the forensic provenance ledger are not covered by IBM's claims. Recommend claim language explicitly specifying 'without a central aggregation server' and 'participant nodes acting as both validators and aggregators' to maximize distance from IBM's central-aggregator claims. Minor YELLOW risk from IBM's broad aggregation claims — design-around is achievable through the decentralized architecture.

ps.s101n: System claims with concrete hardware components (participant nodes, consensus protocol engine, provenance ledger as a physical data structure) are patent-eligible. The forensic query interface and Merkle-tree provenance ledger are specific technical implementations, not abstract ideas. Frame as 'distributed computing system with security-enhanced components.'
ps.s102n: The specific combination of P2P BFT consensus for gradient validation + Merkle-tree provenance ledger + forensic query interface in FL context is novel. Individual elements (BFT protocols, Merkle trees, FL) are known but not combined in this manner.
ps.s103n: Combining HotStuff/Tendermint BFT (blockchain domain) with FL gradient validation requires non-obvious cross-domain synthesis. The gradient-specific validation criteria (cosine similarity, loss curvature) are FL-specific and would not be obvious to a blockchain/distributed systems PHOSITA.
ps.s112n: HotStuff, Tendermint, and Merkle tree implementations are well-documented. FL gradient aggregation is fully specified in literature. Cosine similarity and statistical outlier scoring for Byzantine detection are well-established. Full enablement achievable.

ideas.valM: Licensing to FL infrastructure providers, financial services FL consortia, and defense/intelligence FL deployments where Byzantine resilience + audit trails are mandatory. EU AI Act compliance tooling revenue stream. Potential standards contribution to ISO/IEC FL security standards (ISO/IEC 14764-adjacent).

ideas.title: Personalized Federated Learning System with Architecture-Heterogeneous Knowledge Distillation and Capability-Aware Model Morphing

desc.paragraph_1: Problem: Standard federated learning requires all participating devices to train the same model architecture (homogeneous federation), which is architecturally infeasible in real-world deployments where devices span orders-of-magnitude differences in compute capability (e.g., a hospital's GPU server, a clinical tablet, and a wearable biosensor cannot run the same model). Existing heterogeneous FL approaches (FedMD, FedDF, pFLFE, and Huawei's WO2022013879A1 heterogeneous labels approach) allow different label spaces but still require compatible intermediate representations or shared public datasets, creating privacy and data governance complications.
desc.paragraph_2: Solution: A federated learning orchestration system implementing capability-aware model morphing (CAMM), wherein the FL server maintains a morphing graph — a parameterized mapping between neural architecture families (e.g., ResNet-50 ↔ MobileNetV3 ↔ SqueezeNet) stored as learned transformation matrices. When a device registers for federation, it reports its hardware capability profile (FLOPs budget, memory ceiling, latency target); the server assigns a target architecture tier and a morphing trajectory. Local model updates are exchanged not as raw gradients but as architecture-normalized knowledge vectors (derived via teacher-student distillation with the server's morphing graph as the teacher), enabling cross-architecture gradient-equivalent information transfer without any shared dataset. Personalization is achieved by allowing devices to retain architecture-tier-specific layers while sharing knowledge-vector-equivalent representations of shared semantic features.
desc.paragraph_3: Differentiators: WO2022013879A1 (Huawei, heterogeneous labels) exchanges softmax probabilities over a shared public dataset — this invention requires no shared dataset (critical for strict data isolation domains like healthcare). pFLFE (arXiv 2024) requires all clients to use compatible backbone architectures; this invention supports fundamentally different architecture families via the morphing graph. The morphing graph itself is a novel learnable component of the FL system, trained during federation warm-up on a synthetic capability-diversity dataset generated by the server. This eliminates the dependency on real shared data while achieving architecture-agnostic knowledge transfer.

claim.preamble: A federated learning system for training machine learning models across heterogeneous device architectures, the system comprising:
limitations: (a) a federation orchestration server comprising a morphing graph module storing a parameterized mapping between a plurality of neural network architecture families, wherein each mapping comprises learned transformation matrices trained to convert intermediate feature representations between architecture families;

(b) a capability profiling interface configured to receive, from each participating client device, a hardware capability profile specifying at least a compute budget, memory ceiling, and target inference latency, and to assign the client device to an architecture tier from a predefined tier hierarchy based on the hardware capability profile;

(c) a knowledge vector extraction module configured to convert local model updates from participating client devices into architecture-normalized knowledge vectors by applying the morphing graph's transformation matrices corresponding to the client device's architecture tier;

(d) a cross-architecture aggregation engine configured to aggregate knowledge vectors from participating client devices across different architecture tiers into a unified semantic feature representation without requiring a shared dataset accessible to the aggregation server or the client devices;

(e) a personalization module configured to distribute tier-specific model updates to each participating client device, wherein the updates comprise shared semantic feature representations combined with architecture-tier-specific layer parameters retained on the client device.

depClaims: Morphing graph trained via neural architecture search (NAS) on a synthetic capability-diversity benchmark generated by the server without requiring real data
Dynamic architecture tier assignment based on real-time device load monitoring (battery level, thermal state, concurrent workload) rather than static capability profiles
Privacy-preserving capability profile reporting using locally differentially private mechanisms to prevent server inference of device model/hardware identity
Asynchronous personalized FL where devices with different architecture tiers participate on different update frequencies without round synchronization requirements
Federated meta-learning (MAML-style) integration where the morphing graph serves as the meta-model for few-shot personalization on new device classes
Knowledge vector compression using product quantization to reduce communication overhead of architecture-normalized representations by 10-50x
Cross-silo personalization where different organizations maintain different architecture tiers, with the morphing graph serving as IP-neutral knowledge bridge

refs.ref: Huawei WO2022013879A1 — FL using heterogeneous labels (softmax probability exchange)
refs.diff: Huawei's approach requires a shared public dataset for clients to evaluate; invention requires no shared dataset. Huawei covers label heterogeneity; invention covers architecture heterogeneity.

refs.ref: pFLFE (arXiv:2407.00462) — Cross-silo personalized FL via feature enhancement
refs.diff: pFLFE requires compatible backbone architectures for feature enhancement; invention supports fundamentally different architecture families. pFLFE is unpublished at patent level — academic prior art risk is low for apparatus claims.

refs.ref: FedDF (Ensemble Distillation for Robust Model Fusion) — Lin et al., NeurIPS 2020
refs.diff: FedDF uses unlabeled public data for distillation; invention uses a learnable morphing graph trained on synthetic data, eliminating the public dataset requirement

refs.ref: FedMD (Li & Wang, NeurIPS 2019) — FL with model distillation
refs.diff: FedMD requires all clients to evaluate a shared public dataset; invention's morphing graph eliminates this dependency and supports fundamentally heterogeneous architectures

ideas.ftoJ: No identified patents claiming architecture-heterogeneous FL with a learnable morphing graph and no shared dataset requirement. Huawei's WO2022013879A1 is distinguished by shared-dataset dependency. Apple's on-device learning patents are homogeneous architecture. NVIDIA NVFlare is homogeneous. The morphing graph as a learned FL system component is a novel structural element not found in incumbent portfolios.

ps.s101n: System claims with concrete components (morphing graph module, capability profiling interface, knowledge vector extraction module) are patent-eligible. The morphing graph as a physical data structure (stored transformation matrices) is not an abstract idea. Frame as 'improvement to federated learning systems enabling cross-architecture operation.'
ps.s102n: The morphing graph as a learned, parameterized architectural translation component within FL, combined with capability-aware tier assignment and shared-dataset-free cross-architecture aggregation, is novel. Individual elements (NAS, distillation, FL) are known but not combined as described.
ps.s103n: The insight that morphing graphs eliminate shared dataset requirements — by encoding architectural translation as a learnable system component rather than empirical correspondence on real data — is non-obvious. NAS researchers and FL researchers would not trivially arrive at this combination.
ps.s112n: Transformer-based knowledge distillation, NAS frameworks (DARTS, ProxylessNAS), and FL training protocols are fully documented. The morphing graph's training procedure on synthetic capability-diversity benchmarks requires additional specification detail in the disclosure.

ideas.valM: Platform licensing to FL framework vendors and cloud providers offering heterogeneous-device FL-as-a-service (AWS SageMaker FL, Azure ML FL, NVIDIA NVFlare). Healthcare IoT deployments spanning hospital servers to wearables. Industrial IoT where PLCs, MES servers, and handheld scanners must federate. Moderate licensing surface due to technical complexity of implementation.

ideas.title: FL Model Provenance Watermarking System with Contribution-Proportional Embedding and Adversarial Robustness Verification

desc.paragraph_1: Problem: In commercial federated learning deployments, the trained global model has substantial economic value — but there is no mechanism to enforce intellectual property rights over FL-derived models or to fairly attribute and protect the IP contributions of individual participating organizations. A hospital that contributes three years of rare-disease training data to an FL consortium may find its contribution embedded in a commercial model without compensation or attribution. Existing FL watermarking approaches (FedIPR, WFB blockchain framework from Nature 2024) embed static watermarks owned by a single party or coordinator, not proportional multi-party watermarks reflecting each participant's contribution magnitude — and none have been optimized for adversarial robustness against model compression, fine-tuning, and quantization attacks common in model commercialization pipelines.
desc.paragraph_2: Solution: A multi-party federated watermarking system wherein each participating client embeds a contribution-proportional watermark into the global model during FL training, with watermark strength calibrated to a contribution metric (data volume × data quality score × model improvement delta). The watermarks are embedded using a novel gradient-space steganographic technique: each client's local gradient update is perturbed by a watermark signal derived from a client-specific cryptographic key and the contribution metric, such that the perturbation is below the gradient noise floor of differential privacy but is recoverable by a watermark extractor with the client's key. A robustness verification module periodically tests watermark extractability against a battery of adversarial transformations (pruning at multiple sparsity levels, quantization to INT8/INT4, knowledge distillation to a student model, and adversarial fine-tuning) and alerts the contribution accounting system when watermark strength falls below a forensic recovery threshold.
