desc.paragraph_3: Differentiators: WFB (Nature 2024) uses a coordinator-controlled blockchain for watermark storage and does not address contribution-proportional multi-party watermarking. FedIPR embeds client watermarks but without contribution-proportional calibration or systematic adversarial robustness verification. This invention's gradient-space steganographic embedding is uniquely compatible with existing DP mechanisms — the watermark perturbation is designed to be statistically indistinguishable from DP noise, preventing a model inspector from detecting watermark presence. The multi-party attribution enables a new class of FL business model: data contribution compensation structures enforced by cryptographic IP attribution.

claim.preamble: A federated learning system comprising watermark embedding and provenance verification components, the system comprising:
limitations: (a) a contribution measurement module configured to compute, for each participating client device in each training round, a contribution metric based on at least: a data volume measure, a data quality score derived from gradient alignment with a held-out validation set, and a model improvement delta measured as change in global model loss attributable to the client's gradient update;

(b) a gradient-space watermark encoder configured to generate, for each participating client, a client-specific watermark signal derived from a client cryptographic key and the contribution metric, and to add the watermark signal to the client's gradient update as a steganographic perturbation whose magnitude is calibrated to be within a predetermined fraction of the gradient's differential privacy noise floor;

(c) an aggregation interface configured to receive watermark-embedded gradient updates from participating clients and aggregate them into a global model using a standard federated averaging protocol, whereby the watermark signals accumulate in the global model parameters proportional to each client's contribution metric;

(d) a watermark extractor configured to recover, from a candidate model suspected of being derived from the federated global model, the watermark signals of one or more contributing clients using their respective cryptographic keys;

(e) an adversarial robustness verification module configured to periodically apply a battery of model transformation attacks to the global model, measure watermark recovery rate post-attack, and generate a forensic recoverability report for each participating client.

depClaims: Contribution metric normalization using Shapley value approximation to ensure fair multi-party attribution under non-IID data distributions
Watermark signal design using spread-spectrum encoding to enhance robustness against pruning and quantization attacks
Watermark key escrow protocol using threshold secret sharing (t-of-n) among multiple trusted parties for long-term attribution preservation
Integration with smart contract-based compensation system that automatically triggers royalty payments when watermark extraction confirms model provenance
Watermark embedding calibrated to survive knowledge distillation by embedding in low-frequency components of model weight matrices (SVD decomposition steganography)
Blind watermark verification mode allowing third-party forensic auditors to verify watermark presence without access to client cryptographic keys
Gradient-space watermark interference analysis preventing watermark signals from multiple clients from destructively interfering during aggregation

refs.ref: Nature 2024 WFB — Blockchain-empowered watermarking framework for FL ownership verification
refs.diff: WFB uses coordinator-assigned watermarks with no contribution-proportional calibration; uses backdoor-based triggers visible to adversaries; no adversarial robustness battery testing; no gradient-space steganography compatible with DP

refs.ref: FedIPR — Universal watermarking in FL (parameter-based + backdoor-based)
refs.diff: FedIPR covers single-party or uniform multi-party watermarks without contribution-proportional weighting; no robustness verification battery; no calibration relative to DP noise floor

refs.ref: Digital watermarking patents (Digimarc, ContentAuth) in image/media domain
refs.diff: All media watermarking patents apply to content files, not neural network gradient tensors. The gradient-space steganographic embedding technique is domain-novel.

refs.ref: Shapley value FL contribution measurement (Ghorbani & Zou, ICML 2019)
refs.diff: Existing Shapley FL contribution work focuses on incentive mechanisms without watermark embedding or IP attribution; invention uses Shapley as a calibration input for watermark strength, not as a standalone contribution metric

ideas.ftoJ: Gradient-space steganographic watermarking calibrated to DP noise floor with contribution-proportional multi-party embedding is not found in any identified patent. Existing watermarking patents (Digimarc, Adobe) cover media content. FL-specific watermarking literature (FedIPR, WFB) is primarily in academic/conference venues with no dominant patent holders. The contribution metric + smart contract compensation linkage is a novel commercial application.

ps.s101n: System claims with concrete physical components (gradient encoder, watermark extractor as computational hardware modules, adversarial robustness verification module running specific attack simulations) are patent-eligible. The steganographic embedding in gradient tensors is a concrete technical process. Frame as 'improvement to FL systems for IP protection and attribution.'
ps.s102n: The specific combination of: gradient-space steganography + DP noise floor calibration + contribution-proportional multi-party embedding + adversarial robustness battery is novel. No prior art identified covering this combination.
ps.s103n: The key non-obvious insight — calibrating watermark perturbation to be within the DP noise floor to simultaneously achieve privacy and IP protection — requires cross-domain expertise that a PHOSITA in either DP or watermarking would not trivially possess.
ps.s112n: Spread-spectrum encoding, SVD decomposition, and gradient manipulation are well-documented. Shapley value computation for FL is implemented in multiple open-source frameworks. Adversarial robustness testing (pruning, quantization) procedures are standard. Full enablement achievable with detailed disclosure of watermark signal construction algorithm.

ideas.valM: Licensing to FL platform operators and data marketplace platforms enabling contribution-based compensation for training data providers (healthcare data cooperatives, financial data consortia). Novel business model enablement — data cooperatives licensing FL-trained models can use watermark extraction to enforce attribution in sublicensing agreements. Potential standards contribution to W3C Data Provenance group for AI training data attribution.

ideas.title: Adaptive Semantic Gradient Compression Protocol for Bandwidth-Constrained Federated Learning with Cross-Client Redundancy Elimination

desc.paragraph_1: Problem: Communication overhead is the dominant bottleneck in federated learning at scale — particularly in healthcare IoT, industrial edge, and automotive telematics deployments where FL clients communicate over constrained wireless channels (LTE Cat-M1, sub-GHz ISM, vehicle-to-infrastructure V2X links) with strict latency budgets. Existing gradient compression approaches (Deep Gradient Compression, STC, TopK sparsification, quantization) operate independently on each client's gradient without exploiting the semantic redundancy that exists across clients in the same FL training round. The IJCAI 2024 HGC paper identifies cross-client redundancy but does not patent it and operates only on synchronized uplink/downlink without semantic awareness.
desc.paragraph_2: Solution: A semantic gradient compression (SGC) protocol that treats each FL training round as a distributed source coding problem. Before compression, each client extracts a semantic gradient signature — a compact representation of the gradient's directional properties in a learned semantic embedding space (derived from a pre-trained gradient encoder trained on representative FL tasks). The FL server collects semantic gradient signatures in a lightweight pre-aggregation step, clusters clients by gradient semantic similarity, and assigns client-specific compression schedules: clients in the same semantic cluster receive higher compression ratios (their updates are more redundant and can be inferred from cluster-representative gradients), while clients with semantically unique gradients receive lower compression ratios to preserve their marginal information. The compression schedule is communicated back to clients in a highly compressed control message, and clients apply the assigned compression (quantization level + sparsification mask selection) before transmitting full gradient updates.
desc.paragraph_3: Differentiators: HGC (IJCAI 2024) identifies cross-client redundancy but uses static mask matching; this invention uses learned semantic embedding space clustering for dynamic, per-round redundancy assessment. All prior compression methods operate client-independently; this invention introduces server-coordinated semantic-aware compression scheduling as a novel coordination primitive. The semantic gradient encoder (pre-trained on FL tasks) can be shared as a public model, enabling the compression protocol to operate without additional privacy risk. The dynamic compression schedule allocation uniquely balances communication efficiency against model quality for non-IID data distributions.

claim.preamble: A method for compression-efficient federated learning in a system comprising a plurality of client devices and a federated learning server, the method comprising:
limitations: (a) at each participating client device, extracting a semantic gradient signature from a locally computed gradient update by applying a pre-trained gradient encoder to produce a compact fixed-dimensional semantic embedding vector;
(b) transmitting the semantic gradient signatures from participating client devices to the federated learning server in a pre-aggregation communication step prior to full gradient transmission;
(c) at the federated learning server, clustering the received semantic gradient signatures into semantic similarity groups using a distance metric in the semantic embedding space;

(d) computing, for each semantic similarity group, a compression schedule comprising a quantization bit-width assignment and a sparsification ratio, wherein clients in higher-similarity groups receive higher sparsification ratios and lower bit-width assignments reflecting their gradient redundancy;
(e) transmitting the compression schedules to respective client devices;

(f) at each client device, applying the received compression schedule to the locally computed gradient update to produce a compressed gradient and transmitting the compressed gradient to the federated learning server for aggregation.

depClaims: Gradient encoder trained via contrastive self-supervised learning on a synthetic FL task distribution to generalize across application domains
Privacy-preserving semantic signature computation using locally differentially private mechanisms before signature transmission to the server
Asynchronous SGC protocol where compression schedules are computed from previous-round signatures for zero-latency overhead in each FL round
Adaptive semantic clustering with automatic cluster count selection using silhouette score optimization to handle dynamically joining/leaving clients
Downlink compression symmetry: server applies SGC to global model broadcast using semantic similarity to device architecture tiers
Integration with 3GPP standards for over-the-air FL (OAI-FL) in 5G/6G radio access networks using semantic gradient signatures as MAC layer hints for resource allocation
Multi-task SGC where clients participating in multiple simultaneous FL federations share gradient encoder computation across tasks

refs.ref: IJCAI 2024 HGC — Practical hybrid gradient compression for FL (sparsification + quantization + encoding)
refs.diff: HGC uses static shared sparsification masks for cross-client redundancy; no semantic embedding space clustering; no server-coordinated dynamic compression schedule assignment. Academic paper with no patent filing identified.

refs.ref: Deep Gradient Compression (Lin et al., ICLR 2018) — DGC sparsification
refs.diff: DGC operates client-independently with no cross-client semantic awareness; static top-k sparsification; no pre-aggregation semantic clustering step

refs.ref: Huawei communication compression patents (H04W28/xx cluster at USPTO)
refs.diff: Huawei's FL compression patents focus on network-layer scheduling and quantization for telecom FL; do not disclose semantic gradient embedding space or server-coordinated semantic clustering compression schedules

refs.ref: ScaleCom (Chen et al., 2020) — cyclic top-k with matched masks
refs.diff: ScaleCom uses cyclic mask rotation without semantic similarity assessment; no embedding space; no server-coordinated dynamic compression schedule

ideas.ftoJ: Server-coordinated semantic gradient clustering with dynamic per-client compression schedule assignment is not found in any identified patent. Huawei's communication compression portfolio focuses on network scheduling, not semantic gradient embedding. Google's FL patents do not address gradient compression at the semantic level. The pre-aggregation semantic signature step is a novel communication primitive in FL not covered by any identified claims.

ps.s101n: Method claims with concrete technical steps tied to distributed computing hardware (client devices, FL server, communication protocol) are patent-eligible. The semantic gradient encoder applied to produce embedding vectors is a concrete computational transformation. Potential §101 risk if framed too abstractly as 'data compression' — recommend framing as 'improvement to federated learning communication protocols.'
ps.s102n: The combination of: pre-trained gradient encoder for semantic signature extraction + server-side semantic clustering + dynamic per-client compression schedule assignment is novel. Pre-aggregation semantic signature step is a novel FL communication round structure not found in prior art.
ps.s103n: The insight that FL gradients have semantic redundancy measurable in an embedding space — and that this can be exploited for server-coordinated differential compression — is non-obvious. PHOSITA in source coding would not obviously apply semantic similarity clustering to FL gradient compression.
ps.s112n: Self-supervised contrastive learning for encoder training, k-means/DBSCAN clustering, quantization, and sparsification are all well-documented. FL communication protocol implementation is fully specified in NVFlare/Flower documentation. High enablement confidence.

ideas.valM: Licensing to 5G/6G network equipment vendors (Ericsson, Nokia, Qualcomm) building over-the-air FL into basestation infrastructure. FL platform vendors optimizing for bandwidth-constrained industrial/automotive deployments. Standard essential patent potential if semantic gradient compression becomes part of 3GPP TR 22.874 (FL for 5G) standardization track.

ideas.title: Cross-Silo Federated Learning Orchestration Platform with Regulatory-Partitioned Data Governance and Cryptographic Audit Trail for Healthcare Consortia

desc.paragraph_1: Problem: Cross-silo federated learning — where distinct organizational silos (hospitals, financial institutions, government agencies) collaborate in FL without sharing raw data — faces a critical gap between technical capability and regulatory operationalization. Current FL orchestration platforms (NVIDIA NVFlare, IBM FL, PySyft, FATE) provide technical FL coordination but lack: (1) built-in regulatory jurisdiction partitioning (e.g., EU GDPR vs. US HIPAA vs. Singapore PDPA — a consortium spanning multiple jurisdictions must run each data subject's contribution under its home-jurisdiction rules simultaneously), (2) IRB/DPA approval workflow integration (clinical FL studies require ethics committee approval at each participating institution with an audit trail proving each institution's IRB approved participation before data was used), and (3) machine-readable data governance contracts (DGCs) that automatically enforce data use restrictions on the aggregation server side.
desc.paragraph_2: Solution: A cross-silo FL orchestration platform implementing regulatory-partitioned data governance (RPDG). The platform assigns each FL client device a regulatory profile derived from the data subject's jurisdiction, data category (health, financial, biometric), and applicable regulations. During FL training, gradient updates from each client are tagged with a regulatory metadata vector and routed through jurisdiction-specific aggregation pathways — ensuring that a gradient update tagged with GDPR/health cannot be aggregated with updates from a non-GDPR jurisdiction without explicit data transfer agreement verification. The platform integrates a DPA/IRB approval workflow module that must be completed (with cryptographic signatures from authorized institutional officers) before a silo is admitted to the federation. A cryptographic audit trail (Merkle-tree-based) records every data governance decision, approval, and gradient aggregation event, providing a machine-readable compliance record exportable to regulators in standardized formats (HL7 FHIR for healthcare, ISO 20022 for financial services).
desc.paragraph_3: Differentiators: NVIDIA NVFlare, FATE, and IBM FL all provide technical FL orchestration without built-in regulatory jurisdiction partitioning. IBM's FL patents cover Byzantine robustness and influence weighting, not regulatory compliance workflows. The DGC auto-enforcement mechanism — where the aggregation server programmatically validates data use restrictions before aggregation — is not present in any identified system. The HL7 FHIR / ISO 20022 export interface creates unprecedented interoperability between FL audit trails and existing healthcare/financial regulatory reporting infrastructure. The IRB workflow module with cryptographic signatures addresses a real operational bottleneck that currently takes 6-18 months in clinical FL studies.

claim.preamble: A federated learning orchestration system for cross-silo machine learning with integrated regulatory data governance, the system comprising:
limitations: (a) a regulatory profile assignment module configured to assign, to each participating client device in a cross-silo federation, a regulatory metadata vector identifying applicable data protection regulations, data categories under each regulation, jurisdictional boundaries, and data use restriction terms derived from an institutional data governance contract stored in a structured machine-readable format;

(b) a jurisdiction-partitioned aggregation engine configured to route gradient updates received from participating client devices through regulation-specific aggregation pathways, wherein the engine verifies, before aggregating any two gradient updates, that their respective regulatory metadata vectors are compatible under a cross-border data transfer compliance ruleset, and rejects or quarantines gradient updates whose regulatory metadata vectors indicate an incompatible cross-border transfer;

(c) an institutional approval workflow module configured to manage cryptographic multi-party approval processes for silo admission, wherein admission of a new client silo requires cryptographically signed authorizations from a predetermined set of institutional officers including at least a data protection officer and an institutional review board representative, and records the approval signatures in a tamper-evident approval ledger;

(d) a compliance audit trail module configured to maintain a Merkle-tree-structured append-only log recording, for each FL training round: gradient routing decisions, regulatory compatibility verifications, aggregation events, and model checkpoint hashes, with each entry cryptographically linked to the authorizing institutional approval records;

(e) a regulatory export interface configured to generate machine-readable compliance reports in domain-standard formats including at least one of HL7 FHIR for healthcare regulatory submissions and ISO 20022 for financial services regulatory submissions, incorporating the compliance audit trail and approval ledger records.

depClaims: Dynamic regulatory profile update mechanism when data protection regulations change (e.g., new GDPR adequacy decisions), triggering re-evaluation of existing cross-border aggregation pathways
Data governance contract (DGC) template library with pre-validated templates for common FL use cases (clinical AI, fraud detection, credit scoring) reducing institutional onboarding time
Automatic IRB protocol generation that synthesizes the FL study parameters into a pre-formatted IRB submission document for institutional review
Integration with national data trust frameworks (UK Secure Research Service, NHS Digital Data Access Request Service, IQVIA RWD) for automated data access request submission
Privacy budget sharing protocol across regulatory jurisdictions — ensuring that DP privacy budget expenditure is tracked per-regulatory-regime rather than globally
Immutable silo admission and departure log for GDPR data processing agreement lifecycle management
Cross-silo model performance attribution report disaggregated by institutional silo, enabling consortium governance decisions about resource contribution equity

refs.ref: NVIDIA NVFlare / Clara Train FL framework (healthcare cross-silo)
refs.diff: NVFlare provides technical FL orchestration, data isolation, and SSL communication; no regulatory jurisdiction partitioning, no IRB workflow module, no DGC auto-enforcement, no HL7 FHIR export. NVIDIA's patent (US12072954B1) covers FL training methodology, not governance orchestration.

refs.ref: FATE (Federated AI Technology Enabler, WeBank) — cross-silo FL platform
refs.diff: FATE provides privacy-preserving FL for financial services but without regulatory jurisdiction partitioning or healthcare-specific FHIR export. No identified patents on governance-workflow-integrated FL orchestration.

refs.ref: US12335379B1 — Privacy-preserving transformer with encrypted dimensionality reduction
refs.diff: Covers individual model privacy mechanisms; does not address cross-silo orchestration, regulatory partitioning, or multi-institutional approval workflows

refs.ref: Partners HealthCare / NVIDIA Clara clinical FL deployment (ADOPS, FAIRVIEW) — implementation description
refs.diff: Published case studies describe manual IRB approval processes taking 6-18 months; no automated workflow, cryptographic signatures, or machine-readable audit trail — exactly the gap this invention addresses

ideas.ftoJ: No identified patents combining regulatory jurisdiction partitioning, cryptographic multi-party IRB approval workflows, jurisdiction-gated gradient aggregation, and HL7 FHIR/ISO 20022 regulatory export in an FL orchestration context. Healthcare IT standards (HL7, FHIR) are broadly patented for EHR applications but not for FL audit trail export. NVIDIA, IBM, and FATE FL patents address technical FL mechanics, not regulatory governance orchestration. Clear whitespace for the governance-workflow-integrated FL platform.

ps.s101n: System claims with concrete operational components (regulatory profile module, jurisdiction-partitioned aggregation engine, approval workflow module, audit trail module, export interface) tied to specific physical computing systems are strongly patent-eligible. The integration with HL7 FHIR standard is a concrete technical interface specification. The regulatory governance functions are technical improvements to distributed computing systems, not abstract legal concepts.
ps.s102n: Highly novel: no prior art identified combining regulatory jurisdiction partitioning of FL gradient aggregation + cryptographic multi-party IRB approval workflow + HL7 FHIR regulatory export in a single integrated system. Each component has antecedents in different domains (healthcare IT, FL, cryptography) but the cross-domain integration is entirely novel.
ps.s103n: The specific technical integration of regulatory jurisdiction partitioning into the FL aggregation pipeline — where the aggregation engine actively gates gradient aggregation based on regulatory metadata compatibility — would not be obvious to an FL engineer or a healthcare compliance engineer operating independently. The HL7 FHIR export of FL audit records requires cross-domain knowledge not found in either FL or healthcare IT PHOSITA.
ps.s112n: HL7 FHIR R4/R5 specifications, ISO 20022 message standards, Merkle tree implementations, and digital signature protocols are extensively documented. The regulatory metadata vector format requires detailed specification in the disclosure. IRB approval workflow integration requires detailed description of the cryptographic signing ceremony. Full enablement achievable with comprehensive examples.

ideas.valM: Enterprise SaaS licensing to clinical research organizations, hospital networks running AI consortia, and financial services FL platforms. Addressable market: $2B+ in clinical AI platform software by 2028, with regulatory compliance a primary purchase criterion. IRB automation value proposition alone is worth $50K-$200K per FL study in avoided regulatory consulting fees. Government procurement potential (NIH, FDA, EMA regulatory technology programs). High strategic value as healthcare FL matures from research to clinical deployment.

ideas.title: Orthogonal Frequency-Division Multiplexed Cryo-CMOS Controller with Adaptive Inter-Qubit Interference Cancellation
ideas.desc:
Current frequency-multiplexed qubit controllers—exemplified by Intel's Horse Ridge architecture—allocate distinct RF bands to individual qubits but suffer from inter-qubit crosstalk when drive tones are applied simultaneously. When a microwave tone resonant with qubit A is also off-resonantly detuned from qubit B, it induces spurious rotations that degrade gate fidelity. This problem worsens with qubit count: in a 1,000-qubit FDM system, each qubit experiences interference from hundreds of simultaneous off-resonant tones. Prior art addresses this via pulse shaping or by constraining simultaneous operations, reducing throughput.

This invention discloses a cryo-CMOS ASIC operating at 4K that implements orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) across all qubit drive channels. The controller's digital signal processor enforces orthogonality constraints between simultaneously transmitted microwave waveforms by selecting drive pulse durations τ = k/Δf (where Δf is the qubit frequency spacing and k is a positive integer), ensuring that off-resonant spectral components integrate to zero over each gate period. An on-chip adaptive interference cancellation engine monitors real-time qubit state feedback (from concurrent readout channels) and dynamically adjusts the amplitude-phase weighting of each drive tone to suppress residual crosstalk arising from qubit frequency drift and coupler leakage. The ASIC further includes a frequency-tracking PLL array that maintains phase coherence between all output channels to within 2° even as qubit frequencies vary by up to 10 MHz due to flux noise.

The key differentiator from Horse Ridge and prior FDM art is the combination of: (a) OFDM-enforced orthogonality enabling high-fidelity simultaneous multi-qubit gates on a single coaxial input, (b) active crosstalk cancellation using real-time state telemetry, and (c) a cryogenic DPS (Digital Pulse Sequencer) block that co-optimizes pulse length and frequency allocation to maximize gate throughput within orthogonality constraints. This architecture is modality-agnostic, applying equally to transmon, fluxonium, and spin qubit arrays.

claim.preamble: A cryogenic qubit control integrated circuit configured to operate at a temperature between 1K and 10K, comprising:
limitations: (a) a digital pulse sequencer configured to generate N simultaneous microwave waveforms at N distinct qubit drive frequencies f1...fN, wherein the pulse durations satisfy τ = k/Δf for positive integer k and minimum frequency spacing Δf, enforcing spectral orthogonality between simultaneously applied drive tones;

(b) an adaptive crosstalk cancellation engine coupled to a qubit state readout input, configured to compute and apply amplitude-phase correction weights to each of the N waveforms based on a measured qubit state error signal;

(c) a phase-locked loop array comprising N phase-locked loops, each maintaining phase coherence of a respective drive channel to within 5 degrees across a frequency range of 2-20 GHz at cryogenic operating temperatures;
(d) a single coaxial output bus through which all N waveforms are summed and transmitted to a qubit array, wherein the orthogonality constraints suppress inter-qubit interference to below -40 dB.

depClaims: Claim specifying half-orthogonal frequency spacing (τ = 0.5k/Δf) for doubled qubit density with fidelity compensation
Claim covering DRAG (Derivative Removal via Adiabatic Gate) pulse shaping integrated with OFDM orthogonality constraints for leakage suppression to higher qubit levels
Claim directed to dynamic frequency reallocation when two qubits drift to within 1 MHz of each other, triggering automatic reassignment of drive frequencies
Claim for the adaptive cancellation engine using a neural-network-based predictor trained on historical qubit state error data to pre-emptively compensate for systematic crosstalk
Claim specifying power consumption below 5 mW/qubit at 4K to remain within dilution refrigerator cooling budget
Claim covering modular tile architecture where multiple ASIC instances are daisy-chained to scale to >1,000 simultaneous drive channels
Claim directed to dual-mode operation for both transmon qubits (4-8 GHz) and spin qubits (13-20 GHz) via reconfigurable LO frequency synthesis

refs.ref: Intel Horse Ridge (ISSCC 2020, US patents around 22nm cryo-CMOS SoC)
refs.diff: Horse Ridge uses frequency multiplexing but does not enforce OFDM orthogonality constraints between simultaneous drives; the '4×32' architecture applies RF channels sequentially rather than concurrently with active interference cancellation.

refs.ref: arXiv:2511.16855 (OFDM for simultaneous gate operations, February 2026)
refs.diff: This academic paper analyzes orthogonal FDM theoretically but discloses no integrated circuit implementation, no active cancellation engine, and no PLL architecture operating at cryogenic temperatures. The patent would claim the physical ASIC embodiment.

refs.ref: Physical Review Applied 18, 064046 (2022) - Cryogenic Multiplexing Control Chip (MCC)
refs.diff: The MCC uses adjustable inductors for signal routing, not OFDM; it does not address simultaneous multi-qubit gate orthogonality or adaptive feedback-based crosstalk correction.

refs.ref: EP3788563B1 (Google qubit control electronics)
refs.diff: Google's patent covers a 3-4K cryo-CMOS IC with on-chip waveform memory and FPGA-based signal routing but does not disclose OFDM pulse-length constraints or active inter-qubit interference cancellation.

ideas.ftoJ: Intel's Horse Ridge claims are limited to their specific SoC architecture with DDS-based frequency synthesis and do not cover OFDM orthogonality constraints or adaptive cancellation engines. Google EP3788563B1 covers charge-locking and waveform memory, not OFDM. The specific combination of orthogonality-enforced pulse duration selection with real-time adaptive crosstalk correction in a cryo-CMOS ASIC has no identified blocking patents.

ps.s101n: Concrete apparatus (ASIC with specific hardware components) clearly patent-eligible; not directed to an abstract idea under Alice step 1.
ps.s102n: OFDM applied to cryo-CMOS qubit controllers with adaptive cancellation is not disclosed in any identified prior art; the orthogonality constraint τ=k/Δf for simultaneous multi-qubit gates in a cryo-CMOS circuit is novel.
ps.s103n: Combining OFDM (telecom art) with cryo-CMOS qubit control requires non-obvious insights: (1) that orthogonality is achievable despite qubit frequency drift, (2) that active cancellation via qubit state feedback can operate within the 4K thermal budget, (3) the PLL design constraints at cryogenic temperatures. These require quantum physics + RF engineering expertise that a POSITA in either field alone would not combine.
ps.s112n: IIT Tirupati/IIIT Hyderabad 2026 cryo-CMOS analog chain paper and arXiv OFDM analysis provide sufficient enabling detail; circuit blocks (PLL, DSP, DAC) are well-characterized at 4K.

ideas.valM: FDM/OFDM is the dominant paradigm for scaling quantum control beyond 100 qubits; any OEM (IBM, Google, IonQ, Quantinuum, D-Wave) deploying >200-qubit systems would need to license or design around. Royalty basis: $5-10M/year over 4-year commercialization window at 5-10% of system BOM attributable to control ASIC. Chokepoint value elevated because OFDM orthogonality is difficult to design around while maintaining gate fidelity.

ideas.title: Self-Heating-Aware Cryo-CMOS Layout Synthesis System with Thermal Gradient Routing for Millikelvin Qubit Controllers
ideas.desc:
A critical unsolved problem in cryo-CMOS circuit design is the severe self-heating of MOSFET channels at deep-cryogenic temperatures. Experimental work from TU Delft and Politecnico di Torino (2021-2024) demonstrates that in 40-nm bulk-CMOS at 4K ambient, channel temperatures can rise by over 50K under active operation, propagating thermal disturbances up to 30 µm into the silicon substrate. When cryo-CMOS control circuits are co-integrated with or placed within 100 µm of qubit devices (as required for scalable millikelvin-stage integration), these local hot spots create spatially varying decoherence gradients across the qubit array. No existing EDA tools or design rule sets account for cryogenic self-heating; foundries characterize their processes only from -40°C to +125°C.

This invention discloses a hardware/software co-design system comprising: (1) a cryogenic self-heating model library parameterized for foundry nodes between 14nm and 65nm at temperatures from 4K down to 50mK, capturing channel temperature rise as a function of bias current density, gate geometry, and substrate material; (2) a modified place-and-route algorithm that enforces minimum spatial separation constraints between high-dissipation circuit blocks (DAC output stages, oscillators, PA drivers) and thermally sensitive nodes (qubit bias lines, readout resonators), based on calculated isothermal contours from the model library; (3) an automated thermal budget partitioner that allocates power dissipation budgets to each functional block within a cryo-CMOS SoC, iteratively satisfying both electrical performance specifications and substrate temperature targets at the qubit interface plane; and (4) a physical verification rule deck for tape-out sign-off at cryogenic operating conditions.

The system generates cryo-aware GDSII layouts in which high-dissipation transistors are routed away from qubit-proximal regions, thermally isolating metal segments are inserted at critical junctions, and guard-ring heat sinking structures are placed to redirect thermal energy toward dedicated heat-sinking pillars connected to the dilution refrigerator cold plate. This constitutes the first EDA framework purpose-built for millikelvin cryo-CMOS integration, filling a gap explicitly identified in TU Delft PhD thesis (2022) and IBM's 2024 review article on cryogenic CMOS design challenges.

claim.preamble: A computer-implemented system for designing an integrated circuit configured to operate at temperatures below 1K, the system comprising:
limitations: (a) a cryogenic thermal model database storing parameterized self-heating models for a plurality of MOSFET device types in a sub-65nm semiconductor process node, wherein each model specifies channel temperature rise as a function of drain current density, ambient temperature between 10mK and 4K, and neighboring device density;

(b) a thermal-aware placement engine configured to receive a netlist for the integrated circuit and assign physical coordinates to circuit blocks subject to a thermal isolation constraint that maintains a substrate temperature at a designated qubit-interface boundary below a target threshold temperature T_q;

(c) a power budget partitioner configured to iteratively assign maximum dissipation limits to each circuit block such that the aggregate substrate temperature at the qubit-interface boundary satisfies T_q while electrical performance specifications are met;

(d) a physical verification engine configured to check a completed layout against cryogenic design rules including minimum hot-spot separation, thermal guard-ring insertion requirements, and heat-sinking pillar connectivity.

depClaims: Claim covering the specific thermal model parameterization methodology for FDSOI (FD-SOI) processes, where buried oxide provides additional thermal isolation benefits
Claim directed to automated insertion of superconducting metallic guard rings (e.g., niobium) to redirect thermal flux away from qubit-proximal regions
Claim for a co-simulation mode that feeds back qubit coherence time degradation as a function of substrate temperature gradient into the placement engine objective function
Claim covering the model library extension to III-V HEMT devices (InP, InAlAs) for amplifier stages co-integrated with qubit readout chains
Claim for a machine-learning surrogate model of the self-heating physics, trained on finite-element simulation data, enabling real-time thermal constraint evaluation during interactive layout
Claim directed to generating a certified thermal-aware process design kit (PDK) extension for a designated foundry node, as a licensable product artifact

refs.ref: arXiv:2106.07982 (Self-Heating in Nanometer Bulk CMOS at 4K, 2021)
refs.diff: This paper characterizes self-heating experimentally but discloses no EDA tool, design rule set, or automated layout methodology. The invention converts these physical insights into a commercially deployable design system.

refs.ref: TU Delft PhD Thesis on Cryogenic CMOS Characterization (2022)
refs.diff: Thesis explicitly identifies the lack of cryogenic PDK models as an open problem but presents no automated solution; the invention provides the automated place-and-route and verification framework.

refs.ref: SureCore CryoIP / CryoCMOS Consortium (GlobalFoundries 22FDX, 2023)
refs.diff: CryoIP provides standard cell libraries and SRAM optimized for cryogenic VT shift but does not address self-heating-induced thermal gradients or qubit-proximal layout constraints.

refs.ref: Standard commercial EDA tools (Cadence Virtuoso, Synopsys IC Compiler)
refs.diff: Existing tools model self-heating only at temperatures above -40°C (per JEDEC/military specs); they have no parameterization for millikelvin operation and no qubit-interface thermal boundary conditions.

ideas.ftoJ: No incumbent patent identified covering EDA tools for cryo-CMOS self-heating-aware layout. Intel, Google, and IBM all face this problem internally but have not patented a solution. SureCore's CryoIP patents cover standard cell library elements, not layout synthesis systems.

ps.s101n: Software claims risk Alice challenge; recommend pairing with apparatus claims directed to the physical integrated circuit produced by the system, and to the physical cryo-CMOS IC having thermally isolated layout features. The system claims tie to physical transformation (IC layout to silicon).
ps.s102n: No identified prior art discloses a thermal-aware placement engine or design rule set operating on cryogenic self-heating models below 4K for qubit-proximal layout.
ps.s103n: Combining standard EDA thermal modeling (known at room temperature) with cryogenic self-heating physics and qubit coherence constraints requires non-obvious domain fusion. The qubit-interface boundary condition (T_q target) is conceptually new.
ps.s112n: The self-heating model parameterization is well-supported by published measurement data; the placement algorithm extends known thermal EDA concepts with new constraints that can be clearly specified.

ideas.valM: Every cryo-CMOS ASIC developer (Intel, IBM, Google, startups) must solve this problem before taping out millikelvin-stage chips. EDA tool licensing at $1-3M/seat for specialized quantum EDA, plus royalties on qualified foundry processes. Strategic value as a gatekeeping patent for the millikelvin cryo-CMOS manufacturing ecosystem.

ideas.title: Photonic Qubit Control Bus with Cryogenic UTC-PD Chiplet Array and Wavelength-Division Multiplexed Multi-Channel Delivery
ideas.desc:
The thermal conductivity advantage of optical fibers over coaxial cables is approximately 10,000-fold (0.2 dB/km vs. ~1,000 dB/km for coaxial at 6 GHz), making photonic signal delivery the most promising path to eliminate the thermal wiring bottleneck in large-scale quantum processors. Recent experimental work (arXiv:2512.10706, December 2025) demonstrates that uni-traveling-carrier photodiode (UTC-PD) chiplets can convert optical qubit control signals to microwave with >95% gate fidelity at distances up to 15 km. However, this proof-of-concept uses only one or two channels per fiber and does not address the multiplexed, high-channel-count architecture required for >1,000-qubit processors.

This invention discloses a photonic qubit control bus architecture comprising: (1) a room-temperature optical head unit housing an array of electro-optic modulators (EOMs) driven by the classical control software, each modulator encoding qubit gate waveforms onto a distinct optical carrier wavelength in the telecom C-band (1530-1565nm) or L-band; (2) a wavelength-division multiplexing (WDM) combiner that consolidates N optical channels onto a single low-loss single-mode fiber entering the dilution refrigerator; (3) a cryogenic WDM demultiplexer located at the 4K stage, optically splitting channels to individual UTC-PD chiplets; (4) an array of cryogenically packaged UTC-PD chiplets, each generating a microwave qubit drive signal with bandwidth >20 GHz and phase coherence maintained via a shared optical phase reference; and (5) an in-fridge optical phase reference distribution network ensuring sub-picosecond timing synchronization across all qubit drive channels. The fiber provides essentially zero heat load (thermal conductivity of silica fiber ~1 mW/m/K vs. ~10 W/m/K for copper coaxial), and WDM multiplexing achieves >1,000 drive channels on a single fiber pair, reducing wiring from thousands of cables to a handful of fiber ribbons.

The critical advance over the 2025 paper is the WDM multi-channel architecture with a shared optical phase reference—enabling coherent, phase-locked control of thousands of qubits over a single fiber pair—combined with the cryo-packaged WDM demultiplexer and flip-chip-bonded UTC-PD array operating as a modular, swappable chiplet. This photonic bus is also amenable to integration with optical quantum repeaters for distributed quantum computing architectures.

claim.preamble: A photonic qubit control system for a quantum processor operating at a millikelvin temperature, comprising:
limitations: (a) a room-temperature optical transmitter unit comprising a plurality of electro-optic modulators, each modulator receiving a qubit gate waveform and encoding it onto an optical carrier at a distinct wavelength within a wavelength band between 1530 nm and 1625 nm;

(b) a wavelength-division multiplexing (WDM) combiner optically coupled to the plurality of electro-optic modulators and configured to combine the plurality of wavelength-encoded qubit gate signals onto a single optical fiber;

(c) a cryogenic WDM demultiplexer disposed within a cryogenic enclosure at a temperature between 2K and 10K and optically coupled to the single optical fiber, the demultiplexer splitting the combined signal into individual wavelength channels;

(d) a plurality of cryogenic uni-traveling-carrier photodiode (UTC-PD) chiplets, each chiplet optically coupled to a respective wavelength channel output of the cryogenic WDM demultiplexer and configured to convert the optical qubit gate signal to a microwave signal at a qubit drive frequency between 2 GHz and 20 GHz;

(e) an optical phase reference distribution network coupled to the room-temperature optical transmitter and to the cryogenic WDM demultiplexer, maintaining a phase coherence of less than 1 picosecond between all microwave output signals of the plurality of UTC-PD chiplets.

depClaims: Claim covering the flip-chip bonding of UTC-PD chiplets directly to a qubit carrier substrate, forming a monolithic photonic-quantum assembly
Claim directed to wavelength-stabilized optical frequency comb source at room temperature as the optical carrier generator for all WDM channels, ensuring mutual phase coherence
Claim for integration with optical circulators enabling bidirectional operation: downlink qubit drive signals and uplink qubit readout optical encoding on the same fiber
Claim covering active phase stabilization of the intra-fridge optical path using a pilot tone at a non-qubit wavelength and a cryogenic feed-forward phase modulator
Claim directed to the specific chiplet packaging: low-CTE substrate, cryogenic solder bumps, integrated bias tee, and thermal anchor to the 4K plate
Claim for the optical phase reference network using a star-topology optical splitter to distribute a common phase reference to all UTC-PD chiplets with path-length equalization
Claim covering extension to distributed quantum computing: using the photonic bus to synchronize qubit control across multiple dilution refrigerators in a quantum data center

refs.ref: arXiv:2512.10706 (Scalable Optical Links for Controlling Bosonic Quantum Processors, December 2025)
refs.diff: This paper demonstrates single-channel optical control; it does not disclose WDM multi-channel architecture, cryogenic WDM demultiplexing, optical phase reference distribution for simultaneous multi-qubit control, or flip-chip chiplet packaging.

refs.ref: Physical Review Applied 22, 054043 (2024) - Quantum limits of superconducting-photonic links
refs.diff: Explores millimeter-wave photonic sources at 80 GHz for high-frequency qubit control; the invention covers telecom-wavelength WDM architecture for multi-channel qubit control, a distinct technical approach.

refs.ref: Harvard nano-optics lab microwave-to-optical transduction work (thin-film LiNbO3 electro-optic transducers)
refs.diff: These transducers convert quantum states between microwave and optical; the invention is a classical photonic link for control signal delivery, not quantum state transduction. Entirely different application domain.

refs.ref: Intel Horse Ridge II and Google EP3788563B1
refs.diff: Neither covers photonic signal delivery; both assume electrical coaxial signal delivery into the refrigerator.

ideas.ftoJ: Photonic qubit control links are an emerging area with no dominant patent holder. The UTC-PD chiplet approach is demonstrated in academic literature with no commercial patent filings identified. WDM multi-channel qubit control with cryogenic demultiplexing is novel. Harvard/NIST electro-optic transducer patents cover quantum (coherent) transduction, not classical optical control signal delivery.

ps.s101n: Clear physical apparatus; optical-to-microwave conversion device is unambiguously hardware and patent-eligible.
ps.s102n: WDM multi-channel cryogenic photonic bus with UTC-PD chiplet array, phase reference distribution, and cryogenic WDM demultiplexer is not disclosed in any identified prior art.
ps.s103n: Combining telecom WDM technology with cryogenic packaging of UTC-PDs and phase-coherent multi-qubit control requires non-obvious integration across optical communications, cryogenic packaging engineering, and quantum control—distinct disciplines that a POSITA in any single field would not naturally combine.
ps.s112n: UTC-PD chiplet fabrication and WDM demultiplexer design are well-established in telecom literature; cryogenic packaging requires disclosure of specific thermal anchoring and solder bump materials.

ideas.valM: Photonic qubit control is likely to become the dominant wiring paradigm for >1,000-qubit systems given thermal advantages. Any manufacturer of large-scale quantum processors adopting fiber-based control would need this IP. High chokepoint value as the WDM multi-channel architecture is essentially required for scaling. Licensing potential from quantum computing hardware OEMs, telecom equipment suppliers entering quantum markets, and defense/aerospace cryogenic sensor programs.

ideas.title: Non-Radiative Resonant Inductive Power Transfer System for Cryogenic Quantum Processor Electronics with Active Noise Isolation
ideas.desc:
Power delivery to cryogenic control electronics inside dilution refrigerators presents a fundamental scaling challenge: conventional phosphor-bronze wires impose quadratic thermal load growth with wire count, while copper wires provide better conductivity but greater heat ingress. An arXiv 2025 analysis evaluates four power delivery architectures (wired, high-voltage wired, radiative wireless, non-radiative wireless) and identifies non-radiative resonant inductive power transfer as the most promising for scalable quantum systems due to its elimination of physical wiring, low coupling noise, and compatibility with superconducting receiver coils. However, no implementation has been demonstrated in a quantum computing context, and key challenges remain: operating frequency must avoid the 1-10 GHz qubit operating band, transmitter-receiver alignment within the refrigerator's mechanical structure, and power management noise injection into qubit bias and readout lines.

This invention discloses a non-radiative wireless power delivery system for cryogenic quantum processor electronics comprising: (1) a room-temperature or 4K-stage resonant transmitter coil driven at a coupling frequency between 1-100 MHz (well below the qubit operating band), designed to inductively couple to a receiver coil positioned at the target temperature stage (4K or millikelvin); (2) a cryogenic resonant receiver coil with superconducting winding (e.g., NbTi or Nb3Sn) that achieves near-zero series resistance, maximizing Q-factor and power transfer efficiency; (3) a cryogenic AC-DC rectifier and voltage regulator co-located with the receiver coil, converting the coupled AC power to regulated DC supply rails for control electronics; (4) a multi-layer electromagnetic shielding structure interposed between the power delivery system and qubit-proximal electronics, comprising alternating superconducting and normal-metal layers tuned to provide >80 dB of isolation in the qubit operating band (4-10 GHz); and (5) an active noise cancellation subsystem that monitors power supply ripple and injects compensating signals to suppress noise coupling into qubit drive and bias lines.

This system enables an arbitrary number of cryogenic control ASICs to receive power wirelessly, eliminating the O(N) scaling of power wires and decoupling the wiring count from the qubit count. The superconducting receiver coil maximizes energy transfer at millikelvin temperatures while the multi-layer shielding prevents power delivery electromagnetic fields from corrupting qubit states.

claim.preamble: A wireless power delivery system for electronics operating within a cryogenic enclosure at temperatures below 10K, the system comprising:
limitations: (a) a resonant transmitter coil disposed at a first temperature stage of the cryogenic enclosure, the transmitter coil configured to generate an alternating magnetic field at a coupling frequency below 500 MHz;

(b) a resonant receiver coil disposed at a second temperature stage of the cryogenic enclosure colder than the first temperature stage, the receiver coil comprising superconducting wire and configured to inductively receive power from the transmitter coil at the coupling frequency;

(c) a cryogenic power management circuit co-located with the receiver coil, comprising an AC-DC rectifier and a voltage regulator, configured to convert the inductively received power to regulated DC power rails for qubit control electronics;

(d) an electromagnetic isolation structure interposed between the receiver coil and a qubit array, the isolation structure providing at least 60 dB of magnetic field attenuation in a frequency band between 2 GHz and 20 GHz while transmitting the regulated DC power rails to the qubit control electronics;

(e) an active noise suppression circuit monitoring ripple on the regulated DC power rails and injecting compensating signals to maintain power supply noise below a threshold compatible with qubit coherence.

depClaims: Claim specifying superconducting receiver coil material (NbTi, Nb3Sn, or YBCO) and geometry (planar spiral on silicon substrate for integration with cryo-CMOS chips)
Claim covering beamforming transmitter array with adaptive phase control to maintain coupling efficiency despite mechanical vibration within the refrigerator
Claim directed to the rectifier topology using cryogenic Schottky diodes or Josephson junction-based rectification for millikelvin-stage power conversion

Claim for the multi-layer shielding structure: alternating layers of superconducting niobium and normal-metal copper-nickel alloy, with layer thicknesses tuned by simulation to maximize isolation in the 4-10 GHz qubit band
Claim covering integration of the power receiver coil as a monolithic layer within the dilution refrigerator flange assembly, enabling factory installation
Claim for scalable daisy-chain topology where power is relayed between temperature stages using successive resonant coil pairs to deliver power from 300K to 4K to millikelvin

refs.ref: arXiv:2511.13965 (Power Delivery for Cryogenic Scalable Quantum Applications, November 2025)
refs.diff: This analysis paper proposes non-radiative power transfer as a concept and analyzes trade-offs but presents no circuit implementation, no superconducting receiver coil design, no electromagnetic isolation structure, and no experimental validation. The patent covers the specific engineered system.

refs.ref: Conventional wireless power transfer (Qi standard, resonant coupling systems)
refs.diff: Room-temperature WPT operates at ambient conditions without cryogenic superconducting coils, millikelvin rectification, or qubit-band electromagnetic isolation. The novel elements are all specific to the cryogenic quantum computing environment.

refs.ref: Bluefors High-Density Flex Wiring (FPC) and conventional twisted phosphor-bronze wiring
refs.diff: These are wired solutions that still impose thermal load scaling; the invention eliminates power wiring entirely using wireless delivery.

refs.ref: D-Wave on-chip multiplexed DAC control (announced January 2026)
refs.diff: D-Wave's approach reduces signal wire count via on-chip multiplexed DACs but still uses conventional wired power delivery; the power delivery problem is orthogonal to signal routing and remains unsolved.

ideas.ftoJ: No identified patents cover wireless power delivery inside dilution refrigerators for quantum computing applications. Room-temperature WPT patents (WiTricity, Samsung, Apple) do not cover cryogenic, superconducting-coil, quantum-application-specific implementations. The multi-layer electromagnetic isolation for qubit-band attenuation is novel.

ps.s101n: Physical apparatus; electromagnetic power delivery system is clearly hardware and patent-eligible under 35 USC §101.
ps.s102n: Superconducting receiver coil + cryogenic rectifier + qubit-band isolation structure + active noise cancellation, operating inside a dilution refrigerator, is novel over all identified prior art.
ps.s103n: Adapting WPT to cryogenic quantum environments requires non-obvious choices: superconducting coil geometry for millikelvin Q-factor, isolation structure tuning to qubit bands, and noise cancellation specific to qubit coherence requirements. These cannot be derived from standard WPT literature.
ps.s112n: Superconducting coil design, Schottky rectification at low temperatures, and electromagnetic shielding are well-documented in cryogenic electronics literature, enabling sufficient disclosure for enablement.

ideas.valM: Power delivery is a universal bottleneck affecting every cryogenic quantum computing architecture. If non-radiative wireless power becomes the standard approach for >10,000-qubit systems, this patent family becomes mandatory-license IP for all refrigerator manufacturers (Bluefors, Oxford Instruments, Cryomech) and quantum OEMs. Chokepoint potential is high because the alternative (wired power) scales poorly and becomes physically impossible beyond ~10,000 qubits.

ideas.title: Heterogeneous SFQ-CMOS Multichip Module for Millikelvin Qubit Control with Phonon-Blocking Interposer
ideas.desc:
Single Flux Quantum (SFQ) digital logic offers clock rates exceeding 100 GHz and switching energies of ~10⁻¹⁹ J—orders of magnitude faster and more power-efficient than CMOS—making it highly attractive for on-chip qubit control at millikelvin temperatures. However, the primary barrier to integration is quasiparticle (QP) poisoning: dissipative SFQ driver circuits generate phonons that propagate through the substrate and excite quasiparticles in adjacent superconducting qubit junctions, degrading coherence. The 2023 PRX Quantum paper (McDermott group) demonstrates that a multichip module (MCM) approach—physically segregating the SFQ control chip from the qubit chip—suppresses phonon-mediated QP poisoning sufficiently to achieve >99% fidelity single-qubit gates. However, the MCM still requires bulky wirebond interconnects, lacks integration with CMOS interface electronics for digital command decoding, and does not address the 4K-to-millikelvin signal routing hierarchy.

This invention discloses a heterogeneous SFQ-CMOS multichip module for millikelvin qubit control comprising: (1) a CMOS digital processor chip operating at 4K that receives high-level quantum gate commands from room-temperature electronics, decomposes them into SFQ pulse sequences, and generates digital trigger signals; (2) an SFQ pulse driver chip operating at millikelvin temperatures that receives trigger signals from the CMOS chip and generates precisely timed SFQ pulse trains for qubit coherent control; (3) a phonon-blocking interposer between the SFQ chip and the qubit chip, fabricated from a material with low phonon group velocity (e.g., amorphous silicon dioxide aerogel or patterned phononic crystal slab) to attenuate phonons in the relevant frequency range (10-100 GHz) by at least 40 dB; and (4) superconducting bump-bond interconnects between all three chips enabling sub-nanosecond signal transfer with zero resistive dissipation at the interconnect layer. The CMOS processor handles classical computation overhead (error syndrome decoding, gate compilation) at 4K where CMOS cooling is manageable, while the SFQ driver handles time-critical pulse delivery at millikelvin. This separation of concerns enables each chip to be optimized independently.

The phonon-blocking interposer is the key innovation distinguishing this from prior MCM art. By engineering a phononic bandgap structure in the interposer material, phonons generated by SFQ switching are reflected back toward the control chip rather than transmitted to the qubit substrate. Combined with superconducting bump-bond interconnects (replacing resistive wirebonds), this architecture achieves the fidelity benefits of physical separation while maintaining the tight integration and signal timing of a single-package device.

claim.preamble: A multichip module for controlling superconducting qubits, the module comprising:
limitations: (a) a first semiconductor chip comprising CMOS circuits configured to operate at a temperature between 1K and 10K, the first chip receiving quantum gate instructions and generating digital trigger signals encoding qubit gate operations;

(b) a second chip comprising single flux quantum (SFQ) logic circuits configured to operate at a temperature below 100 mK, the second chip receiving the digital trigger signals from the first chip and generating SFQ pulse trains for driving transitions in superconducting qubit circuits;

(c) a phonon-blocking interposer disposed between the second chip and a qubit chip on which the superconducting qubit circuits are formed, the phonon-blocking interposer comprising a material or structure exhibiting a phononic bandgap that attenuates phonon propagation in a frequency range between 1 GHz and 200 GHz by at least 30 dB, thereby suppressing quasiparticle poisoning of the superconducting qubit circuits;

(d) superconducting electrical interconnects electrically coupling the second chip to the qubit chip through the phonon-blocking interposer, wherein the superconducting interconnects exhibit zero DC resistance at the operating temperature.

depClaims: Claim specifying the phonon-blocking interposer as a two-dimensional phononic crystal slab with periodic holes or pillars, with lattice constant selected to create a phononic bandgap centered at the dominant SFQ phonon emission frequency
Claim covering amorphous aerogel interposer materials (SiO2, Al2O3 aerogel) with thermal conductivity below 0.01 W/m/K at 100 mK, providing both phonon attenuation and thermal isolation

Claim directed to the superconducting bump-bond interconnect material system (In-Bi or Pb-In alloys) with critical temperature above 100 mK and mechanical compliance for differential thermal contraction

Claim for the CMOS processor chip incorporating a real-time error syndrome decoder (surface code decoder) that feeds back qubit measurement results to adaptively update SFQ pulse sequences within one qubit coherence time

Claim covering the 4K-to-millikelvin signal routing: digital signals transmitted as RSFQ pulses between temperature stages via Josephson transmission lines with repeater junctions at intermediate stages
Claim directed to co-integration of a Josephson parametric amplifier (JPA) readout chain on the same multichip module, enabling both drive and readout in a single packageable unit

refs.ref: PRX Quantum 4, 030310 (2023) - SFQ multichip module for qubit control (McDermott group, UW-Madison)
refs.diff: This paper demonstrates the MCM concept with wirebond interconnects and no phonon-blocking interposer; it achieves >99% fidelity via physical separation alone. The invention adds the engineered phononic bandgap interposer and superconducting bump-bonds, enabling tighter integration while maintaining phonon isolation.

refs.ref: McDermott/UW-Madison SFQ coprocessor paper (2018, MDPI npj Quantum Information)
refs.diff: Proposes SFQ coprocessor concept but predates multichip demonstrations and does not disclose phonon-blocking structures or CMOS-SFQ heterogeneous integration.

refs.ref: D-Wave on-chip cryogenic control (superconducting bump bonding, January 2026)
refs.diff: D-Wave's approach uses multiplexed flux DACs (not SFQ logic) for annealing qubit control; no phonon-blocking interposer is disclosed.

refs.ref: Google EP3966938A2 (charge-locking circuits for qubit gates)
refs.diff: Google's patent covers CMOS charge-locking topology; no SFQ logic, no phonon-blocking interposer, and no multichip module architecture.

ideas.ftoJ: The basic SFQ-qubit integration concept has been demonstrated by the McDermott group (UW-Madison); any patents from that work could cover fundamental aspects of SFQ-based qubit control. Recommend claim narrowing to specifically require the phonon-blocking interposer structure (novel) and the CMOS-SFQ hierarchical architecture (novel combination). The bump-bond superconducting interconnect may also require design-around of D-Wave's January 2026 announcement.

ps.s101n: Physical device with specific structural elements; clearly patent-eligible apparatus.
ps.s102n: The phononic bandgap interposer between SFQ driver and qubit chip is novel; CMOS-SFQ heterogeneous integration with separate temperature stage operation is novel.
ps.s103n: Phononic crystals are well-known in acoustic metamaterials literature; applying them to qubit-SFQ integration may face obviousness challenge if examiner combines phononic crystal art with MCM art. Recommend strong secondary considerations (unexpected results in coherence improvement) and specific structural limitations.
ps.s112n: Phononic crystal design and aerogel thermal properties are well-documented; superconducting bump-bond interconnect processes are described in D-Wave and IBM flip-chip literature.

ideas.valM: SFQ-based qubit control is likely required for fault-tolerant quantum computing at scale due to speed and power advantages over CMOS. The phonon-blocking interposer is an enabling component that may become a required element of any SFQ-qubit co-integration. Patent value scales with adoption of SFQ in quantum computing, which accelerates as systems move to fault-tolerant operation requiring >10⁶ qubit operations/second.

ideas.title: Cryo-CMOS DAC with Sub-Millikelvin Charge-Retention Sample-and-Hold for Massively Parallel Spin Qubit Bias Generation
ideas.desc:
Semiconductor spin qubits require precise DC voltage biasing of gate electrodes to define quantum dots, tune tunnel couplings, and set exchange interactions—typically requiring 3-5 voltage signals per qubit with 18-22 bit precision and stability better than 1 µV over gate cycle periods. At room temperature, these voltages are generated by rack-mounted precision DACs with one-to-one coaxial wiring to the dilution refrigerator, creating a wiring count that scales as 3N-5N with qubit number N. The 2025 Bluefors/TU Delft cryo-CMOS paper (IEEE Trans. Quantum Eng., early access) demonstrates a 22nm FinFET cryo-CMOS DAC with demultiplexer operating at 66mK with 120µW total power, controlling 648 single-hole transistors via 96 unique bias voltages. This represents a major step forward, but critical gaps remain: (1) voltage drift of 60µV/s to 18mV/s requires frequent refresh cycles that interrupt quantum gate operations; (2) the system uses sample-and-hold (S&H) capacitors with room-temperature leakage models that do not account for sub-100mK operation physics; (3) the DAC resolution (inferred as ~12 bits from the 3V/96 channel analysis) is insufficient for fault-tolerant spin qubit operation requiring 18+ bits.

This invention discloses a cryo-CMOS DAC architecture specifically optimized for sub-millikelvin operation, exploiting two unique physical effects at these temperatures: (a) MOSFET subthreshold leakage in a properly biased FD-SOI device approaches 10⁻²⁰ A at 50mK, enabling sample-and-hold capacitors to retain charge for >10⁶ qubit gate cycles without refresh; and (b) the extreme reduction in kT noise (kT at 50mK ≈ 4×10⁻²⁴ J) enables intrinsically lower quantization noise floors. The architecture comprises: (1) a high-resolution delta-sigma DAC front-end operating at 4K where CMOS performance is well-characterized, generating precision analog voltages; (2) a sample-and-hold distribution network that stores these voltages on ultra-low-leakage FD-SOI capacitors at 50-100mK; (3) a sparse refresh controller that schedules refresh cycles only for capacitors whose stored voltage has drifted beyond a programmable threshold, using an on-chip self-monitoring circuit that measures S&H output deviation without disturbing qubit operation; and (4) a 2D crossbar addressing matrix enabling O(√N) wire routing for N qubit gate voltages, with the crossbar decoder co-located on the same millikelvin ASIC. The result is a self-managing precision voltage source requiring only O(log N) wires from room temperature to control N qubit gate electrodes with >18-bit effective resolution.

claim.preamble: A cryogenic digital-to-analog converter system for biasing semiconductor qubit gate electrodes, the system configured to operate at temperatures below 200 millikelvin, comprising:
limitations: (a) a plurality of sample-and-hold circuits, each comprising a fully-depleted silicon-on-insulator (FD-SOI) pass transistor coupled to a hold capacitor, wherein the FD-SOI transistor exhibits a subthreshold leakage current below 10⁻¹⁸ A at the operating temperature, enabling charge retention on the hold capacitor for a duration exceeding 10⁴ qubit gate periods without refresh;

(b) a digital-to-analog converter disposed at a temperature stage above the sample-and-hold circuits and below room temperature, configured to generate precision analog voltages with at least 16-bit resolution and provide them sequentially to selected sample-and-hold circuits via a multiplexed distribution network;

(c) a sparse refresh scheduler implemented in digital logic at the operating temperature, configured to monitor stored voltage levels of the plurality of sample-and-hold circuits and schedule refresh operations only for sample-and-hold circuits whose stored voltage has deviated beyond a programmable threshold, such that refresh operations do not interrupt qubit gate operations in progress;

(d) a two-dimensional crossbar addressing matrix coupling the distribution network to the plurality of sample-and-hold circuits, wherein the addressing matrix requires a number of signal lines proportional to the square root of the number of sample-and-hold circuits.

depClaims: Claim specifying FD-SOI process node (GlobalFoundries 22FDX or equivalent) with back-bias control for independent VT adjustment at cryogenic temperatures to further reduce leakage

Claim for in-situ charge drift monitoring using a correlated double sampling (CDS) technique that measures hold capacitor voltage without applying a readout current, thereby not disturbing the stored qubit bias voltage

Claim covering a leakage-compensated hold capacitor design using a series-connected compensation transistor biased to inject charge at a rate equal to the measured leakage rate, extending effective hold time by 10-100x
Claim directed to the 2D crossbar decoder with Josephson-junction-based current switches at the millikelvin stage for zero-dissipation column/row selection

Claim for an adaptive resolution mode where precision is dynamically allocated: high resolution (20-bit) to qubits undergoing active gate operations, lower resolution (12-bit) to qubits in idle states, reducing total power consumption

Claim covering calibration methodology: factory calibration of individual S&H circuit offsets using a cryogenic probe, stored in on-chip non-volatile memory (superconducting phase-slip memory or MRAM), applied as digital pre-distortion at the delta-sigma DAC input

refs.ref: Bluefors/TU Delft 22nm FinFET cryo-CMOS DAC at 66mK (IEEE Trans. Quantum Eng. 2025, early access)
refs.diff: This demonstration achieves 120µW operation at 66mK but suffers from 60µV/s-18mV/s voltage drift requiring frequent refresh. The invention specifically addresses this drift limitation using FD-SOI leakage physics, sparse refresh scheduling, and leakage-compensated capacitor designs—all absent from the prior art.

refs.ref: Google EP3966938A2 (charge-locking circuits with charge-shuffle)
refs.diff: Google's charge-locking circuit operates by shuffling charge between capacitors to generate pulse signals; it does not address precision DC biasing with 18-bit resolution, sparse refresh scheduling, or crossbar addressing. The operating principle and application are distinct.

refs.ref: Intel Horse Ridge II (22 DACs for gate biasing, 4K operation)
refs.diff: Horse Ridge II operates at 4K where leakage is orders of magnitude higher than at millikelvin; its S&H design is not optimized for sub-millikelvin ultra-low-leakage operation and does not disclose sparse refresh scheduling or the FD-SOI leakage physics exploitation.

refs.ref: D-Wave multiplexed flux DAC (tens of thousands of qubits with 200 wires)
refs.diff: D-Wave's approach uses flux DACs (inductance-based, not voltage-based) for annealing qubit control; it does not provide the precise voltage biasing (sub-µV stability, 18-bit resolution) required for gate-model spin qubit operation.

ideas.ftoJ: Google's charge-locking patent (EP3966938A2) covers capacitive voltage storage at cryogenic temperatures; claims should be carefully drafted to distinguish from Google's specific 'charge-shuffle' topology. The FD-SOI process-based leakage exploitation and sparse refresh scheduler are novel elements providing clear differentiation. Recommend independent claims that require the sparse refresh scheduler and FD-SOI FET subthreshold leakage below 10⁻¹⁸ A as distinguishing limitations.

ps.s101n: Physical integrated circuit with specific structural elements; clearly patent-eligible.
ps.s102n: FD-SOI leakage exploitation for extended S&H hold time at sub-millikelvin temperatures combined with sparse refresh scheduling is novel. The specific combination of leakage-compensated capacitor + crossbar + adaptive resolution is not found in identified prior art.
ps.s103n: Extending FD-SOI sample-and-hold to exploit sub-millikelvin leakage physics requires understanding of cryogenic semiconductor physics that a POSITA in DAC design would not arrive at without the quantum computing application context and the specific cryogenic measurements.
ps.s112n: TU Delft 22nm FinFET cryo-CMOS characterization data provides excellent enabling disclosure; FD-SOI cryogenic models from CryoCMOS Consortium enable detailed design specifications.

ideas.valM: Precision voltage biasing is an unavoidable requirement for all semiconductor spin qubit systems (Si/SiGe, GaAs/AlGaAs, Ge/SiGe). Intel (spin qubits), Microsoft (topological), QuTech, CEA-LETI, and others are all pursuing spin qubit processors requiring this functionality. The O(√N) wiring reduction is a fundamental architectural advantage that creates strong economic pressure to adopt this approach.

ideas.title: Thermal-Zone-Aware Hierarchical Signal Routing Fabric for Multi-Stage Cryogenic Quantum Processors Using Superconducting Flex Interconnects
ideas.desc:
Modern dilution refrigerators for quantum computing contain 5-7 temperature stages (300K, 50K, 4K, 800mK, 100mK, 20mK) each with distinct cooling power budgets, signal integrity requirements, and component density constraints. Signal routing between stages currently relies on semi-rigid coaxial cables (for RF signals) and phosphor-bronze twisted pairs (for DC/low-frequency), with attenuation values and thermal load budgets manually optimized by refrigerator engineers on a case-by-case basis. As qubit counts grow to thousands, this manual optimization becomes untenable: the CERN/UTS cryogenic wiring optimization paper (2022 abstract) identifies that even small routing improvements yield exponential increases in computational power. No systematic, software-automated hierarchical routing fabric exists for multi-stage cryogenic signal distribution.

This invention discloses a hierarchical signal routing fabric for multi-stage cryogenic quantum processors comprising three layers: (1) an inter-stage superconducting flex cable architecture where each temperature transition is bridged by a custom flexible printed circuit (FPC) incorporating superconducting signal traces (niobium or aluminum for millikelvin stages, YBCO for 4K-50K transitions) with integrated attenuation elements, filter structures, and thermal anchor pads, fabricated to maintain bend radius compatible with refrigerator geometry; (2) a software-defined signal routing controller (implemented as a room-temperature FPGA or ASIC) that maintains a thermal-zone model of the dilution refrigerator, tracking the thermal load contribution of each signal line, the available cooling power at each stage, and the current assignment of qubits to signal channels; and (3) a cryogenic microelectromechanical (MEMS) switch matrix at the 4K stage enabling dynamic reconfiguration of signal paths between room-temperature electronics, intermediate-stage cryo-CMOS controllers, and millikelvin-stage qubit arrays. The MEMS switch matrix allows the same room-temperature electronics to be time-multiplexed across a larger qubit array than would otherwise be possible, effectively extending the control bandwidth without increasing cable count.

The critical innovation is the software-defined thermal-zone model that enables automated routing optimization: given a quantum circuit workload, the controller pre-computes the minimum thermal-budget routing assignment that satisfies all signal integrity constraints, and programs the MEMS matrix accordingly. This enables dynamic load balancing across the refrigerator's thermal stages, preventing hot spots from forming during high-qubit-activity periods. The superconducting flex cable architecture replaces individual semi-rigid coaxials with a modular, high-density FPC that integrates filtering, thermal anchoring, and attenuation in a single replaceable assembly.

claim.preamble: A hierarchical signal routing system for a multi-stage cryogenic quantum processor, the system comprising:
limitations: (a) a plurality of superconducting flexible circuit interconnects, each interconnect spanning a temperature transition boundary between adjacent temperature stages of a cryogenic enclosure, each interconnect comprising superconducting signal traces, integrated attenuators dimensioned for the thermal budget of the lower temperature stage, and thermal anchor structures thermally coupled to the temperature stage mounting flanges;

(b) a cryogenic microelectromechanical (MEMS) switch matrix disposed at a temperature stage between 1K and 10K, the MEMS switch matrix having a plurality of input ports connectable to a plurality of signal sources and a plurality of output ports connectable to a plurality of qubit control channels, and configured to dynamically assign signal sources to qubit control channels under program control;

(c) a thermal-zone routing controller storing a thermal model of the cryogenic enclosure, the model comprising available cooling power, current thermal load, and temperature at each stage, the controller configured to compute a routing assignment for the MEMS switch matrix that satisfies signal integrity constraints for all active qubit control channels while maintaining thermal load at each stage within the available cooling power budget;

(d) a reconfiguration interface through which the thermal-zone routing controller programs the MEMS switch matrix to implement the computed routing assignment within a reconfiguration latency below a qubit coherence time.

depClaims: Claim specifying the superconducting flex cable materials: niobium traces for millikelvin stage, YBCO (yttrium barium copper oxide) coated conductor for 4K-50K transitions, with CTE-matched substrate (polyimide or LCP polymer)

Claim covering integrated microwave filters fabricated within the flex cable structure: LC low-pass filters for DC bias lines and band-pass filters for RF drive lines, with filter pole frequencies set by stage-specific noise rejection requirements

Claim for the MEMS switch matrix cryogenic design: electrostatically actuated MEMS bridges using silicon-on-insulator membranes with niobium contact pads, achieving <0.1 Ω insertion resistance at cryogenic temperatures
