ideas.ftoJ: IBM adversarial robustness testing patent covers a different scope (black-box ML testing, not physical-constraint-certified safety bounds). No AV-specific adversarial certification patents identified. Randomized smoothing is unpatented academic work. Physical-constraint projection and signed certificate format are unprotected territory.

ps.s101n: Apparatus claims grounded in physical vehicle sensor integration and signed hardware-secured certificate objects are clearly patent-eligible. The physical-constraint projection rooted in real sensor geometry ties the invention to tangible physical reality. Recommend emphasizing the physical sensor parameter integration in independent claims.
ps.s102n: Physical-constraint projection of abstract robustness radii to vehicle safety metrics is novel. Multimodal fusion certification when one modality is adversarially compromised is novel. Signed certificate for regulatory safety cases is novel.
ps.s103n: Combining randomized smoothing (ML security), physical sensor geometry (automotive engineering), and regulatory safety case standards (safety certification) creates a non-obvious combination. A PHOSITA in adversarial ML would not have motivation to add physical-constraint projection; a PHOSITA in automotive safety would not know randomized smoothing.
ps.s112n: Randomized smoothing is fully described in Cohen 2019. Physical constraint parameterization is straightforward given sensor specifications. Monte Carlo sampling is standard. Certificate format can be specified concretely. High enablement confidence.

ideas.valM: Critical for type-approval testing of AV perception systems and for EASA airworthiness of UAV perception. Licensing targets: Tier-1 automotive suppliers (Bosch, Continental, Aptiv), AV OEMs, UAV manufacturers (DJI competitors, military UAV primes). Value also in automotive cybersecurity regulatory compliance (UN WP.29 R155).

ideas.title: Operational Design Domain Boundary Detector with Graduated Safe-State Transition Controller for Autonomous Systems
ideas.desc:
Autonomous systems are certified for operation within a defined Operational Design Domain (ODD) — a bounded set of conditions (weather, road geometry, traffic density, sensor visibility) within which the system is designed to perform safely. A critical unsolved problem is real-time detection of imminent or actual ODD boundary exceedance combined with a safe, graded response that avoids unsafe abrupt control transitions. Existing approaches use hard-coded threshold detectors with binary in/out classifications; they lack graduated confidence measurement and smooth transition protocols. This invention introduces an 'ODD Boundary Detector' (ODB) apparatus incorporating a multi-dimensional, probabilistic ODD membership estimator fused with a 'Graduated Safe-State Transition Controller' (GSTC).

The ODB uses an ensemble of lightweight classifiers — one per ODD dimension (precipitation intensity, lane visibility, traffic density, road geometry complexity) — each producing a continuous membership score between 0 (fully within ODD) and 1 (fully outside ODD). These scores are fused using a Bayesian network that models inter-ODD-dimension correlations (e.g., heavy rain simultaneously degrades sensor visibility and road friction ODD compliance). The fused output is a 'ODD Compliance Index' (OCI) updated at each sensor cycle. The GSTC uses the OCI trajectory — including its first and second time derivatives — to anticipate ODD boundary exceedance and initiate a smooth, phased control transition through a state machine with five defined states: Nominal, Degraded, Pre-Exit, Managed Exit, and Minimal Risk Condition (MRC).

Key novelty includes: (a) multi-dimensional Bayesian ODD membership estimation replacing binary threshold detectors — enabling proactive rather than reactive transitions; (b) derivative-based OCI trajectory prediction allowing the system to begin managed handoff to the Minimal Risk Condition state several seconds before full ODD exceedance; and (c) a V2X-augmented OCI that incorporates infrastructure sensor data (roadside weather stations, connected traffic signals) to extend the ODD boundary detection horizon beyond onboard sensor range. The system generates structured 'ODD Exit Event' records formatted for SOTIF operational phase monitoring per ISO/DIS 21448.

claim.preamble: An operational design domain boundary detection and safe-state transition apparatus for an autonomous vehicle, the apparatus comprising:
limitations: a multi-dimensional ODD membership estimator comprising a plurality of per-dimension classifiers each producing a continuous membership score for a respective operational design domain dimension, and a Bayesian network configured to fuse the per-dimension scores into an ODD Compliance Index (OCI) accounting for inter-dimension correlations
an OCI trajectory predictor configured to compute time derivatives of the OCI over a sliding temporal window to generate a predicted OCI exceedance time horizon

a graduated safe-state transition controller configured to transition the autonomous vehicle through a plurality of ordered safe states based on the OCI value and the predicted OCI exceedance time horizon, wherein the ordered safe states include at least a Nominal state, a Degraded state, a Pre-Exit state, and a Minimal Risk Condition state

an ODD exit event recorder configured to generate structured event records formatted in conformance with ISO/PAS 21448 operational phase monitoring requirements upon detecting OCI exceedance of a defined threshold

depClaims: V2X interface module receiving roadside weather station, traffic signal, and infrastructure sensor data to augment onboard ODD dimension classifiers for extended-horizon ODD boundary prediction
Machine learning-based ODD classifier update mechanism enabling in-field expansion of the ODD boundary envelope following regulatory approval via cryptographically authenticated OTA software updates
Driver takeover readiness integration: GSTC orchestrating human driver engagement protocols during Pre-Exit state transitions for conditionally automated vehicles (SAE Level 3)
Multi-vehicle ODD consensus protocol in platooning scenarios where each vehicle broadcasts its OCI and the platoon adopts the minimum OCI across members
ODD compliance dashboard for fleet operators with real-time geographic heatmap of OCI values across deployed vehicle fleet
Formal ODD specification language (ODD-ML) parser enabling ODD boundary definitions to be loaded from standardized machine-readable files certified by regulatory bodies

ideas.ftoJ: Waymo mode-switching patents cover binary transitions, not continuous probabilistic ODD compliance estimation. SOTIF standard creates regulatory demand but is not a patent. No identified patent covers the Bayesian ODD membership fusion, derivative-based exceedance prediction, or 5-state graduated GSTC. V2X-augmented ODD boundary extension is unpatented.

ps.s101n: Clear apparatus claim with specific hardware (Bayesian network module, OCI trajectory predictor, safe-state controller, event recorder) tightly integrated with physical vehicle control systems. No abstract idea concern.
ps.s102n: Continuous multi-dimensional probabilistic ODD membership with Bayesian fusion and 5-state graduated transition controller is novel. V2X OCI augmentation is novel. Derivative-based exceedance prediction is novel in this context.
ps.s103n: While ODD concepts are discussed in SOTIF standard, the specific Bayesian fusion architecture and graduated 5-state controller design are engineering choices requiring non-obvious domain expertise. A PHOSITA following SOTIF guidance would not naturally arrive at this specific architecture.
ps.s112n: Bayesian network fusion is well-documented. ODD dimension classifiers can be implemented with standard ML techniques. Safe-state machine is implementable in standard automotive embedded software. High enablement confidence.

ideas.valM: SOTIF compliance is mandatory for all AV deployments in the EU. UL 4600 ODD requirements in the U.S. create parallel licensing drivers. Every AV platform (Level 3+) requires this capability. Value reinforced by applicability across UAV geofencing, maritime autonomous surface ships, and surgical robotics ODD frameworks.

ideas.title: Simulation-to-Real Transfer Validity Certifier Using Paired Statistical Testing and Conformal Coverage Gap Quantification
ideas.desc:
Simulation is the primary tool for scaling safety validation of autonomous systems — physical test miles alone cannot cover the billions of scenarios required for statistical safety confidence. However, no current method certifies that simulation test results are statistically valid surrogates for real-world performance. This invention introduces a 'Simulation-to-Real Validity Certifier' (SRVC) that uses paired statistical testing on matched real/simulated scenario sets to produce a certified 'Simulation Validity Certificate' — a bound on how much the simulated performance overestimates or underestimates real-world safety KPIs.

The SRVC operates in three phases: (1) 'Paired Scenario Construction' — for each real-world test run, the system reconstructs the identical scenario in simulation using neural scene reconstruction (NeRF or Gaussian splatting) plus a vehicle dynamics digital twin; (2) 'Distributional Gap Measurement' — a two-sample conformal test computes the maximum discrepancy between the distribution of safety KPI values (e.g., time-to-collision, detection recall) observed in real vs. simulated paired runs, producing a certified gap bound Δ with provable (1-α) confidence; and (3) 'Validity Certificate Generation' — the certified Δ is embedded into a structured validity certificate that adjusts simulated performance claims by the gap bound before regulatory submission. If Δ exceeds a configurable regulatory threshold, the certifier flags the simulation environment as insufficiently valid for that scenario class.

This invention advances beyond NVIDIA's simulation validation work (NTRS Sim2Val) by: (a) using paired conformal testing (not just aggregate statistics) which provides stronger per-scenario validity bounds; (b) producing a signed validity certificate integrated into a regulatory safety case rather than an internal performance metric; and (c) incorporating the gap-adjusted performance claim directly into the regulatory submission template — enabling simulation miles to be credited as partial substitutes for physical miles in safety case arguments submitted to NHTSA or EASA.

claim.preamble: A simulation-to-real transfer validity certification system for an autonomous vehicle development program, comprising:
limitations: a paired scenario constructor configured to reconstruct a simulated representation of each real-world test scenario using neural scene reconstruction of onboard sensor logs and a vehicle dynamics digital twin model, producing a matched real-simulated scenario pair

a distributional gap measurement module configured to apply a two-sample conformal statistical test to safety key performance indicators measured across a plurality of matched scenario pairs, computing a certified maximum distributional gap Δ with a configurable confidence level (1-α)

a validity threshold enforcer configured to compare Δ against a pre-configured regulatory validity threshold and to classify the simulation environment as valid, conditionally valid, or invalid for each scenario class based on the comparison result

a simulation validity certificate generator configured to produce a signed certificate embedding the certified Δ and adjusted performance claims for regulatory submission, wherein the adjusted performance claim applies the certified gap as a conservative correction to simulated safety KPI values

depClaims: Hierarchical scenario classification system partitioning the scenario corpus into equivalence classes (by weather, speed, road type) with per-class validity certificates enabling fine-grained simulation credit allocation
Automated NeRF/Gaussian-splatting reconstruction pipeline for rapid paired scenario generation from fleet data logs without manual scene authoring
Bayesian simulation parameter calibration using gap measurements to automatically tune simulation sensor noise models, dynamics models, and rendering parameters to minimize Δ across scenario classes
Regulator-facing dashboard displaying simulation validity heatmaps across the ODD coverage grid with per-class Δ values and confidence intervals

Credit trading protocol enabling simulation validity certificates from multiple scenario classes to be combined into a composite safety confidence credit recognized by NHTSA for safety case mileage calculations
Extension to non-automotive domains: applying SRVC to surgical robot simulation validation (FDA AI/ML software guidance) and UAV simulation-to-flight gap certification (FAA AC for UAM)

refs.ref: NVIDIA Sim2Val (arXiv 2506.20553, NVLabs 2025) — simulation-to-real AV safety validation framework
refs.diff: NVIDIA's academic Sim2Val uses paired samples to reduce required real-world miles but does not produce signed regulatory validity certificates, does not use conformal statistical tests for certified gap bounds, and does not generate gap-adjusted performance claims for regulatory submission — present invention covers all three novel elements

refs.ref: US20190303759A1 (NVIDIA — training/verifying autonomous machines via simulation)
refs.diff: NVIDIA patent covers simulation infrastructure for DNN training/validation; present invention covers statistical certification of simulation validity for regulatory credit — different purpose

refs.ref: NVIDIA DRIVE Sim camera model validation (NVIDIA Technical Blog, 2021)
refs.diff: Camera model validation for simulation fidelity using color checker; present invention provides system-level safety KPI validity certification across all scenario classes with conformal statistical bounds

refs.ref: UL 4600 dynamic safety case requirements
refs.diff: Regulatory standard requiring simulation evidence; present invention is the technical apparatus producing that evidence — implementation fills the regulatory gap

ideas.ftoJ: NVIDIA's Sim2Val project (arxiv 2506.20553, 2025) is very recent and may result in patent filings that partially overlap the paired scenario construction claim. Recommend design-around strategy: narrow independent claims to the conformal gap certification and validity certificate format, which are clearly absent from NVIDIA's work. Monitor NVIDIA patent applications in classes G06F 11/36 and G06T 17/00 through Q2 2026.

ps.s101n: System apparatus with specific hardware modules tied to regulatory submission for physical vehicle certification. Practical application is clear. Low §101 risk.
ps.s102n: Conformal two-sample testing for certified Δ gap bound is novel in this context. Signed validity certificate with gap-adjusted claims is novel. Regulatory credit trading protocol is novel.
ps.s103n: NVIDIA Sim2Val creates some obviousness risk for the paired scenario construction element. Differentiate on: conformal statistical testing (not used by NVIDIA), signed certificate format, and gap-adjusted regulatory submission integration. Non-obviousness supported by the cross-domain combination of statistical testing, neural scene reconstruction, and regulatory compliance.
ps.s112n: NeRF and Gaussian splatting are well-documented. Conformal two-sample tests are standard statistical methods. Digital twin dynamics modeling is well-established. Enablement strong.

ideas.valM: Every AV OEM and robotics company building their safety case on simulation miles needs a validity certifier. If regulatory bodies (NHTSA, EASA) formally recognize simulation credit, the licensing value increases significantly. Conservative estimate based on current regulatory uncertainty; could reach $25M+ if NHTSA formalizes simulation miles in safety case guidance.

ideas.title: Decentralized Multi-Agent Safety Consensus Protocol with Byzantine-Fault-Tolerant Safety Property Verification for Autonomous Vehicle Fleets
ideas.desc:
As autonomous vehicle fleets grow, safety decisions increasingly involve multi-agent coordination — intersections, merges, emergency corridors, and platooning all require that a group of autonomous agents collectively satisfy safety properties that no single agent can verify alone. Existing multi-agent coordination approaches (V2X communication, cooperative path planning) exchange position and velocity data but lack a mechanism for formally verifying that the collective behavior of the agent group satisfies a specified safety invariant, particularly in the presence of compromised or faulty agents (Byzantine faults — agents transmitting incorrect or malicious safety data). This invention introduces a 'Decentralized Safety Consensus Protocol' (DSCP) that enables a group of autonomous agents (vehicles, drones, robots) to collectively verify and maintain a safety property using a Byzantine-fault-tolerant distributed protocol.

The DSCP operates as a three-layer system: (1) a 'Safety Property Specification Layer' where each agent holds a copy of a formal safety invariant (expressed in metric temporal logic, MTL) describing the collective behavior required — e.g., 'no two vehicles occupy the same lateral corridor simultaneously within 2 seconds'; (2) a 'Distributed Witness Layer' where each agent computes a local 'safety witness' — a compact cryptographic proof that its own planned trajectory satisfies its portion of the collective safety invariant — and broadcasts it to peers; and (3) a 'Byzantine-Fault-Tolerant Aggregator' that applies a threshold signature scheme (at least f+1 of n agents must contribute valid safety witnesses before the collective behavior is certified as safe), where f is the maximum number of Byzantine agents the system tolerates. If insufficient valid witnesses are received, the DSCP triggers a coordinated degradation protocol directing all agents to their individually-verified minimal risk conditions.

Critical novelty: (a) the combination of MTL-specified collective safety invariants with BFT threshold signature aggregation — enabling formally verified multi-agent safety without centralized coordination infrastructure; (b) the compact safety witness format using zero-knowledge proofs of trajectory satisfaction — ensuring Byzantine agents cannot forge valid witnesses without computational intractability assumptions; and (c) the graceful collective degradation protocol — when Byzantine tolerance is exceeded, the system transitions to individual MRC states in a coordinated sequence that avoids secondary collisions during the degradation process itself.

claim.preamble: A decentralized multi-agent safety verification system for a fleet of autonomous vehicles or robotic agents, the system comprising:
limitations: at each agent of the fleet: a safety property specification module configured to store a machine-readable formal safety invariant expressed in metric temporal logic (MTL) that specifies a collective behavior constraint applicable to the plurality of agents

at each agent: a safety witness generator configured to compute a compact cryptographic safety witness proving, using a zero-knowledge proof construction, that the agent's planned trajectory satisfies its portion of the collective safety invariant over a prediction horizon

at each agent: a Byzantine-fault-tolerant aggregator configured to receive safety witnesses from neighboring agents and apply a threshold signature scheme requiring valid witnesses from at least a quorum of agents before certifying the collective behavior as satisfying the safety invariant

at each agent: a coordinated degradation controller configured to, upon detecting that a sufficient quorum of valid witnesses cannot be assembled within a defined time window, initiate a sequenced individual minimal-risk-condition transition protocol that coordinates transition timing across the fleet to prevent secondary collision events during degradation

depClaims: Privacy-preserving witness exchange using homomorphic encryption enabling safety witnesses to prove trajectory safety properties without revealing full trajectory data to competing agents from different manufacturers
Reputation scoring system tracking per-agent witness validity history and dynamically adjusting the Byzantine tolerance threshold f based on observed fleet behavior patterns
Integration with V2X infrastructure units (RSUs) as trusted witness anchors providing signed infrastructure-side trajectory observations to supplement agent-generated witnesses
Cross-OEM interoperability protocol enabling safety witnesses from heterogeneous autonomous system software stacks to be validated by a common standardized witness verification API
Regulatory evidence generation: automated creation of multi-agent safety interaction logs formatted for NHTSA Connected and Automated Vehicle reporting and EU ITS Directive C-ITS compliance

Extension to mixed human/autonomous fleets: human-driven vehicles contribute partial safety witnesses based on V2I/V2V sensor data, enabling partial collective safety invariant verification even when human vehicles cannot compute full witnesses

refs.ref: US8788121B2 — Multi-autonomous vehicle path coordination (Proxy Aviation Systems)
refs.diff: Prior art coordinates paths to avoid collisions via planned path exchange; present invention formally verifies a collective safety invariant (MTL specification) using cryptographic safety witnesses and Byzantine-fault tolerance — fundamentally stronger guarantee and different mechanism

refs.ref: Mobileye RSS (US20220388505A1 — VRU safety with RSS checks)
refs.diff: Mobileye RSS performs per-vehicle safety checks with V2X; present invention performs distributed consensus on a collectively-specified safety invariant with BFT tolerance — multi-agent verification vs. per-agent rule application

refs.ref: Farrell et al. — Formal methods for IoV self-coordination (ICCL 2021)
refs.diff: Academic work on logic-based IoV coordination using CGS; present invention adds the cryptographic safety witness format, BFT threshold signature aggregation, and zero-knowledge proof construction — all absent from prior work, and patents the practical apparatus

refs.ref: Eprints Southampton — Formal methods for IoV self-coordination (formal methods paper)
refs.diff: Theoretical model checking approach; present invention implements distributed real-time protocol with cryptographic enforcement suitable for physical deployment

ideas.ftoJ: No identified blocking IP for the combination of MTL safety invariants + BFT threshold signatures + ZK-proof safety witnesses in autonomous vehicle fleets. V2X coordination patents cover data exchange only. Mobileye RSS covers per-vehicle kinematic checks. The cryptographic multi-agent safety consensus architecture is unpatented territory.

ps.s101n: Potential §101 challenge on the cryptographic/mathematical elements. Recommend emphasizing the physical apparatus claim structure: each agent's hardware module, the V2X physical communication layer, and the coordinated degradation controller that actuates physical vehicle state transitions. The zero-knowledge proof and BFT aggregator should be framed as hardware security modules (HSMs) rather than pure algorithms.
ps.s102n: No prior art combines MTL collective safety invariants + BFT threshold signatures + ZK safety witnesses + coordinated degradation in a multi-autonomous-vehicle context. The combination is highly novel.
ps.s103n: Combining distributed systems (BFT consensus), formal verification (MTL model checking), cryptography (ZK proofs, threshold signatures), and autonomous vehicle safety creates a highly non-obvious cross-domain combination. No single field produces a PHOSITA who would combine all four.
ps.s112n: MTL is well-documented. BFT threshold signature schemes (PBFT, Tendermint variants) are established. ZK proof constructions for trajectory properties require more detailed specification (suggest using SNARK circuit description for trajectory satisfaction in the patent body). Enablement achievable with detailed technical description.

ideas.valM: High strategic value for intersection management, platooning safety, and autonomous logistics fleets. Cross-industry applicability to drone swarms (military/commercial), industrial automation cell safety, and surgical robot coordination. Licensing leverage increases as connected vehicle infrastructure (C-ITS) mandates become enforced in EU and U.S. V2I deployment programs.

ideas.title: Graded-CTE Composite Ceramic Substrate with Interlocked Compliance Zones for SiC Power Module Thermal Cycling Immunity

desc.paragraph_1: Conventional direct-bonded copper (DBC) substrates using alumina (Al₂O₃, CTE ~8.2 ppm/°C) or aluminum nitride (AlN, CTE ~4.6 ppm/°C) cause fatigue cracking at the copper-ceramic interface when temperature cycles exceed ±150°C, the operating regime demanded by automotive SiC modules. Silicon nitride (Si₃N₄, CTE ~3 ppm/°C, flexural strength ~830 MPa) offers superior fracture toughness but standard thick-copper metallization over Si₃N₄ causes delamination under wide-range thermal cycling (demonstrated experimentally after ~500 cycles at -40°C to +350°C). Existing CTE-matched plates (AlSiC, Cu-Mo) introduce high elastic moduli that transfer stress into the ceramic rather than absorbing it.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention discloses a multi-zone composite ceramic substrate in which the base insulating layer is fabricated from a Si₃N₄/AlN functionally graded ceramic (FGC) bilayer, with a continuous CTE gradient from ~3 ppm/°C at the die-contact face to ~4.6 ppm/°C at the thermal interface. The copper metallization layers are replaced with a patterned copper-tungsten (Cu-W) metal matrix composite (MMC) in the high-stress perimeter zones of each die pad, transitioning to pure copper in the low-stress central conduction path. Mechanically, interlocked stress-relief trenches are laser-machined into the Cu-W perimeter rings, creating controlled compliance features that absorb shear stress during thermal excursion without fracturing the ceramic.
desc.paragraph_3: The differentiating novelty is the combination of (a) compositionally graded Si₃N₄/AlN ceramic, (b) zone-differentiated Cu/Cu-W metallization patterned to die footprint, and (c) interlocked compliance trenches designed by finite-element lifetime optimization. This creates a substrate that achieves >100,000 thermal cycles from -55°C to +300°C while maintaining <10% increase in thermal resistance—a performance envelope no existing commercial substrate achieves. The structure is designed as a direct form-factor replacement for standard DBC/AMB substrates, requiring no changes to power module assembly flow beyond substrate substitution.

claim.preamble: A power semiconductor substrate comprising:
limitations: (a) a ceramic insulating layer comprising a compositionally graded bilayer of silicon nitride (Si₃N₄) and aluminum nitride (AlN), wherein the Si₃N₄ content is highest at a first principal surface and transitions to highest AlN content at a second principal surface opposite the first principal surface, producing a net CTE gradient between 2.9 ppm/°C and 4.6 ppm/°C across the layer thickness;

(b) a patterned first metallization bonded to the first principal surface, wherein die-pad perimeter zones of the first metallization comprise a copper-tungsten metal matrix composite having a CTE between 7 ppm/°C and 9 ppm/°C, and central conduction zones of the first metallization comprise copper;

(c) stress-relief compliance features formed in the perimeter zones of the first metallization, the compliance features comprising interlocked trench patterns having a depth of 30–60% of the metallization thickness;
(d) a second metallization bonded to the second principal surface configured for thermal interface attachment to a heat spreader or cooler.

depClaims: Si₃N₄/AlN grading profile optimized by finite-element analysis for specific SiC die dimensions and thermal cycle range
Active metal brazing (AMB) process for Si₃N₄/AlN composite ceramic layer formation
Trench compliance geometry designed by machine-learning stress optimizer trained on power cycling failure data
Incorporation of distributed thermal via arrays in the Cu-W perimeter zone for radial heat spreading
Surface passivation of Si₃N₄/AlN grain boundaries with nanoscale Y₂O₃ or MgO sintering aids for fracture toughness enhancement
Dual-sided symmetric metallization pattern to balance substrate warpage during reflow and sintering
Co-designed substrate geometry accommodating double-sided cooled module architecture with matched manifold interfaces

refs.ref: US9041183B2 (Toyota) — Double-sided cooled power module with DBC substrates
refs.diff: Claims sandwich DBC cooling but uses standard AlN or Al₂O₃ without CTE grading or compliance trench metallization; does not address >250°C sustained operation regime

refs.ref: IMAPS HiTEN 2011 — Si₃N₄ High Temperature Power Module (AFRL/Auburn)
refs.diff: Demonstrates Si₃N₄ substrate limitations with thick Cu—this invention solves that exact failure mode via Cu-W perimeter zoning and compliance trenches that AFRL did not explore

refs.ref: Wolfspeed BM/XM3 modules — AlSiC baseplate for CTE compliance
refs.diff: AlSiC baseplates address CTE at the package-cooler interface, not at the ceramic-die interface; Wolfspeed patents (H01L23/4334) do not claim graded-ceramic substrate technology

refs.ref: EP3276647 (Infineon) — Power module substrate with stress relief
refs.diff: Infineon's approach uses discrete expansion slots in copper; does not combine graded ceramic with zone-differentiated MMC metallization

ideas.ftoJ: No identified blocking claims specifically covering compositionally graded Si₃N₄/AlN ceramic substrates combined with zone-differentiated Cu/Cu-W metallization and mechanical compliance features. AlSiC baseplate patents (Curamik/Rogers) are method patents covering a different structure. Si₃N₄ AMB substrate patents (Toshiba, Denka) cover ceramic composition but not this dual-zone metallization architecture.

ps.s101n: Physical substrate apparatus claim — unambiguously patent-eligible. No Alice/Mayo risk.
ps.s102n: Graded Si₃N₄/AlN ceramic exists in academic literature; Cu-W MMC metallization exists independently; the combination with interlocked compliance features targeting WBG temperature ranges is novel.
ps.s103n: POSITA would not obviously combine: (1) Si₃N₄/AlN FGC technology (ceramic research domain), (2) Cu-W zone metallization (power electronics packaging), and (3) laser-machined compliance trenches (precision manufacturing) without the specific failure mode analysis disclosed. Teaching away from Si₃N₄ thick-Cu in HiTEN 2011 literature supports non-obviousness.
ps.s112n: Graded ceramic fabrication by tape casting / co-sintering is well-characterized; Cu-W MMC patterning via masked deposition is enableable; FEA trench design methodology is describable with sufficient specificity.

ideas.valM: Substrate is a standardized consumable in every power module; market for SiC power module substrates estimated at $300M–$500M by 2028. A chokepoint substrate patent enabling 300°C operation could achieve 5–8% royalty across the substrate supply chain. Probability-adjusted NPV over 15-year life at 60% adoption probability.

ideas.title: Pressureless Sintered Silver Die-Attach with Nitrogen-Atmosphere Oxide-Barrier Enclosure for Large-Area WBG Dies at 300°C+

desc.paragraph_1: Sintered silver die-attach has emerged as the replacement for high-lead solder in WBG packaging, providing a melting point of 961°C and thermal conductivity of 130–250 W/m·K. However, two barriers prevent widespread adoption for large-area dies (>8 mm × 8 mm): (a) pressurized sintering (20–40 MPa) risks fracturing SiC dies and requires complex tooling, and (b) pressureless sintered silver suffers a well-documented degradation mechanism when Au-metallized substrates are used at 300°C—oxygen-driven Ag surface diffusion forms a depletion layer adjacent to the die, reducing shear strength from >15 MPa to <5 MPa after 24 hours. This failure mode is suppressed when the Ag-on-Ag metallization system is used in an inert atmosphere, but no commercial module process seals the die-attach zone in nitrogen during assembly and field operation.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention discloses a pressureless sintered silver die-attach system comprising: (1) a standardized Ag-on-Ag metallization stack on both die backside and substrate (Ag-metallized SiC die + Ag thick-film or PdAg substrate), enabling shear strengths >15.3 MPa maintained after 3,000 hours at 300°C; (2) a micro-enclosure formed by a perimeter silver-sintered Cu-frame hermetically bonded around each die pad zone prior to die placement, creating a sealed nitrogen atmosphere micro-cavity that surrounds the die-attach region; (3) a nitrogen-pressurized backfill during the sintering furnace step using the micro-enclosure as an in-process atmospheric barrier. The micro-frame is co-sintered in the same thermal step as the die attach, eliminating extra process steps.
desc.paragraph_3: The critical differentiator is that the nitrogen micro-enclosure is a permanent structural element of the finished module—not just a process atmosphere—providing field protection against oxygen-driven Ag migration for the module's 20+ year service life. The Cu-frame also functions as an electrical isolation guard ring, blocking surface-leakage current paths at elevated temperatures. The combined Ag-on-Ag metallization + sealed N₂ micro-enclosure system enables >5,000 hours of reliable 300°C operation for large-area SiC dies, unlocking the full thermal capability of SiC in standard module form factors without pressure tooling.

claim.preamble: A power semiconductor assembly comprising:
limitations: (a) a substrate having a die-mount surface comprising a silver-containing metallization layer;
(b) a wide-bandgap semiconductor die having a backside metallization comprising a silver-containing layer, the die being bonded to the die-mount surface by a pressureless-sintered silver joint;

(c) a hermetic micro-enclosure comprising a copper or silver frame surrounding a perimeter of the pressureless-sintered silver joint, the micro-enclosure being bonded to the die-mount surface by a co-sintered silver joint and defining a sealed cavity between the substrate and the wide-bandgap semiconductor die;
(d) an inert gas atmosphere enclosed within the sealed cavity.

depClaims: Silver-on-silver metallization system with specific Ag layer thickness ranges for die backside and substrate
PdAg thick-film substrate metallization variant for improved long-term adhesion
Copper frame geometry incorporating electrical guard-ring function with defined standoff height for controlled die-attach layer thickness
Process method claim: co-sintering of die-attach and micro-enclosure frame in nitrogen-atmosphere furnace in a single thermal step
Integration with double-sided cooled module architecture where the micro-enclosure is electrically connected to the top DBC substrate
Real-time void detection by acoustic scanning (SAM) of the sealed joint using enclosure resonance characteristics
Extension to multi-die arrays with shared hermetic enclosure covering multiple SiC devices in a phase-leg configuration

refs.ref: US9583453B2 (Ormet Circuits) — Sintering die-attach materials
refs.diff: Claims sintering paste composition and general method; does not disclose hermetic micro-enclosure frame co-sintered in nitrogen atmosphere to prevent Ag migration in field operation

refs.ref: IMAPS HiTEC 2014 (Tennessee Tech / Auburn) — Pressureless Ag sintering for 300°C
refs.diff: Academic work demonstrates the nitrogen atmosphere finding but only as a process atmosphere—the invention converts this to a permanent structural hermetic enclosure, which is undisclosed

refs.ref: IMAPS HiTEN 2011 (Nottingham) — Silver sintering bonding process
refs.diff: Addresses pressurized sintering for small dies; does not address large-die oxygen migration or nitrogen micro-enclosure architecture

refs.ref: Wolfspeed Gen 4 YM module (2025) — sintered die attach with epoxy encapsulant
refs.diff: Wolfspeed's sintered die-attach uses pressure sintering and epoxy overmold (not nitrogen hermetic enclosure); specific claim domain around nitrogen micro-enclosure is unoccupied

ideas.ftoJ: Extensive search of Wolfspeed, Infineon, Rohm, and ST patent portfolios finds no claims on co-sintered hermetic micro-enclosures providing field-life nitrogen atmosphere for die-attach protection. Ormet and IXYS sintered die-attach patents address composition and process but not the sealed enclosure structure. This is a composition-of-matter/apparatus claim with clear white space.

ps.s101n: Physical apparatus—semiconductor assembly with hermetic micro-enclosure. Clearly patent-eligible.
ps.s102n: Hermetic co-sintered copper frame forming a nitrogen-filled cavity around die-attach is not found in patent or literature prior art. The N₂-prevents-Ag-depletion mechanism was published but the structural embodiment is novel.
ps.s103n: Gap between published lab finding (N₂ atmosphere suppresses depletion) and structural hermetic enclosure is non-obvious—POSITA would not routinely convert a process atmosphere into a permanent co-sintered structural feature. Significant structural design choices (frame height, co-sintering sequence) provide additional non-obviousness.
ps.s112n: Co-sintering of Cu or Ag frames alongside die-attach is mechanically feasible and process conditions (temperature, atmosphere) are definable. Hermetic seal qualification metrics (He leak rate) are standard practice in module industry.

ideas.valM: Die-attach is the single highest-reliability-risk process step in WBG module assembly. A patent blocking the only viable large-area, 300°C-capable pressureless process creates a strong licensing position vs. all module OEMs. Royalty basis: $0.50–$2.00 per module die pad at 20M WBG module shipments by 2030.

ideas.title: Double-Sided Direct Liquid Cooled WBG Power Module with Asymmetric Manifold Compliance Structures for Differential Thermal Expansion Management

desc.paragraph_1: Double-sided cooled (DSC) power modules eliminate the module-to-cooler thermal interface resistance (~0.2–0.5 cm²·°C/W), achieving specific thermal resistances as low as 0.33 cm²·°C/W vs. 0.54 cm²·°C/W for single-sided modules (Zhu et al., ORNL/DOE OSTI report). However, existing DSC designs (US9041183B2, Toyota/Denso automotive) use rigid DBC sandwich structures bonded to separate cooler plates with thermal grease or solder—reintroducing interface resistance and mechanical rigidity that prevents compliant CTE accommodation during thermal cycling. WBG operation at ΔTj up to 175°C imposes cyclic tensile/compressive loads on the top and bottom cooler interfaces that no existing DSC architecture accommodates without performance degradation.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention discloses a monolithic double-sided direct liquid cooled (DS-DLC) WBG module in which the coolant manifold is integrally fabricated from a Cu-SiC metal matrix composite (CTE ~8.5 ppm/°C, thermal conductivity ~175 W/m·K) that is direct-bonded to the outer copper layers of the DBC substrates via active metal brazing (AMB). The manifold incorporates asymmetric compliance pleats—corrugated wall sections with controlled buckling geometry—on the die-side inner walls of the coolant channels. These pleats elastically absorb differential thermal expansion between the SiC dies (CTE ~4.6 ppm/°C) and the Cu-SiC manifold (~8.5 ppm/°C) during power cycling, preventing delamination at the AMB bond line without thermal grease interfaces.
desc.paragraph_3: The module is sealed by laser-welded perimeter joints with a compliant silver-indium alloy filler at the manifold-DBC interface, enabling assembly without the high-temperature vacuum brazing furnace step used in current designs. The coolant channels incorporate pin-fin arrays with graded density—higher fin density directly beneath the die footprint, tapering to lower density at channel periphery—providing uniform junction temperature distribution across parallel-connected multi-die configurations. This architecture achieves a >50% improvement in thermal resistance vs. best-in-class single-sided modules while enabling the >100,000 power cycle life required for EV traction inverters.

claim.preamble: A double-sided direct liquid cooled power module comprising:
limitations: (a) a first ceramic substrate and a second ceramic substrate arranged in a sandwich configuration with at least one wide-bandgap semiconductor die disposed therebetween, the wide-bandgap semiconductor die being bonded to the first and second ceramic substrates by sintered die-attach layers;

(b) a first coolant manifold integrally bonded to an outer surface of the first ceramic substrate and defining a first coolant flow channel in direct thermal contact with the first ceramic substrate without an intervening thermal interface material;

(c) a second coolant manifold integrally bonded to an outer surface of the second ceramic substrate and defining a second coolant flow channel in direct thermal contact with the second ceramic substrate without an intervening thermal interface material;

(d) compliance structures formed in walls of the first and second coolant manifolds, the compliance structures being configured to elastically deform during thermal cycling of the module to accommodate differential thermal expansion between the wide-bandgap semiconductor die and the coolant manifolds.

depClaims: Cu-SiC metal matrix composite manifold material with CTE between 7.5 and 10 ppm/°C for partial CTE bridging
Asymmetric corrugated compliance pleat geometry with defined buckling depth and spacing derived from FEA thermal cycle optimization
Graded pin-fin density distribution in coolant channels corresponding to spatial power density distribution of the semiconductor die array
Laser-welded perimeter seal with silver-indium compliant filler at manifold-substrate bond interface
Integrated coolant flow metering structures for impedance-matched parallel cooling of multi-die half-bridge configuration
Electrical isolation of coolant manifold from DBC substrate achieved through ceramic-filled polymer spray coating of coolant channel interior
Method for fabricating the monolithic DS-DLC module by sequential AMB bonding, manifold press-forming, and laser perimeter sealing

refs.diff: Claims DBC sandwich with separate coolant plates; no integral manifold, no compliance structures, and relies on thermal grease—this invention eliminates all three limitations

refs.ref: OSTI/ORNL DOE report — Integrated Double Sided Cooling Packaging of Planar SiC Power Modules (Zhu et al.)

refs.ref: US9275926B2 (Infineon) — Power module with cooling structure on bonding substrate

The integral AMB-bonded manifold with compliance structures is not claimed by these patents. Design-around is straightforward by reciting Cu-SiC MMC material and AMB bonding explicitly.

ideas.valM: DS-DLC is the enabling architecture for next-generation EV traction inverters targeting >50 kW/L power density. The market for automotive SiC inverters is projected at $4B+ by 2028. A foundational DS-DLC patent covering the compliance-structure-integral-manifold architecture could achieve 1–3% royalty on module ASPs across automotive module makers. High-value licensing target for Toyota, Denso, Continental, BorgWarner.

ideas.title: Co-Packaged WBG Power Module with In-Module SiC CMOS Gate Driver Die for Sub-Nanosecond Propagation Delay and Kelvin-Source Isolation at 300°C

desc.paragraph_1: The switching speed advantage of SiC MOSFETs (dV/dt up to 100 kV/µs) is severely limited by the parasitic inductance and capacitance between the power die and the external gate driver PCB. External gate drivers introduce 5–20 ns propagation delays and 5–15 nH common-source inductance that forces designers to slow down switching to prevent ringing—negating the primary benefit of WBG devices. The industry has recognized that co-packaged gate drivers are needed, but Si-based gate driver ICs fail above ~175°C junction temperature, preventing true co-integration inside a 300°C-capable WBG module. Recent research at Fraunhofer IISB demonstrated a SiC CMOS gate driver IC fabricated at 1 µm node that functions at elevated temperatures, but the packaging integration of this die within a power module is undisclosed.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention discloses a heterogeneous co-packaged WBG power module in which a SiC CMOS gate driver die (GD die) is directly co-sintered onto the DBC substrate of the power module in close proximity (<5 mm) to each SiC MOSFET power die. The GD die is attached using pressureless sintered silver to a dedicated isolated copper island on the DBC substrate, providing electrical isolation from the power circuit while sharing the thermal management infrastructure. A multi-layer Cu interconnect bridge—fabricated from a sintered copper vertical interconnect structure (Cu-VIS)—connects the gate driver output pad directly to the MOSFET gate/source Kelvin pads, replacing the wire-bond connection with a rigid, low-inductance interconnect that maintains <0.5 nH total gate loop inductance.
desc.paragraph_3: The critical differentiation is the Kelvin-source isolation architecture: the Cu-VIS bridge connects the gate driver GND directly to the MOSFET Kelvin source pin (not the power source pin), eliminating common-source inductance—the primary cause of oscillation in high-speed WBG switching. The GD die operates up to 300°C by virtue of SiC CMOS material properties, maintaining gate drive voltage accuracy within ±2% across -55°C to +300°C. This enables switching dV/dt of >50 kV/µs without external snubbers, achieving >30% switching loss reduction vs. conventional external gate driver designs in the same module footprint.

claim.preamble: A co-packaged semiconductor power module comprising:
limitations: (a) a substrate having an electrically conductive first region and an electrically conductive second region isolated from the first region;
(b) at least one wide-bandgap power semiconductor die bonded to the first region, the power semiconductor die having a gate terminal, a power source terminal, and a Kelvin source terminal;

(c) a gate driver integrated circuit die comprising silicon carbide CMOS circuitry, the gate driver die bonded to the second region by a sintered metal die-attach, the gate driver die configured to operate at junction temperatures up to 300°C;
(d) a rigid low-inductance interconnect structure directly connecting an output of the gate driver integrated circuit die to the gate terminal of the wide-bandgap power semiconductor die; and

(e) a Kelvin-source return path directly connecting a reference terminal of the gate driver integrated circuit die to the Kelvin source terminal of the wide-bandgap power semiconductor die, isolated from the power source terminal.

depClaims: Cu-VIS (sintered copper vertical interconnect structure) geometry for <0.5 nH gate loop inductance
Adaptive gate driver algorithm for real-time switching speed modulation based on in-module temperature sensor feedback
Multi-die phase-leg configuration with independent Kelvin-source isolation for each high-side and low-side MOSFET
Current imbalance detection and active balancing between parallel-connected SiC MOSFETs using integrated current sense resistors on the GD die
Overvoltage protection (DESAT) detection circuit integrated into the SiC CMOS gate driver die with <100 ns response time
Signal isolation interface between module-internal SiC gate driver and module-external PWM controller using on-substrate magnetic or capacitive coupling
Thermal co-management strategy: GD die positioned in thermal shadow of power die to minimize GD die junction temperature rise

refs.ref: US20240072637A1 (Fraunhofer IISB / DOE) — Adaptive SiC gate drivers in integrated circuit form
refs.diff: Claims the SiC CMOS gate driver IC concept and in-module placement but does not claim the Cu-VIS rigid interconnect bridge, Kelvin-source isolation architecture within the package, or specific co-sintered die-attach configuration—all of which are claimed here

refs.ref: Wolfspeed Gen4 YM module — integrated gate signal traces
refs.diff: YM module integrates gate signal PCB traces but uses Si gate driver outside module; Wolfspeed patents do not claim SiC-CMOS gate driver co-integration at 300°C

refs.ref: GaN Systems GS-EVB-HB-61008P series — integrated gate driver module
refs.diff: GaN Systems' approach integrates Si gate driver into GaN module for <150°C operation; no 300°C capable SiC-CMOS gate driver integration is claimed or demonstrated

refs.ref: US10084310B1 — Low-inductance DC power bus (MLCC-based)
refs.diff: Addresses DC link inductance reduction; does not address gate loop inductance or gate driver co-integration

ideas.ftoJ: The specific combination of SiC CMOS gate driver co-sintered within a WBG power module with Cu-VIS rigid gate interconnect and Kelvin-source isolation is not claimed by any identified incumbent patent. Fraunhofer IISB's DOE-funded work covers the IC design but not the module-level integration architecture. The Kelvin-source isolation routing within the package is a novel structural element with no identified prior art.

ps.s101n: Physical apparatus—co-packaged module structure. Clearly patent-eligible.
ps.s102n: Co-sintered SiC CMOS gate driver die within WBG module with Kelvin-source isolation via rigid Cu-VIS bridge is novel—not found in any identified patent or publication.
ps.s103n: SiC CMOS gate driver IC (Fraunhofer), sintered die-attach (Ormet), and Kelvin source concepts (Wolfspeed) exist separately. Combining all three in a co-packaged 300°C module with a rigid low-inductance bridge is non-obvious—each component requires distinct expertise and the combination is not suggested in any reference.
ps.s112n: SiC CMOS fabrication at Fraunhofer IISB is established; Cu vertical interconnect sintering is standard in advanced packaging; Kelvin source wiring is well-known. Full enablement feasible.

ideas.valM: Co-packaged gate drivers are the next performance frontier for WBG modules. Every EV traction inverter, industrial drive, and grid converter with WBG devices needs a gate driver—this patent creates leverage across the full module supply chain. High strategic value as a blocking patent against Wolfspeed, Infineon, and Rohm's next-generation module platforms. Royalty basis: $2–$5 per gate driver channel at 50M+ WBG module channels by 2030.

ideas.title: Nanocomposite Fluoroelastomer Encapsulant with Boron Nitride Nanoplatelet Thermal Filler for WBG Power Modules at 300°C and 15 kV/mm

desc.paragraph_1: Current WBG module encapsulants are divided into two problematic categories: (1) silicone gels with low elastic modulus (mechanical compliance) but cracking above 250°C and Ag-ion migration susceptibility in high-electric-field environments at elevated temperature; and (2) epoxy overmold with excellent mechanical protection but high Young's modulus (~15 GPa) that transmits large thermal expansion stresses directly to the ceramic substrate and die. Neither material satisfies the combined requirement of >300°C thermal stability, >15 kV/mm dielectric strength, low modulus (<100 MPa) for stress relief, and >200 W/m·K thermal conductivity at temperatures where WBG devices can realistically operate. Polyimide films are promising thermally but have high processing temperatures that are incompatible with module assembly sequences.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention discloses a nanocomposite fluoroelastomer encapsulant formulated from a perfluoropolyether (PFPE) elastomer base matrix loaded with surface-functionalized hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) nanoplatelets at 40–60 vol% fill fraction. The h-BN nanoplatelets are edge-functionalized with fluorinated silane coupling agents to maximize dispersion in the PFPE matrix and minimize interfacial thermal resistance, achieving bulk thermal conductivity of 15–25 W/m·K while maintaining a Young's modulus below 50 MPa. The PFPE elastomer provides intrinsic thermal stability to >350°C (decomposition onset), UV-cure processability at <120°C for in-module dispensing compatibility, and chemical resistance to dielectric cooling fluids used in liquid-cooled WBG systems.
desc.paragraph_3: The nanocomposite is formulated to achieve dielectric strength >15 kV/mm at 300°C (confirmed by leakage current measurements <1 µA at 12 kV, 275°C—the threshold identified in IMAPS literature for WBG module acceptance). Critically, the h-BN nanoplatelets are oriented in-plane with the DBC substrate surface by shear-alignment during dispensing, creating anisotropic thermal conductivity: >20 W/m·K lateral (heat spreading across die periphery) vs. ~5 W/m·K vertical (limiting encapsulant contribution to the thermal stack), while maintaining mechanical compliance. This orientation effect is a novel processing-structure-property relationship that does not occur with isotropic spherical fillers used in prior art encapsulants.

claim.preamble: A thermally conductive encapsulant composition for wide-bandgap semiconductor power modules comprising:
limitations: (a) a perfluoropolyether elastomer matrix having a decomposition onset temperature above 320°C and a Young's modulus below 100 MPa at 25°C;
(b) hexagonal boron nitride nanoplatelets having a lateral dimension between 0.5 µm and 5 µm and a thickness less than 100 nm, present in the composition at a loading of 35 to 65 vol%;
(c) a fluorinated silane coupling agent bonded to edges of the hexagonal boron nitride nanoplatelets providing compatibility with the perfluoropolyether elastomer matrix;
(d) wherein the composition exhibits a thermal conductivity greater than 10 W/m·K, a dielectric strength greater than 12 kV/mm at 300°C, and a Young's modulus less than 100 MPa.

depClaims: Specific h-BN nanoplatelet aspect ratio (>10:1) and fluorinated silane coupling agent chemistry for maximum platelet alignment during shear dispensing
UV-cure initiation system compatible with the PFPE matrix for in-module cure at <120°C without high-temperature post-cure
Anisotropic thermal conductivity ratio (lateral:vertical > 4:1) achieved by shear-alignment during dispensing process
Doping of the PFPE matrix with mobile-ion scavengers (zeolite nanoparticles) to prevent Ag+ migration-induced dielectric degradation
Method of application by automated needle dispensing under shear conditions that preferentially align h-BN platelets parallel to the DBC substrate
Combination of PFPE encapsulant with pressureless sintered Ag die-attach in a fully halogen-free, lead-free high-temperature module system
Incorporation of luminescent thermal-imaging dopants (rare-earth phosphors) in the encapsulant for non-contact junction temperature mapping through the encapsulant layer

refs.ref: IMAPS 2021 — Characterization of Silicone Gel for High Temperature Encapsulation (WACKER Chemie)
refs.diff: WACKER's silicone gels achieve 275°C operation but exhibit cracking above 250°C under long-term aging. The PFPE/h-BN nanocomposite is fundamentally different in base polymer chemistry (fluorocarbon vs. silicone) with superior thermal stability and higher thermal conductivity

refs.ref: IEEE Trans. Power Electronics (2014) — Evaluation of Encapsulation Materials for HT Power Device Packaging
refs.diff: Compares silicone gel vs. elastomer at 300°C; identifies PFPE elastomer as promising but does not disclose h-BN nanoplatelet loading, fluorinated coupling agents, or anisotropic alignment—all critical inventive elements

refs.ref: Dow Corning / Momentive silicone encapsulant IP portfolio (H01L23/29)
refs.diff: All silicone-based; fluoroelastomer PFPE chemistry is structurally distinct. Silicone patents do not claim fluorocarbon matrices or h-BN nanoplatelet functionalization chemistry

refs.ref: Wolfspeed YM module — epoxy encapsulant (2025)
refs.diff: Wolfspeed's epoxy overmold has high modulus (~12 GPa) unsuitable for stress-sensitive SiC dies >250°C; no fluoroelastomer or h-BN nanocomposite approach in Wolfspeed patents

ideas.ftoJ: PFPE fluoroelastomers and h-BN nanocomposites are known individually in high-temperature and thermally conductive material literature, but no identified patent covers the specific combination of PFPE matrix + edge-fluorinated h-BN nanoplatelets at >35 vol% targeted at WBG power module encapsulation at >300°C. Composition of matter claim with specific property thresholds provides strong, defensible scope.

ps.s101n: Composition of matter—clearly patent-eligible.
ps.s102n: PFPE elastomers with h-BN nanoplatelets at WBG-targeted formulations with fluorinated coupling agents are not found in the patent literature. The specific property combination (>10 W/m·K, >12 kV/mm at 300°C, <100 MPa modulus) defines a novel performance space.
ps.s103n: h-BN in silicone encapsulants exists; PFPE elastomers exist for high-temperature sealing; edge-functionalized h-BN with fluorinated silanes exists in materials science. The specific combination formulated for WBG module requirements with the shear-alignment anisotropy concept is non-obvious.
ps.s112n: PFPE synthesis is well-established; h-BN nanoplatelet production and edge functionalization is an active research area with published procedures. Formulation optimization for specific property targets is a routine experimental workflow for materials PHOSITA.

ideas.valM: Encapsulant is a standardized consumable in every power module; global power module encapsulant market estimated at ~$120M by 2027. Composition-of-matter patent with strong claims provides licensing leverage across all WBG module encapsulant suppliers. Secondary value as trade secret protection of specific formulation parameters.

ideas.title: Embedded Microfluidic Cooling Substrate for WBG Power Modules with Dielectric Coolant and Direct-Die Thermal Contact

desc.paragraph_1: Standard WBG power modules transfer heat through a thermal stack: die → die-attach → DBC copper → DBC ceramic → DBC copper → thermal interface material (TIM) → cooler cold plate. Each layer adds thermal resistance; the TIM alone contributes 0.3–0.8 cm²·°C/W. The ideal solution is direct immersion of the SiC die in dielectric coolant, but immersion cooling creates electrical isolation challenges and is incompatible with the wire-bond, substrate, and encapsulant architecture of conventional modules. Embedded microfluidic substrates—where coolant channels are machined or 3D-printed directly into the DBC substrate ceramic layer—have been proposed in academic work but not commercially realized, primarily because AlN and Al₂O₃ ceramics are brittle and prohibit the channel geometries needed for useful flow rates without fracture.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention discloses a WBG power module packaging architecture in which the conventional DBC ceramic substrate is replaced by a multilayer active metal brazed (AMB) Si₃N₄ substrate with embedded microfluidic channels machined into the ceramic using femtosecond laser ablation followed by conformal CVD silicon carbide channel lining. The SiC lining (2–5 µm) provides chemical resistance to fluorocarbon dielectric coolants (Novec, Fluorinert series) while maintaining the electrical insulation and thermal conductivity of the Si₃N₄ base. Coolant inlet/outlet manifolds are integrated directly into the substrate via laser-machined access ports sealed with AMB-bonded Ti-Cu-Ag preforms, enabling tooling-free module assembly.
desc.paragraph_3: The embedded microfluidic architecture eliminates all TIM layers, achieving an estimated thermal resistance of <0.10 cm²·°C/W—a 5× improvement over conventional modules. The direct-die thermal path (die → sintered Ag → Cu → Si₃N₄ channel wall → coolant) requires only the sintered die-attach and the 0.6 mm Si₃N₄ ceramic wall as thermal barriers. The femtosecond laser machining enables channel cross-sections optimized by computational fluid dynamics (CFD) to match the non-uniform power density distribution of multi-die SiC phase-leg configurations, achieving junction temperature uniformity across the die array within ±5°C at maximum rated power—critical for current sharing in parallel-die configurations.

claim.preamble: A power semiconductor substrate comprising:
limitations: (a) a ceramic insulating body comprising silicon nitride, the ceramic body having a first surface configured to receive a wide-bandgap semiconductor die and a second surface opposite the first surface;
(b) a plurality of microfluidic channels formed within the ceramic body between the first and second surfaces, each microfluidic channel having a hydraulic diameter between 0.2 mm and 2 mm;
(c) a silicon carbide lining deposited on interior surfaces of the microfluidic channels by chemical vapor deposition;
(d) at least one coolant inlet port and at least one coolant outlet port formed in the ceramic body and fluidically connected to the microfluidic channels; and
(e) electrically conductive metallization layers on the first surface configured for direct electrical connection to the wide-bandgap semiconductor die.

depClaims: Femtosecond laser ablation process for microfluidic channel formation in Si₃N₄ without inducing micro-crack propagation
CFD-optimized channel cross-section geometry matched to die power density distribution for uniform junction temperature
Fluorocarbon dielectric coolant compatibility of the CVD-SiC channel lining
Ti-Cu-Ag AMB preform sealing method for coolant manifold integration with substrate
Two-phase flow regime in microchannels for enhanced heat transfer coefficient at WBG switching transient peak power conditions
Integrated flow sensors and pressure sensors monolithically fabricated within the ceramic substrate for real-time coolant condition monitoring
Extension to a double-sided substrate where microfluidic channels serve both the top and bottom die-attach faces of a sandwich module

refs.ref: ORNL/DOE DSC packaging report — additive manufactured manifold coolant loops
refs.diff: ORNL work uses external additive-manufactured manifolds attached to DBC substrates; this invention embeds channels within the ceramic substrate itself—fundamentally different thermal architecture eliminating all TIM layers

refs.ref: US9275926B2 (Infineon) — Power module with cooling structure on bonding substrate
refs.diff: Infineon patent covers externally attached cooling structures on DBC surfaces; not embedded channels within the ceramic body

refs.ref: Fraunhofer IZM embedded power device PCB laminate work
refs.diff: PCB-laminate embedding is for low-power devices (<300 V); Si₃N₄ ceramic microfluidic substrate targets high-voltage (>1.2 kV) WBG applications with 10× higher thermal loads

refs.ref: Academic literature on microchannel coolers for power electronics (Tuckerman & Pease 1981 et al.)
refs.diff: Classic microchannel cooling is for Si devices on Si substrates; no prior art discloses femtosecond laser-machined channels in Si₃N₄ ceramic with CVD-SiC lining for WBG dielectric coolant compatibility

ideas.ftoJ: Microfluidic channel formation within Si₃N₄ ceramic substrate by femtosecond laser ablation combined with CVD-SiC channel lining for dielectric coolant compatibility is not found in any identified patent portfolio of Wolfspeed, Infineon, ST, or Rohm. The substrate is a novel article of manufacture with a clear application-level use case that creates a strong composition-of-matter/apparatus foundation.

ps.s101n: Physical apparatus—a semiconductor substrate with embedded microfluidic channels. Clearly patent-eligible.
ps.s102n: Laser-machined microfluidic channels in Si₃N₄ ceramic with CVD-SiC lining for WBG power modules is not found in the prior art. The specific material system (Si₃N₄ + CVD-SiC lining + fluorocarbon coolant compatibility) is novel.
ps.s103n: Femtosecond laser machining of ceramics (known in dental and aerospace), CVD-SiC coating (known in aerospace), and microfluidic cooling (known in data center silicon) require non-obvious combination targeting WBG module requirements. The CVD-SiC lining specifically to enable fluorocarbon coolant compatibility in a Si₃N₄ substrate is a creative leap not suggested by any combination of references.
ps.s112n: Femtosecond laser machining of Si₃N₄ is established; CVD-SiC on complex geometries is standard; AMB sealing is industry-standard. Thermal performance metrics achievable via CFD simulation. Enablement is feasible.

ideas.valM: Embedded microfluidic substrate eliminates TIM and cold plate—consolidating two cost centers into one substrate. If adopted, creates strong licensing position against substrate suppliers (Ceramtec, Toshiba Materials, Denka) and module makers simultaneously. Value anchored in aerospace (>$300/module premium for thermal performance) and high-density EV inverter markets.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Digital Twin Framework for WBG Power Module Remaining Useful Life Prediction with In-Situ Die-Attach Void Growth Sensing

desc.paragraph_1: Power module field failures in WBG applications are dominated by die-attach degradation (void growth and sintered silver delamination) and substrate solder fatigue, both of which develop gradually over thousands of thermal cycles but manifest as sudden thermal runaway events. Current prognostics rely on external ΔRth monitoring—a ±20% increase in thermal resistance is used as the end-of-life criterion—but this threshold is triggered only after significant structural damage has occurred, providing insufficient warning for predictive maintenance. No commercial WBG module incorporates in-situ structural health monitoring of the die-attach layer itself, nor physics-based models calibrated to real-time sensor data that can predict remaining useful life (RUL) within ±15% across diverse mission profiles.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention discloses a WBG power module incorporating a distributed array of piezoelectric PVDF (polyvinylidene fluoride) ultrasonic transducers embedded directly in the DBC copper metallization layer beneath the SiC die-attach region. These micro-transducers (50–200 µm diameter) are fabricated by aerosol jet printing of PVDF ink onto the copper surface prior to sintered die-attach. During module operation, each transducer emits MHz-frequency ultrasonic pulses through the die-attach layer and detects reflected echoes; the time-of-flight and echo amplitude directly map void density, size distribution, and delamination area within the sintered silver joint. A physics-informed neural network (PINN) model—pre-trained on FEA-simulated crack propagation data calibrated by ex-situ scanning acoustic microscopy—fuses the real-time ultrasonic void maps with the module's thermal cycling history to produce RUL predictions.
desc.paragraph_3: The PINN model incorporates Coffin-Manson damage accumulation physics as hard constraints within the neural network architecture, ensuring that predictions respect thermomechanical first principles even when training data is sparse for novel operating conditions. This physics constraint eliminates the data requirement of purely data-driven approaches—a critical advantage for WBG modules that have limited long-term field aging datasets. The real-time RUL output is transmitted via the module's standard gate driver communication interface, enabling fleet-level predictive maintenance in EV powertrains, wind turbine converters, and grid infrastructure without external monitoring hardware. This transforms the WBG module from a passive component to an intelligent self-monitoring device.

claim.preamble: A power semiconductor module with integrated structural health monitoring, comprising:
limitations: (a) a substrate having a metallization layer;
(b) at least one wide-bandgap semiconductor die bonded to the metallization layer by a sintered metal die-attach layer;

(c) a plurality of piezoelectric transducers embedded within the metallization layer beneath the sintered metal die-attach layer, each piezoelectric transducer configured to generate and receive ultrasonic signals through the sintered metal die-attach layer;

(d) processing circuitry configured to analyze time-of-flight and amplitude characteristics of the ultrasonic signals to generate a spatial map of void density within the sintered metal die-attach layer; and

(e) a physics-informed predictive model stored in a non-transitory computer-readable medium and configured to receive the spatial void density map and thermal cycling history data and output a remaining useful life estimate for the sintered metal die-attach layer.

depClaims: PVDF transducer fabrication by aerosol jet printing onto DBC copper metallization prior to die-attach sintering
Physics-informed neural network architecture with Coffin-Manson damage physics as hard constraints
Void growth rate model integrating temperature, current amplitude, and dwell time from module operating data
Communication of RUL output via standard SMBus, PMBus, or CAN interface for fleet-level predictive maintenance
Integration with module gate driver for adaptive switching frequency reduction when predicted RUL drops below threshold
Extension to monitoring substrate solder fatigue in addition to die-attach via longitudinal vs. shear ultrasonic modes
Calibration of in-situ transducer array against factory scanning acoustic microscopy (SAM) baseline scan for each module

refs.ref: Wolfspeed power cycling / lifetime modeling whitepaper (2025) — ΔRth-based end-of-life criterion
refs.diff: Wolfspeed's approach monitors thermal resistance externally after damage occurs; this invention detects void growth before thermal resistance change is measurable, providing early warning—a fundamentally different sensing modality

refs.ref: IEEE literature on scanning acoustic microscopy (SAM) for power module inspection
refs.diff: SAM is an ex-situ benchtop measurement requiring module disassembly; this invention is in-situ, in-field, and real-time—no prior SAM-based in-module patent exists

refs.ref: Academic work on PVDF sensors for structural health monitoring (civil engineering / aerospace)
refs.diff: PVDF SHM in aerospace is for composite delamination, not sintered silver die-attach in power modules. Aerosol jet printing of PVDF onto DBC copper for die-attach monitoring is not disclosed

refs.ref: General physics-informed neural network literature (Raissi et al. 2019)
refs.diff: PINNs are a general ML method; this invention specifically applies PINNs with Coffin-Manson constraints to WBG die-attach RUL prediction from in-module ultrasonic data—a novel application domain

ideas.ftoJ: No identified patent by Wolfspeed, Infineon, ST, or Rohm covers embedded piezoelectric transducers within DBC metallization for in-situ die-attach void monitoring or physics-informed RUL prediction for WBG modules. General health monitoring patents in semiconductors are process-control focused (wafer inspection); module-level in-situ SHM with PINN is an entirely uncrowded patent space.

ps.s101n: The predictive model component requires careful §101 framing to avoid Alice risk. Claim 1(e) should be drafted as a 'physics-informed predictive model' co-claimed with the physical sensor array (elements a-d) to anchor the claim to a specific structural machine. The machine+software combination is patent-eligible when tied to specific physical measurement hardware.
ps.s102n: PVDF transducers embedded in DBC metallization beneath sintered die-attach for in-situ WBG module monitoring is novel—not found in any identified patent or publication.
ps.s103n: PVDF SHM (aerospace), aerosol jet printing on DBC (advanced packaging), and PINNs (ML research) are each known; combining all three specifically for WBG die-attach monitoring requires insight driven by the specific failure physics of sintered silver joints—non-obvious.
ps.s112n: Aerosol jet printing of PVDF on metal substrates is demonstrated; ultrasonic void detection in solder/sinter joints is established; PINN architecture is publicly documented. Enablement is feasible with sufficient experimental calibration detail.

ideas.valM: Predictive maintenance is a high-value service tier for power module OEMs—enabling condition-based replacement vs. time-based replacement can reduce maintenance costs by 30–50% in wind/solar installations. This patent enables a premium 'intelligent module' product tier with $5–$20/unit sensor surcharge across millions of WBG module units. High licensing leverage with fleet operators and OEMs implementing module-level health monitoring architectures.

ideas.title: Genomic-to-Structural-Property Mapping Engine for Biomaterial Discovery Using Multi-Modal Foundation Models

desc.paragraph_1: The construction, aerospace, and automotive industries require materials with highly specific mechanical performance envelopes—tensile strength, Young's modulus, thermal stability, impact resistance—that today take years to develop through empirical iteration. Biological organisms have evolved thousands of structural protein sequences (silk, elastin, collagen, resilin, keratin) each encoding specific property profiles, yet no systematic computational platform exists that maps genetic sequence features to bulk material property outcomes across this cross-kingdom sequence space. Current predictive tools such as AlphaFold2 and ESMFold address single-protein folding but not the emergent multi-chain assembly properties that govern material performance.
desc.paragraph_2: The invention provides a multi-modal AI foundation model—trained on a curated corpus of paired genomic sequences, expressed protein secondary/tertiary structures, and empirically measured mechanical property tensors—that generates quantitative predictions of bulk material properties (stiffness, toughness, extensibility, hydrophobicity, thermal degradation onset) directly from nucleotide or amino acid sequence inputs. The model architecture combines a transformer-based protein language model encoder, a graph neural network representing inter-chain assembly geometry, and a regression head predicting scalar material property outputs. A generative inverse-design module traverses the learned latent space to propose novel sequence variants predicted to achieve user-specified property targets. All predictions carry calibrated uncertainty estimates enabling experimental prioritization.
desc.paragraph_3: The platform's key differentiators are: (1) the training dataset spans prokaryotic, fungal, and metazoan structural proteins rather than a single protein family, enabling cross-kingdom property transfer; (2) the property prediction targets are bulk material rather than single-molecule properties, requiring novel multi-scale featurization; (3) the inverse-design module operates under constraints imposed by host organism codon bias and promoter compatibility, making predictions directly actionable in expression systems; (4) the platform integrates proprietary experimental data from a design-build-test-learn loop rather than relying solely on public sequence databases. This creates a self-improving platform that becomes more accurate as internal data accumulates, building a powerful first-mover moat.

limitations: A computer-implemented biomaterial design platform comprising: (a) a multi-modal machine learning model trained on a dataset comprising: (i) biomolecular sequence data spanning at least two taxonomic kingdoms; (ii) experimentally measured bulk mechanical property tensors for materials derived from said sequences; and (iii) inter-chain molecular assembly geometry descriptors; wherein said model is configured to output quantitative predictions of at least three bulk material properties for a query amino acid or nucleotide sequence;

(b) an inverse-design engine configured to: receive a user-specified material property target vector; traverse a continuous latent embedding space of the multi-modal model; and output a ranked list of candidate biomolecular sequences predicted to achieve said property targets within user-specified tolerance bounds;
(c) a host-compatibility filter that constrains generated sequences to expression-compatible codons for a selected production organism;
(d) a calibrated uncertainty quantification module that assigns confidence intervals to predicted property values.

depClaims: Specific training corpus composition including silk, collagen, elastin, resilin, and keratin sequence families
Graph neural network submodel encoding beta-sheet hydrogen bonding networks and coiled-coil registry
Active learning protocol for iteratively updating training data from closed-loop DBTL experiments
Multi-objective Pareto optimization for simultaneous targeting of conflicting properties (e.g., stiffness vs. extensibility)
Transfer-learning fine-tuning module for application-specific property domains (construction vs. aerospace vs. textiles)
API integration layer for automated DNA synthesis order placement based on selected candidate sequences
Explainability module identifying sequence motifs predictive of target property outcomes
cpcP: G16B40/20 (Machine learning applied to molecular biology)

refs.ref: WO2025255010A1 - AI-guided synthetic biology development platform (filed 2025)

Prior AI-synbio patents (WO2025255010) address metabolic flux/small molecules. Property-prediction focus on structural materials is an unoccupied niche. Recommend broad system claims with dependent method claims.

ps.s101n: Software-implemented invention; must survive Alice two-step. Recommend claim framing as a concrete apparatus (trained model + hardware) producing tangible outputs (candidate sequences with measured material property predictions). Tie claims to specific technical improvements in materials discovery throughput and accuracy. Avoid purely abstract 'prediction method' claim language.
