Upper bound elevated by SaaS licensing potential.

ideas.title: Cell-Free Protein Synthesis Reactor System for Scalable Structural Biomaterial Production with In-Line Rheological Feedback Control

desc.paragraph_1: Cell-free protein synthesis (CFPS) has been commercialized primarily for pharmaceutical applications—antibody fragments, vaccine antigens, enzyme variants—where sub-milligram quantities at high purity are sufficient. Structural biomaterials, however, require kilogram-scale protein outputs to be economically viable for construction, automotive, and packaging end-uses. Existing CFPS platforms (Sutro XpressCF scaled to 4,500L GMP at Boehringer Ingelheim) achieve high throughput for pharmaceutical targets but lack the process control architecture needed for structural protein synthesis: precise monitoring and control of protein self-assembly state, fiber formation kinetics, and bulk rheological properties in real time.
desc.paragraph_2: The invention describes a continuous-exchange cell-free (CECF) bioreactor system comprising: (a) a dialysis membrane zone that continuously replenishes ATP, amino acids, and co-factors while removing inhibitory byproducts, enabling synthesis runs >72 hours; (b) an integrated inline rheometer that measures solution viscosity and elastic modulus of the nascent protein solution in real time via microfluidic oscillatory shear channels; (c) a feedback control algorithm that adjusts synthesis temperature, salt concentration, and pH within the reactor to modulate protein self-assembly kinetics toward a target rheological profile; and (d) an output processing zone where the synthesized protein is continuously drawn off, assembled into fibers or films via a shear-alignment extrusion nozzle, and collected. The system eliminates cell viability constraints that limit conventional CFPS, enabling operation under pH, temperature, and solvent conditions that would kill living cells but promote optimal protein assembly.
desc.paragraph_3: The critical innovation is the closed-loop coupling between synthesis and assembly: the rheological feedback controller prevents premature aggregation (which wastes material) and ensures that the protein solution entering the extrusion zone is consistently within the optimal assembly window. A secondary innovation is the use of a semi-synthetic cell extract modified by deletion of endogenous proteases and addition of orthogonal tRNA/aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase pairs, enabling site-specific incorporation of non-canonical amino acids (e.g., azide-bearing lysine derivatives) that allow post-synthesis click-chemistry crosslinking to tailor material toughness without secondary chemical treatment steps.

limitations: A cell-free biomaterial synthesis apparatus comprising: (a) a continuous-exchange cell-free protein synthesis (CECF) reaction chamber containing a cell lysate supplemented with a structural protein expression template, amino acids, and energy regeneration substrates, wherein the reaction chamber is in fluid communication with a dialysis module configured to continuously supply substrates and remove inhibitory byproducts;

(b) an inline rheological sensor disposed in a fluid flow path downstream of the reaction chamber, configured to measure at least one of dynamic viscosity and elastic modulus of protein solution exiting the reaction chamber at a sampling frequency of at least 0.1 Hz;

(c) a process controller coupled to the rheological sensor and to at least two of: reactor temperature, ionic strength, and pH actuators, configured to maintain measured rheological parameters within a target window corresponding to a protein assembly-competent state;

(d) an assembly zone downstream of the rheological sensor comprising a shear-alignment nozzle configured to convert the protein solution into a continuous biomaterial form selected from fiber, film, or foam.

depClaims: Incorporation of non-canonical amino acids via orthogonal tRNA systems for post-synthesis crosslinking
Dual-protein co-synthesis mode producing protein blends with defined composition ratios
Lyophilization module for on-demand rehydration and re-extrusion
Protease-deletion cell extract composition enabling >72-hour stable synthesis runs
Multiplexed template switching enabling sequential production of protein layers with gradient properties
Integration with the genomic-to-property prediction platform (Invention 1) for automated template selection
Field-deployable containerized format for in-situ construction site biomaterial production
cpcP: C12P21/00 (Preparation of peptides or proteins by fermentation)

refs.ref: Sutro XpressCF platform (4,500L GMP validation, 2025)

ideas.ftoJ: CFPS apparatus patents are concentrated in pharmaceutical application space. No identified patent claims a CFPS system with inline rheological feedback coupled to structural material extrusion. The apparatus claim (hardware + control method) is structurally distinct from existing pharma-oriented CFPS patents.

ps.s101n: Physical apparatus and process claims; no Alice/Mayo concerns. Concrete machine with structural components.
ps.s102n: Novel integration of CECF with inline rheological feedback and shear-alignment extrusion. Individual components exist separately; their combination in a single apparatus is novel.
ps.s103n: PHOSITA would not combine pharmaceutical CFPS with continuous rheological feedback for material assembly because the problem of structural protein aggregation kinetics is not recognized in pharma CFPS literature. The inventive step is recognizing that assembly state control is the rate-limiting variable in structural material quality.
ps.s112n: Well-enabled; prototype CECF systems exist; inline microfluidic rheometry is commercially available; shear-alignment extrusion is established. Specification should provide working examples of at least one structural protein (e.g., silk or resilin) produced to target modulus spec.

ideas.valM: Apparatus claims on enabling equipment platform; comparable to bioprocess equipment patents. Licensing to CDMO/CRO sector and construction material manufacturers. Valuation anchored on replacement cost of alternative scale-up routes (traditional fermentation bioreactor infrastructure costs $5M-$50M per line at scale) and royalty stream on licensed manufacturing runs.

ideas.title: Synthetically Programmed Bacterial-Fungal Consortium Biocomposite for Load-Bearing Construction Applications with Controlled Compressive Strength Gradient

desc.paragraph_1: Ecovative Design's mycelium composite platform (US11920126) demonstrated that bacterial-fungal co-culture can produce biocomposite materials with enhanced mechanical properties: co-cultivation with PGA-producing Bacillus doubled the elastic modulus versus fungus-alone composites. However, Ecovative's approach uses wild-type or minimally engineered microorganisms focused on packaging (low-density, low-mechanical requirement) applications. Load-bearing construction panels, structural insulated panels, and building formwork require compressive strengths >500 kPa and thermal conductivity <0.1 W/m·K—performance targets that current mycelium composites fail to reach without petrochemical binders.
desc.paragraph_2: The invention describes a synthetically programmed bacterial-fungal consortium where both organisms are genetically engineered to execute a coordinated materials synthesis program. The fungal component (a Ganoderma or Trametes species) is engineered to: (1) overexpress a chitin deacetylase (converting cell-wall chitin to chitosan for increased stiffness); (2) produce a surface-displayed cellulose-binding domain that anchors hyphae to agricultural fiber substrates; and (3) secrete a tyrosinase enzyme that oxidatively crosslinks hyphal surface proteins to neighboring bacterial biofilm. The bacterial component (a Bacillus subtilis strain) is engineered to: (1) overproduce poly-gamma-glutamic acid (PGA) as a structural biopolymer matrix; (2) express a carbonic anhydrase that catalyzes localized CaCO3 precipitation within the biocomposite interstices; and (3) secrete a quorum-sensing regulated shutoff signal that terminates PGA production when material density reaches a target threshold, preventing over-consolidation.
desc.paragraph_3: The resulting biocomposite exhibits a controlled compressive strength gradient: outer surfaces consolidated by hyphal-bacterial crosslinking provide a hard, moisture-resistant shell (compressive modulus >600 kPa) while the interior retains a lower-density insulating core (thermal conductivity <0.08 W/m·K). The quorum-sensing gradient control is the key inventive differentiator—it produces a functionally graded material architecture analogous to engineered lumber without any post-processing. Unlike Ecovative's patent US11920126, the consortium is fully synthetically programmed with defined genetic modifications, and the CaCO3 mineralization within the biofilm is a novel structural reinforcement mechanism not present in existing mycelium composite patents.

limitations: A biocomposite material comprising: (a) a fungal component comprising a filamentous fungus genetically engineered to overexpress at least one of: chitin deacetylase, a cellulose-binding domain surface display construct, or tyrosinase;

(b) a bacterial component comprising a Bacillus species genetically engineered to produce poly-gamma-glutamic acid and to express carbonic anhydrase, wherein said bacterial component is co-cultivated with said fungal component in an agricultural substrate;
(c) a calcium carbonate mineral phase precipitated within the biocomposite interstices by the carbonic anhydrase activity of the bacterial component;
(d) wherein the biocomposite exhibits a compressive modulus of at least 500 kPa measured at a density of 200-400 kg/m³ and a thermal conductivity of less than 0.10 W/m·K.

depClaims: Quorum-sensing-regulated shutoff of PGA production to achieve density gradient
Specific fungal species selection from Ganoderma, Trametes, or Pleurotus genera
Agricultural substrate comprising hemp hurd, corn stalks, or rice straw
Post-cultivation heat treatment protocol to terminate biological activity while preserving mechanical properties
Fire-retardant layer formed by engineered fungal expression of calcium oxalate at biocomposite surface
Building panel format with defined aspect ratios for structural insulated panel (SIP) applications
Bio-concrete formwork application where biocomposite degrades post-curing leaving no waste
cpcS: C08L99/00 (Compositions based on natural macromolecular compounds)

refs.ref: Fraunhofer cyanobacteria bio-concrete (2024 press release)

ideas.ftoJ: Ecovative's US11920126 claims co-cultivation of bacterial and fungal species with genetic processing of native microbial species. The present invention's carbonic anhydrase/CaCO3 element and quorum-sensing control are novel non-overlapping features, but some overlap in the co-culture genus exists. Recommend: (1) claim chitin deacetylase + CaCO3 mineralization combination as composition of matter (clearly non-overlapping); (2) define performance thresholds (>500 kPa compressive modulus) in claims to distinguish from Ecovative's packaging-grade compositions; (3) seek design-around confirmation from patent counsel.

ps.s101n: Composition of matter and method claims on engineered organisms; patent-eligible under Diamond v. Chakrabarty for genetically modified organisms with specific utility.
ps.s102n: CaCO3 mineralization via engineered bacterial carbonic anhydrase within a fungal-bacterial biocomposite is novel. Quorum-sensing density gradient control is novel. Functional grading achievement without post-processing is novel.
ps.s103n: Potential for obviousness argument combining Ecovative bacterial-fungal co-culture + MICP bio-cement literature. Counter-argument: PHOSITA would not combine these domains without recognizing that quorum-sensing can create functional gradients in biocomposite density.
ps.s112n: Well-enabled via Ecovative prior examples for fungal cultivation; CDA overexpression in Ganoderma demonstrated in US11920126; Bacillus PGA overproduction well-characterized. Need working examples demonstrating compressive strength gradient.

ideas.valM: Construction materials market $1.3 trillion globally; sustainable insulation panels growing at 8% CAGR; chokepoint on load-bearing mycelium-bacterial composites with defined structural performance. Royalty potential on SIP panel manufacturing licenses. Value anchored on EPS/XPS foam replacement market (~$25B) in construction.

ideas.title: Automated Closed-Loop Design-Build-Test-Learn Pipeline for Biomaterial Property Optimization with In-Line Multi-Modal Characterization Array

desc.paragraph_1: Materials discovery pipelines for synthetic biology face a unique challenge compared to drug discovery: the relevant property space is multi-dimensional and includes mechanical (stiffness, toughness, fatigue life), thermal (degradation onset, thermal conductivity), surface (contact angle, adhesion energy), and optical (reflectance, birefringence) properties, many of which are slow to measure and require distinct instrumentation. Current manual DBTL cycles in academic and industrial synthetic biology labs take weeks per iteration, limiting the number of design cycles achievable in a development program. The US20240096454 high-throughput materials screening patent addresses inorganic formulation optimization via additive manufacturing; no equivalent closed-loop system exists for biological/protein-based materials produced from genetically encoded sequences.
desc.paragraph_2: The invention provides an integrated hardware-software platform for fully automated biomaterial DBTL cycles. The Build module comprises an automated liquid-handling robot coupled to a miniaturized CFPS reactor array (96-well plate format) that synthesizes protein variants from pre-designed DNA templates in parallel. The Test module comprises an in-line multi-modal characterization array including: (a) a quartz-crystal microbalance array for measuring protein film deposition kinetics and viscoelasticity; (b) a contact angle goniometer for surface wettability measurement; (c) a micro-tensile testing stage for mechanical property measurement of fiber samples; and (d) a fluorescence microscopy module for secondary structure quantification via thioflavin T (beta-sheet content) and Congo Red staining. Data from all modalities feed in real time to the Learn module.
desc.paragraph_3: The Learn module implements a multi-objective Bayesian optimization algorithm that updates a Gaussian process surrogate model of the sequence-to-property landscape after each test cycle. The algorithm selects the next batch of DNA templates to synthesize by maximizing expected improvement across the multi-dimensional property space, with explicit constraints encoding regulatory or cost boundaries (e.g., maximum allowable non-canonical amino acid incorporation cost, minimum production titer requirements). A design history database accumulates all experimental results in a structured format compatible with the genomic-to-property AI platform (Invention 1), creating a self-reinforcing data flywheel. The platform reduces a typical biomaterial DBTL cycle from 3-4 weeks to 48-72 hours and achieves optimal material properties in 5-10 iterations versus 50-100 manual experiments.

limitations: An automated biomaterial design-build-test-learn system comprising: (a) a build module comprising a liquid-handling robot operably coupled to a cell-free protein synthesis reactor array, configured to synthesize a plurality of protein variants from respective DNA templates in parallel within a 72-hour cycle time;

(b) a multi-modal test module comprising at least three of: a quartz-crystal microbalance array, a contact angle goniometer, a micro-tensile testing stage, and a fluorescence microscopy module, each configured to characterize synthesized biomaterials without manual sample transfer;

(c) a learn module implementing a multi-objective Bayesian optimization algorithm that: receives characterization data from the test module; updates a surrogate model of a sequence-to-property mapping; and generates a next-iteration design set of DNA template sequences predicted to improve at least two material properties simultaneously;
(d) an automated design history database that stores paired sequence and multi-property characterization data in a structured format.

depClaims: Specific surrogate model architecture using Gaussian processes with automatic relevance determination kernel
Constraint encoding module for production cost, regulatory approval requirements, or sustainability metrics
Integration with third-party DNA synthesis providers via API for automated template ordering
Anomaly detection module flagging contaminated or failed synthesis reactions before test module consumption
Transfer-learning seed protocol pre-loading Bayesian prior from prior campaigns in related protein families
Parallel campaign management enabling simultaneous optimization of multiple protein family design spaces
Digital twin simulation layer pre-filtering candidates before physical synthesis to reduce reagent consumption

refs.diff: Directed to inorganic/additive manufacturing formulation optimization; uses direct-ink writing; not cell-free protein synthesis; no biological sequence design; no quartz-crystal microbalance or secondary structure fluorescence characterization

refs.ref: WO2025255010A1 - AI-guided synthetic biology development platform (2025)
refs.diff: Addresses metabolic pathway optimization for small-molecule bioproduction; does not include mechanical property characterization; no multi-modal material test module; no CFPS build module

refs.ref: Zymergen (acquired by Gingko Bioworks) DBTL platform (general)

Zymergen patents (e.g., US10787690) cover strain selection algorithms, not material property measurement arrays.

refs.ref: Wyss MAGE multiplex genomic engineering (licensed to enEvolv/Zymergen)

ideas.ftoJ: The specific combination of CFPS build module + multi-modal material property test array + Bayesian optimization learn module for structural biomaterials is unoccupied. Existing DBTL patents address metabolic engineering or inorganic materials, not protein-based structural material synthesis and characterization.

ps.s101n: Physical system with concrete hardware components and measurable outputs (material properties). Software-implemented optimization is tied to physical measurement data; frame claims as apparatus + computer-readable medium to strengthen §101 posture.
ps.s102n: Novel combination: CFPS + multi-modal physical property measurement + Bayesian optimization for biomaterials. No prior art integrates all three.
ps.s103n: Non-obvious because the integration of cell-free synthesis with material property (not biological activity) characterization requires recognizing that structural biomaterials require a different measurement paradigm than pharmaceutical protein function assays.
ps.s112n: All individual components are commercially available; integration is straightforwardly described. Bayesian optimization algorithms are well-published. Strong enablement.

ideas.valM: Platform instrument system; comparable to high-throughput screening platform patents in pharma ($10-30M per enabling platform). Revenue from instrument licensing plus data platform subscriptions. Additional value as defensive anchor blocking competitor DBTL platform development in biomaterials space.

ideas.title: Engineered Bacterial Cellulose Nanocomposite Scaffold with Programmable Fiber Orientation for Aerospace and Automotive Structural Panels

desc.paragraph_1: Bacterial cellulose (BC) produced by Komagataeibacter xylinus (formerly Gluconacetobacter xylinus) generates nanofibers with Young's modulus of 78-114 GPa—comparable to Kevlar—and a tensile strength of 2 GPa at the single-fiber level. However, pellicle-format BC materials have isotropic fiber orientation and bulk mechanical properties far below theoretical limits due to random network architecture. Carbon fiber-reinforced polymer (CFRP) panels achieve high specific strength through oriented continuous fiber architecture; no equivalent oriented-fiber format has been achieved in bacterial cellulose at scale, preventing BC from competing in aerospace interiors (weight-sensitive), automotive structural panels, or high-performance packaging where anisotropic mechanical properties are required.
desc.paragraph_2: The invention provides an engineered BC production platform that programs fiber orientation in three dimensions. A Komagataeibacter strain is engineered with: (a) a synthetic promoter circuit that responds to an external magnetic field gradient by upregulating expression of cellulose synthase subunit BcsA at polar cell locations; (b) expression of a cellulose-binding magnetic nanoparticle nucleation domain (derived from magnetotactic bacteria MamC protein) fused to the cellulose synthase complex, causing nascent cellulose nanofibers to align with applied magnetic fields; and (c) a CRISPR-edited cellulose synthase operon with tunable operon copy number enabling titratable fiber density. The bioreactor applies a programmable rotating magnetic field array that directs fiber deposition angle layer-by-layer, creating a quasi-isotropic, unidirectional, or cross-ply nanocomposite architecture analogous to pre-preg carbon fiber layups.
desc.paragraph_3: A secondary innovation is the incorporation of a plant-derived poly-hydroxyalkanoate (PHA) biopolymer into the BC matrix in situ: a second engineered organism (Cupriavidus necator) co-cultivated in the bioreactor secretes PHA that infiltrates the forming BC network, creating an all-biological nanocomposite equivalent to a resin-infused prepreg without any petrochemical matrix component. The resulting panels achieve specific strength (strength/density ratio) comparable to glass fiber-reinforced epoxy at fully biodegradable, end-of-life compostable specification. Target applications are aircraft interior panels (FAA-regulated, fire-retardant versions achievable via engineered flame-retardant biopolymer coating), automotive door inlays, and protective packaging for high-value electronics.

limitations: A bacterial cellulose nanocomposite material comprising: (a) an oriented bacterial cellulose nanofiber network wherein a plurality of nanofibers are aligned within ±15 degrees of a programmed orientation axis, said alignment produced by expression of a cellulose synthase complex fused to a magnetic nanoparticle nucleation domain in the presence of an applied magnetic field gradient;
(b) a poly-hydroxyalkanoate matrix phase infiltrating the nanofiber network, said matrix phase produced by a co-cultivated PHA-secreting microorganism;
(c) wherein the nanocomposite exhibits an anisotropy ratio of tensile modulus along the programmed orientation axis to the perpendicular axis of at least 3:1;

(d) a method of producing said material comprising applying a programmable rotating magnetic field array to a bioreactor containing the magnetic domain-fused cellulose synthase-expressing organism during cellulose deposition.

depClaims: Multi-layer architectures with programmed inter-layer orientation angles for quasi-isotropic laminates
Fire-retardant outer layer formed by engineered expression of nitrogen-rich proteins at BC surface
Specific MamC fusion protein domain used for magnetic cellulose alignment
CRISPR-controlled titratable fiber density enabling gradient density panels
Carbon nanotube or graphene incorporation via functionalized cellulose binding domains for electrical conductivity
Automotive interior panel format with defined dimensional tolerances and surface finish specifications
Biodegradability certification pathway under ASTM D6400 standard composting conditions
cpcS: D21H11/20 (Non-fibrous material added to fibrous raw material)

refs.ref: General bacterial cellulose production patents (multiple, Ajinomoto, Genzyme, 1990s-2000s)
refs.diff: All prior BC patents produce isotropic pellicle format; none disclose magnetic field-directed fiber orientation; none disclose MamC-cellulose synthase fusion proteins

refs.ref: Magnetotactic bacteria patents (Iron Ox, etc.)
refs.diff: Magnetotactic organisms produce magnetosomes for sensing; not cellulose; no structural material application; no cellulose synthase fusion

refs.ref: Ecovative US10537070 - Rolled mycelium composite

refs.ref: Ford/Automotive components patents on mycelium vehicle interiors (US8227225)

ideas.ftoJ: Magnetic field-directed bacterial cellulose fiber orientation using MamC-fused cellulose synthase is a genuinely novel concept with no identified prior art. PHA co-infiltration in a co-culture BC bioreactor has no identified blocking patent. The resulting anisotropic nanocomposite composition is novel.

ps.s101n: Composition of matter on engineered organism and resulting nanocomposite. No §101 concerns.
ps.s102n: Highly novel: MamC-BcsA fusion for magnetically directed cellulose is not in the literature or patents. Oriented BC nanocomposite with PHA matrix is novel composition.
ps.s103n: Non-obvious: combining magnetotactic bacteria magnetic protein (MamC) with cellulose synthase complex from a different bacterial species in a way that transfers magnetic orientation to cellulose deposition requires interdisciplinary insight not obvious to PHOSITA in bacterial cellulose field.
ps.s112n: Requires working examples of functional MamC-BcsA fusion protein; enablement may require experimental verification. Magnetic particle nucleation via protein domains is established in magnetotactic bacteria literature. Specification must demonstrate at least 2:1 anisotropy before filing.

ideas.valM: Aerospace interior composites market $2.5B; automotive interiors bio-material growing 12% CAGR; chokepoint on oriented BC nanocomposites. Composition-of-matter claims on novel material are premium-valued. Comparable to CFRP process patent portfolios held by Hexcel/Toray ($20-50M per enabling technology).

ideas.title: Synthetic-Biology-Enhanced Microbially-Induced Calcite Precipitation System with Programmable Aggregate Binding Geometry for 3D-Printed Bio-Cement Structures

desc.paragraph_1: Conventional concrete production accounts for approximately 8% of global CO2 emissions. Microbially-induced calcite precipitation (MICP) using ureolytic bacteria (Sporosarcina pasteurii) has been demonstrated as a bio-cement alternative with substantially lower embodied carbon, but the technology faces three critical barriers preventing commercial construction adoption: (1) wild-type ureolytic bacteria produce uncontrolled and spatially heterogeneous CaCO3 crystal morphologies leading to inconsistent compressive strength; (2) MICP processes are slow (days to weeks for structural consolidation) versus conventional concrete (hours); and (3) integration of MICP with modern 3D-printed construction (concrete 3D printing market growing at 15% CAGR) requires bacteria that remain viable within extrudable bio-ink formulations and activate on demand.
desc.paragraph_2: The invention provides a synthetically engineered ureolytic bacterial strain equipped with three genetic modules: (a) a programmable CaCO3 crystal morphology control module, wherein expression of specific calcite-binding peptide anchors (derived from sea urchin spicule matrix proteins SM30 and SM50) directs CaCO3 nucleation toward calcite (rhombohedral, high strength) rather than vaterite (spherical, low strength) polymorphs; (b) a synthetic metabolic valve controlling urease expression rate via a synthetic promoter regulated by a small-molecule inducer, enabling on-demand activation of consolidation after extrusion; and (c) a dormancy module expressing sporulation genes at low temperature or under mechanical shear stress, allowing the bacteria to survive extruder nozzle transit in a metabolically quiescent state and resume activity after deposition.
desc.paragraph_3: When formulated with aggregates and extruded through a 3D printing nozzle, the engineered bacteria remain dormant during extrusion (low-temperature storage, mechanical shear). Post-deposition, addition of urea/CaCl2 solution and a chemical inducer activates urease expression and calcite nucleation, causing the deposited structure to consolidate in situ within 4-8 hours. The calcite-binding peptide anchors ensure that CaCO3 crystals form preferentially at aggregate-aggregate contact points, maximizing cementation efficiency compared to wild-type MICP where precipitation is diffuse. The resulting bio-printed structures achieve compressive strengths of 20-40 MPa—within the range of conventional concrete—with near-zero Portland cement content. Applications span construction, rapid hardening field applications (military, disaster response), and aerospace-grade cellular lattice structures achievable only by 3D printing.

limitations: An engineered ureolytic microorganism comprising: (a) an expression construct encoding a urease enzyme operably linked to a synthetic inducible promoter responsive to a non-naturally occurring chemical inducer;
(b) an expression construct encoding at least one calcite crystal morphology-directing peptide derived from a sea urchin spicule matrix protein fused to a bacterial surface display anchor;
(c) an expression construct encoding a sporulation-activating factor responsive to at least one of: temperature below 15°C, mechanical shear force above 100 Pa, or hypoxic conditions;

(d) wherein the microorganism, when formulated in a cementitious aggregate slurry and extruded through a nozzle at extrusion pressures above 50 kPa, maintains viability of at least 60% and, upon addition of urea and calcium chloride solution and chemical inducer post-deposition, produces a calcite-consolidated biocomposite with a compressive strength of at least 15 MPa within 8 hours.

depClaims: Specific sea urchin spicule matrix protein peptide sequences (SM30, SM50 domains) as crystal direction anchors
3D-printable bio-ink formulation comprising the engineered bacterium, Portland cement partial replacement, and hydrogel binder
Gradient strength architecture where inducer concentration gradient produces density-graded consolidation
Self-healing mechanism where residual dormant bacteria reactivate upon crack formation to re-seal voids
Carbon capture integration where engineered carbonic anhydrase co-expression sequesters CO2 into the CaCO3 matrix
Bioremediation compatibility enabling heavy-metal uptake by biofilm during consolidation
Field-deployable tablet formulation containing lyophilized bacteria plus activating reagents for on-site rehydration

refs.diff: Uses wild-type or minimally modified Sporosarcina pasteurii; no synthetic inducible promoter; no crystal morphology-directing peptide display; no dormancy module for 3D printing extrusion compatibility; no on-demand activation

refs.ref: Wyss Institute / MIT Media Lab parametric bio-cement work (Duro-Royo et al., cited in EP3665135)

ideas.ftoJ: The three-module genetic engineering approach (inducible urease + crystal morphology direction + dormancy for extrusion) is not claimed by any identified patent. MICP patents are thin on genetic engineering and absent on 3D printing integration. Clear to file.

ps.s101n: Genetically engineered microorganism with specific utility. Clearly patent-eligible.
ps.s102n: Three-module synthetic circuit for controlled MICP in 3D printing context is novel. Crystal morphology-directing peptide display on MICP bacteria is novel.
ps.s103n: Non-obvious: combining sea urchin spicule matrix protein knowledge from biomineralization literature with ureolytic bacteria from MICP literature and dormancy circuits from sporulation biology requires cross-domain synthesis not obvious to PHOSITA in any single field.
ps.s112n: Sea urchin SM30/SM50 peptides are published; Bacillus subtilis sporulation system is well-established; synthetic inducible promoters are routine. Working examples should demonstrate dormancy during extrusion and post-deposition consolidation. Enable with at least one structural demonstration achieving ≥15 MPa.

ideas.valM: Global concrete market $600B+; 1% penetration of 3D-printed bio-cement at 5% royalty generates $30M/year in perpetuity; composition-of-matter on engineered MICP strain anchors the licensing stack. CO2 credit value for carbon-sequestering concrete adds regulatory tailwind. Comparable to specialty cement additive IP portfolios.

ideas.title: Cross-Kingdom Hybrid Structural Protein Alloy Expressed from Consortial Fermentation with Tunable Phase-Separated Microarchitecture

desc.paragraph_1: The most mechanically exceptional natural materials—spider dragline silk, mussel byssus thread, abalone nacre—derive their extraordinary property combinations from hierarchical microarchitectures formed by multiple interacting protein components, not single proteins. Bolt Threads' MicroSilk and Spiber's Brewed Protein are single-protein materials that, while impressive, achieve only a subset of natural silk's properties because they lack the co-assembled NTD/MaSp1/MaSp2/MaSp3 multi-component architecture of real dragline silk. Modern Meadow's Bio-Alloy represents the only current approach to multi-protein material design, but is limited to collagen-polymer blends for leather-like applications. No commercial platform exists for designing and producing multi-protein structural alloys from different biological kingdoms that achieve emergent property combinations impossible in single-protein materials.
desc.paragraph_2: The invention provides a co-expression platform for producing cross-kingdom protein alloys—defined as materials containing at least two structural proteins from different taxonomic kingdoms (e.g., a spider silk spidroin from Arthropoda + a mussel byssus thread prepolymer domain from Mollusca, or a plant resilin-like polypeptide combined with a bacterial silk-like protein) in a single fermentation host. The platform uses a polycistronic expression cassette in Saccharomyces cerevisiae engineered with: (a) a ribosomal skipping sequence (P2A or T2A self-cleaving peptide) between coding sequences for each protein component, ensuring equimolar production of both proteins from a single mRNA; (b) orthogonal protein-protein interaction domains fused to each protein (e.g., SpyCatcher/SpyTag pairs from Streptococcus pyogenes) that drive phase-separated co-assembly of the two proteins into defined microarchitectures upon concentration or pH change; and (c) block-topology design rules encoded in the protein sequences that favor formation of soft beta-sheet crystallite domains from one protein component alternating with elastomeric random-coil domains from the other, creating a nanostructured hard-soft alternating phase that dissipates energy via sacrificial bond breakage.
desc.paragraph_3: The resulting cross-kingdom hybrid protein alloys exhibit mechanical properties that cannot be achieved by either component alone: a silk-byssus alloy achieves both the high initial modulus of silk and the self-healing capability of mussel adhesive proteins; a resilin-spidroin alloy achieves both the extreme extensibility of resilin (800% strain at break) and the post-yield stiffening of silk due to beta-sheet crystallite formation. The tunability of phase-separated microarchitecture is controlled by varying the relative expression ratios of the two protein components (adjustable via promoter engineering) and the SpyCatcher/SpyTag interaction affinity, giving manufacturers an unprecedented ability to dial in mechanical properties within a single material system. This platform creates composition-of-matter claims on entire classes of novel protein alloy materials, forming a patent thicket that is very difficult for competitors to design around without abandoning multi-protein material strategies.

limitations: A cross-kingdom structural protein alloy material comprising: (a) a first structural protein or protein domain derived from an invertebrate organism; (b) a second structural protein or protein domain derived from a taxonomic kingdom different from that of the first protein; (c) wherein the first and second proteins are co-expressed from a single polycistronic expression cassette in a microbial production host using ribosomal skipping sequences to produce equimolar quantities; (d) wherein the first and second proteins are covalently crosslinked via orthogonal protein-protein ligation domains fused to each protein; (e) wherein the alloy exhibits a phase-separated microarchitecture comprising alternating hard and soft domains with a domain period of 10-500 nm; and (f) wherein the alloy exhibits a toughness modulus at least 50% greater than either protein component alone when tested as a film under equivalent conditions.

depClaims: Specific protein combinations: spidroin (MaSp1 or MaSp2) + mussel foot protein (Mefp-3 or Mefp-5)
Resilin-like polypeptide + silk-like polypeptide combination with specific repetitive block architectures
SpyCatcher/SpyTag vs. SnoopCatcher/SnoopTag vs. disulfide-forming orthogonal ligation pair selection
Tuning of hard:soft domain ratio via inducible promoter titration in fermentation
Self-healing variant incorporating mussel DOPA-mediated metal-coordination reversible crosslinks
Textile fiber format: wet-spun fiber from cross-kingdom alloy with defined draw ratio
Construction panel format: compression-molded sheet with defined density and fire performance rating

refs.ref: Bolt Threads US11192982 - Synthetic block copolymer silk proteins
refs.diff: Single-protein system derived from spider silk only; no cross-kingdom second protein; no polycistronic equimolar co-expression; no orthogonal ligation-driven phase separation; no toughness modulus improvement versus single-protein comparator claimed

refs.ref: Spiber US12145971 / modified fibroin portfolio
refs.diff: Single-protein modified fibroin chemistry; no cross-kingdom co-expression; no phase-separated microarchitecture from protein-protein interactions; no SpyCatcher/SpyTag or equivalent ligation

refs.ref: Modern Meadow US11352497 - Layered collagen/polymer matrix
refs.diff: Single protein (collagen) in blended matrix with synthetic polymer (not a second structural protein); no cross-kingdom structural protein component; no phase-separated co-assembly; collagen/polymer interface vs. protein-protein ligation

refs.ref: Wyss Institute / Harvard GRO Biosciences non-canonical amino acid protein engineering
refs.diff: Focused on drug therapy proteins with non-canonical amino acids; not structural material alloys; no cross-kingdom co-expression; no phase-separated microarchitecture

ideas.ftoJ: No identified patent claims a cross-kingdom structural protein alloy using polycistronic co-expression with orthogonal protein-protein ligation for phase-separated microarchitecture. Bolt Threads and Spiber are single-protein platforms. Modern Meadow is collagen-specific. The composition-of-matter on multi-protein co-assembled alloys with defined nano-domain architecture is unoccupied.

ps.s101n: Composition of matter on non-naturally occurring protein alloy with specific measurable microarchitecture and property improvements over natural materials. No §101 issues.
ps.s102n: Cross-kingdom co-assembly via orthogonal ligation with defined phase-separated microarchitecture achieving emergent toughness improvement is highly novel. No prior art identified.
ps.s103n: Non-obvious because PHOSITA in silk protein engineering (familiar with single-protein approaches of Bolt Threads/Spiber) would not combine SpyCatcher/SpyTag cross-kingdom ligation to achieve phase-separated microarchitecture; this requires knowledge from both protein materials science and synthetic protein-protein interaction design literature.
ps.s112n: Polycistronic co-expression of proteins in yeast via P2A sequences is well-established; SpyCatcher/SpyTag ligation is extensively published (Zakeri et al., PNAS 2012); resilin-like polypeptide synthesis in E. coli is established. Need working example demonstrating ≥50% toughness improvement in at least one cross-kingdom pair.

ideas.valM: Highest-value invention in portfolio due to composition-of-matter scope across novel material class. Comparable to early silk protein composition patents (estimated $30-60M by Bolt Threads investors). Multi-industry applicability (textiles, aerospace interiors, automotive, packaging) multiplies licensing opportunities. Patent thicket anchor for cross-kingdom protein alloy class is premium-valued. First-mover window is narrowing as synthetic biology protein design tools become more accessible.

ideas.title: Universal Photonic Chiplet Interface Protocol (UPCIP): Optical Physical Layer Extension for Multi-Vendor Chiplet Interoperability

desc.paragraph_1: Current die-to-die chiplet interconnect standards such as UCIe 2.0 define electrical physical layer (PHY) specifications but lack a native optical PHY sublayer. Multi-vendor photonic I/O chiplets—including Ayar Labs TeraPHY, Intel OCI, and Lightmatter Passage—each implement proprietary wavelength initialization, link-training, power-level negotiation, and fault signaling sequences. This proprietary fragmentation prevents interoperability between photonic chiplets and host dies from different vendors, creating a structural barrier to a multi-vendor CPO ecosystem analogous to the pre-UCIe electrical chiplet landscape.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention defines a Universal Photonic Chiplet Interface Protocol (UPCIP) as a standardized optical PHY sublayer specification that operates as an extension layer beneath UCIe (or independently as a standalone photonic chiplet bus). UPCIP specifies: (a) a wavelength initialization sequence in which a photonic chiplet broadcasts its supported DWDM wavelength grid (e.g., CWDM4, LR4, or micro-ring resonator grid) and power capability via a low-rate out-of-band sideband channel embedded on a reserved wavelength (designated the 'pilot wavelength'); (b) a link training protocol using predetermined optical test patterns transmitted over the pilot wavelength for loss, dispersion, and BER characterization prior to full-rate data transmission; (c) power budget negotiation registers accessible via a standardized management interface (photonic-CMIS extension); and (d) a fault and loopback mode register set for in-system diagnostic use.
desc.paragraph_3: UPCIP differentiates from prior art UCIe optical retimer specifications (which merely relay electrical UCIe signals over optical fiber) by defining a photonic-native signaling layer in which wavelength, modulation format, and optical power are first-class protocol parameters. The pilot wavelength sideband concept—reserving one DWDM channel for protocol management rather than data—is novel relative to existing CMIS management approaches that rely on I2C/I3C electrical sideband buses. This creates a chokepoint claim covering any multi-vendor photonic chiplet assembly that performs optical link initialization using a reserved management wavelength.
desc.differentiator: Extends UCIe's electrical PHY framework into the optical domain with a wavelength-native management channel; all multi-vendor CPO deployments requiring interoperability must implement or license UPCIP.

limitations: A photonic integrated circuit (PIC) chiplet comprising: a wavelength-division multiplexed (WDM) optical transceiver array configured to transmit and receive data on a plurality of data wavelengths within a standardized wavelength grid;

a pilot wavelength transceiver operating on a reserved management wavelength distinct from the data wavelengths, the pilot wavelength transceiver configured to transmit and receive optical link management messages;

a protocol state machine configured to execute a link initialization sequence comprising: broadcasting chiplet capability parameters including wavelength grid type, per-channel optical power range, and modulation format, over the pilot wavelength; receiving a corresponding capability response from a host die or peer chiplet; and negotiating a joint operating point for data channel wavelength assignment and per-channel launch power;
a management register set accessible via a standardized sideband interface, the register set comprising link training status fields, optical power level fields, and fault status fields; and

wherein the photonic chiplet is compliant with a multi-vendor photonic chiplet interface specification such that the chiplet is operatively interoperable with a second photonic chiplet from a different vendor implementing the same specification.

depClaims: Specific pilot wavelength reserved frequency ranges (e.g., 1260-1270 nm for O-band CWDM management channel)
Optical power level negotiation protocol steps specifying minimum and maximum per-channel launch power registers
Link training sequence using pseudo-random bit sequence (PRBS) patterns transmitted over data wavelengths for optical BER pre-qualification
Fault signaling protocol specifying optical alarm codes transmitted over pilot wavelength (LOS, LOF, power degradation thresholds)
Software stack API mapping photonic link parameters to OS-level device enumeration (analogous to PCIe capability structures)
Integration with UCIe 2.0 DFx Architecture (UDA) management fabric for unified electrical-optical chiplet management
Extension to multi-die photonic network-on-chip (NoC) for on-chip wavelength arbitration using pilot channel

refs.ref: UCIe 2.0 Specification (UCIe Consortium, August 2024)
refs.diff: UCIe defines electrical die-to-die PHY; UCIe optical retimer use case forwards electrical signals over fiber without defining a photonic-native wavelength management layer. UPCIP introduces wavelength grid negotiation and optical power management as first-class protocol parameters not present in UCIe.

refs.ref: OIF Common Management Interface Specification (CMIS) v5.x
refs.diff: CMIS specifies management of pluggable optical modules via I2C/I3C electrical sideband. UPCIP replaces the electrical sideband with an optical pilot wavelength embedded within the same fiber infrastructure, enabling management without a separate electrical management bus—critical for die-embedded photonic chiplets lacking accessible I2C pads.

refs.ref: Intel OCI Chiplet (US20230090863, US12266608)
refs.diff: Intel's patents describe physical integration of PIC and EIC via EMIB and fan-out RDLs. None disclose a standardized inter-vendor link initialization protocol or wavelength assignment handshake. UPCIP targets the protocol layer above Intel's physical integration layer.

refs.ref: Ayar Labs TeraPHY UCIe integration (Ayar Labs blog, November 2024)
refs.diff: TeraPHY uses UCIe electrical protocol to carry optical link data. No published IP from Ayar Labs describes a wavelength-native management protocol; TeraPHY management relies on UCIe sideband electrical channels. UPCIP's pilot wavelength approach is structurally distinct.

ideas.ftoJ: No identified blocking IP in Intel, Broadcom, Ayar Labs, or Lightmatter portfolios covers a wavelength-native optical management protocol embedded within the WDM data fiber. Standards-layer protocol IP is structurally distinct from physical packaging integration patents. Recommend apparatus claims focused on the pilot wavelength transceiver and protocol state machine to avoid any overlap with UCIe electrical PHY claims.

ps.s101n: Apparatus claim directed to a concrete PIC chiplet with specific hardware components (wavelength transceiver, protocol state machine, register set). No Alice/Mayo abstract idea risk; well-defined physical device with specific technical improvements to interoperability.
ps.s102n: Novel element: embedding a dedicated pilot wavelength transceiver within a photonic chiplet for link management purposes. No prior art combines optical pilot wavelength sideband with chiplet capability negotiation registers in a standardized multi-vendor context.
ps.s103n: PHOSITA would not obviously combine: (1) coherent telecom pilot tone concepts (used in ROADM networks, not in-package chiplets), (2) UCIe electrical sideband management, and (3) DWDM inter-chiplet data links to arrive at an optical-domain chiplet management protocol. The non-obvious step is defining optical power and wavelength grid parameters as protocol registers accessible to a host system software stack.
ps.s112n: Fully enabled: pilot wavelength frequency, register formats, and protocol state machine can be specified to PHOSITA level using existing silicon photonics and networking standards. No new materials or fabrication techniques required.

ideas.valM: Standards-essential patent (SEP) potential if adopted by OIF/UCIe Consortium as part of photonic chiplet interoperability specification. Royalty basis: per-unit licensing on all multi-vendor CPO deployments. TAM for CPO is projected at $3B-$10B by 2030; SEP royalty rate of 0.1-0.3% on relevant portion yields $5M-$30M NPV. Strategic blocking value against fragmented proprietary ecosystems adds premium.

ideas.title: Hierarchical WDM Wavelength Assignment and Thermal Compensation Protocol for Multi-Vendor Silicon Photonic Micro-Ring Resonator Arrays

desc.paragraph_1: Silicon photonic micro-ring resonators (MRRs) are the dominant modulator and demultiplexer technology in DWDM optical I/O chiplets (e.g., Ayar Labs TeraPHY uses 16 MRR wavelengths per fiber, 32 Gbps per channel). However, MRR resonant wavelengths drift with temperature at approximately 70-80 pm/°C, and with fabrication process variation at ±2-5 nm from nominal. In multi-vendor CPO assemblies, where photonic chiplets from different foundries (GlobalFoundries 45SPCLO, TSMC SiPho, Intel SiPho) are integrated on the same organic substrate or interposer alongside high-power switch ASICs (dissipating 300-500 W), thermal gradients of 30-60°C across the package create systematic wavelength collisions between MRR channels from adjacent photonic chiplets. No multi-vendor standard exists for wavelength domain assignment that accounts for cross-chiplet thermal coupling.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention defines a Hierarchical WDM Wavelength Assignment Protocol (HWAP) that operates in two phases: a static wavelength domain partitioning phase and a dynamic thermal compensation phase. In the static phase, each photonic chiplet is assigned a non-overlapping wavelength domain partition from a shared wavelength grid (e.g., 1271-1331 nm O-band, 8 channels at 800 GHz spacing) via a host-coordinated wavelength arbitration register exchange. The assignment accounts for per-chiplet process variation offsets reported in a foundry-programmed non-volatile memory (NVM) offset register. In the dynamic phase, each chiplet continuously adjusts MRR heater currents using an on-chip thermal feedback loop that references a shared temperature gradient model distributed by the host system management controller, rather than relying solely on local die temperature sensors. The shared thermal model encodes the spatial thermal gradient across the CPO substrate derived from an array of distributed temperature sensors co-packaged on the organic substrate.
desc.paragraph_3: The hierarchical architecture—combining host-level wavelength domain partitioning with chiplet-level thermal compensation guided by a substrate-wide thermal model—is novel relative to existing approaches that either (a) rely on each chiplet independently managing its own MRR temperatures without coordination, or (b) use coarse per-lane wavelength locking via slow thermal tuning. The substrate-distributed temperature sensor array feeding a shared thermal gradient model enables proactive compensation of thermally-induced wavelength drift before channel collisions occur, rather than reactive correction after BER degradation is detected. This creates a chokepoint claim applicable to any CPO assembly using MRR-based DWDM across multiple vendors' photonic chiplets.
desc.differentiator: Shared-substrate thermal gradient model driving cross-chiplet wavelength coordination; no prior art addresses multi-vendor MRR thermal coordination at the package level.

limitations: A method for wavelength assignment and thermal management in a co-packaged optics (CPO) assembly comprising a plurality of photonic chiplets from at least two different vendors integrated on a shared package substrate, the method comprising:

reading, from a non-volatile memory of each photonic chiplet, a per-chiplet wavelength offset register encoding foundry-programmed process variation offsets for each micro-ring resonator channel of that chiplet;

assigning, by a host management controller, non-overlapping wavelength domain partitions to each photonic chiplet from a shared DWDM wavelength grid, wherein the wavelength domain partition boundaries are offset by the per-chiplet wavelength offset values;
acquiring temperature measurements from a plurality of temperature sensors distributed across the package substrate at positions between and adjacent to the photonic chiplets;
computing, from the plurality of temperature measurements, a spatial thermal gradient model of the package substrate; and

transmitting the spatial thermal gradient model to each photonic chiplet via a management interface, wherein each photonic chiplet adjusts micro-ring resonator heater currents based on the received thermal gradient model to maintain assigned wavelength channel positions within a collision-free operating range.

depClaims: Specific NVM register format encoding per-channel center wavelength, thermal sensitivity coefficient, and maximum heater power
Wavelength domain partition assignment algorithm accounting for inter-chiplet optical crosstalk penalty thresholds
Distributed temperature sensor array integration in organic CPO substrate—sensor type, placement density, and communication protocol
Thermal gradient model update rate and latency requirements for dynamic compensation during thermal transients (ASIC load steps)
Wavelength collision detection and recovery protocol triggered when predicted thermal drift exceeds channel guard band
Integration with OIF CMIS photonic management registers for remote wavelength status monitoring by network management system
Extension to 3D photonic interposer configurations (Lightmatter Passage-style) where thermal gradient is three-dimensional

refs.ref: Ayar Labs TeraPHY micro-ring resonator thermal tuning (Gazettabyte, January 2026)
refs.diff: TeraPHY manages MRR wavelengths per-chiplet using local die thermal control. No disclosed coordination mechanism with adjacent chiplets or substrate-wide thermal model. HWAP's shared thermal gradient model and host-coordinated domain partitioning are novel.

refs.ref: US9229169B2 (IBM, lens array optical coupling, 2011)
refs.diff: Addresses optical coupling geometry, not wavelength management or thermal compensation. No teaching of multi-vendor wavelength domain coordination.

refs.ref: WDM wavelength assignment prior art in telecom (US6631018B1, 1997)
refs.diff: Telecom WDM wavelength assignment operates at network node level over km-scale fiber links; no thermal compensation required. CPO intra-package context with mm-scale distances and 30-60°C thermal gradients driven by co-integrated ASIC dies is structurally distinct.

refs.ref: Intel US20220413236 (thermal die for PIC)
refs.diff: Intel's thermal die provides heat extraction for a single PIC die using a specialized thermal management structure. HWAP addresses the inter-chiplet coordination problem across multiple vendors' chiplets using a shared substrate thermal model—a higher-level system architecture concept not covered by Intel's single-die thermal management patent.

ideas.ftoJ: Ayar Labs has undisclosed IP around MRR thermal tuning that may partially overlap with dependent claims on per-chiplet heater control. Independent claim is differentiated by the shared substrate thermal gradient model and host-coordinated wavelength domain partitioning—elements not apparent in Ayar Labs published technical disclosures. Recommend narrowing dependent claims to the substrate-sensor-array feedback architecture and avoiding per-chiplet heater control as an independent limitation.

ps.s101n: Method claim tied to specific physical operations on hardware CPO assembly. No abstract idea risk—addresses concrete thermal and wavelength management problem in a physical device.
ps.s102n: Novel element: distributing a spatial thermal gradient model from substrate-level sensors to individual photonic chiplets to drive proactive MRR tuning. Multi-vendor wavelength domain partitioning using foundry-programmed NVM offset registers is also novel.
ps.s103n: PHOSITA might combine MRR thermal tuning (known) with substrate temperature sensing (known) but would not obviously arrive at the shared gradient model distribution architecture or the NVM-offset-based domain partitioning. The non-obvious insight is treating inter-chiplet thermal coupling as a shared resource requiring coordinated management rather than independent per-chiplet mitigation.
ps.s112n: Fully enabled with existing silicon photonics fabrication, embedded NVM technology (standard in modern SoCs), and substrate temperature sensor arrays. PHOSITA can implement the thermal gradient model computation using standard polynomial or finite element interpolation methods.

ideas.valM: Blocking value against any multi-vendor CPO deployment using DWDM MRR modulation with thermal management. Applicable to Broadcom Gen 3/4 CPO, Nvidia Spectrum-X CPO, and future multi-vendor CPO assemblies. Licensing basis: per-port royalty on CPO optical engines. At projected 2027 CPO shipment volumes of ~500K ports, royalty of $50-100/port on licensed products yields $5M-$10M annual stream.

ideas.title: Standardized Passive Photonic Coupling Alignment Architecture with Fiducial-Encoded Substrates for Multi-Vendor CPO Assembly

desc.paragraph_1: A fundamental barrier to a multi-vendor photonic chiplet ecosystem is the absence of standardized passive alignment specifications for fiber-to-PIC and PIC-to-PIC optical coupling. Active alignment—where optical output is monitored while physically manipulating fiber positions to maximize coupling—achieves low insertion loss (<0.5 dB) but requires expensive precision robotics and adds 30-60 seconds per alignment per fiber, making it economically untenable for high-volume CPO manufacturing. Passive alignment, where photonic components snap to predefined mechanical stops without active monitoring, reduces assembly cost by 10-50× but requires tight mechanical tolerances (±0.5-2.0 µm) that currently depend on vendor-specific substrate fiducial patterns, PIC coupler designs, and carrier geometries. No cross-vendor standard specifies these parameters.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention defines a Standardized Passive Photonic Coupling Alignment Architecture (SPPCA) comprising: (1) a hierarchical fiducial marker system encoded in the photonic chiplet and the package substrate using both optical (reflective/transmissive) and physical (etched feature) markers at three precision levels—coarse (±50 µm, for pick-and-place), medium (±5 µm, for substrate mechanical stop engagement), and fine (±0.5 µm, for grating coupler or edge coupler alignment via mode-field-diameter-optimized lens array); (2) a standardized mode-field adapter specification defining the acceptable divergence angle, beam waist diameter, and polarization state at the PIC coupling port that any compliant fiber attachment unit (FAU) or photonic bridge chiplet must match; and (3) a substrate-side coupling landing zone specification defining the mechanical stop geometry, keepout areas, and optical window dimensions on the organic CPO substrate.
desc.paragraph_3: The three-level fiducial hierarchy is the core novelty: existing approaches either use two-level fiducials (coarse machine vision + fine active alignment) or rely on single-level passive stops with relaxed coupling tolerance and high insertion loss. The SPPCA fine-level fiducial uses a diffractive optical element (DOE) encoded in the PIC surface oxide layer as a passive alignment reference that, when illuminated by a standard reference beam during assembly, generates a diffraction pattern that can be imaged to determine sub-micron misalignment—enabling passive high-precision alignment without active optical power monitoring. The DOE-encoded fiducial approach is applicable to both edge-coupled and grating-coupled PIC designs across multiple foundry platforms.
desc.differentiator: Three-tier fiducial hierarchy with diffractive optical element fine-alignment reference enables sub-micron passive coupling accuracy across vendors without active alignment infrastructure.

limitations: A photonic chiplet assembly configured for passive optical coupling alignment, comprising:

a photonic integrated circuit (PIC) die comprising: an optical coupling port configured to receive or emit an optical mode; a diffractive optical element (DOE) encoded in a surface layer of the PIC die adjacent to the optical coupling port, wherein the DOE generates a predetermined diffraction pattern in response to illumination by a reference beam at a specified wavelength; and a medium-precision mechanical stop feature protruding from or recessed into the PIC die surface;

a package substrate comprising: a coupling landing zone defining a mechanical engagement surface configured to mate with the medium-precision mechanical stop feature of the PIC die to constrain lateral position within ±5 µm; and an optical window aligned to the coupling landing zone for transmitting optical modes between the PIC coupling port and an external fiber attachment unit (FAU);

wherein the DOE diffraction pattern is detectable by an assembly system to determine misalignment of the PIC die relative to the optical window with a precision of ±0.5 µm or better without requiring active optical power monitoring during assembly; and
an FAU comprising a mode-field adapter element configured to transform a fiber mode-field diameter to match a PIC coupling port mode-field specification within a defined insertion loss budget.

depClaims: DOE diffractive pattern design: binary phase, grayscale, or holographic encoding; wavelength specificity (reference beam wavelength separate from data wavelengths)
Mechanical stop geometry: V-groove, mesa, or etched pit stop configurations; tolerance analysis for thermal expansion mismatch between substrate and PIC materials
Mode-field adapter element designs: GRIN lens array, 3D-printed freeform optic, or inverse-tapered waveguide coupler; insertion loss specification
Multi-die CPO substrate: optical window array layout specification for simultaneously coupling an array of photonic chiplets to a fiber ribbon cable
Rework procedure specification: DOE-guided re-alignment after thermal cycling or mechanical stress events
Integration with Nanoscribe 2PP direct-write technology for printing mode-field adapters directly on PIC coupling ports during assembly
Compliance testing protocol: reference test jig and illumination system specification for verifying SPPCA-compliant photonic chiplet alignment accuracy

refs.ref: US9229169B2 (IBM, lens array optical coupling to photonic chip, 2011)
refs.diff: IBM patent addresses lens array coupling geometry for a single PIC; does not define a multi-vendor standardized alignment architecture with hierarchical fiducials or DOE-encoded alignment references. SPPCA's cross-vendor standardization aspect and DOE fine-alignment mechanism are not taught.

refs.ref: Nanoscribe 2PP microoptical printing for passive packaging (PIC Magazine, 2022)
refs.diff: Nanoscribe technique prints custom freeform optics on individual PICs but relies on confocal mapping for alignment reference, not a standardized DOE fiducial pattern. No cross-vendor specification framework.

refs.ref: OIF Co-Packaging Framework IA (2020)
refs.diff: OIF Framework IA identifies co-packaging as an area for standardization but does not specify passive alignment architectures, mechanical stop geometries, or coupling port specifications. SPPCA fills this gap with specific technical definitions.

refs.ref: Intel US20240178207 (glass-core TGV interposer with waveguides)
refs.diff: Intel's glass interposer patent describes a specific substrate architecture; coupling alignment is achieved by hybrid bonding (active process), not by passive fiducial-guided assembly. SPPCA targets assembly-level passive alignment across different substrate types.

ideas.ftoJ: No identified blocking IP in incumbent portfolios covers a hierarchical three-tier fiducial system with DOE-encoded fine alignment for cross-vendor CPO assembly. IBM's lens array patent (US9229169B2) is expired or covers specific configurations not overlapping with the SPPCA DOE approach. Recommend composition-of-matter and apparatus claims emphasizing the DOE encoding in the PIC surface layer as the novel enabling element.

ps.s101n: Pure apparatus claim directed to concrete physical structure (PIC with DOE, substrate with landing zone, FAU with mode adapter). Zero §101 risk.
ps.s102n: DOE encoding in PIC surface layer for passive sub-micron alignment reference without active monitoring is novel. Three-tier hierarchical fiducial system for CPO assembly is not found in prior art.
ps.s103n: DOEs are known in optical alignment (e.g., holographic alignment marks in semiconductor lithography). PHOSITA would not obviously transfer DOE alignment concepts from lithography mask alignment to CPO photonic chiplet assembly, particularly combining them with standardized mode-field adapters and mechanical substrate stops into a unified cross-vendor specification framework.
ps.s112n: Fully enabled. DOE design, PIC surface layer encoding, mechanical stop fabrication, and mode-field adapter design are all within standard silicon photonics and optical engineering practice. Detailed enabling disclosure can specify DOE grating equations, mechanical tolerance calculations, and mode-field matching criteria.

ideas.valM: Composition-of-matter and apparatus claims covering all SPPCA-compliant PIC chiplets, substrates, and FAUs. Royalty basis: per-unit licensing on photonic chiplet packages requiring passive alignment. At projected CPO shipment volumes of 1M+ optical engine assemblies by 2028, royalty of $20-40/unit yields $10M-$20M annual stream. Additional value from licensing to fiber connector manufacturers (MPO, APC connectors) adopting SPPCA coupling specifications.

ideas.title: Photonic Chiplet Built-In Self-Test (PhoBIST) Architecture with Optical Loopback and On-Chip Optical Power Monitoring for Multi-Vendor CPO Quality Assurance

desc.paragraph_1: Post-assembly testing of co-packaged photonic chiplets presents a fundamental challenge: once sealed within a CPO package, external optical probe access to individual PIC channels is physically impossible. Existing electrical BIST standards (e.g., JTAG IEEE 1149.1, UCIe DFx Architecture) test the electrical domain but cannot characterize optical insertion loss, optical crosstalk, BER, or laser performance. As a result, optical defects (waveguide cracks, coupling misalignment, laser degradation) in CPO assemblies can only be detected after full system commissioning, dramatically increasing the cost of defective unit removal and limiting yield improvement feedback to the manufacturing process.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention defines a Photonic Built-In Self-Test (PhoBIST) architecture embedded within each photonic chiplet that enables comprehensive optical characterization without external probe access. PhoBIST comprises: (1) an on-chip optical power monitor (OPM) array—implemented as a set of low-insertion-loss directional couplers tapping 1% of optical power from each waveguide channel into integrated germanium photodetectors—providing per-channel optical power readback accessible via management registers; (2) an optical loopback switch—an integrated Mach-Zehnder switch element—that, when activated by a PhoBIST control register, routes transmitted optical signals back through the receiver chain to enable round-trip BER measurement and insertion loss characterization without external fiber connections; (3) a reference optical source mode in which the on-chip laser (or an external laser coupled via the light source path) transmits a known PRBS pattern under PhoBIST control for link characterization; and (4) a signature register set that stores measured optical parameters (per-channel power, BER at reference conditions, wavelength offset vs. nominal) as a photonic 'health fingerprint' programmed during wafer-level or module-level screening.
desc.paragraph_3: The combination of OPM array, optical loopback switch, reference source mode, and health fingerprint registers into a standardized register-accessible architecture—analogous to electrical BIST in logic chips—is novel. Existing silicon photonics characterization approaches use external bench equipment (OSAs, optical power meters, vector network analyzers) that require physical probe access. The PhoBIST architecture enables in-system photonic health monitoring throughout the CPO product lifecycle, from wafer probe through package assembly, system integration, and field operation, with zero additional optical fibers or probe pads required. This is a chokepoint claim applicable to any CPO or optical I/O chiplet product with diagnostic requirements.
desc.differentiator: Complete in-package photonic test and health monitoring without external optical probes; standardized register interface enables multi-vendor CPO system-level diagnostic software.

limitations: A photonic integrated circuit (PIC) comprising a built-in self-test (BIST) architecture for optical link characterization, the BIST architecture comprising:

an optical power monitor array comprising a plurality of directional coupler elements, each directional coupler element positioned along a respective optical waveguide channel and configured to tap a predetermined fraction of optical power from that channel to a corresponding photodetector, each photodetector providing a digitized optical power reading;

an optical loopback switch integrated within the PIC and configured, upon assertion of a loopback enable signal in a control register, to redirect an optical output of a transmitter section of the PIC to an optical input of a receiver section of the PIC without routing the optical signal off the PIC die;

a photonic health register set comprising fields for storing per-channel optical power values measured by the optical power monitor array, a bit-error-rate measurement result obtained during a loopback test, and a wavelength offset value for at least one wavelength-selective element;

a BIST controller configured to execute a self-test sequence comprising activating the optical loopback switch, transmitting a test pattern via the transmitter section, receiving the test pattern via the receiver section, computing a bit-error-rate from the received test pattern, and storing the bit-error-rate in the photonic health register set; and
a management interface providing read and write access to the photonic health register set and the control register from an external management controller.

depClaims: Wafer-level PhoBIST execution: grating coupler-based optical probe pads activated only during wafer test, disabled (shorted) post-dicing to avoid coupling loss in final assembly
PhoBIST health fingerprint: NVM storage of baseline optical parameters at final test for lifetime degradation monitoring in field
Multi-die PhoBIST orchestration: host controller executing coordinated PhoBIST sequences across multiple photonic chiplets in a CPO assembly to characterize inter-die coupling paths
Fault isolation register: identifying specific waveguide segment, coupler, modulator, or detector contributing to BER degradation
PhoBIST integration with UCIe DFx Architecture (UDA) management fabric for unified electrical-optical chiplet health reporting
Athermal PhoBIST: temperature-normalized optical power readings using integrated thermistor to separate thermal drift from genuine optical degradation
PhoBIST compliance test specification: standardized test sequence, pass/fail thresholds, and report format for multi-vendor CPO module certification

refs.ref: ArXiv 2503.14784 (Defect Analysis and BIST for FOWLP Chiplet Interconnects, 2025)
refs.diff: This paper addresses electrical BIST for fan-out wafer-level package interconnects using equivalent electrical circuit models. Does not address optical domain BIST, optical power monitoring, or loopback characterization in photonic chiplets.

refs.ref: Cadence PIC Characterization using ASE spontaneous emission (Cadence blog, 2023)
refs.diff: Cadence's ASE characterization method uses one PIC element to characterize another via thermal imaging—requires external thermal measurement equipment and cannot be executed in-package without probe access. PhoBIST operates entirely within the packaged chiplet using integrated photodetectors and loopback switches.

refs.ref: OIF CMIS v5.x (optical module management)
refs.diff: CMIS specifies management registers for pluggable optical modules including Tx/Rx power thresholds. However, CMIS targets externally accessible modules with fiber port probe access; it does not define an integrated on-chip loopback switch or on-chip OPM array for die-embedded photonic chiplets.

refs.ref: Intel OCI chiplet (OFC 2024 demonstration)
refs.diff: Intel's OCI demonstrates integrated laser and optical I/O but no published disclosure describes an integrated loopback switch or multi-channel OPM array for in-package BIST. The PhoBIST loopback switch architecture is not taught in Intel's published IP.

ideas.ftoJ: No identified incumbent patent portfolio covers an integrated photonic BIST architecture with on-chip optical loopback switch and OPM array for CPO chiplets. CMIS management registers (OIF) are at the module level and rely on electrical sideband; they do not describe integrated optical test circuits. Recommend apparatus claims with specific structural recitations of the directional coupler OPM tap fraction and loopback switch architecture to maximize claim strength.

ps.s101n: Apparatus claim for concrete physical PIC structure with specific optical test components. No abstract idea risk.
ps.s102n: Integration of optical loopback switch, OPM array, and standardized health register set into a unified BIST architecture within a photonic chiplet is novel. Individual components exist separately but their combination as a standardized multi-vendor diagnostic architecture is not found in prior art.
ps.s103n: PHOSITA skilled in photonics would know optical loopback testing (used in telecom at module level with external equipment) and directional coupler power monitors (used in PICs for gain control). Combining them into a register-accessible embedded BIST architecture within an in-package CPO chiplet, integrated with a management fabric analogous to electrical JTAG/UCIe DFx, would require insight from both photonics and digital semiconductor testing domains—a non-obvious combination.
ps.s112n: Fully enabled using existing silicon photonics components. Directional couplers with 1% tap ratios, Mach-Zehnder optical switches, and germanium photodetectors are standard silicon photonics foundry elements. PRBS generation and BER measurement circuits are standard SerDes components.

ideas.valM: Chokepoint value: any photonic chiplet requiring diagnostic capability (virtually all commercial CPO products will require in-system monitoring for data center reliability SLAs) must implement or license PhoBIST. Applicable to Intel OCI, Ayar TeraPHY, Broadcom optical engines, and all future photonic I/O chiplets. Royalty basis: per-chiplet license fee of $5-15 at projected volumes of 2M+ photonic chiplets/year by 2028 yields $10M-$30M annual stream. PHOSITA enablement and clear scope make this a strong litigation asset.

ideas.title: Software-Defined Photonic I/O Abstraction Layer (SDPAL): OS-Level Optical Interconnect Resource Management for Heterogeneous CPO Systems

desc.paragraph_1: In the compute software stack, PCIe-connected devices are enumerated, configured, and managed through a well-defined device driver model: the PCIe configuration space, with its standardized capability registers, enables operating systems and hypervisors to discover, allocate, and monitor PCIe endpoints without device-specific drivers for basic enumeration. No analogous software abstraction exists for optical I/O chiplets. Today, each photonic chiplet vendor provides proprietary firmware and driver software for configuring optical link parameters (wavelength assignment, modulation format, FEC mode, optical power), monitoring link health (BER, optical power, laser temperature), and managing power states. This proprietary fragmentation prevents OS-level management of photonic I/O resources, blocks multi-vendor CPO system integration, and prevents cloud hypervisors from migrating virtual machines across servers based on optical interconnect topology.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention defines a Software-Defined Photonic I/O Abstraction Layer (SDPAL) comprising: (1) a standardized Photonic Device Configuration Space (PDCS)—a memory-mapped register space analogous to PCIe configuration space—that each photonic chiplet exposes to the host system, including a photonic capability structure encoding wavelength grid type, maximum channel count, per-channel power range, supported modulation formats, and FEC capabilities; (2) a Photonic Link Manager (PLM) kernel driver API providing OS-agnostic function calls for link_init(), link_status(), power_optimize(), wavelength_remap(), and loopback_test() across any PDCS-compliant photonic chiplet; (3) a Photonic Resource Orchestrator (PRO) operating at the hypervisor layer that maintains a topology graph of photonic interconnects in a multi-server CPO fabric and supports dynamic bandwidth allocation and wavelength remapping for workload migration; and (4) a Photonic Telemetry Interface (PTI) publishing per-channel optical power, BER, and thermal data to standard infrastructure monitoring frameworks (OpenTelemetry, Prometheus) via a photonic metrics schema.
desc.paragraph_3: The SDPAL PDCS register space is the critical chokepoint claim: any multi-vendor photonic chiplet ecosystem requiring OS-level management must either implement the PDCS or provide an equivalent capability structure. This is analogous to the role the PCIe Type 0/1 configuration space header plays in the PCIe ecosystem. The Photonic Resource Orchestrator further extends the value by enabling AI/ML workload schedulers (SLURM, Kubernetes) to factor optical interconnect topology and available bandwidth into job placement decisions—a capability entirely absent in current HPC scheduler frameworks. The SDPAL is foundry-agnostic and packaging-agnostic, applicable to any photonic I/O chiplet regardless of physical integration approach.
desc.differentiator: PCIe-analogous configuration space for photonic devices enables OS-level enumeration and management; no prior art defines a standardized software interface for photonic I/O chiplets.

limitations: A photonic I/O abstraction system comprising:
