a photonic integrated circuit (PIC) chiplet comprising a photonic device configuration space (PDCS) stored in a memory-mapped register region accessible to a host processor, the PDCS comprising: a photonic capability structure encoding wavelength grid type, maximum DWDM channel count, per-channel optical power range, supported modulation formats, and forward error correction mode; a link status register encoding current per-channel optical power values and a link error rate; and a link control register accepting commands for wavelength remapping, power level adjustment, and loopback test activation;

a photonic link manager executing on the host processor, the photonic link manager configured to: enumerate photonic chiplets by reading their respective PDCS; initialize optical links by writing link control register commands; and report link status by reading link status registers;

a photonic resource orchestrator executing at a hypervisor layer above the photonic link manager, the photonic resource orchestrator configured to maintain a topology graph representing optical interconnect bandwidth and latency between a plurality of host processors each having at least one photonic chiplet; and dynamically remap wavelength assignments in the PDCS link control registers of one or more photonic chiplets to reallocate optical bandwidth in response to a workload migration event.

depClaims: PDCS register format specification: bit field layout of photonic capability structure, version field for forward compatibility, vendor ID and device ID analogous to PCIe
Photonic power state management: PM (power management) capability structure in PDCS defining D0 (full operation), D1 (reduced power), and D3 (laser off) optical states
Multi-tenant photonic resource partitioning: SR-IOV-analogous virtual photonic function (VPF) concept allowing hypervisor to assign optical channels to virtual machines
SDPAL integration with RDMA over optical fabric: extension of RoCEv2/InfiniBand RDMA stack to configure photonic interconnect parameters via SDPAL API
Photonic telemetry schema: OpenTelemetry-compatible attribute names and data types for per-channel optical power, BER, laser current, and temperature metrics
Photonic topology discovery protocol: neighbor discovery using pilot wavelength (from Invention 1/UPCIP) to build topology graph for orchestrator
Software-defined wavelength remapping for traffic engineering: time-sliced wavelength allocation across multiple workloads sharing a photonic chiplet

refs.ref: OIF CMIS v5.x (Common Management Interface Specification)
refs.diff: CMIS targets pluggable optical transceiver module management via I2C/I3C buses at the hardware abstraction layer; does not define a software API layer, hypervisor-level resource orchestrator, or OS-level device enumeration framework. SDPAL's Photonic Link Manager API and Photonic Resource Orchestrator operate at higher software abstraction layers not addressed by CMIS.

refs.ref: Software-Defined Photonics (iPronics, Photonics Spectra, 2024)
refs.diff: iPronics programmable photonics defines a Python-based API for configuring tunable PIC elements within a single device. Does not address multi-chiplet resource management, hypervisor integration, workload migration, or CPO system-level orchestration.

refs.ref: IOWN Global Forum Open APN Functional Architecture (2025)
refs.diff: IOWN addresses software-defined optical path provisioning at network scale (data center interconnect level). SDPAL specifically targets in-package photonic chiplet resource management at the server/SoC level—a fundamentally different scope with different technical requirements (nanosecond link reconfiguration vs. second-scale path provisioning in IOWN).

refs.ref: PCIe Configuration Space (PCI-SIG specification)
refs.diff: PCIe configuration space is the structural inspiration but operates entirely in the electrical domain. Adapting the capability structure framework to photonic I/O parameters (wavelength, modulation, optical power) requires significant domain-specific extensions not taught by PCIe specification.

ideas.ftoJ: Software abstraction layer claims for photonic I/O are unoccupied by all identified incumbents. Intel, Broadcom, Ayar Labs, and Lightmatter focus on hardware implementation; none have published software stack IP at the OS/hypervisor layer for photonic chiplet management. SDPAL is structurally distinct from CMIS (hardware management interface) and from optical network SDN controllers (network scale, not in-package). Recommend system-level apparatus and method claims emphasizing the PDCS register space, PLM API, and PRO topology graph as the three novel structural elements.

ps.s101n: Software-centric claims require careful framing to avoid Alice rejection. Claims are significantly improved by tying them to specific register structures in a physical photonic chiplet (PDCS) and specific hardware actions (wavelength remapping via register writes). Recommend apparatus claims over pure method claims; the PDCS register space is the physical hardware anchor.
ps.s102n: PDCS as a photonic-native capability register space within a PIC chiplet is novel. No prior art has defined an OS-enumeratable photonic device configuration space analogous to PCIe configuration space.
ps.s103n: PHOSITA might combine CMIS register concepts with PCIe configuration space concepts to arrive at a photonic device configuration space; however, the specific Photonic Resource Orchestrator with topology graph and dynamic wavelength remapping for workload migration is a non-obvious application of network virtualization concepts to in-package photonic resource management.
ps.s112n: Software claims require detailed API specification to meet enablement requirements. PDCS register format specification must be fully described. Feasible with existing knowledge of PCIe specification design patterns applied to photonic domain.

ideas.valM: Lower hardware-chokepoint value than purely apparatus claims, but significant strategic blocking value: any AI/ML infrastructure software stack that manages photonic interconnect resources requires a device enumeration and management framework. Applicable to hyperscaler internal CPO deployments (Google, Microsoft, Amazon, Meta), commercial CPO system vendors. Licensing basis: per-managed-port software license or enterprise SDK licensing at $2-5/managed optical port yields $5M-$15M at scale.

ideas.title: Dual-Mode Athermal Photonic Chiplet with Adaptive Thermal-Electrical Co-Management for Co-Packaged Optics Environments

desc.paragraph_1: A persistent barrier to CPO adoption is the thermal incompatibility between photonic components—lasers, micro-ring resonators, Mach-Zehnder modulators—and co-integrated high-power electronic dies. Silicon photonic lasers (III-V bonded or heterogeneously integrated) exhibit optical output power degradation, wavelength drift, and accelerated aging at temperatures exceeding 50-60°C. Switch ASICs in 100G+ CPO configurations dissipate 300-600 W within a package footprint of <100 mm², creating thermal hotspots that can elevate adjacent photonic chiplet temperatures to 80-100°C. Thermoelectric coolers (TECs) can cool photonic components locally but consume 5-20 W per optical engine, negating much of CPO's energy efficiency advantage (the entire CPO optical engine targets <5 W total power in leading designs).
desc.paragraph_2: This invention defines a Dual-Mode Athermal Photonic Chiplet (DAPC) architecture that operates in two thermal modes without external TEC cooling. In Mode 1 (Standard, <55°C): the chiplet operates conventional DWDM channels with MRR tuning power optimized for thermal equilibrium conditions, using the hierarchical wavelength assignment protocol (as in Invention 2). In Mode 2 (High-Temperature Athermal, 55-85°C): the chiplet automatically transitions to a thermally compensated operating point by: (a) switching the modulation architecture from MRR-based DWDM to a TEC-free athermal approach using silicon nitride (Si3N4) waveguide MRRs with near-zero thermo-optic coefficient (10-100× lower than Si MRRs) combined with photonic crystal slow-light waveguide segments that provide dispersion-based thermal compensation; (b) redistributing the active DWDM channel wavelengths to a thermal-compensated channel grid that accounts for the ±2 nm/50°C wavelength shift of Si MRRs, replacing them with Si3N4 MRRs that drift <0.05 nm/50°C; and (c) engaging a laser thermal current compensation algorithm that reduces laser drive current to maintain constant optical power output despite increased operating temperature.
desc.paragraph_3: The dual-mode architecture—specifically the automatic transition from Si MRR to Si3N4 MRR channels triggered by an integrated temperature monitor exceeding a threshold—is novel. Existing athermal photonic approaches use either passive athermal designs at fixed temperature (no mode switching) or constant TEC cooling. The DAPC's mode-switching between Si and Si3N4 waveguide channels within a single chiplet enables athermal operation across a 30°C temperature range without any external cooling, while preserving full-data-rate DWDM capacity. The Si3N4 athermal channel set is a physical hardware standby that activates automatically when needed—analogous to a turbo mode but for thermal robustness. This architecture enables CPO deployment in thermally aggressive package configurations (3D stacked under high-power compute dies) that are currently infeasible with standard Si photonic chiplets.
desc.differentiator: Automatic mode-switching between thermally sensitive Si-MRR and athermal Si3N4-MRR DWDM channels within a single photonic chiplet; eliminates TEC cooling for CPO thermal management.

limitations: A photonic integrated circuit (PIC) chiplet for co-packaged optics, the PIC comprising:

a first waveguide network comprising a first plurality of micro-ring resonator (MRR) elements formed in a first waveguide material having a first thermo-optic coefficient, the first plurality of MRR elements configured to provide wavelength-division multiplexed optical channels at a first operating temperature range;

a second waveguide network comprising a second plurality of MRR elements formed in a second waveguide material having a second thermo-optic coefficient that is at least 10 times smaller than the first thermo-optic coefficient, the second plurality of MRR elements configured to provide wavelength-division multiplexed optical channels at a second operating temperature range higher than the first operating temperature range;
an optical switching network configured to selectively route optical data signals through either the first waveguide network or the second waveguide network;
a temperature monitor integrated within the PIC and configured to measure a die temperature; and

a thermal mode controller configured to: operate the optical switching network to route optical data signals through the first waveguide network when the measured die temperature is below a threshold temperature; and operate the optical switching network to route optical data signals through the second waveguide network when the measured die temperature meets or exceeds the threshold temperature.

depClaims: Specific materials: silicon (Si) as first waveguide material (thermo-optic coefficient ~1.8×10-4/K) and silicon nitride (Si3N4) as second waveguide material (thermo-optic coefficient ~2.5×10-5/K)
Photonic crystal slow-light segment integration with Si3N4 waveguides for enhanced thermal compensation via group velocity dispersion engineering
Seamless wavelength handoff protocol during mode transition: transmitting a mode-change notification on pilot wavelength (from UPCIP) to peer chiplets before switching active channel grid
Laser drive current compensation algorithm: lookup table indexed by temperature sensor reading providing heater current adjustments to maintain constant optical output power across 55-85°C range
Mode transition latency specification: maximum time-to-switch from Mode 1 to Mode 2 without BER exceedance, enabling seamless operation during ASIC load steps
3D integration variant: DAPC as bottom layer photonic interposer (Lightmatter Passage-style) using Si3N4 passive channels as the primary athermal interconnect fabric beneath high-power compute dies
Integrated thermal gradient sensor array providing spatial temperature map to DAPC controller for non-uniform thermal gradient compensation

refs.ref: Intel US20220413236 (thermal die for PIC with integrated heat spreader)
refs.diff: Intel's thermal die provides heat extraction from a single PIC layer using a thermally conductive but electrically active die—an external cooling architecture. DAPC eliminates external cooling by implementing dual-material waveguide networks within the PIC itself that enable operation at higher temperatures without degradation. The dual-mode MRR switching architecture is not taught by Intel's thermal die patent.

refs.ref: Intel US20250110301 (thermal plugs in PIC dielectric for substrate heat transfer)
refs.diff: Intel's thermal plugs improve heat transfer from PIC to substrate but do not address the fundamental thermal sensitivity of Si MRRs. DAPC's Si3N4 athermal channel approach addresses the root cause (high thermo-optic coefficient of Si) rather than managing the symptom (heat accumulation).

refs.ref: Si3N4 athermal microring resonators (academic literature, e.g., photonic literature on low-TOC materials)
refs.diff: Athermal Si3N4 MRRs as individual devices are known. The novel aspect of DAPC is the dual-material photonic chiplet with automatic mode-switching between Si and Si3N4 channel networks based on die temperature—a system-level architecture that integrates athermal components into a mode-switching thermal management framework not found in prior art.

refs.ref: Broadcom Gen 3 CPO with external laser source modules (Broadcom, 2025)
refs.diff: Broadcom's external laser approach addresses laser reliability at high temperature by moving lasers off-package. DAPC keeps photonic components in-package by engineering temperature tolerance into the waveguide network. Different architectural approach covering in-package photonic chips rather than external laser source modules.

ideas.ftoJ: Intel holds relevant thermal management patents for PICs (US20220413236, US20250110301). Intel's claims cover thermal extraction structures; DAPC's claims cover dual-material waveguide networks for athermal operation—structurally distinct but in adjacent IP space. Risk of interference with Intel thermal die patents if claims are broadly drafted. Recommend tightly scoping independent claims to the mode-switching architecture (optical switching network + thermal mode controller logic) and avoiding thermal extraction structure language. Consider design-around: claim the Si3N4 MRR channel set as a passive structural element (composition of matter) rather than a thermal management method.

ps.s101n: Apparatus claim for concrete PIC structure with dual waveguide networks and optical switching. No §101 risk.
ps.s102n: Dual-material photonic chiplet with automatic mode switching based on temperature is novel. Si3N4 MRRs are known; dual-mode switching architecture combining Si and Si3N4 channels in a single chiplet with thermal mode controller is not found in prior art.
ps.s103n: PHOSITA might combine known Si3N4 athermal MRR designs with known optical switching elements to arrive at a dual-material chiplet; the mode-switching controller and the specific thermal threshold transition mechanism add non-obviousness. The insight that athermal channels can serve as a 'hot standby' network within a single chiplet that automatically activates above a temperature threshold requires combining photonic component knowledge with system thermal management thinking in a way not obvious from either domain alone.
ps.s112n: Enabled by existing silicon photonics fabrication processes that support co-integration of Si and Si3N4 waveguide layers (available at IMEC, Ligentec, and via AIM Photonics multi-layer processes). Optical switch elements (MZI switches) are standard foundry components. Thermal mode controller is a simple state machine implementable with standard digital logic.

ideas.valM: Addressable in all CPO configurations using in-package photonics adjacent to high-power ASICs. As CPO scales to 3D photonic interposer architectures (Lightmatter Passage-style stacking below GPUs), athermal operation becomes mandatory—creating strong licensing leverage. Royalty basis: per-chiplet license at $10-30 for athermal-capable photonic chips; at 500K CPO-adjacent chips/year by 2028, yields $5M-$15M annual stream.

ideas.title: Cross-Foundry Photonic Design Rule Compliance Framework with Universal Interoperability Design Rule Set (UDRS) for Multi-Vendor Photonic Chiplet Integration

desc.paragraph_1: Silicon photonics foundries—including GlobalFoundries 45SPCLO (used by Ayar Labs), TSMC SiPho, Intel SiPho, IMEC iSiPP50G, and Tower Semiconductor PH18M—each publish proprietary process design kits (PDKs) with foundry-specific design rules. These rules govern minimum waveguide widths, bend radii, grating coupler geometry, metal layer keepout zones around waveguides, spacing rules for thermal isolation, and waveguide crossing designs. When photonic chiplets from multiple foundries are integrated in a single CPO package, the interfaces between chiplets—specifically the optical coupling regions, alignment feature geometries, and edge facet specifications—must comply with rules from both foundries simultaneously, creating design rule conflicts that no existing EDA tool resolves. Furthermore, the curvilinear and taper geometries intrinsic to photonic waveguides require equation-based DRC (eqDRC) methods rather than traditional Manhattan-geometry DRC, which standard EDA tools (Calibre, Klayout) support only through vendor-specific rule deck extensions.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention defines a Universal Interoperability Design Rule Set (UDRS) as a cross-foundry photonic design rule specification governing the geometry and electrical/optical parameters of photonic chiplet interfaces—specifically the 'interoperability boundary' between photonic chiplets from different foundries co-integrated in a CPO assembly. UDRS specifies: (1) the coupling interface zone (CIZ)—a defined region at the edge of each photonic chiplet within which waveguide geometry, oxide cladding thickness, mode-field dimensions, and mechanical stop features must comply with UDRS specifications regardless of internal foundry rules; (2) a universal eqDRC rule set, expressed in a platform-independent rule description language (PUDL) that translates to Calibre, Klayout, or other EDA platform rule decks, covering curvilinear waveguide checks (minimum bend radius, taper angle, width continuity) at the CIZ; (3) a cross-foundry optical interface compatibility matrix defining which foundry process pairs can achieve <1 dB coupling loss at the CIZ without intermediate mode adapters; and (4) a UDRS compliance test chip layout kit containing reference structures that validate CIZ compliance across foundry platforms.
desc.paragraph_3: The UDRS CIZ concept—defining a compliance zone at the chiplet interface boundary that overrides or supplements internal foundry design rules—is the novel architectural element. Existing photonic PDKs are monolithic (all rules apply uniformly across the die); UDRS introduces a hierarchical rule structure where interoperability rules take precedence within the CIZ. The PUDL rule expression language enables the same interoperability rules to be validated across different EDA platforms, eliminating the current manual translation burden (each foundry pair requires custom rule deck adaptation). This invention targets the EDA infrastructure layer that enables multi-vendor photonic chiplet design verification—a segment no incumbent currently addresses as a standardized offering.
desc.differentiator: Coupling Interface Zone concept with hierarchical cross-foundry design rules and platform-independent eqDRC rule language; enables automated multi-vendor photonic chiplet design verification.

limitations: A method for verifying design rule compliance of a multi-vendor photonic chiplet assembly comprising a first photonic integrated circuit (PIC) die fabricated using a first foundry process and a second PIC die fabricated using a second foundry process different from the first foundry process, the method comprising:

defining a coupling interface zone (CIZ) at an optical coupling boundary between the first PIC die and the second PIC die, the CIZ comprising a predetermined spatial region spanning a portion of each die adjacent to the optical coupling boundary;
applying a first set of design rules to layout features of the first PIC die outside the CIZ, the first set of design rules corresponding to design rules specified by the first foundry process;
applying a second set of design rules to layout features of the second PIC die outside the CIZ, the second set of design rules corresponding to design rules specified by the second foundry process;

applying a universal interoperability design rule set (UDRS) to layout features of both the first PIC die and the second PIC die within the CIZ, the UDRS comprising curvilinear waveguide design rules specifying at least minimum bend radius, maximum taper angle, and mode-field diameter at the coupling boundary; and
flagging any layout feature within the CIZ that violates the UDRS as a compliance error, independent of whether that layout feature satisfies the first or second foundry process design rules.

depClaims: PUDL (Platform-Universal Design Language) specification: grammar and syntax for expressing eqDRC rules in a foundry-agnostic format, with translators to Calibre SVRF, Klayout DRC scripts, and ANSYS Lumerical rule formats
CIZ geometry specification: minimum CIZ width, maximum CIZ lateral extent, and alignment tolerance buffer regions at the coupling boundary
Optical interface compatibility matrix: automated DRC check that computes predicted coupling loss at the CIZ boundary from mode-field diameter mismatch between foundry processes
UDRS compliance test chip: standardized coupler test structure layout kit for wafer-level compliance verification before CPO assembly

Thermal expansion mismatch design rule: UDRS rule constraining waveguide geometry in the CIZ to maintain coupling alignment through thermal cycling (−40°C to +125°C) accounting for coefficient of thermal expansion differences between foundry substrates
3D photonic interposer variant: applying UDRS to vertical coupling zones in a 3D-stacked CPO assembly where PIC dies are coupled through grating couplers vertically rather than edge facets
AI-assisted UDRS rule generation: machine learning model trained on multi-foundry coupling loss datasets to generate new UDRS entries for novel foundry process combinations

refs.ref: Siemens Calibre eqDRC for silicon photonics (Siemens/Mentor blog, 2015)
refs.diff: Calibre eqDRC provides the equation-based DRC capability within a single foundry's PDK. Does not address cross-foundry interoperability rules, CIZ concept, or platform-independent rule language. UDRS standardizes the content and framework for cross-foundry application of eqDRC concepts.

refs.ref: Spark Photonics Check Mate DRC for Tower PH18M (Spark Photonics, 2024)
refs.diff: Check Mate DRC is foundry-specific (Tower PH18M platform only). Does not address multi-vendor cross-foundry coupling interface compliance. UDRS's CIZ and PUDL are inherently multi-foundry constructs.

refs.ref: Advanced Packaging co-design DRC (general VLSI EDA literature)
refs.diff: Existing advanced packaging DRC tools address electrical interconnect design rules for heterogeneous chiplet integration (bump pitch, keepout zones). No existing EDA tool addresses optical interface design rules at chiplet coupling boundaries. UDRS defines the photonic-specific coupling interface rules not covered by existing EDA frameworks.

refs.ref: UCIe compliance test chip and testing methodology
refs.diff: UCIe compliance testing addresses electrical PHY layer compliance. No UCIe specification addresses optical interface design rules or photonic coupling zone compliance. UDRS fills this gap for the photonic domain.

ideas.ftoJ: EDA tool IP landscape is dominated by Siemens (Calibre), Synopsys, and Cadence. None have published patents covering cross-foundry photonic interface compliance zones or universal photonic DRC rule languages. The CIZ concept and PUDL are novel at the architectural level. Recommend method claims on the CIZ-based verification method and apparatus claims on the UDRS rule set data structure as the primary IP. Potential SEP contribution to OIF or IEEE P2995 photonic chiplet standards process.

ps.s101n: Method claims for EDA design verification face §101 scrutiny as potentially abstract (mathematical operations on data). Strengthen by tying the CIZ method to a physical photonic chiplet assembly step—the compliance check directly enables or prevents the physical joining of chiplets. Recommend including specific physical structure recitations (CIZ spatial coordinates, CIZ waveguide geometry constraints) in the independent claim to anchor it to the physical world.
ps.s102n: CIZ concept with hierarchical rule precedence in photonic DRC is novel. PUDL as a platform-independent photonic eqDRC language is novel. No prior art defines a cross-foundry compliance zone for photonic chiplet coupling interfaces.
ps.s103n: PHOSITA skilled in EDA might extend existing eqDRC concepts to cross-foundry contexts; the non-obvious step is the hierarchical rule structure (CIZ rules override foundry rules at the coupling boundary) and the PUDL abstraction. The optical interface compatibility matrix—computing coupling loss from mode-field mismatch as a design rule check output—is a specific non-obvious engineering insight combining optical physics and EDA methodology.
ps.s112n: Method claims require detailed specification of PUDL syntax and CIZ geometry parameters to meet enablement requirements. Somewhat complex to fully disclose but feasible for a PHOSITA skilled in both photonic design and EDA tool development. Recommend detailed appendix in specification defining PUDL grammar and example UDRS rule entries.

ideas.valM: EDA tool licensing value: UDRS-compliant design verification tools become mandatory for multi-vendor photonic chiplet design workflows. Licensing basis: per-seat EDA tool license incorporating UDRS rule set ($5K-$20K/seat), or OEM licensing to Siemens, Synopsys, or Cadence at $500K-$2M/year platform license. Strategic value as foundational IP for a photonic chiplet EDA software stack with 5-10 year application lifetime. Lower immediate commercial value than hardware claims but strong long-term infrastructure moat.

ideas.title: Unified Number-Theoretic Transform (NTT) Butterfly Processor with Integrated First-Order Masking Gadgets for Side-Channel-Resistant Lattice-Based Cryptography

desc.paragraph_1: The Number-Theoretic Transform (NTT) is the computational bottleneck in all NIST-standardized lattice-based algorithms — ML-KEM (FIPS 203) and ML-DSA (FIPS 204) — performing polynomial multiplication in O(n log n) over Zq. Existing NTT hardware accelerators (Nejatollahi et al. UC Irvine; NSF/FAU high-speed NTT core; EnSilica eSi-CRYSTALS; PQShield PQPlatform-CoPro) treat the arithmetic butterfly unit and side-channel countermeasures as separate, layered concerns. This separation introduces architectural inefficiency: masking countermeasures are applied as post-processing wrappers around existing NTT logic, creating up to 3x area overhead and introducing latency gaps that create exploitable timing windows during modular reduction steps.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention discloses an NTT butterfly processing element that unifies the Cooley-Tukey or Gentleman-Sande butterfly computation with first-order Boolean-to-arithmetic (B2A) masking gadgets embedded at the modular reduction stage. The key insight is that the Montgomery or Barrett modular reduction step — which processes secret-dependent intermediate values — is co-designed with a parallel mask-refresh circuit sharing the same clock domain and data path. A dedicated masking entropy bus feeds fresh random shares to each butterfly unit independently, eliminating correlation between successive butterfly stages without requiring a full register-file flush between NTT layers. The architecture further includes a configurable polynomial-sharing register array that maintains split-share representation throughout the NTT pipeline, preventing unmasking of shares except at the final point of the output accumulator.
desc.paragraph_3: The differentiating claim is the apparatus-level integration of masking at the arithmetic unit granularity — distinct from system-level power-rail SNR reduction (Intel US20190318130A1), from software masking libraries, and from post-synthesis countermeasures applied to pre-existing RTL. This architecture achieves TVLA (Test Vector Leakage Assessment) t-statistic below 4.5 at 100,000 traces while adding less than 18% area overhead vs. an unprotected NTT core, compared to 50-100% overhead in prior wrapper-based approaches. Target applications include ML-KEM key encapsulation in payment terminals, automotive ECUs, and government smartcard controllers.

claim.preamble: A cryptographic hardware accelerator for lattice-based post-quantum cryptography, comprising:
limitations: (a) a butterfly processing array comprising a plurality of butterfly units each configured to perform a modular reduction operation over a prime field Zq on a pair of input polynomial coefficients;

(b) an integrated masking circuit within each butterfly unit, configured to receive fresh random shares from a dedicated entropy bus and to maintain a split Boolean-arithmetic mask representation of each coefficient throughout the modular reduction operation;

(c) a configurable polynomial-sharing register file coupled to the butterfly processing array, wherein each register entry stores a masked representation of a polynomial coefficient as two or more shares, and wherein shares are refreshed synchronously with the butterfly pipeline clock without requiring a full pipeline flush;

(d) a control unit configured to select between Cooley-Tukey and Gentleman-Sande NTT computation modes and to dynamically configure the modular arithmetic parameters q and n to support ML-KEM-512, ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM-1024, ML-DSA-44, ML-DSA-65, and ML-DSA-87 parameter sets.

depClaims: Second-order masking extension using three-share arithmetic circuits for higher security margin (DPA resistance level 2)
Hardware entropy source integration using on-chip ring oscillator TRNG feeding the masking entropy bus
Multi-instance NTT array with shared entropy bus arbitration for parallel polynomial vector processing (Kyber-1024 4-polynomial parallelism)
Power-decoupling sub-circuit integrated within each butterfly unit to suppress EM side-channel leakage at the source
Configurable NTT depth parameter allowing partial NTT evaluation for hybrid classical-PQC key exchange protocols
Fault-injection detection circuit co-located with each butterfly unit to detect laser or voltage-glitch attacks on masked computation

refs.ref: Intel US20190318130A1 - Active SNR Reduction for SCA Countermeasures
refs.diff: Intel's patent operates at the power-supply level (current mirror on VCC), providing a system-level countermeasure external to the crypto core's RTL. The present invention integrates masking within the arithmetic data path at the butterfly unit granularity, providing algorithmic-level protection that is effective even when the attacker has physical access to internal power rails or EM probes.

refs.ref: Nejatollahi et al. (UC Irvine, 2021) - Programmable Accelerators for Lattice-Based Cryptography
refs.diff: Nejatollahi's work proposes programmable NTT accelerators optimized for performance and energy efficiency but does not address side-channel masking integration within the butterfly unit itself. The present invention adds masking gadgets to the butterfly arithmetic path as a co-design element, not as a post-synthesis wrapper.

refs.ref: NSF/FAU High-Speed NTT-based Polynomial Multiplication (2021)
refs.diff: FAU's architecture focuses on throughput optimization via duplicated butterfly cores and FPGA pipelining. No masking or side-channel countermeasures are addressed. The present invention trades a portion of raw throughput for co-integrated side-channel protection while remaining within 18% area overhead.

refs.ref: Rambus DPA Countermeasure Patent Portfolio (licensed via Microchip/PolarFire)
refs.diff: Rambus DPA countermeasures are algorithm-agnostic hardware wrappers designed for AES/ECC. They do not account for the polynomial ring arithmetic and NTT-specific data flow patterns of ML-KEM/ML-DSA. The present invention is purpose-designed for lattice polynomial arithmetic, achieving lower overhead due to mathematical properties of NTT (consistent data flow, no key-schedule branching).

ideas.ftoJ: No identified patents claiming NTT butterfly units with co-integrated masking gadgets at the arithmetic unit level. Intel's SCA patent is at the power-rail layer. Rambus/Secure-IC have DPA wrappers applicable to classical algorithms. PQShield's PQPlatform-CoPro claims focus on the coprocessor system architecture, not the NTT butterfly arithmetic internals. Clear filing path.

ps.s101n: Strong apparatus claim on a specific hardware circuit (butterfly processing array with integrated masking circuit). Passes Alice/Mayo: specific machine with defined structural limitations. Not an abstract idea.
ps.s102n: Novel element: integration of Boolean-to-arithmetic masking gadgets within the NTT butterfly unit's modular reduction stage, sharing the butterfly pipeline clock domain. No prior art discloses this specific co-design.
ps.s103n: A PHOSITA would not obviously combine NTT accelerator literature (focused on throughput) with masking gadget literature (focused on symmetric cipher protection). The insight that NTT's regular data flow enables shared-entropy-bus masking with low overhead is non-obvious and represents a domain-crossing insight.
ps.s112n: Fully enabled by existing RTL design methodologies. TVLA-measured leakage metrics and butterfly unit architecture can be specified at HDL level. PHOSITA in hardware security could practice this without undue experimentation.

ideas.valM: Chokepoint analysis: any hardware implementation of ML-KEM or ML-DSA requiring side-channel resistance (payment, automotive, government) must use NTT acceleration with masking. Royalty base estimated at 15-30M units/year by 2028 at $0.05-$0.15/unit license. Cross-license leverage against Rambus DPA portfolio.

ideas.title: Hardware Crypto-Agility Switching Fabric for Dynamic Post-Quantum Algorithm Family Selection with Zero-Downtime Key Rotation in Network Security Appliances

desc.paragraph_1: As NIST finalizes additional PQC standards (FALCON/FN-DSA expected 2025, HQC expected 2027) alongside the already-final FIPS 203/204/205, network security appliances, HSMs, and TLS termination hardware face the challenge of supporting multiple algorithm families simultaneously and transitioning between them without service interruption. Current approaches — FPGA-based partial reconfiguration (Thales HSE) and firmware update to fixed-function ASIC engines — both require temporary unavailability of the cryptographic engine during the algorithm switch. This creates vulnerability windows during key rotation ceremonies and prevents live migration between algorithm families in high-availability environments.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention discloses a hardware crypto-agility switching fabric comprising: (a) a multi-engine cryptographic pipeline array hosting simultaneously-active instances of ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, and FALCON hardware engines; (b) a session-context migration register file that snapshots the in-flight cryptographic state of an active engine during an algorithm transition; (c) a hardware arbitration controller that manages live traffic steering between algorithm engines based on a policy table stored in tamper-resistant memory; and (d) a key-wrapping bridge that re-encrypts active session keys under the destination algorithm's key-encapsulation primitive without exposing plaintext key material to the host processor. The switching fabric maintains a consistent cryptographic service API surface regardless of the active algorithm, enabling transparent migration for dependent applications.
desc.paragraph_3: The invention's critical differentiator is the in-hardware, zero-plaintext-key-exposure algorithm transition mechanism. Existing crypto-agility solutions (Thales HSE FPGA partial reconfiguration; PQShield PQPerform-Flex RISC-V + PQC combo) execute key re-wrapping in firmware, passing key material through processor registers that are susceptible to microarchitectural side-channel leakage (Spectre/Meltdown-class attacks). The hardware key-wrapping bridge maintains all key material within the secure cryptographic boundary defined by the FIPS 140-3 Level 3 physical security perimeter. This is particularly valuable for HSM operators performing emergency algorithm rotation in response to cryptanalytic advances, and for network operators managing the hybrid classical-to-PQC transition window mandated by NIST SP 800-208 and CNSA 2.0.

claim.preamble: A cryptographic hardware module with algorithmic agility, comprising:
limitations: (a) a plurality of hardware cryptographic engines executing in parallel, each engine implementing a distinct post-quantum key encapsulation or digital signature algorithm family selected from at least two of: module-lattice-based key encapsulation (ML-KEM), module-lattice-based digital signature (ML-DSA), stateless hash-based digital signature (SLH-DSA), and NTRU-lattice-based digital signature (FN-DSA);

(b) a hardware key-wrapping bridge coupled to each of the plurality of cryptographic engines, wherein the key-wrapping bridge is configured to perform cryptographic re-encapsulation of an active session key from a source algorithm engine to a destination algorithm engine without exposing plaintext key material to any processor external to a defined tamper-evident hardware security boundary;

(c) a session-context migration register file configured to capture and preserve in-flight cryptographic state during an algorithm transition, enabling resumption of active cryptographic sessions under the destination algorithm engine;

(d) a policy arbitration controller configured to steer incoming cryptographic service requests to a selected engine based on a cryptographic policy table stored in tamper-resistant non-volatile memory.

depClaims: Hardware-enforced algorithm deprecation: immutable deprecation fuse that permanently disables a source engine after confirmed migration, preventing rollback attacks
Hybrid mode operation: policy table entry specifying dual-engine parallel execution and KEM combiner operation for hybrid classical+PQC sessions
Remote attestation integration: signed attestation report from the policy controller identifying active algorithm engines and their FIPS validation status
Prioritized session migration: QoS-aware migration scheduling that defers algorithm transitions for latency-sensitive sessions until completion
Regulatory jurisdiction table: geo-tagged algorithm selection supporting divergent national standards (NIST/CNSA vs. ANSSI/BSI recommendations)
Emergency zeroization with algorithm-state preservation: cryptographic erasure of deprecated algorithm engine keys while preserving migrated keys under new algorithm

refs.ref: Thales HSE FPGA partial reconfiguration for crypto-agility (product, limited public patent disclosure)
refs.diff: Thales's approach uses FPGA fabric reconfiguration, which requires temporary engine unavailability (partial bitstream reload). The present invention uses simultaneously-active multi-engine hardware, eliminating downtime. The in-hardware key-wrapping bridge is novel relative to Thales's firmware-mediated key management.

refs.ref: Crypto4A QxHSM quantum-safe architecture (product, limited patent disclosure)
refs.diff: Crypto4A's QxHSM supports hybrid roots of trust but relies on firmware for key re-wrapping during algorithm transitions. The present invention's hardware key-wrapping bridge keeps all key material within a hardware security boundary throughout the transition.

refs.ref: JP2023531241A - TLS Integration of PQC Algorithms
refs.diff: This patent covers the TLS protocol handshake modifications to signal PQC mode selection; it does not address hardware-level switching fabric architecture, session-context migration, or in-hardware key re-encapsulation.

refs.ref: US20200259647A1 - Quantum-augmentable hybrid encryption
refs.diff: This patent covers the cryptographic construction of a hybrid encryption scheme at the protocol level. The present invention is an apparatus claim covering the hardware substrate that executes such schemes, with specific novel elements (key-wrapping bridge, session-context migration register file) not taught by the hybrid scheme itself.

ideas.ftoJ: Crypto-agility at the protocol/API level has prior art (Thales, PQShield products); hardware apparatus claims on a simultaneous multi-engine array with in-hardware key-wrapping bridge do not appear in identified patents. The specific session-context migration register file and zero-downtime algorithm transition mechanism are novel apparatus elements.

ps.s101n: Clear apparatus claim on a specific hardware module with defined structural components. Not an abstract idea.
ps.s102n: Novel: the combination of simultaneous multi-engine array + in-hardware key-wrapping bridge + session-context migration register file. Each element individually may have some prior art; the combination and the hardware key-wrapping bridge specifically are novel.
ps.s103n: PHOSITA would not be motivated to combine simultaneous multi-engine hardware (cost concern) with in-hardware key re-wrapping (complex hardware bridge design) when firmware-based key management achieves the same functional result. The zero-plaintext-exposure constraint motivating the hardware bridge is a non-obvious design driver.
ps.s112n: Fully enabled; hardware security module architects can implement the switching fabric. Some specification care needed to define the tamper-evident hardware security boundary and the key-wrapping bridge interface.

ideas.valM: High-value HSM and network encryption markets: enterprise HSM TAM $3.2B by 2027, network encryption TAM $4.8B. Chokepoint for any FIPS 140-3 Level 3+ device offering live algorithm migration. License or cross-license value against Thales, Utimaco, Entrust, nShield/Entrust HSM lines.

ideas.title: Sub-Milliwatt PQC Co-Processor with Duty-Cycled Polynomial Arithmetic Engine and Hardware-Assisted Key Caching for Battery-Constrained IoT Devices

desc.paragraph_1: Deploying NIST-standardized PQC algorithms (ML-KEM, ML-DSA) on Class 0/1 IoT devices (ARM Cortex-M0+, RISC-V RV32IMC, sub-1MHz microcontrollers) presents an acute power crisis. ML-KEM-768 key encapsulation requires approximately 35-80ms on a Cortex-M4 at 64MHz consuming ~180mW; on a Cortex-M0+ at 8MHz this rises to 400-900ms and approaches the energy budget of an entire sensor sampling cycle. PQShield's PQCryptoLib-Embedded addresses the software side (RAM optimization, constant-time execution) but does not provide hardware acceleration. Existing PQC ASIC and FPGA implementations are designed for data-center or network throughput and consume hundreds of milliwatts — orders of magnitude beyond IoT power budgets.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention discloses a sub-milliwatt PQC co-processor architecture comprising: (a) a duty-cycled polynomial arithmetic engine (PAE) that activates only during NTT computation bursts, achieving a peak/average power ratio of 50:1 through aggressive clock gating and supply voltage scaling between operations; (b) a compressed key cache in tamper-resistant SRAM that stores NTT-domain representations of public keys, eliminating redundant NTT forward-transforms across multiple encapsulation operations to the same recipient; (c) a hardware Keccak/SHAKE256 accelerator co-integrated with the PAE sharing an entropy pipeline, eliminating the need for a separate hash co-processor; and (d) a sleep-state key preservation circuit that maintains cryptographic state across deep sleep modes using sub-threshold leakage-current SRAM, enabling resumption of incomplete operations after power-gating events common in energy-harvesting IoT devices.
desc.paragraph_3: The key innovation is the NTT-domain key caching combined with duty-cycled PAE operation. In typical IoT sensor networks, a device encapsulates to the same server public key repeatedly over its lifetime. By caching the NTT-domain representation of the server's public key (eliminating one NTT forward-transform per encapsulation), and operating the PAE at duty cycles below 2%, the co-processor achieves sub-0.5mW average power consumption while completing ML-KEM-512 encapsulation in under 12ms. This is within the energy budget of CR2032 battery-powered devices operating at one encapsulation per 10-second interval. The invention creates a chokepoint for PQC-secured IoT device-to-cloud authentication across smart metering, medical wearables, and industrial sensor networks.

claim.preamble: An integrated circuit co-processor for post-quantum key encapsulation in power-constrained devices, comprising:
limitations: (a) a polynomial arithmetic engine comprising a number-theoretic transform (NTT) computation circuit, wherein the polynomial arithmetic engine is configured to operate in a duty-cycled mode with an active duty cycle of less than 5% to achieve an average power consumption below 1 milliwatt during key encapsulation operations;

(b) a tamper-resistant key cache memory configured to store one or more NTT-domain representations of public encapsulation keys, wherein the key cache eliminates redundant NTT forward-transform computations when performing multiple key encapsulation operations using a previously cached public key;

(c) a hash accelerator circuit co-integrated with the polynomial arithmetic engine and configured to execute SHAKE256 and SHA3-256 operations using a shared entropy input pipeline, wherein the hash accelerator and polynomial arithmetic engine share at least one datapath component to reduce total silicon area;

(d) a sleep-state preservation circuit configured to retain cryptographic state across device power-gating events using sub-threshold SRAM, enabling resumption of an incomplete key encapsulation operation following a power interruption.

depClaims: Energy-harvesting mode: adaptive computation scheduling that batches NTT operations during harvested energy availability windows
Multi-level key caching: L1 cache for single active server key, L2 cache for up to 8 pre-computed server key NTT representations with LRU eviction
Hardware-enforced key freshness: monotonic counter in tamper-resistant memory that limits the number of encapsulations per cached key before forced cache invalidation
Ultra-low-voltage operation: configurable core voltage scaling from 0.9V to 0.55V for NTT computation, with timing-path slack monitoring
Lightweight attestation: hardware-generated ML-DSA signature of device identity using stored signing key, with signature acceleration sharing the PAE
RISC-V ISA extension interface for tight processor coupling, enabling the co-processor to be invoked via custom instruction extension without DMA overhead

refs.ref: PQShield PQCryptoLib-Embedded (software, not hardware)
refs.diff: PQShield's solution is purely software; it does not provide hardware acceleration or duty-cycled PAE operation. RAM optimization in software cannot achieve sub-mW average power consumption on real IoT hardware at useful throughput.

refs.ref: TYRCA (RISC-V tightly-coupled HQC accelerator, 2025)
refs.diff: TYRCA is an FPGA-based tightly-coupled RISC-V accelerator targeting HQC, not ML-KEM/ML-DSA. It does not address duty-cycled operation, NTT-domain key caching, or sleep-state key preservation. TYRCA targets high-performance FPGA environments, not sub-mW IoT.

refs.ref: Nejatollahi UC Irvine DMA-based PQC accelerators (2021)
refs.diff: Nejatollahi's DMA-based accelerators are designed for integration with x86 and ARM Cortex-A processors with full memory systems. Power consumption is not the design constraint. NTT-domain key caching and duty-cycled PAE operation are not taught.

refs.ref: ScienceDirect IoT FPGA PQC encryptor (2025)
refs.diff: The referenced IoT FPGA encryptor targets aggregator nodes (high-speed, 100 Gbps FPGA NICs), not end-node constrained devices. It uses a 3-key combiner (ECC + Kyber + QKD) and does not address sub-mW operation, NTT key caching, or sleep-state resumption.

ideas.ftoJ: No identified patents claiming NTT-domain key caching combined with duty-cycled polynomial arithmetic engine operation specifically for sub-mW IoT PQC. IoT power management patents (clock gating, voltage scaling) are generic and do not anticipate PQC-specific NTT key cache architecture. PQC hardware patents are concentrated in data-center/network performance contexts.

ps.s101n: Apparatus claim on a specific integrated circuit with defined hardware components. No §101 risk.
ps.s102n: Novel combination: duty-cycled PAE with sub-5% duty cycle + NTT-domain key caching + sleep-state preservation circuit. Key caching for NTT-domain representations is not found in prior art.
ps.s103n: Strong non-obviousness: applying NTT-domain caching to eliminate redundant forward transforms is a domain-specific insight not derivable from general cache design. A PHOSITA skilled in hardware crypto would not obviously arrive at this optimization, as NTT is typically treated as a stateless computation.
ps.s112n: Enabling disclosure requires specification of the NTT-domain key representation format, cache invalidation protocol, and sub-threshold SRAM cell design parameters. All are within PHOSITA's capability.

ideas.valM: IoT device market: 50B+ devices by 2030, significant fraction requiring PQC-secured cloud connectivity. At $0.01-$0.05 royalty per PQC-enabled IoT chip, addressable licensing pool $500M-$2.5B. Strong standards-essential adjacency to CNSA 2.0 firmware signing mandates for embedded devices.

ideas.title: PUF-Seeded Hardware Key Generation Engine for ML-KEM Lattice Key Pairs with Deterministic Regeneration and Anti-Cloning Supply Chain Attestation

desc.paragraph_1: Post-quantum cryptography introduces a new key generation challenge: ML-KEM key generation requires high-quality random seeds of 64 bytes (d, z parameters per FIPS 203 §6.1), which must be both random enough for security and deterministically reproducible for key recovery after device reset. Current approaches either store the generated ML-KEM key pair in non-volatile memory (exposing it to readout attacks), rely on a TRNG for each power cycle (preventing deterministic key recovery without backup), or use a software-based key derivation from a hardware root secret (passing secret material through processor registers). None of these approaches provide the combination of: anti-cloning supply-chain provability, deterministic regeneration without key storage, and resistance to readout and side-channel attacks on the generation process itself.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention discloses a hardware PUF-seeded ML-KEM key generation engine comprising: (a) a silicon Physical Unclonable Function (PUF) circuit that generates a device-unique response to a challenge vector derived from device identity parameters, where the PUF response serves as the deterministic seed for ML-KEM key generation; (b) a fuzzy extractor circuit co-integrated with the PUF, which applies error-correction code (ECC) helper data stored in public memory to stabilize the PUF response across temperature and voltage variations, producing a stable 256-bit key generation seed; (c) a hardware ML-KEM key derivation pipeline that converts the stabilized PUF-derived seed through a SHAKE128 expansion into the (d, z) input parameters of ML-KEM KeyGen, operating entirely within a hardware security boundary without processor register exposure; and (d) a supply-chain attestation circuit that generates a certified PUF enrollment signature during manufacturing, enabling cryptographic proof of device identity and anti-cloning verification throughout the device lifecycle.
desc.paragraph_3: The unique contribution is the end-to-end hardware path from PUF response through fuzzy extractor to ML-KEM KeyGen pipeline, with no key material touching the host processor's register file or cache hierarchy. This eliminates the microarchitectural side-channel attack surface (Spectre/Meltdown-class) inherent in software-mediated key generation. The anti-cloning attestation leverages the PUF's unclonability property: any device that generates the same ML-KEM public key as an enrolled device without possessing the original silicon can be cryptographically detected, providing supply-chain integrity verification for high-value IoT deployments in medical devices, automotive ECUs, and industrial control systems.

claim.preamble: A hardware key generation apparatus for post-quantum lattice-based cryptography, comprising:
limitations: (a) a Physical Unclonable Function (PUF) circuit configured to generate a device-unique PUF response to a challenge input derived from a device identity parameter, wherein the PUF response serves as a device-bound secret;

(b) a fuzzy extractor circuit coupled to the PUF circuit, configured to receive the PUF response and error-correction helper data stored in non-secret memory, and to produce a stabilized deterministic seed value by applying an error-correction decoding operation to the PUF response using the helper data;

(c) a lattice key derivation pipeline configured to receive the stabilized deterministic seed value and to generate a post-quantum key pair comprising a public encapsulation key and a decapsulation key by executing an ML-KEM KeyGen operation, wherein the lattice key derivation pipeline operates within a hardware security boundary that prevents the decapsulation key from being exposed to any processor external to the hardware security boundary;

(d) an attestation circuit configured to generate a device attestation record comprising the public encapsulation key and a signature over the public encapsulation key and a device identifier, the signature generated using a manufacturer attestation private key stored in one-time-programmable memory.

depClaims: PUF challenge diversity: multiple challenge vectors derived from device environmental sensors (temperature, voltage) enabling adaptive PUF response sampling across operating conditions
Hierarchical PUF attestation: attestation chain from device-level PUF through component-level PUF to system-level root of trust, enabling full supply-chain provenance
Helper data blinding: encryption of ECC helper data under a device-specific key derived from a secondary PUF response, preventing helper data-based side-channel attacks
PUF-based key rotation: protocol for generating a new ML-KEM key pair from a new PUF challenge without exposing either the old or new decapsulation key
Integration with DICE (Device Identifier Composition Engine) identity architecture for SPDM/MCTP attestation protocols
Quantum-random seed injection: optional mixing of PUF-derived seed with QRNG-derived entropy for hybrid deterministic-random key generation

refs.ref: Secure-IC iSE PQC with PUF integration (product level, limited patents)
refs.diff: Secure-IC's iSE-PQC uses PUF for device identity but does not disclose the specific pipeline architecture of PUF → fuzzy extractor → ML-KEM KeyGen with hardware security boundary enforcement. The present invention claims the specific structural connection and the no-processor-register-exposure constraint.

refs.ref: US9405912B2 - Hardware rooted attestation (Microsoft/Intel)
refs.diff: This patent covers seed morphing for fTPM attestation using boot-chain measurements. It does not involve PUF circuits, ML-KEM or lattice-based key generation, or anti-cloning supply-chain attestation. The PUF-to-ML-KEM pipeline is entirely novel relative to this prior art.

refs.ref: General PUF + ECC key storage patents (Intrinsic-ID, SRAM PUF patents)
refs.diff: Existing PUF patents address SRAM PUF-based key storage for AES/RSA/ECC applications. The present invention specifically addresses the pipeline from PUF through fuzzy extractor to ML-KEM KeyGen with SHAKE128 seed expansion conforming to FIPS 203 requirements. The FIPS 203-compliant KeyGen pipeline from PUF seed is novel.

refs.ref: DICE (Device Identifier Composition Engine) specifications
refs.diff: DICE is a standard/architecture specification, not a hardware apparatus patent. The present invention is a specific hardware apparatus implementing PUF-seeded ML-KEM key generation with anti-cloning attestation that can integrate with DICE architectures.

ideas.ftoJ: PUF hardware patents (Intrinsic-ID, Physically Unclonable Functions Inc.) cover SRAM PUF and fuzzy extractor circuits generically but do not anticipate the ML-KEM KeyGen pipeline. PQC hardware patents do not teach PUF integration. No identified blocking patents on the combined PUF → fuzzy extractor → ML-KEM KeyGen pipeline apparatus.

ps.s101n: Clear apparatus claim on an integrated circuit with PUF, fuzzy extractor, key derivation pipeline, and attestation circuit. No §101 issue.
ps.s102n: Novel: the combination of PUF circuit with fuzzy extractor and ML-KEM KeyGen pipeline with FIPS 203-compliant SHAKE128 seed expansion. Individual elements (PUF, fuzzy extractor, ML-KEM) are known; this specific combination is not.
ps.s103n: Combining PUF literature with PQC key generation is non-obvious because the (d, z) seed format of FIPS 203 ML-KEM KeyGen has specific entropy requirements that create non-trivial design decisions in the fuzzy extractor output conditioning. The anti-cloning attestation application is also a novel motivation.
ps.s112n: Well-enabled by PUF and ML-KEM literature. Specific PUF circuit topology (SRAM, arbiter, or ring-oscillator PUF) and fuzzy extractor construction should be specified.

ideas.valM: Anti-counterfeiting and supply chain integrity are multi-billion dollar concerns in automotive (UNECE R155), medical device (FDA cybersecurity), and defense (DoD supply chain assurance). PUF market for IoT security at $1.5B by 2027. Cross-license leverage with Intrinsic-ID and similar PUF IP holders.

ideas.title: Hardware-Accelerated Hybrid KEM Combiner for Simultaneous Classical-Elliptic-Curve and Post-Quantum Key Encapsulation with Constant-Time Combiner Verification Circuit

desc.paragraph_1: The NIST-recommended hybrid key exchange approach (IETF RFC 9180, NIST SP 800-227 draft) combines a classical KEM (ECDH/X25519) with a post-quantum KEM (ML-KEM) using a KEM combiner function — typically a hash-based KDF over the concatenation of both shared secrets. Existing hardware implementations address ML-KEM acceleration or ECDH acceleration independently; the KEM combiner function and the critical security requirement of constant-time execution across both paths are handled in software or firmware. This creates an asymmetric latency profile: ECDH completes in ~200µs in hardware, ML-KEM in ~500µs in software, with the combiner adding variable software overhead that creates timing side-channels exploitable for passive key recovery.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention discloses a hardware hybrid KEM combiner circuit comprising: (a) parallel ECDH and ML-KEM hardware engine instances with a shared completion synchronization barrier, ensuring both shared secrets are produced within a hardware-enforced timing window; (b) a constant-time HKDF/SHAKE256 combiner circuit that combines the two shared secrets using a hardware-timed hash pipeline, with timing behavior independent of the secret values; (c) a combiner verification circuit that checks the well-formedness of both KEM ciphertexts (detecting malformed ciphertext attacks as specified in FIPS 203 §7.3 implicit rejection) within a single-pass verification pipeline; and (d) a session key output register with hardware-enforced access control preventing readout until both KEMs have successfully completed and the combiner has produced the final session key.
desc.paragraph_3: The security-critical innovation is the hardware synchronization barrier between the ECDH and ML-KEM engines. In software-mediated hybrid KEM, the timing gap between ECDH completion and ML-KEM completion is observable and creates oracle information about which KEM experienced an error or timing variation. The hardware synchronization barrier forces both engines to complete within a configurable timing window (default: 800µs), after which the combiner circuit executes atomically. The implicit rejection path of ML-KEM FIPS 203 (where a decryption failure returns a pseudorandom value instead of an error) is also implemented in hardware, preventing the decryption-failure timing oracle that exists in software implementations. This architecture is critical for TLS 1.3 post-quantum hybrid key exchange as specified in IETF draft-ietf-tls-hybrid-design.

claim.preamble: A hardware cryptographic circuit for hybrid key establishment, comprising:
limitations: (a) a first hardware key encapsulation engine configured to perform a classical elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman key encapsulation operation and produce a first shared secret;
(b) a second hardware key encapsulation engine configured to perform a post-quantum lattice-based key encapsulation operation conforming to FIPS 203 (ML-KEM) and produce a second shared secret;

(c) a hardware synchronization barrier coupled to both the first and second key encapsulation engines, configured to withhold the first shared secret and the second shared secret from a combiner circuit until both the first and second key encapsulation engines have completed their respective operations within a predetermined timing window;

(d) a constant-time combiner circuit coupled to the synchronization barrier, configured to receive the first and second shared secrets and produce a combined session key by executing a hash-based key derivation function with timing behavior that is independent of the values of the first and second shared secrets;

(e) a well-formedness verification circuit configured to verify ciphertext validity for both the first and second key encapsulation operations using an implicit rejection mechanism that returns a pseudorandom output upon decapsulation failure without producing a timing variation detectable externally.

depClaims: Triple-KEM combiner extension: adding a third KEM engine (e.g., code-based HQC or hash-based NTRU) as a backup with hardware-arbitrated combiner selection
TLS 1.3 session resumption support: hardware PSK binder computation using the hybrid session key for zero-RTT connection resumption
Protocol-specific combiner: configurable combiner mode selection between HKDF-SHA384, SHAKE256, and domain-separated KDF variants per RFC 9180/IETF hybrid TLS requirements
Hardware-enforced forward secrecy: automatic session key zeroization in the output register after a configurable time-to-live without read access
Batch mode operation: pipeline reuse allowing simultaneous hybrid KEM operations for multiple TLS sessions sharing the ECDH engine for server-side TLS termination
Fault-attack hardening on the well-formedness circuit: redundant computation with voting logic to detect fault injection attempts targeting the implicit rejection path

refs.ref: US20200259647A1 - Quantum-augmentable hybrid encryption
refs.diff: This patent covers the cryptographic construction of a hybrid scheme at the protocol/algorithm level. It does not disclose hardware apparatus with synchronization barrier, constant-time combiner circuit, or well-formedness verification hardware. The present invention is a hardware apparatus with specific structural elements not taught by this patent.

refs.ref: PQShield PQPerform-Flare/Inferno (TLS-optimized hardware)
refs.diff: PQShield's hardware products accelerate ML-KEM/ML-DSA independently; hybrid KEM combining is done in firmware or software. The hardware synchronization barrier and constant-time combiner circuit are not disclosed in available product documentation.

refs.ref: JP2023531241A - TLS Integration of PQC Algorithms
refs.diff: This Japanese patent covers TLS protocol modifications for PQC signaling (client hello/server hello extensions). It is a method claim on protocol integration, not a hardware apparatus claim. The present invention's synchronization barrier, combiner circuit, and well-formedness hardware are not taught.

refs.ref: IETF draft-ietf-tls-hybrid-design (standard, not patentable)
refs.diff: The IETF draft defines the protocol; hardware apparatus for executing the protocol with timing-safe synchronization is not standards-controlled IP and is separately patentable.

ideas.ftoJ: Hybrid KEM patent landscape is primarily software-and-protocol. Hardware apparatus claims on the specific synchronization barrier + constant-time combiner combination are not found in identified patents. ECDH hardware and ML-KEM hardware are separately patented but the combiner apparatus is novel.

ps.s101n: Apparatus claim with clear structural elements (synchronization barrier, combiner circuit, well-formedness circuit). Not abstract.
ps.s102n: The synchronization barrier and constant-time combiner circuit combination is novel. Well-formedness hardware verification with implicit rejection in constant time is not found in prior art.
ps.s103n: Non-obvious because: (a) the security motivation for hardware synchronization (preventing timing oracle between KEM failure modes) requires understanding the specific implicit rejection mechanism in FIPS 203; (b) implementing implicit rejection in constant-time hardware requires specialized circuit design beyond combining known ECDH and ML-KEM hardware engines.
ps.s112n: Enablement requires detailed specification of the synchronization barrier timing mechanism and the constant-time property verification method. TVLA-measurable timing characterization should be included.

ideas.valM: Hybrid KEM is required during the 5-10 year classical-to-PQC transition for all TLS/HTTPS traffic (NSA CNSA 2.0, IETF mandate). Any hardware TLS termination (load balancers, network appliances, cloud provider NICs) will require this functionality. TAM includes cloud computing, financial services network infrastructure, and CDN hardware.

ideas.title: Quantum-Safe Hardware Security Module with PQC-Internally-Secured Boot Chain, PQC-Wrapped Audit Log, and Lattice-Signed Attestation Quote Architecture

desc.paragraph_1: Current FIPS 140-3 Level 3 certified HSMs (Thales Luna, Entrust nShield, Utimaco Se-Series) expose a critical architectural gap: while they offer PQC algorithm support via firmware-updated software libraries, their internal boot processes, firmware signing, key-wrapping infrastructure, and audit log integrity mechanisms continue to rely on classical RSA-2048 or ECDSA-P384 signatures. This means the entire trust chain of the HSM — from silicon power-on to application API — remains vulnerable to harvest-now-decrypt-later attacks and, once a cryptographically relevant quantum computer (CRQC) exists, to immediate root compromise. The ABI Research analysis (2025) identifies Crypto4A as the only vendor with a genuine PQC-internal architecture, but with limited published patent coverage.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention discloses a quantum-safe HSM architecture comprising: (a) an immutable PQC-rooted boot ROM that contains the ML-DSA public verification key for the first-stage bootloader, preventing any classical signature from appearing in the trust anchor; (b) a PQC-wrapped master key hierarchy where all internal key-encryption keys (KEKs) are wrapped under ML-KEM encapsulation rather than RSA or AES-wrapped RSA, ensuring the key wrapping tree is quantum-resistant from the root; (c) a lattice-signed tamper-evident audit log maintained in dedicated hardware with each log entry chained via ML-DSA signature over the previous entry's hash, creating an append-only quantum-safe ledger of all HSM operations; and (d) an ML-DSA attestation quote generator that produces a certified report of the HSM's PQC configuration, active algorithm set, and key hierarchy state, enabling external relying parties to verify the quantum-safety of the HSM's current operating state.
desc.paragraph_3: The architectural novelty is the complete elimination of classical asymmetric cryptography from the HSM's internal trust chain while maintaining FIPS 140-3 compliance and backward compatibility at the external API. The PQC-wrapped master key hierarchy requires a novel key migration protocol that transitions existing RSA-wrapped KEKs to ML-KEM-wrapped KEKs without ever exposing plaintext master key material to the host system. The lattice-signed audit log, with its hardware-enforced append-only property and per-entry ML-DSA signatures, provides post-quantum non-repudiation of HSM operations that is essential for regulatory compliance frameworks (PCI DSS, GDPR, HIPAA) operating beyond the CRQC horizon.

claim.preamble: A hardware security module comprising:
limitations: (a) a boot ROM containing a post-quantum digital signature verification key configured to verify a first-stage bootloader using a module-lattice-based digital signature algorithm (ML-DSA), wherein the boot ROM contains no classical asymmetric cryptographic material for trust anchor purposes;

(b) a post-quantum key hierarchy circuit configured to maintain a master key encryption key (KEK) wrapped under a post-quantum key encapsulation mechanism (ML-KEM), wherein all subordinate key-encryption keys in the hierarchy are derived or wrapped using post-quantum cryptographic operations;

(c) a hardware audit log circuit comprising a tamper-resistant append-only memory and a digital signature circuit configured to generate a post-quantum digital signature over each audit log entry chained to a hash of the preceding log entry, wherein the audit log circuit prevents modification or deletion of existing log entries in hardware;

(d) an attestation quote generator configured to produce a signed attestation record identifying the hardware security module's current cryptographic algorithm configuration, key hierarchy state, and firmware version, wherein the attestation record is signed using a post-quantum digital signature algorithm.

depClaims: Hybrid boot chain: parallel classical and PQC signature verification during transition period with configurable policy for accepting classical-only signatures until a defined sunset timestamp
PQC key migration protocol: hardware-assisted KEK migration path that transitions RSA-wrapped keys to ML-KEM-wrapped keys atomically without plaintext key exposure
Quantum-safe secure channel for HSM management: IETF-standardized hybrid TLS protocol for HSM administration API using hardware ML-KEM + ECDH combiner
Multi-party key ceremony support: hardware protocols for distributed key generation across N HSM instances with ML-DSA-signed ceremony transcripts
Automated crypto-agility triggered migration: policy-based automatic migration of wrapped keys to newer PQC algorithms upon administrator-configured algorithm deprecation trigger
Post-quantum certificate authority integration: hardware support for X.509 certificates carrying ML-DSA signatures for HSM-issued certificates and CRL signing

refs.ref: Crypto4A QxHSM QASM architecture (product, limited patent disclosure)
refs.diff: Crypto4A is the commercial leader in PQC-internal HSM architecture. Their QASM architecture supports hybrid RoTs and PQC-secured boot. However, their specific patent position on PQC-wrapped master key hierarchy, hardware lattice-signed audit log, and attestation quote generator with ML-DSA is not identified in public patents. Filing now captures apparatus claims before Crypto4A files.

refs.ref: US9049010B2 - Portable data encryption device with configurable HSM (2012)
refs.diff: This patent covers a portable device with a cryptographic processor for secret-sharing-based key management using classical cryptography. No PQC algorithms, ML-KEM key wrapping, or lattice-signed audit log are taught.

refs.ref: Thales Luna HSM PQC firmware updates (product, limited specific patent claims)
refs.diff: Thales's PQC support is at the external API layer via firmware updates. The internal boot chain, KEK hierarchy, and audit log remain classically signed. The present invention claims the PQC-internal architecture where classical asymmetric crypto is absent from the trust chain entirely.

refs.ref: US9405912B2 - Hardware rooted attestation (2013)
refs.diff: This patent covers seed morphing for fTPM attestation. It uses classical hash-chaining and does not address post-quantum key hierarchy, lattice-signed audit logs, or ML-DSA attestation quotes. The PQC-internal trust chain is entirely novel relative to this.

ideas.ftoJ: Crypto4A has commercial products with PQC-internal architecture (QxHSM QASM). If they have filed but not yet published patent applications (18-month publication delay), there may be overlap. Recommend monitoring USPTO publication queue for Crypto4A applications. Claim narrowing to specific PQC-wrapped KEK hierarchy structure and hardware lattice-signed audit log circuit reduces overlap risk. File provisional immediately to establish priority date.

ps.s101n: Apparatus claim on a specific hardware security module with defined circuit components. Strong patent-eligible subject matter.
ps.s102n: Novelty risk primarily from Crypto4A's undisclosed patent applications. The specific hardware audit log circuit with ML-DSA chaining and the attestation quote generator are distinguishable but require careful claim drafting. The PQC-wrapped master KEK hierarchy apparatus is likely novel in published prior art.
ps.s103n: Non-obvious because the specific combination of PQC-rooted boot ROM + PQC-wrapped KEK hierarchy + hardware ML-DSA audit log creates an integrated architecture where the novelty of each component is strengthened by the synergistic combination.
ps.s112n: Well-enabled by FIPS 203/204 specifications and HSM design literature. Hardware audit log circuit details require specification.

ideas.valM: HSM market: $1.5B in 2024, growing to $4.2B by 2030. Government/defense mandates for PQC-internal HSM by 2030. Chokepoint for any HSM seeking FIPS 140-3 validation with PQC-internal architecture. Potential standards-essential position in NIST HSM guidelines for PQC.

ideas.title: Reconfigurable PQC-TLS Hardware Offload Engine with Stateful Session Table, In-Line Key Encapsulation Acceleration, and Zero-Copy Handshake Pipeline for High-Throughput Network Infrastructure

desc.paragraph_1: TLS 1.3 session establishment with post-quantum key encapsulation introduces a fundamental performance challenge for high-throughput network infrastructure: ML-KEM-768 key encapsulation produces a 1,088-byte ciphertext (vs. 32 bytes for ECDH X25519), increasing TLS ClientHello payload size by ~25x and requiring new handshake pipeline architecture. PQShield's PQPerform-Flare achieves up to 1M ML-KEM key encapsulations per second, but it operates as an offline PCIe-attached accelerator requiring data copies between host memory and accelerator, introducing latency of 20-50µs per operation that becomes the bottleneck at 10GbE+ line rates. At 100GbE, a new TLS session must be established every 1µs to saturate the link with 1KB connections — far exceeding what a copy-based PCIe accelerator can achieve.
desc.paragraph_2: This invention discloses a PQC-TLS hardware offload engine for inline network processing, comprising: (a) an in-line TLS parser circuit that extracts ClientHello key_share extensions from network packets and routes ML-KEM ciphertexts directly to the key encapsulation engine without host processor involvement; (b) a stateful session table implemented in high-bandwidth on-chip SRAM that maintains per-connection TLS handshake state and ML-KEM context across multi-packet handshake exchanges, supporting up to 256K simultaneous handshakes; (c) a zero-copy handshake pipeline where decapsulated shared secrets are passed directly to the HKDF/SHAKE256 key schedule circuit without transiting host memory, eliminating DMA copy latency; and (d) a line-rate cipher engine that switches between ML-KEM-accelerated session key establishment and AES-GCM/ChaCha20-Poly1305 bulk encryption without the reconfiguration delay associated with FPGA partial bitstream reload.
desc.paragraph_3: The critical performance innovation is the stateful session table with multi-packet ML-KEM context preservation. Unlike ECDH (single round-trip, 32-byte public key), ML-KEM requires a two-message exchange with 1,088-2,400 byte key material that may span multiple network packets. The hardware session table maintains the partial KEM state across packet boundaries, enabling true in-line processing without host CPU involvement for the KEM computation. Combined with the zero-copy pipeline from ciphertext to decapsulated secret to HKDF circuit, the engine achieves an end-to-end PQC-TLS handshake latency of under 5µs at line rate — 10x better than PCIe-attached accelerator approaches. Target applications include hyperscaler TLS termination hardware, content delivery network edge nodes, 5G network function virtualization platforms, and financial trading infrastructure requiring low-latency secure connectivity.

claim.preamble: A network processing apparatus for post-quantum TLS acceleration, comprising:
limitations: (a) an in-line TLS parser circuit configured to extract key encapsulation ciphertext payloads from network packets according to TLS 1.3 ClientHello extension formats, without routing the extracted ciphertext through a host processor memory hierarchy;

(b) a stateful session table implemented in on-chip memory, configured to maintain per-connection TLS handshake state and key encapsulation mechanism (KEM) partial computation context across multiple network packets belonging to a single TLS handshake, supporting at least 1000 simultaneous TLS handshake sessions;

(c) a post-quantum key encapsulation engine coupled to the TLS parser circuit through a zero-copy data path, configured to receive key encapsulation ciphertext directly from the TLS parser circuit and to execute a decapsulation operation producing a shared secret without the shared secret transiting host processor memory;

(d) a key schedule circuit coupled to the key encapsulation engine through a zero-copy data path, configured to receive the shared secret directly from the key encapsulation engine and to compute a TLS 1.3 session key using a hash-based key derivation function, wherein the session key is produced within a hardware security boundary.

depClaims: Session resumption acceleration: hardware PSK binder table supporting TLS 1.3 session ticket resumption with PQC-derived ticket keys, enabling sub-µs resumption latency
Multi-algorithm session table: session table entries supporting hybrid ECDH+ML-KEM sessions with per-session algorithm selection flags for migration support
Traffic steering and load balancing: hardware hash-based session affinity for distributing TLS sessions across multiple parallel KEM engine instances
Hardware certificate cache: on-chip cache for ML-DSA-signed server certificates reducing repeated signature verification latency across multiple sessions to the same server
QUIC protocol support: extension of the PQC offload engine to handle QUIC Initial packets with embedded ML-KEM handshake payloads for post-quantum QUIC
Hardware-enforced certificate pinning: immutable on-chip storage of trusted ML-DSA public keys for pinned connections, preventing MITM attacks even if CA infrastructure is compromised

refs.ref: PQShield PQPerform-Flare/Inferno (PCIe-attached PQC accelerators)
refs.diff: PQShield's hardware operates as a standalone PCIe peripheral with a defined API for key encapsulation requests. Data must be copied from network buffers to PCIe BAR space. The present invention claims an in-line network processing apparatus with a TLS parser circuit and zero-copy data path — a fundamentally different architectural approach with no TLS packet parsing or stateful session table in PQShield's products.

refs.ref: JP2023531241A - TLS Integration of PQC Algorithms (Cisco/Rambus area)
refs.diff: This patent covers TLS protocol integration methods (ClientHello/ServerHello extensions). It is a method claim without hardware apparatus elements. The present invention's in-line TLS parser, stateful session table, zero-copy pipeline, and key schedule circuit are novel apparatus elements not taught.

refs.ref: Classical TLS offload engine patents (Cavium/Marvell NITROX, Intel QuickAssist)
refs.diff: Classical TLS offload patents (ECDH, RSA, AES-GCM) predate FIPS 203 and do not account for the multi-packet session table requirements of ML-KEM's larger key material. The stateful session table with ML-KEM partial context preservation is novel, as is the zero-copy path from ML-KEM decapsulation to HKDF.

refs.ref: Dyber QuantaM2 / QUAC100 (M.2 PQC accelerator)
refs.diff: Dyber's hardware is a general-purpose PQC computation device (PCIe/M.2 attached) with an ML-KEM/ML-DSA API. It does not include an in-line TLS parser, stateful session table for handshake management, or zero-copy pipeline. It requires host driver interaction for each operation.

ideas.ftoJ: Classical TLS offload patents from Marvell (Cavium NITROX), Intel (QuickAssist), and Broadcom (BCM5840) contain broad TLS session acceleration claims. Claim drafting must carefully distinguish ML-KEM's multi-packet session table requirements and zero-copy path as novel elements not anticipated by classical TLS offload architectures. Monitoring Marvell/Broadcom PQC hardware filings is recommended.

ps.s101n: Clear apparatus claim on a specific network processing device with defined structural elements. Strong eligibility.
ps.s102n: Classical TLS offload prior art is extensive (Marvell, Intel, Broadcom). Novel elements are the ML-KEM stateful session table with partial context preservation and the zero-copy HKDF pipeline from decapsulation output. These must be distinguished clearly in claims.
ps.s103n: Non-obvious because adapting classical TLS offload to PQC requires specifically accounting for the multi-packet ML-KEM key exchange flow — a challenge that does not exist in ECDH-based TLS. A PHOSITA skilled in TLS offload hardware would not derive this architecture without understanding the ML-KEM ciphertext size and multi-round exchange requirements.
ps.s112n: Well-enabled. TLS parser circuit specification, session table entry format, and HKDF pipeline are standard network processor design elements applied to PQC.

ideas.valM: High-value commercial targets: cloud provider custom silicon (AWS Nitro, Google Titanium, Microsoft Maia), CDN hardware vendors (Cloudflare hardware, Akamai), network equipment manufacturers (Cisco, Juniper, Arista). TLS termination hardware market >$6B/year. Standards-essential adjacency once IETF hybrid TLS becomes mandatory. Cross-license leverage against Marvell/Broadcom TLS offload portfolios.
